###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Greg Leman <gleman@metagenix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DCB040.DEF5BEB9@metagenix.com>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop> <7b9ask$7gm$2@news-2.news.gte.net> <7b9ghb$3uk@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <7b9rel$d4q$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`

```
Not exactly an impossible task.  Not a fun task, but what you need here is a
metadata profiling tool that will allow you to reverse engineer the metadata
from the actual data.  We happen to have such a tool, named MetaRecom (tm).  You
can read all about it at http://www.metagenix.com.

If you have the copybooks, there are plenty of tools that will extract the FDs.
We happen to have one -- Cobol/Stage -- that will also preload the jobs into
DataStage (the industry leading extract, transformation, and load tool).  Just
push a button and you've generated a complete job that will take your legacy
cobol data into almost anything, for instance an ODBC compliant RDBMS.

PAULO CRUZ wrote:

> Am I wrong or have already heard about cobol utilities that, given a
> Cobol file, can map out its description in terms of size of records,
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>>structure??
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** "Topcoder" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bnv1n$9s@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>`

```

Kevin Gagnon wrote in message
<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>...
>I am tasked with TRYING to pull data from an existing COBOL program
>(programmer unknown) and convert it to a DB4 database.  I know
nothing
>about a COBOL data structure.  How is the data stored.....
Customers,
>Invoices..... etc?
>
>Is it possible to pull data from a COBOL program and map into a DB4
>structure??
>
Let's start with a clarification:  Did you mean IBM's DB2, or
Borland's dBase IV.

Second point:  The first step in any successfull systems analysis
and design project is to define the problem.  So be careful about
using 'expressions like "pull data from an existing COBOL program".
I *think* I know what you mean - you want to convert a database
generated by the COBOL program you referred to to "DB4"?

If so, that will entail writing a conversion program or three.

You should be able to determine the current segment layouts
from the COBOL source code or listing, if available.  If not,
the job will be a little trickier.  You also need to define
the DB2/4? segment layouts.

One solution would be to write COBOL programs to walk the
current Customer, Invoice, Sales Order, etc databases
and create flat files to be input into your new DB load programs.

As you say know nothing about COBOL, you, I am afraid,
are not the ideal candidate for this task.

I am not a contractor, so it is not self-serving for me to suggest
that your company should outsource this task.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** boblorenze@aol.com (BobLorenze)
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990307104416.15819.00005333@ng141.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7bnv1n$9s@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Well, I am a consultant with 20+ years COBOL experience, 10+ years C/C++, and a
past partner at Mathematica, Inc., and am involved in a similar task right now.
It is a formidable, but not impossible task but I agree with the people who say
you need the FDs. Sure makes life a lot easier.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** Harry Mason <hcmason@shell5.ba.best.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.BSF.4.05.9903121525460.7581-100000@shell5.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>`

```
Hi:
I did something like this years ago.  DB4 was that ashton-tate
then borland database....Nice XBASE language...
Kevin: What you need to do is the following:

Get a copy of the COBOL Source code.
Look at the select assign statements and the FD (file definition)
language details.  Look for the record layout of the file.

Write a cobol program to create a new file delimited by semicolons
(the output fields).

Use the import fucntion of DB4.  Append from or some such...
That is how I would do it.  I could do it in a week.
But I have 14 years experience and can read cobol and immediately
(depending on my mood and how many cups of coffeee)
understand cobol data descriptions...

Email me the record layout and I'll code you a dump program
for a small fee...

Good luck,
Chris "show me the money" mason.

On Sat, 27 Feb 1999, Kevin Gagnon wrote:

> I am tasked with TRYING to pull data from an existing COBOL program
> (programmer unknown) and convert it to a DB4 database.  I know nothing
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Newbie needs help with move — 9 this month / 55 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-ac805e222a)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w5.t5.h02q30XoRsq4boMs4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36c37985.1325319@news> <36C3BC70.40274D12@att.net> <x4Ww2.7794$Fs.8593441@news4.mia>`

```
"JM" == "Judson McClendon" writes:
--- major snip ---

JM> How about it Report Writer advocates?  Any comments? :-)
JM> --
JM> Judson McClendon      judmc123@bellsouth.net  (remove numbers)
JM> Sun Valley Systems    http://personal.bhm.bellsouth.net/~judmc
JM> "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son,
JM> that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have
JM> everlasting life."
JM> 
I'm definately pro RW.  Different levels of breaks & everything else
in the report using RW forces you to be structured.  But, the* big
*downside to RW is twofold, namely:

1) Many mainframe shops forbid its use, and now with COBOL II, won't
spend the money for the RW pre-compiler option, thereby rendering its
coding worse than useless because when it compiles, it will return
errors.

2) COBOL programmers themselves not understanding its usefulness in
maintaining structure & dreading having to learn it & the
accompanying coding techniques involved to be able to use it.

Personally, I /*can't*/ think of a situation where RW can't handle a
report.  It does present problems for less experienced RW coders who
need to change their way of thinking & coding for reports via RW.  I
coded a statistics report for a life insurance company.  This
involved many USE BEFORE REPORTING... and the use of MOVE 1 TO
PRINT-SWITCH, which on IBM mainframes, would be the equivalent of
SUPPRESS on other systems in the DECLARATIVES SECTION.  In fact, my
DECLARATIVES SECTION had the bulk of the PROCEDURE DIVISION code in
it.

Nowadays, there are many 3rd party report programs to code ad hoc
reports much faster than using COBOL for reports, whether you use RW
or not.

Jay
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bj7uf$al5@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36c37985.1325319@news> <36C3BC70.40274D12@att.net> <x4Ww2.7794$Fs.8593441@news4.mia> <w5.t5.h02q30XoRsq4boMs4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`

```
Jay Siegel wrote in message ...
>"JM" == "Judson McClendon" writes:
>--- major snip ---
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>


As a strong RW "supporter" (and user), let me mention that there are two
"common report" features that you can't do now with ANSI/ISO RW - or with
the next Standard (although they were briefly discussed).

1) Page N of Y processing on headers/footers

2) Automated Odd/Even Page footers/headers

Both of these CAN be done if you really struggle with your code (and are
willing to process your report twice - either in "virtual" or "disk").

However, I should make it clear that I agree with your assessment on why
people don't use it - and in most cases - it would really make their
programming easier.
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie needs help with move

- **From:** "ddrider@banet.net" <ddrider@banet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DB4E6E.481D@banet.net>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
junkee@bigfoot.com wrote:
> 
> Hi, i am in an intro to cobol class and am having problems with my first
…[126 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
I remember what it was like when I first started troubleshooting COBOL
code. Beginners tend to misspell their commands and field names often,
as well as miss their periods and quotation marks (heck, I still do it).
Keep trying, and remember, the answer is always in front of you. Just
wait until you start tables, control-break logic, and master/transaction
file processing. There are plenty of other system and logic errors you
have yet to explore.

COBOL RDBMS
Dan DeMoura  
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie needs help with move

hi,
the record lengths for hdr-1 and your print-area are not the same.  

plus you keep overlaying your data with your multiple moves

hope this helps

eric

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie needs help with move

- **From:** "Charlie Silverman" <csilverman@cwix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tmdE2.1413$rs4.906@news.cwix.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DB4E6E.481D@banet.net>`

```
The move error is due to referencing HDR1 in your move statement,
you defined it as HDR-1.  Also you should be setting the CC field, then
move the data to the print area, then print each heading line in your
INIT section. For example:
   MOVE '1' TO PRINT-CC
   MOVE HDR-1 TO PRINT-AREA
   WRITE PRINT-RECORD
      and so on.
ddrider@banet.net wrote in message <36DB4E6E.481D@banet.net>...
>junkee@bigfoot.com wrote:
>>
…[139 more quoted lines elided]…
>BMv
```

> 
> The move error is due to referencing HDR1 in your move statement,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>       and so on.

As I read this message, I'm wondering what what's the future of control
characters.  I've switched back and forth between platforms which made
these visible and not since I started programming.  Which method do
people prefer?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Print control-characters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c19r7$95v@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DB4E6E.481D@banet.net> <tmdE2.1413$rs4.906@news.cwix.com> <36E404A2.F87B816E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E404A2.F87B816E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Charlie Silverman wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>people prefer?

I much prefer the "WRITE AFTER ADVANCING" (or - to refer to another thread -
Report Writer) to do my "carriage control" stuff.  This is portable across
platforms and compilers. Obviously, if you are trying to write out PCL or
AFP "formatted" stuff, you need to get into sending "funny control
sequences" - but for a simple text report, I don't see using the (at least
on IBM) "ASA" characters.
```

Actually, I've had three flavors:

And moving to the OS's control language is another issue altogether.
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi Donald.  I use a little bit of everything.  Since I have a number
of business and personal email addresses, I use Outlook 98, Netscape
Communicator 4.5, and Free Agent.

Outlook 98 is vastly better than Outlook 97.  A better and more
accurate name for Outlook 97 is Outlook 1.0.  And as we all know,
never buy version 1.0 of anything made by Microsoft.  I use Outlook 98
for my business email.

Free Agent is what I use for newgroups.  My only complaint is there
isn't any hyperlinking and no spellchecker, but it's freeware and I
like the way it lays the posts out.

And I use Netscape Communicator (Messenger) for personal email.  

Both Outlook98 and Messenger have spellchecker and can be setup to
handle more than one email address.



Tim Oxler
TEO Computer Technologies Inc.
http://www.teo-computer.com

<a id="t-a70469f2a8"></a>
## Thread: Obsolete coding methods — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
decades since I have seen either.
```

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MDyC2.19$Ek.172039@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
>with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
>decades since I have seen either.

I do not remember the last time I saw an ALTER, but it has been over
20 years at least.  To my embarrassment, I admit to using a few as a
young pup, before learning better (my assembly background you see,
and structured programming hadn't been invented yet).

But it has only been since about 1995 that I worked with a Unisys 'V'
Series (the old Burroughs Medium Systems) computer, where Procedure
Division segmenting was quite common because of the architecture.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** Peter W. Smith <Peter_W._Smith@LNOTES3.bankofny.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7beue0$53f$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com> <MDyC2.19$Ek.172039@news1.mia>`

```
I just finished rewriting a 60 program reporting system that used ALTERs. It
also did things I didn't know could be done
ex.

Procedure Division using xxx.
Return-exit.
    exit.
100-para-a.
    display 'XXX ' xxx.                     <-- executed first time through
    perform 200-para-b thru return-exit.
    display 'XXX ' xxx.                     <-- executed second time through
    perform 300-para-c thru return-exit.
    display 'XXX ' xxx.                     <-- executed third time through
    goback.
200-para-b.
    add 1 to xxx.
    goback.
300-para-c.
    add 2 to xxx.
    goback.

The calling program calls the sub-program repeatedly passing xxx
The output would be :
    XXX 000
    XXX 001
    XXX 003

This is much simpler than the real logic, but you get the idea.


In article <MDyC2.19$Ek.172039@news1.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

*************************************************************
*  He's thinking for himself, quick, hit him with a stick.  *
*************************************************************

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

-Paul

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** Clifford E Williams <williams@southern.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DABA86.42C6CB9B@southern.edu>`
- **References:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The only ALTER that I have ever seen was one I used in a classroom
exercise in 1976 (when I was learning to program).  Oddly enough the
teacher did not seem to think there was anything wrong with it.

We use memory segmentation every day.  How else do you get humongous
programs to work _well_ on a minicomputer?  I do not just mean get them
to work at all, but _well_.



Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
> with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
> decades since I have seen either.
```

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dae5cd.30258789@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Mon, 01 Mar 1999 08:08:48 -0700, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
>with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
>decades since I have seen either.

Not used ALTER but had to use procedure div. segmentation whilst
working with micros with less than 64K. (no typo - 65535 bytes)

I used to maintain FORMS II - Micro Focus' early screen designer which
was an add on to Level II COBOL. That ran quite happily in less than
64K. 3 segments if I recall correctly.

It was written in 1979 before the PC and ran on an old Altos (I think)
with 8 inch floppies. Still being sold up until 1991 I think but only
in Japan.
```

Actually, it was when I worked with a MAINFRAME with partitions that

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KnJC2.3031$Nz.1087439@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Well, ALTER sure is but sometimes they insist that a big program stay one
source module, but you know that you will stay within one are then leave and
stay in another for awhile.  EVen on CICS with MVS, this can still
help performance.  (I've worked on 75K line On-Line Programs)
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
>with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
>decades since I have seen either.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** iscdavid@aol.com (ISCDavid)
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990305111553.23503.00003572@ng-fu1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<KnJC2.3031$Nz.1087439@news2.mia>`

```
I knew about ALTER since the days of DOD COBOL 60...but the only time I
actually used it was in the mid-70s on an online banking system that ran on
Interdata minis.

And what we did was legit. For a whole bunch of reasons we were driving a lot
of dissimilar devices over a single phone line, so we'd ALTER the code to poll
each type. Really it was just setting a switch...and once Interdata gave us the
code to OS32, we took the ALTER out of the COBOL and stuck it into the PASLA
(Async line adapter) driver at the OS level instead:)

Ah...the good ole days...multitasking OS that worked on a 32K machine, source
to the OS, AND source for the COBOL compiler itself:) Yep...we rolled our own
extensions to COBOL...like a working SCREEN SECTION long before most compilers
had one.

David
```

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** WDS.WDS@WDS.WDS (WDS)
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Mon, 01 Mar 1999 08:08:48 -0700, Howard Brazee wrote:

>I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
>with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
>decades since I have seen either.

I think that we got rid of our last program containing ALTERs several
years ago.  Debugging them could be quite a challenge.

On Unisys V-Series systems, segmentation is still fairly common,
because there is no such thing as virtual memory on these systems.

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

On Mon, 01 Mar 1999 08:08:48 -0700, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs

Not that long ago.

My last run into ALTER was in 1994.  And thankfully, the entire app
was replaced.

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING has only been recently (@ 6mths ago) replace
at one of my the client sites.  And they have clients using older
versions of their code still using it.

Other oldie, and not so goldie:  
ROSCOE  -  1995
Mainframe Line Editor  -  1986 (for a Honeywell 5000)
PC Line Editor - 1985
A mainframe shop that didn't have any debugging tools (such as
Abend-Aid, InterTest, Viasoft, etc.) or utilities (other than IBM
utilities and SyncSort)  -  1992
	Oh the joys of Dump reading.  Or Deblocking and Reblocking
records with lengths greater than 256 bytes, to edit those records.

#### ↳ Re: Obsolete coding methods

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E1BBB1.5239@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<36DAAD80.E42514A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I wonder how many of us are still around who have worked with programs
> with ALTERs and PROCEDURE DIVISION memory segmentation.  It's been
> decades since I have seen either.
In the earlier days of MVS virtual memory systems (S370-158, 1970's), we
used segmentation frequently. By using sections in the source, Csects
were generated, and you would then use the linkage editor to page align
and order the Csects in your load module for efficiency.

Bob 
```

<a id="t-5bd95c00c9"></a>
## Thread: Exit program vs. Goback — 24 msgs

### Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** "Lanny Levine" <llevine@sprint.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
Readers,

    I just recently came across an 'EXIT PROGRAM' in a called module. At
work, as far as I know, we use GOBACK exclusively in batch programs. I have
read the appropriate sections in 'IBM COBOL for MVS & VM - Language
Reference' SC26-4769-01 and can only see one possible difference: GOBACK can
be used in a main program to return control to the OS whereas EXIT PROGRAM
cannot be coded in a main program.

    I can see no difference between GOBACK and EXIT PROGRAM in called
programs. Do you know of any?

    Thanks,

    Lanny
```

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)

```

wrote:


>    I can see no difference between GOBACK and EXIT PROGRAM in called
>programs. Do you know of any?

EXIT PROGRAM is ANSI standard and GOBACK isn't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)

<simonc@casegen.co.uk> wrote:

>Every COBOL program I have ever seen has used a GOBACK (Lots of IBM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990302103436.13571.00002420@ng120.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net>`

```
Shaun C. Murray writes...

[snip]

>No idea. Certainly not wanted in any of my non-CICS programs. GOBACK
>in a main program just doesn't make sense to me. GOBACK where?
>

Actually, GOBACK makes perfect sense. It says GOBACK to whoever called you. If
you are a subroutine, it returns to the mainline; if you are a main program, it
returns to the operating system. There is no need to remember EXIT PROGRAM vs.
STOP RUN: GOBACK satisfies both needs, a gentle multi-purpose statement.

Regards,




Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net>`

```

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net>...
>On Mon, 1 Mar 1999 21:32:05 -0000, "Simon Cordingley"
><simonc@casegen.co.uk> wrote:
>
>>Every COBOL program I have ever seen has used a GOBACK (Lots of IBM
>>experience) for both MAIN and SUB programs/routines. Could be lots and
lots
>>of maintenance in the future! We even use it for documentation purposes in
>>CICS programs.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Because (as I thought this stream made quite clear) GoBack and Exit Program
are really quite different.  They are IDENTICAL in a subprogram, but in a
MAIN program, GoBack is equivalent to Stop Run.  There was (and is) no
ANSI/ISO Standard way of ending a program if you don't know whether you are
in a SubProgram or Main program.  This is what the statement solves (and
will solve in the next Standard).
```

On Tue, 2 Mar 1999 12:08:02 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:


>
>
>Because (as I thought this stream made quite clear) GoBack and Exit Program

No, I understood that quite clearly. Worked that out the first time I
came across GOBACK in other peoples source. 

>  There was (and is) no
>ANSI/ISO Standard way of ending a program if you don't know whether you are
>in a SubProgram or Main program.  This is what the statement solves (and

I guess I always know if I'm in a sub or main program in the software
I design but it's pretty much not your typical CICS style system. The
other solution for that problem I've seen is to put EXIT PROGRAM
followed by STOP RUN in your programs.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <kerrye@ms.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DEB2CF.A11C1B72@ms.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net> <7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> There was (and is) no ANSI/ISO Standard way of ending
> a program if you don't know whether you are in a
> SubProgram or Main program.
>

Oh? What was (and is) wrong with Exit Program immediately
followed by a Stop Run (plus, of course, the appropriate para-
graph headers inserted so that the code conforms to the std)?
A bit unwieldy perhaps, but perfectly standard (and it works,
too!).  The introduction of the GOBACK statement into the
standard just makes coding such "I don't know what invoked
me" programs easier (and turns thousands of nonstandard batch
Cobol programs written without any concern for the standard
(but with a nod and a "Thank you" to IBM extensions that allow
the programmer to save precisely two keystrokes) into standard
ones).

William M. Klein wrote:

> Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net>...
> >On Mon, 1 Mar 1999 21:32:05 -0000, "Simon Cordingley"
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bn4v4$ehd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net> <7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36DEB2CF.A11C1B72@ms.com>`

```
It is true that the following ANSI/ISO conforming code is "equivalent" to a
GoBack,

   Perform GoBack-Para1
   Perform GoBack-Para2
   .
GoBack-Para1.
    Exit Program.
 GoBack-Para2.
    Stop Run.

On the other hand, it is also true that you can code "equivalents" to
STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT via "simpler" verbs - but I wouldn't call that
really "existing" functionality.  Several people (used to) claim that you
could just code

   Exit Program
   Stop Run.

as a sequence of statements in an ANSI/ISO conforming program, but it
violated the rules of NOT having other statements in a "sequence" with an
EXIT PROGRAM statement.  I don't think that any of us are really arguing
about the usefulness of GoBack.

P.S.  to Kerry and others,
  If possible, please use "Plain text" rather than HTML when posting to
comp.lang.cobol.  This isn't required, but it sure makes life easier for
many readers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<36DFEA20.54726A72@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net> <7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36DEB2CF.A11C1B72@ms.com> <7bn4v4$ehd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

The first time I worked with IBM, we didn't use STOP RUN nor GOBACK. 
Instead we did a call - was it CALL EXIT?   I don't remember why except
that's what we were supposed to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <kerrye@ms.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E04E56.31E459C6@ms.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net> <7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36DEB2CF.A11C1B72@ms.com> <7bn4v4$ehd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> Several people (used to) claim that you
> could just code
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I do not know who those "several people" were or are, but I am not among
them.  However, I think that you are misinterpreting the Standard -- which
concerns me because, as I understand it, you are currently contributing to
the new Revision (if not, then please accept my most humble apologies).

I assert that the following is perfectly correct; i.e.: standard:

     .
     .
     .
    CLOSE INPUT-FILE, OUTPUT-FILE.

998-RETURN-TO-CALLER.
    EXIT PROGRAM.

999-RETURN-TO-OPERATING-SYS.
    STOP RUN.

where the program falls from the last statement in the paragraph prior to
#998 (i.e.: the CLOSE statement; assuming that that paragraph is not
part of a PERFORMed procedure), and either executes the EXIT PROGRAM
if this is a called subprogram, or treats it as a no-op, falls through it, and
executes the STOP RUN, thereby terminating the "run unit" if this is a main
program.

Although IBM's manual is not a verbatim copy of the ANSI or ISO standard
documents, I doubt that it would have misinterpreted the standard lo these
several decades.  Here is what a recent IBM "COBOL for MVS Language
Reference" manual says:

>  If control reaches an EXIT PROGRAM statement, and no CALL statement
>  is active, control passes through the exit point to the next executable statement.

Your example of a sequence of Performed paragraphs, one containing an EXIT
PROGRAM and one containing a STOP RUN is -- IMHO -- spurious because
(despite the presence of a plethora of PERFORM statements) Performing a
STOP RUN statement is NOT well structured.

And -- finally -- I would claim that GoBack is NOT very useful, but it is slightly
more convenient.  I think that it is highly disingenuous to compare the kind of
code (usually pages and pages) necessary to provide "equivalences" to the
STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT statements with the coding of two
statements and two paragraph headers instead of a single GoBack statement.

Mind you, I cannot get myself all worked up about the presence of the GoBack
statement in the new standard; however, in the larger scheme of things, it is not
nearly as important as, say, adding a statement permitting the "early" exit
from a Performed procedure (I gather through the use of some new form of
the EXIT statement), or the implementation of recursive capabilities, or the
introduction of 4-digit year features into the ACCEPT (which will be hopelessly
moot by the time that the Standard is finally approved).

BTW, I just noticed that the restriction on EXIT PROGRAM being the last
statement in a sequence of imperative statements will be lifted at the SAME
time as GOBACK is introduced; therefore, the code fragment:

     IF INPUT-IS-AT-END-OF-FILE
       THEN
          CLOSE INPUT-FILE
          EXIT PROGRAM
          STOP RUN
     END-IF

will be perfectly legal (i.e.: "standard") as soon as GoBack is....

William M. Klein wrote:

> It is true that the following ANSI/ISO conforming code is "equivalent" to a
> GoBack,
…[85 more quoted lines elided]…
>             wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ "Nice to have" Enhancement in draft/next Standard (was Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bpshc$ap5@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net> <7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36DEB2CF.A11C1B72@ms.com> <7bn4v4$ehd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36E04E56.31E459C6@ms.com>`

```
You are correct that using "fall thru logic" is valid with the current
Standard. (However, it is something that I just can't ever imagine
"recommending".)  You are also correct that the restriction on EXIT Program
being the last statement in a sequence of statements is being removed at the
same time as the addition of GoBack.

Given both of these facts, I still can see no "advantage" of using either of
these techniques over the currently commonly available (as an extension) and
planned inclusion of GoBack. Your term is "slightly convenient" and I would
not claim that it is much more than that.  However, I don't understand any
objection to "slightly convenient" and actually think that it is a "nice to
have" as are several other enhancements in the draft Standard.

The following is a list of several (but certainly not ALL) "slightly
convenient" features that (when added together) all seem to be VERY useful -
in the draft Standard. Almost all of these - like GoBack - are available
today as extensions in one or more implementations. (These are "summary"
phrases. For the exact feature, see the draft Standard)

    - 31 digit numeric items
    - Implementor defined successful status codes
    - Apostrophe as synonym for quotation symbol
    - Positive and Negative conditional phrases in any order (E.g.. NOT AT
END before AT END)
    - Call using non-elementary or 01-level arguments
    - Relative COLUMN number
    - Optional PICTURE Clause with VALUE clause (report writer)
    - Constants
    - literals is COPY member and library name (also program-id name)
    - Partial expressions in EVALUATE statement
    - Method of making FUNCTION an optional word
    - Expansion of where you can use numeric functions
    - In-line comments
    - INSPECT CONVERTING with multiply occurring items
    - Optionally of paragraph-name at beginning of Procedure Division
    - AFTER phrase with Inline PERFORM
    - REDEFINES of other than the 1st item
    - Dynamic file assignment via Select/Using (rather than Select/Assign)
    - SORT of tables
    - STOP with STATUS
    - DELIMITED optional in STRING
    - Subscripting with arithmetic expressions
    - Underscore in COBOL character set
    - Value clause allowed (but ignored) in Linkage Section and External
data
    - WRITE from a literal
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36db97b0.7262172@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```
On Mon, 1 Mar 1999 21:32:05 -0000, "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk> wrote:

>Every COBOL program I have ever seen has used a GOBACK (Lots of IBM
>experience) for both MAIN and SUB programs/routines. Could be lots and lots
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Standard answer number 1: It depends.

a)  The compiler in use is OLD (OS/VS COBOL).  Then the compiler complains it it doesn't
find a GOBACK or EXIT PROGRAM or STOP RUN anywhere in the program.  Therefore standard
programming practice was to put a GOBACK immediately after the EXEC CICS RETURN END-ENXEC,
where, of course, it would never be reached

b)  The CICS program in question is a VS COBOL II (or higher) program and has been invoked
via a COBOL CALL.  Then it should return to the MAIN program using GOBACK, and not EXEC
CICS RETURN


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36da8d86@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
Lanny Levine wrote in message ...
>    I can see no difference between GOBACK and EXIT PROGRAM in called
>programs. Do you know of any?


If you don't know (or will allow both) if a program is a main program or a
called
program, GOBACK is useful to exit the program. It is strictly speaking not
needed as the construction:

RETURN-FROM-PROGRAM.
     EXIT PROGRAM
     .
STOP-THE-RUN.
    STOP RUN
    .

has the same effect has GOBACK, but GOBACK is handier that
the above construction.
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990301084045.11956.00002567@ng-cg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
The difference is that Exit Program MUST appear on a line by it iteself and
GOBACK does not.  I understand that GOBACK was included in VS COBOL II for
backwards compatibility with older programs and is not an ANSI standard.  The
recommendation is to use EXIT PROGRAM as support for GOBACK may be dropped
soon.

Tom Wymer
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7beco3$m13@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <19990301084045.11956.00002567@ng-cg1.aol.com>`

```
This post isn't true.

  Exit Program can be coded any way that you want.

GoBack will be in the next Standard.

The original question was whether or not GoBack and Exit Program were the
same in a subprogram and the answer is YES, they are defined to be
identical.  The only difference is that GoBack is also equivalent to Stop
Run in a main program - so you don't need to know whether you are in a
subprogram or a main program when you code it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7beff6$bav$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <19990301084045.11956.00002567@ng-cg1.aol.com> <7beco3$m13@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <7beco3$m13@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>This post isn't true.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>subprogram or a main program when you code it.

Oh dear... well, at the risk of making myself look foolish let me, once
again, state my understandings, preferences and prejudices... which, of
course, are better'n anyone elses:

GOBACK returns control to the invoking program; in the case of a MAIN
program it returns control to the operating system.  There are *no*
difficulties or potential difficulties of which I am aware which one might
encounter when terminating execution of a program with a GOBACK.

STOP RUN does... something else which I do not have the terminology to
describe; STOP RUN stops, in my experience, *everything* associated with a
program and returns control to the operating system (or something like
that).  If a STOP RUN is issued in a subroutine (in certain environments
and platforms) then not only is the subroutine terminated but the MAIN
program is terminated as well.. Big Fun for debugging!

Since *anything* can be done with *any* program at *any* time it can be
concluded that, at any time, any MAIN program can be turned into a
subroutine for another program.  It is with this eventuality in mind that
I, personally, *only* code GOBACKs.

DD
>--
>Bill Klein (wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com)
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wvJC2.3035$Nz.1091329@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
You're close but no see gar: (sic)

GOBACK results in an UNCONDITIONAL RETURN to CALLER.  NO MATTER whether Main
or SUB.  In a SUB there is no difference
HOWEVER, (the ONLY difference between a Main and SUB is whether
the Linkage Section has a 2byte length item.  MVS/OSVS provides this
to ALL EXEC PGM= programs (so that the length of parms can be determined).
SO, if you run a Sub as a EXEC PGM= (say no data is passed)
the EXIT PROGRAM is 'NOP'ed so the NEXT SEQUENTIAL INSTRUCTION IS EXECUTED.

(I just finished a 2000 line assembler Dynamic Allocation/Delete
subroutine/Main program that I made able to be 'called' or 'Execed' by
checking byte 1 of the passed data for X'00' (it will never get more
than 116 bytes which is  X'7F' and so the 2 byte Comp pointer is always
less than X'0080'.  If byte 1 is X'00' then I bump up the pointer by 2 bytes
whichthen points the my data from mvs.
Lanny Levine wrote in message ...
>Readers,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Reference' SC26-4769-01 and can only see one possible difference: GOBACK
can
>be used in a main program to return control to the OS whereas EXIT PROGRAM
>cannot be coded in a main program.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

>You're close but no see gar: (sic)
>
>GOBACK results in an UNCONDITIONAL RETURN to CALLER.  NO MATTER whether
Main
>or SUB.  In a SUB there is no difference
>HOWEVER, (the ONLY difference between a Main and SUB is whether
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Which is JUST exactly what the ANSI/ISO Standard *demands* will happen when
you do an EXIT PROGRAM in a "main" program (i.e. it is treated like a
CONTINUE).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature

- **From:** Sundial Services <info@sundialservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<36DAD49B.56DC@sundialservices.com>`
- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net>`

```
Primoz Gabrijelcic wrote:
> 
> Edward Hill wrote:
> >I though it could be turned off in the BIOS and the only
> >worry was to people who didn't....


Bruce Scheirer recently posted the observation that Intel may have
introduced this feature as much to attack the problem of counterfeit or
stolen Pentium chips than for programmatic use.  Programs can never be
certain that, when querying to find out what the ID# is, they have been
told the truth.  Software could have intercepted the request and
substituted a forged ID# instead of the actual one.  (Software to do
this has already been published.)

"Cookies" and other readily-obtainable information are already widely
distributed, in clear, on the Internet to allow web-sites to recognize
users who have visited them before.  A built-in ID# would not really
make the situation any worse than it already is.  (You are already
embarrassingly "identifiable" on the net.)

All that a built-in ID# would really do is make the =chip=
identifiable.  Mainframe computers have had CPUIDs since time
immemorial, and they are used chiefly for copy protection purposes.  How
successful they would be for this purpose in the rough-and-tumble world
of PCs is ... doubtful at best.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard M. Smith" <smiths@tiac.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00

- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36DAD49B.56DC@sundialservices.com> <MPG.114492b5dcacd33298a8a9@news.dimensional.com>`

```
Kenny Chaffin wrote:

>>> I think it's Intel's biggest marketing Faux-pas yet. I think the public
>>> will react very negatively and (hopefully) intel will have to remove it
>>> from future releases....

I think that Microsoft is way ahead of Intel on serial numbers.

I've been following this Pentium III serial number controversy since last Wednesday
and became curious how serial numbers are already being used in Windows
software.  I made a very interesting discovery this afternoon.  Apparently
Microsoft is "fingerprinting" Excel and Word files with people's hardware
Ethernet adapter addresses.  Yikes!  (Ethernet adapter addresses are 48-bit
numbers which are designed to be unique just like the Pentium III serial
numbers.)

The big question is why is Microsoft doing this?

Here is a hex dump of a Word .DOC file with my Ethernet adapter address written out
in Unicode:

 3360:02 00 00 00 0A 00 00 00  5F 50 49 44 5F 47 55 49   ........|_PID_GUI
 3370:44 00 02 00 00 00 E4 04  00 00 41 00 00 00 4E 00   D.......|..A...N.
 3380:00 00 7B 00 45 00 38 00  36 00 43 00 33 00 42 00   ..{.E.8.|6.C.3.B.
 3390:36 00 30 00 2D 00 44 00  30 00 31 00 41 00 2D 00   6.0.-.D.|0.1.A.-.
 33A0:31 00 31 00 44 00 32 00  2D 00 41 00 36 00 37 00   1.1.D.2.|-.A.6.7.
 33B0:46 00 2D 00 30 00 30 00  32 00 30 00 37 00 38 00   F.-.0.0.|2.0.7.8.
 33C0:39 00 30 00 30 00 33 00  33 00 37 00 7D 00 00 00   9.0.0.3.|3.7.}...

(According to the WINIPCFG utilty that comes with Windows, my Ethernet adapter
address is 00-20-78-90-03-37.)

BTW, I'm running Office 97 for Windows.

Richard
```

Do you work for Intel? <g>.  Actually, the problem that I see with it is that
it will make it _even easier_ than it already is with cookies and the like to
find your browsing/shopping habits.  Now, at first this is not viewed as such a
huge problem.  I, however, belive that if a user feels like checking out some
porn on the web they should be free to do so without having everyone else know
about it.  I personally, don't do this and don't mind if "Big Brother" monitors
my net usage.  I do, however, feel it is my moral obligation, esp. as an
American to protect the privacy rights of others.  At least if you enable
cookies you know that others will see your info.  With the I.D. you will have
no idea who is "watching" you.  Also, the "give them an inch, they'll take a
mile" idea bothers me.  What next?

> I get it.  If they have your cpu's ID and your name, they have your
> name.
> If they have it and your SSN (SSN# is redundant), they have your SSN.
> With such tricks, they can find out what you like and stop sending you
> spam for things you don't like.
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Joachim Flodqvist <joachim@ecs.se>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<36DCE0F6.71A5@ecs.se>`
- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36DAD49B.56DC@sundialservices.com> <MPG.114492b5dcacd33298a8a9@news.dimensional.com> <36DAEC16.1C700CC7@NOSPAMhome.com> <MPG.11449cb54520206a98a8af@news.dimensional.com> <36DAFE25.20AE247B@NOSPAMhome.com> <MPG.1144c5b22c10217b98a8b6@news.dimensional.com> <36DB1AD1.5E216A51@NOSPAMhome.com> <36DBD863.A7DC3B39@mindless.com>`

```
Kathryn Taylor Jennings wrote:
> 
> Do you work for Intel? <g>.  Actually, the problem that I see with it is that
> it will make it _even easier_ than it already is with cookies and the like to
> find your browsing/shopping habits.

Lo-tech solution;
Why not start a chip-swingers club? I'll trade my cpu for John Doe's.
There's NO way they're gonna figure out my shopping habits then.

Hi-tech ditto:
There's bound to emerge one or max two major ways to generate the
cookie/tell the other side your cpu ID. Then we have a few ways to do
the hack;
- Patch the code in the browser so it doesn't pick _your_ cpu id, but
instead picks a random one.
- If cookie generation is done in hardware too, then just patch the
browser so it forwards the request-to-generate-cookie to a random
computer in the network.

It doesn't matter how it's done. There's always a way to break it.

The question is what the interpreters of the 'broken' information do
about it, and if they even realize the information can't be trusted.

>  Now, at first this is not viewed as such a
> huge problem.  I, however, belive that if a user feels like checking out some
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> American to protect the privacy rights of others.

Sounded good up until the 'I personally don't do this' etc. Why the need
to get defensive and point out that you don't? ...  and the stuff about
'moral obligation as an American to protect the others'.... By listening
to you, it sounds as if the only ones in need of protection are the ones
surfing for porn.

What about _your_own_ privacy? Are you sure you want people to know
about your liking Bourgogne wines, or looking up information on
apartments in London? These are just examples, and I do not in any way
imply that you DO like Bourgogne wines or are currently looking for an
apartment in London. BUT, continuing this hypothetical situation, if
your boss finds out you're looking for an apartment in london, he/she
might think you're thinking of quitting the company, and let some other
person get the promotion you'd otherwise have got.

It doesn't matter if the information is 'harmless'. If put in the wrong
hands/minds, somebody will be able to conclude you're a spy from Mars,
and that you in a previous reincarnation secretly lead an international
drug-ring from Koala Lumpur...

Regards
/Joachim
```

> > But why do you dislike it?
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> possibilities).

The fact is that anyone can know far more about you by interrogating the
cookies you have accepted than they would by obtaining your CPU-ID. 
Certainly they would obtain no less information.

Furthermore, the only way they could obtain the CPU-ID would be to ask
your browser to send it to them, and this would be subject to "spoofing"
by anyone who wanted to conceal their identity.  It would, frankly, be
simpler for them to ask for cookies.
```

- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36DAD49B.56DC@sundialservices.com> <MPG.114492b5dcacd33298a8a9@news.dimensional.com> <36DAEC16.1C700CC7@NOSPAMhome.com> <MPG.11449cb54520206a98a8af@news.dimensional.com> <36DB0509.1521@sundialservices.com> <36DC773F.37717FF@mbhsys.com>`

```
It was: Tue, 02 Mar 1999 18:41:51 -0500 and with STARTLING insight,  "Ed Hochman
<ed@mbhsys.com>" posted "Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID
feature" to "comp.lang.cobol" :

-->I don't think your cookies are available to anyone except the one that put them
-->there.
-->
-->Ed
-->
-->Sundial Services wrote:
-->
-->> The fact is that anyone can know far more about you by interrogating the
-->> cookies you have accepted than they would by obtaining your CPU-ID.
-->> Certainly they would obtain no less information.

-->Ed Hochman - MBH Systems - ed@mbhsys.com

<rant>
I believe that depends on the properties of the cookie and how the browser is configured.
The whole notion is abhorrent to the spirit of the internet.  I read some discussion by a
consortium of advertisers and ad brokers recently.  They explained how all this "free" stuff on
the web is there because advertising pays for it.  Why, if weren't for good old advertisers,
the web wouldn't even exist (this in the context of banner ads, graphics flopping about, hit
tracking and cookies.)  Funny me, I remember when the relatively first ads hit the net, and how
everyone objected, vocally, and at great length.  Up to that point, it was just a bunch of fun
loving beanie heads, and other winners, giving away info like it was going out of style.  It
was much easier, even with a text based browser, to find what you sought, if it was there and
findable.  You didn't have to wade through the advertising.  Everyone acted like it was
desirable to contribute, and perhaps garner a little bit of credit, sort of a Panglossian
world.  All of the techniques and innovation which are now touted by advertising concerns were
actually fomented and developed in that period; improvements to date are refinements and
implementation.  I'll warrant that the measurable content (remember that term?) has grown, and
is leaping and bounding all over the place, but how much is sales gibber and how much is
additional worthy content?  Of the worthy content, how much is actually attributable to web
advertisers?  I'm not saying that I own it, nor ever did, but can lament the passing of a
revolutionary moment not often seen for human kind.  Sure, advertising and advertisers are
going to force their place and attempt to turn the internet into an even more cheesy version of
the classifieds in the back of many magazines.  Sure, manufacturers will attempt to tattoo
permanent serial numbers on everyones foreheads till proper IC chips can be implanted in
everyone's brains, not just showing where they are and what they are doing but directing
pavlovian responses to things such as colors:  "Yellow -- gotta have a ratburger, quick!!"  I
guess that as the world fills up, and the generations pass on, each new one knows a little less
about personal space, and dare we say it, freedom from being tracked, among other little
inconsequential losses.  The importance of documenting your every preference to advertise you
more effectively into submission belies the premise of choice, and the freedom to choose.  It
is replaced by the freedom not to choose, at the moment.  Remember that it is important that
you be told, again & again, until you submit, that you need a ratburger NOW, lest you forget.
We all need this constant conditioning or we would never make these desirable choices.
Cookies, et al, are just a helpful way to make sure we never forget.  If we should, the very
helpful advertisers will courteously remind us and they know exactly what we need because they
keep such great records.  It's the best of all possible worlds.
    (yellow:ratburger, NOW!!)   (orange:ratsoda, too:NOW!!!)   (green:ratfries, NOW!!!)
</rant>

              tenox  @  home  dat   com
                                                                             /ts
```

There is a way by all that Unc. First, you get rid of all your
credit cards, and start dealing in cash only.  For that, you
use a debit card under a false name. The debit card is every
bit as convenient as a credit card ... in fact a good deal more
convenient.  You can get one not linked to you in an accesable
method through a bank in a dozen or so countries.  For best
results, go to any country but your own.

Next step is to use it to set up a new ISP. Then have caller
ID turned off on your telephone.  Let them play their games.

Any technology can be used both ways.  If you think about it
for more than a few moments, you can have far better privacy
right now than has been possible for the last few thousand
years.  You could actually purchase a PC in another country,
pay for it in funds nobody knows you have, and and surf under
a completely anomynous CPU number for the rest of your life.

OldUncleMe wrote in message <36de20ef.49149767@news>...
>It was: Tue, 02 Mar 1999 18:41:51 -0500 and with STARTLING insight,  "Ed
Hochman
><ed@mbhsys.com>" posted "Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already
have crack for ID
>feature" to "comp.lang.cobol" :
>
>-->I don't think your cookies are available to anyone except the one that
put them
>-->there.
>-->
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>-->> The fact is that anyone can know far more about you by interrogating
the
>-->> cookies you have accepted than they would by obtaining your CPU-ID.
>-->> Certainly they would obtain no less information.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I believe that depends on the properties of the cookie and how the browser
is configured.
>The whole notion is abhorrent to the spirit of the internet.  I read some
discussion by a
>consortium of advertisers and ad brokers recently.  They explained how all
this "free" stuff on
>the web is there because advertising pays for it.  Why, if weren't for good
old advertisers,
>the web wouldn't even exist (this in the context of banner ads, graphics
flopping about, hit
>tracking and cookies.)  Funny me, I remember when the relatively first ads
hit the net, and how
>everyone objected, vocally, and at great length.  Up to that point, it was
just a bunch of fun
>loving beanie heads, and other winners, giving away info like it was going
out of style.  It
>was much easier, even with a text based browser, to find what you sought,
if it was there and
>findable.  You didn't have to wade through the advertising.  Everyone acted
like it was
>desirable to contribute, and perhaps garner a little bit of credit, sort of
a Panglossian
>world.  All of the techniques and innovation which are now touted by
advertising concerns were
>actually fomented and developed in that period; improvements to date are
refinements and
>implementation.  I'll warrant that the measurable content (remember that
term?) has grown, and
>is leaping and bounding all over the place, but how much is sales gibber
and how much is
>additional worthy content?  Of the worthy content, how much is actually
attributable to web
>advertisers?  I'm not saying that I own it, nor ever did, but can lament
the passing of a
>revolutionary moment not often seen for human kind.  Sure, advertising and
advertisers are
>going to force their place and attempt to turn the internet into an even
more cheesy version of
>the classifieds in the back of many magazines.  Sure, manufacturers will
attempt to tattoo
>permanent serial numbers on everyones foreheads till proper IC chips can be
implanted in
>everyone's brains, not just showing where they are and what they are doing
but directing
>pavlovian responses to things such as colors:  "Yellow -- gotta have a
ratburger, quick!!"  I
>guess that as the world fills up, and the generations pass on, each new one
knows a little less
>about personal space, and dare we say it, freedom from being tracked, among
other little
>inconsequential losses.  The importance of documenting your every
preference to advertise you
>more effectively into submission belies the premise of choice, and the
freedom to choose.  It
>is replaced by the freedom not to choose, at the moment.  Remember that it
is important that
>you be told, again & again, until you submit, that you need a ratburger
NOW, lest you forget.
>We all need this constant conditioning or we would never make these
desirable choices.
>Cookies, et al, are just a helpful way to make sure we never forget.  If we
should, the very
>helpful advertisers will courteously remind us and they know exactly what
we need because they
>keep such great records.  It's the best of all possible worlds.
>    (yellow:ratburger, NOW!!)   (orange:ratsoda, too:NOW!!!)
(green:ratfries, NOW!!!)
></rant>
>
>              tenox  @  home  dat   com
>
/ts
```

Does that mean you don't use a credit card. There is so much information
available
about each of us because of credit cards, checks, phone records, medical
bills
etc, etc, etc that the use of a processor id is hardly going to add anything
of value.
This does not mean that I am in favor of cpu id's, I am not. I just think
that this will
not add much that couldn't be determined by other means. Face it, the more
computerized
our lives becomes, the more privacy we lose. Fight the computer, bend you
data cards;)

Tigger

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<36DAEB12.1D42FD05@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36DAD49B.56DC@sundialservices.com>`

```
I would very much like an improvement to the way we use passwords all
over the place.  maybe a user-id combined with a computer chip ID would
work (although the NIC idea is just as good).

Do the people who object to this serial number object to the same thing
which is by necessity on every NIC?

Right now a big security hole is caused by human nature.  When we use 5
ID's at work and a dozen on the internet, we tend to either use the same
password or a slight variation of the same password so that we can
remember them.  When The Denver Post asks you to log on before posting
to its web page, people may do mypasswordDP.  So a cracker can set up a
web page offering you something and require a password.  After looking
at that password, he breaks into your company's intranet.

This can be a big problem - what solutions are likely?
```

On Mon, 01 Mar 1999 15:11:55 -0700, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>Please explain more.  I would very much like to be informed on this
>issue, not only when my work considers making changes, but when my
>household uses a dozen passwords on the WWW as well.  

Heck, I wrote such a scheme years ago - with AT's and above you can
query the BIOS data and the machine type.  Even back in the early 90's
there were enough different machines that in one installation they
rarely had two machines with the same bios data and machine id.  

I did it with Realia COBOL too!
```

>I would very much like an improvement to the way we use passwords all

>work (although the NIC idea is just as good).

Hardwiring pwds/uids creates more problems than it solves.

>Do the people who object to this serial number object to the same thing
>which is by necessity on every NIC?

The only solution is for users to act sensibly. There is absolutely no
reason for me to enter anything other than password=pass for my ny
times subscription. I definitely will not enter my credit card pin
number or my work login password. Once you separate your junk accounts
from the critical ones, it shouldn't be too much of a mental load to
come up with well thought out passwords and remember them.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc

Who cares? It doesn't bother me that it's serial numbered.

Lets face it - with the high volumes of production and the many different
routes to the marketplace and eventually your pc, how the hell are 'they'
going to track who has what serial number let alone what you're doing with
it....then there's all those second hand pc's....processors sold second
hand.......

To be honest 'they' can track you with credit card#s, telephone#s bank, acc#s,
network access points, nic mac addresses....the list goes on.

How do you know that your windoze os hasn't already been ratting on you for
the last 4 years for looking at penthouse.com when the wife was
out...........?


Kev
```

- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36dbd87c.187581066@news.concentric.net> <36DAEBDA.7981F308@NOSPAMhome.com> <MPG.11449da26308035698a8b0@news.dimensional.com> <36DB0107.FA21F3E9@forge-cs.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Mon, 01 Mar 1999 21:05:11 +0000, "Kevin E. Kenny"
<kkenny@forge-cs.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>Who cares? It doesn't bother me that it's serial numbered.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>out...........?

Total abandon vs full grown paranoia? 

The middle course seems to be to reasonably object to attempts for yet
another way to track. Big brother is inevitable. With growing
processing power, storage capacities, network speeds, it would be
utterly foolish for any business capable of implementing tracking
technologies not to at least try. All one can hope to accomplish is
delay yet another implementation and try to have a say in the method.
The suggestion that since there are already numerous ways to track, we
should welcome yet another one is logically flawed. P3 needs to be
boycotted IMO, not because the processor serial number itself is evil
but because accepting it without a fight would send the wrong message.
```

I'm not taking out the crossposts for a specific reason:
As developers (or in some cases future developers ( : ) we _must_ "police"
ourselves when it comes to this sort of invasion of privacy by _whatever means_ it
is implemented with.  I would urge all of you to consider the ethics involved
here.  PLEASE think twice before buying the P3 or producing or being part of a
company that produces software that gathers _any_ information from the user without
specifically asking their permission and informing them of _everything_ you will be
acquiring from them.  I'm so sick of everyone saying that others are already doing
it by other means.  Not the point!  The point is that we must do everything in our
power to keep these invasions from continuing.  Please, if only on principle,
boycott this!


> ....P3 needs to be boycotted IMO, not because the processor serial number itself
> is evil
> but because accepting it without a fight would send the wrong message.

well said
```

- **Message-ID:** `<01be64e8$6d284ae0$2a01a8c0@arc7>`
- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36dbd87c.187581066@news.concentric.net> <36DAEBDA.7981F308@NOSPAMhome.com> <MPG.11449da26308035698a8b0@news.dimensional.com> <36DB0107.FA21F3E9@forge-cs.demon.co.uk> <36dcb4ad.32884937@news.cal.shaw.wave.ca> <36DBDD2A.FC34E745@mindless.com>`

```
Kathryn Taylor Jennings <kat_j@mindless.com> wrote in article
<36DBDD2A.FC34E745@mindless.com>...
> I'm not taking out the crossposts for a specific reason:
> As developers (or in some cases future developers ( : ) we _must_
"police"
> ourselves when it comes to this sort of invasion of privacy by _whatever
means_ it
> is implemented with.  I would urge all of you to consider the ethics
involved
> here.

<snip>

Kathryn, this time I've left the cross-posts in, in the vain hope that you
might read this, wherever you are.

Newsgroups have topics for a reason. It's so that people who are interested
in a particular topic can go to a particular newsgroup and find postings
relevant to their interest. Many people browse more than one newsgroup,
because they are interested in more than one thing. Thus, if people are
interested in PIII IDs (and clearly many people are), they can go to a
newsgroup to do with hardware issues and, possibly, one to do with the
sociological and political implications of advances in technology.

Deviating from this principle results in massive amounts of confusion and
annoyance. In comp.lang.c, the ANSI C language is discussed. People who
browse comp.lang.c expect and anticipate informed (and sometimes, not so
informed) discussion of issues and questions to do with the ANSI C
programming language. Many of the people who frequent the group do not use
PCs either at home or at work. There are a myriad of other platforms out
there other than the Intel-driven IBM-compatible PC. People who do not use
PCs and never intend to buy one (they might be running Sparcstations or
Apple Macs at home) gain no advantage or benefit from threads such as this
one, which are targeted to one particular chip vendor for one particular
platform. Because of the huge number of cross-postings, there are a
corresponding huge number of postings in the thread. This disrupts the
usefulness of the newsgroup to those who have no interest in that thread.

By continuing to discuss this topic in newsgroups in which it is completely
irrelevant to the topic of those newsgroups, you, and many others like you,
are breaching several important rules of netiquette, which are there for
good and well-established reasons (such as those explained above, for
example).

Before I finish up, let me just add one more reason why I personally don't
wish this thread to continue to post in comp.lang.c. I'm a C programmer in
the UK, where bandwidth is expensive. Yes, we PAY THE PHONE COMPANY FOR OUR
LOCAL CALLS to our ISP! On a per-minute basis! Therefore, this thread is
costing me money which I shouldn't have to spend.

If I want to discuss PIII IDs, I'll go to a hardware group. I notice that
there aren't any in your cross-posting list. Odd, that.

> "The difference between stupidity and genius is that
> genius has limits" - Albert Einstein
(From your sig block)

It would appear that Einstein has already predicted this thread.
```

Hi, 
I agree with you! It is IMPOSSIBLE to accept the fact that others can look
in your computer and identify you by your CPU ID! With pentium 3 we are
giving the means to others for checking our computer-live:
	what kind of programs I use
	what kind of information on Internet I see (hard of course! :^) )
	  (can you imagine what will happen if my girlfriend knowthat?)

Then I will receive tons of advertising mail!

I'm just kidding now, but the very point is that at least we MUST have the
possibility to choose if use the ID or not, and if we choose to use it we
must be sure that our will is respected, and I don't think it is so right 
now!

So I boycot the P3 (even becouse I heard it is not so different from P2
(just 8 more register and faster floating point calculation) and I don't
use my computer for gaming (so I don't need a lot of power!))
			
Regards
						Stefano Liboni

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** pjm@see_my_sig_for_address.com (paul milligan)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<36dafcdc.7803556@news.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36dbd87c.187581066@news.concentric.net> <36DAEBDA.7981F308@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> pondered briefly, and wrote:

>>         Personally, I will never purchase a machine that has one of
>> these chips.  AMD makes some very nice processors, which will serve my
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>they already can know the ID in your NIC.  Is that any worse or better?

	I'm not on a LAN, this is my home machine.  I object to the
invasion of my privacy.  But the same applies to any LAN - having the
owner of that system know things about my machine ( actually _his_
machine that I borrow every day ) is one thing.  Having info about
that broadcast to anyone besides the owner of the machine is
intolerable.

	How long do you think it will be before M$oft incorporates (
if they haven't already ) a 'feature' into W2000 that any time you get
on the 'net, or once a week, or whatever, it collects your ID ( which
will be tied to your name, address, SS #, and everything else ) along
with key 'helpful bits' from your registry ?  Don't think they'll do
it ?  I'm convinced they will, if they can get away with it.
Certainly the technolgy is simple, it's the social factor that may (
or may not ) stop them.

	The 'helpful bits' might start out with 'what software is
installed, because we want to ehlp you keep it updated', and progress
to things like 'What URL's have you visited more than once this week,
we want to 'help' you find 'attractive offers' in your inbox, then on
your answering machine, then.....

	Hell, no !

 

Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III chips released - Germans already have crack for ID feature

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<36DB736E.AD51F7DE@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36d9bfc9.4461629@news.earthlink.net> <36DAC2B2.6AF7@gec.nospam.com> <nNzC2.373$q85.8148243@news.siol.net> <36dbd87c.187581066@news.concentric.net> <36DAEBDA.7981F308@NOSPAMhome.com> <36dafcdc.7803556@news.concentric.net>`

```
paul milligan wrote:
>         How long do you think it will be before M$oft incorporates (
> if they haven't already ) a 'feature' into W2000 that any time you get
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is probably being done now, if not by Microsoft then by any of the
free trial programs we casually download and run. Whenever we run any
program we haven't written ourselves, we are turning over complete
control of our computer to a complete stranger (this assumes a binary
program--as opposed to Java--and an OS without supervisor mode).

It's a strong argument for sticking to open source software. With that,
we can know for certain what's happening under the covers.

It's also a strong argument for stoutly defending the "inalienable right
to disasemble", something that most software licenses seek to deny.

Paranoid perhaps, but then "Eternal paranoia is forever the price of
freedom" or something like that...

--John Hicks
johnhicks at ibm dot net
```

- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7bgu0v$91t@wellspring.us.dg.com>`
- **References:** `<36e3fbf9.6298553@news.earthlink.net> <7bf1q2$cui$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <MPG.1144cea6d34f8a3698a8bb@news.dimensional.com> <36DB485A.79499011@purdue.edu>`

```
Jon Helms wrote in message <36DB485A.79499011@purdue.edu>...
<cut>
> What are you trying to hide that you don't want people to have your CPU
ID?
<cut>

The argument that "if you have nothing to hide then you should not object to
being searched" has been used many times to take away the basic right to
privacy.  Sometimes the principle is more important than the specific
instance.  I would agree that there are reasons for having this and in the
particular case of CPU IDs I have mixed feelings and no decision yet.  The
above argument, however, is a strong case for fighting it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III Chips Released with IDs - Intel won't budge

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Al Christians <achrist@easystreet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.python,comp.lang.tcl
- **Message-ID:** `<36DC1F37.F2A38999@easystreet.com>`
- **References:** `<36e3fbf9.6298553@news.earthlink.net> <7bf1q2$cui$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <MPG.1144cea6d34f8a3698a8bb@news.dimensional.com> <36DB485A.79499011@purdue.edu> <7bgu0v$91t@wellspring.us.dg.com>`

```
I'm afraid that the really big (money) issue wrt CPU ID's is one 
that no one has noticed much yet.  It has to do with electronic 
commerce apps.  The application development opportunities here 
are endless.  There is already a great deal of work on custom 
content in the development of web and e-business apps.  What the 
CPU ID allows is the identification of particular customers as 
suckers who will not get a very good price offer, and of others 
as smart shoppers who must be given competitive pricing.  Our 
typical markets today with posted pricing do not facilitate 
changing the price based on what it looks like the particular 
customer will stand -- it is only worthwhile in negotiaing big 
ticket items like cars, houses,  mainframe computer systems, 
enterprise-wide software licenses.  E-business changes all that, 
and what it will allow, particularly if big companies get to 
dominate  electronic markets, is the elimination of list prices 
and their replacement with customer-specific pricing.  

Imagine a database circulated amongst sellers that identifies you 
as someone who is willing to pay high prices, so that high prices 
are  what is offered to you, while others are getting better deals.  
This is far from the optimal free market economy described by Adam 
Smith and other expositors of the advantages of economic freedom,  
and it is something that we ought to try to prevent.  It is not 
benign technology.  Socially responsible software developers should 
not promote or assist it.

Al


Bob Butler wrote:
> 
> Jon Helms wrote in message <36DB485A.79499011@purdue.edu>...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> above argument, however, is a strong case for fighting it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pentium III Chips Released with IDs - Intel won't budge

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00

On Tue, 2 Mar 1999 17:26:15, Al Christians <achrist@easystreet.com> 
wrote:

> I'm afraid that the really big (money) issue wrt CPU ID's is one 
> that no one has noticed much yet.  It has to do with electronic 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> as smart shoppers who must be given competitive pricing

I mentioned in a earlier message that I wrote a copy protection scheme
for my software based on BIOS data and machine type.  This was a 
FIASCO from day one, as users would change machines, upgrade machines 
etc.  Trying to ID a PERSON from a CPU ID is nuts.  The best you can 
do is ID the CPU.  This will be a good thing for software licenses - 
to an individual CPU.  I see this coming, actually, from MicroSoft.  
Buy Windows 98/2000 and install it on "THIS" CPU - but no other.  
Great for software sales.  But as a method to ID a particular 
consumer?   Not a chance.
```
