```
OK, here's the problem...

I just downloaded Fujitsu V.3.0 (about 9 megs). I find it very cool but the
readme file tells me to refer to the Fujitsu User Guide for more details...
But what are they talking about when they say "User Guide"??? Is the online
help supposed to be the User Guide or is there a file which contains the
User Guide or do I have to download this guide from somewhere on the web????
Maybe I'm completely wrong and that user guide doesn't exist... Hummmmm....

Can someone help me resolving this "existential" problem?

Thank you very much!!!

Happy programming! :))))
```

```
HyperReal-Anon wrote:
> Suppose you had an IMS database defined to hold Bills Of Material, a common
> manufacturing task. Structurally the database is made up of two segments:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> back to where you started.

Sounds like you need a field on the Root level segment, perhaps called
"Referenced Next Assembly" which gives you a key value to use with a
second PCB to follow the recursive chain.  I used this technique at
Sikorsky Aircraft (helicopters) in the late 70's.

Bob
```

- **From:** "Jim" <jimyt@REMOVEMEyahoo.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<51AC2.27639$yv3.13594@news2.giganews.com>`

```
OS: Win98
Compiler: Microfocus For windows
Language: Cobol

Dear Group

I'm having problems with an Indexed file, that my college have given me for
a course. They have told me that the file is 'random access' but when I
access it using the 'Valid/Invald key' clause I get a Runtime error when the
program reads a line sequential Transaction file as below:
**No current record defined for sequential read  (ERROR 146)**

If I use the 'Start Valid/Invalid key' clause I get a Runtime error (163)
telling me that there is numeric data in an Alphanumeric item. The item
causing this is the record key for the indexed file.


These are the relavant lines of code:-

 FILE-CONTROL.
000060     SELECT TRANS-FILE ASSIGN TO "ZENSD.DAT"
000070         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     8         SELECT CUSTOMER-MASTER ASSIGN TO "CUSTMAST.MF"
000100         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000090     SELECT NEW-CUST-MASTER ASSIGN TO "MT25NF.DAT"
000100         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
                SELECT STOCK-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "STCKMAST.DAT"
                      ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                       ACCESS MODE RANDOM
                       RECORD KEY S-PART-NUM.
000080     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "PRINTFILE.DAT".
000130
       DATA DIVISION.
000140 FILE SECTION.
000310
000320 FD  TRANS-FILE.
000330 01  TRANS-IR-REC.
000340     03  T-IR-REC-TYPE       PIC X.
000350     03  T-IR-CUST-CODE    PIC X(5).
                           88  END-OF-TRANS  VALUE HIGH-VALUES.
000360     03  T-IR-PART-NUM      PIC X(6).
000370     03  T-IR-QUANTITY       PIC 9(4).
000380 01  TRANS-D-REC.
000390     03  T-D-REC-TYPE        PIC X.
000400     03  T-D-CUST-CODE     PIC 9(5).
000410 01  TRANS-C-REC.
000420     03  T-C-REC-TYPE            PIC X.
000430     03  T-C-CUST-CODE          PIC X(5).
000440     03  T-C-CUST-NAME          PIC X(20).
000450     03  T-C-CUST-ADDRESS    PIC X(60).
000460     03  T-C-CUST-BALANCE    PIC 9(7)V99.
000470     03  T-C-CRED-LIMIT           PIC 9(7).
000480
       FD  CUSTOMER-MASTER.
       01  CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           03  CUST-CODE             PIC X(5).
               88  END-OF-MAST     VALUE   HIGH-VALUES.
           03  CUST-NAME             PIC X(20).
           03  CUST-ADDRESS       PIC X(60).
           03  CUST-BALANCE        PIC 9(7)V99.
           03  CUST-CRED-LIMIT     PIC 9(7).
           03  LAST-MOVE-DATE     PIC 9(6).

       FD  NEW-CUST-MASTER.
       01  NEW-CUST-RECORD.
           03  N-CUST-CODE               PIC X(5).
           03  N-CUST-NAME              PIC X(20).
           03  N-CUST-ADDRESS        PIC X(60).
           03  N-CUST-BALANCE        PIC 9(7)V99.
           03  N-CUST-CRED-LIMIT     PIC 9(7).
           03  N-LAST-MOVE-DATE    PIC 9(6).

       FD  STOCK-MASTER-FILE.
       01  STOCK-REC.
           03  S-PART-NUM                 PIC X(6).
           03  S-PART-DESC               PIC X(19).
           03  S-SUPPLIER-CODE       PIC 9(2).
           03  S-FREE-STOCK             PIC 9(6).
           03  S-MIN-STOCK-LEVEL     PIC 9(4).
           03  S-LAST-MOVE-DATE      PIC 9(6).
           03  S-SELLING-PRICE          PIC 9(4)V99.
 ____________________________________________________________________

 IF T-IR-REC-TYPE = "I" OR T-IR-REC-TYPE = "R"
                  MOVE T-IR-PART-NUM TO S-PART-NUM
                  READ STOCK-MASTER-FILE
                     INVALID KEY
                       MOVE "NEP" TO W-IR-ERROR-CODE
                       PERFORM IR-ERROR-DETAILS
                       PERFORM READ-TRANS-FILE
                     NOT INVALID KEY
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
-------------------------------------------
READ-TRANS-FILE.
           READ TRANS-FILE
               AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO T-IR-CUST-CODE
           END-READ.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
-------------------------------------------
Any help would be much apprieciated
Many Thanks

Matt T
Trainee Programmer (Cobol)
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with an indexed file

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bfidg$c8h$1@news.igs.net>`

```

you get an error when you read it as a sequential file,
then you talk about the record key for the indexed file.

Which of the three different file types have you declared
it to be?

>OS: Win98
>Compiler: Microfocus For windows
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>access it using the 'Valid/Invald key' clause I get a Runtime error when
the
>program reads a line sequential Transaction file as below:
>**No current record defined for sequential read  (ERROR 146)**
…[80 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
>-------------------------------------------
>READ-TRANS-FILE.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
>-------------------------------------------
>Any help would be much apprieciated
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with an indexed file

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FmCC2.1$303.246@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<51AC2.27639$yv3.13594@news2.giganews.com>`

```

Jim wrote in message <51AC2.27639$yv3.13594@news2.giganews.com>...
[...]
>I'm having problems with an Indexed file, that my college have given me for
>a course. They have told me that the file is 'random access' but when I
>access it using the 'Valid/Invald key' clause I get a Runtime error when
the
>program reads a line sequential Transaction file as below:
>**No current record defined for sequential read  (ERROR 146)**

Probably from reading past end of file.

>If I use the 'Start Valid/Invalid key' clause I get a Runtime error (163)
>telling me that there is numeric data in an Alphanumeric item. The item
>causing this is the record key for the indexed file.

A 163 is caused by non-numeric data in a numeric field. See below.

[...]
>000320 FD  TRANS-FILE.
>000330 01  TRANS-IR-REC.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>000400     03  T-D-CUST-CODE     PIC 9(5).

For example, when TRANS-FILE is at end of file the
T-x-CUST-CODE fields will conatin HIGH-VALUES. Accessing
T-D-CUST-CODE at end of file will cause an error 163 since
HIGH-VALUES is not a valid value for numeric display.

It appears that reading past end of file may be the larger problem.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
Jim Dolan wrote:
> 
> I'm studying COBOL and have several programming assignments to
> complete. For the past couple hours I have been looking online for
> sources of COBOL coding forms. At this point I'm going crazy trying to
> design an "official" form using a forms designer. Man, what a pain!

Someone posted one once - check out Deja News.

Us old timers remember coding sheets.  They're what we used to write our
programs on before we handed them off to keypunch operators to punch the
cards - or we punched the cards ourselves.  Coding sheets disappeared
pretty quickly when terminals became available.
```

```
> The wording of GR12 clearly makes the "implicit CONTINUE statement" part
> of paragraph E.  Thus an EXIT SECTION exits the perform range iff the end
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> worry, it will be in COBOL 21XX.

If I had my way, SECTION would be as obsolete as ALTER.  So would the

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DAA527.9A4956C5@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net> <36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5EA25.CA353875@bigfoot.com> <36D6ABC8.A46B48C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <7b76h6$2ls@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> Anyone want to tell me what happens when you EXIT SECTION in a performed
> (from another Section) paragraph?   The Standard sure doesn't make this
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> coding practices.

However today, shops which care about standards designed to reduce goto
type looping errors disagree on whether they should allow, disallow, or
mandate going to an exit paragraph following the paragraph being
performed.  With this command, a standard can be created to require all
paragraphs be stand-alone.  The first paragraph ends with a program
terminator (GOBACK), and all other paragraphs are performed as stand
alone paragraphs.

Sure a standard less shop can have ALTER and all sorts of bad
practices.  (I work in a shop with standards which allow goto loops
within paragraph groups - the paragraph group gets performed once with
goto's returning the flow to the top).

But if all of a shop's compilers allow EXIT PARAGRAPH, it is easier to
set and accept a standard with no GOTO's.  And most any shop which will
have standards which use EXIT PARAGRAPH, will have thought through the
other bad programming practices and proscribed them as well.
```

<a id="t-e20fbab334"></a>
## Thread: Casegen Professional Beta — 1 msgs

### Casegen Professional Beta

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36db151f.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```
Announcement from Casegen Systems Ltd.


"Casegen COBOL for Windows Professional is now available on Beta and we are
looking for a small number of beta testers.  We especially want to hear from
you if you already have Casegen COBOL for Windows or you are interested in
writing ODBC Windows applications in COBOL.  Casegen will provide the Beta
FREE on CD, but those wishing to develop ODBC applications MUST have a copy
of Fujitsu Version 4 which we do not supply.  We can supply a trial version
of SQL Anywhere upon request with the Casegen CD.  It must be noted that
this is a Beta version and does NOT include latest documentation or help
facilities and elements of the features and facilities could change.  A
second Beta MAY be made available subject to problems/enhancements added to
the product".

Casegen COBOL for Windows Professional includes the following features over
the existing product:


Full ODBC support including "point and click" SQL specification. After
selecting an ODBC Data Source, all table import, keys, Cobol variable
definitions etc. are automatic.

New "Explorer-style" List View - new Message View window.

Support for bit-mapped buttons.

Dynamic bitmaps providing support for loading .bmp files at execution with
simple syntax. For example - click on a country to see its flag.

Toolbar support (not docking) with tooltips on all controls.

Modified and simplified generator dialogs.

Launch and support sub-forms and subroutines from main form

Double-click on compiler messages takes you straight to error line in event
code.

Several fixes and removal of some size limitations.


The new product looks better and is easier to use. SQL/COBOL applications
are now easier to write than ever before.

Please email us at support@casegen.co.uk even if you have already advised us
of your interest. Please indicate which, if any, relational databases you
are interested in testing.


Simon Cordingley
Development Manager
Casegen Systems Ltd
www.casegen.co.uk
```

<a id="t-dc61ff3b0d"></a>
## Thread: Illegal File name Problem — 1 msgs

### Illegal File name Problem

- **From:** charlie.smith@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<920270346.1@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com>`

```
 "<> From: "Jim" <jimyt@REMOVEMEyahoo.com>

 "<> OS: MS Windows 98
 "<> Compiler: Microfocus Animator 2

 "<> Dear Group,

 "<> I am having problems with a file in the following program, the
 error I
 "<> am getting suggests that I have illegal characters in the file
 name
 "<> "CUSTMAST.MF". I have tried renaming the file, rewriting the
 code,
 "<> changing case etc but an unable to find an answer, please help.

 "<> Error: illegal character in filename (004)

 "<> Relevant sections of code:

 "<>
 ---------------------------------------------------------------------
-
 "<> ----- --------------
 "<> 000050 FILE-CONTROL.
 "<> 000060     SELECT SORTED-FILE ASSIGN TO "MT25SD.DAT"
 "<> 000070         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
 "<> 000090     SELECT CUSTOMER-MASTER ASSIGN TO "CUSTMAST.MF"
 "<> 000100         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
 "<> 000090     SELECT NEW-CUST-MASTER ASSIGN TO "MT25NF.DAT"
 "<> 000100         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
 "<> SELECT STOCK-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "STCKMAST.IDX"
 "<> ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
 "<> ACCESS MODE RANDOM
 "<> RECORD KEY IS  S-PART-NUM.
 "<> 000080    SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
 "<>
 ---------------------------------------------------------------------
-
 "<> ----- ----------------
 "<> 001551 INITIAL-ROUTINE.
 "<> 001560     OPEN INPUT  CUSTOMER-MASTER    *error reported here*
 "<>                        SORTED-FILE
 "<>                        STOCK-MASTER-FILE
 "<> 001570     OUTPUT NEW-CUST-MASTER
 "<> 001580                 PRINT-FILE
 "<> 001590     PERFORM PRINT-REPORT-HDGS
 "<> 001621     PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE
 "<> PERFORM READ-MASTER-FILE.

I believe you actually reading the error incorrectly.

The error message is really saying that you have an illegal filename
in file number 004.  I translate that to be the STOCK-MASTER-FILE.

Since it is defined as an INDEXED fle, MF COBOL reserves the .IDX
 file
extension.  You can NOT use that extension youself.  You should
probably call the file STCKMAST.DAT and let MF automatically use the
IDX extension.

Sorry for the way the lines wrapped when my reader quoted the
 original
message.

Also, if my analysis is wrong, I hope some of the other folks will
chime in.

Charlie Smith

-----------------------------------------------------------------
Charlie Smith                      Smith Data Processing Services
csmit002@sdpsbbs.sdps-bbs.com      The CyberSpace BBS - 1:231/992
Decatur Township Info Network      BBS/PPP/FAX --- 1 317 856 9020
-----------------------------------------------------------------

... Backup not found: (A)bort (R)etry (S)lap nearest innocent
 bystander.
```

<a id="t-94566dff27"></a>
## Thread: TekMetrics is offering free certifications to the "COBOL" community... — 1 msgs

### TekMetrics is offering free certifications to the "COBOL" community...

- **From:** Michael Littman <mikelittman@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DA9E6C.338C60DA@erols.com>`

```
Dear COBOL Programmer,

For a limited time, TekMetrics is offering free 'COBOL'' certifications
through it's internet-based CAT engine.  Simply go to
http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/certtop.html to order your free
certification.  You can either elect to take the test right away, or
come back later, using the PIN you are provided when you order.

To qualify as a Certified COBOL Programmer, you must pass the
examination with a score of 2.75 or higher.  To be certified as a Master
COBOL Programmer, you must obtain a score of 4.00 or higher.  At the end
of the test, your score will be provided (with a scoring range of 1-5).
Once certified, you can add the logo associated with the test (standard
or master logo, depending on how well you do) to your resume or
website.  Furthermore, those that pass will be mailed a certificate,
once again, at no cost.  There's no catch.  We're simply trying to prove
a concept during a beta trial period.  Thus far, the feedback has been
extremely positive!

Why bother getting certified by TekMetrics?

1)  More and more corporations recognize TekMetrics Certifications as an
indication not only of a technical workers knowledge level, but it also
shows that they took the time to demonstrate their skills to them.

2)  It's free and it's fun.  They won't be free forever, but they are
now.  So take advantage of them.  Furthermore, those who have completed
the tests generally indicate that they enjoyed the experience and
considered both the delivery of the test and the items themselves to be
of high quality.

3)  2000 technical workers from around the world are ordering our
certifications each week, and that number is steadily rising.  To see
the specific figures, go to the bottom of the 'certifications' link off
the homepage.

4)  Strut your stuff.  See if you are good enough to make the top 10
list.

5)  Find out both your strengths and weaknesses in 'COBOL'.  At the end
of the test, you can request specifics on how you did, by subtopic.  And
yes, that too is free.

All we ask for in return is your feedback at the end of the exam.  We
continually focus on the improvement of the delivery of the test and
quality of the questions.  Any feedback you can provide, positive or
negative, will help us greatly.

Another FAQ is, "How do you know the person who supposedly took the test
was actually the same person?  What if it was his hotshot 'COBOL'
Programmer brother, sister or friend?  In other words, what is to
prevent him from cheating?"  Our solution:  we allow employers to
spotcheck the technical workers with a short test at no cost at their
corporate locations, if they so choose.  Corporations have unanimously
applauded this solution.

Lastly, data you provide in the certification process is not used
without your permission other than to store your certification record
and verify to valid employers at their request.  We do, however, publish
names on our site in a Top Ten Scores list for each category, but no
addresses, phone numbers, email, etc.. are shown.  For more information,
see our privacy statement at
http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/privacy.html

Well, that's pretty much it!  We're trying to establish a new
cost-effective and convenient alternative to today's certification
environment.  During this beta trial period, you will be the benefactor
of this fun and challenging experience.

If you have any questions/concerns regarding the 'COBOL' certification,
please do not hesitate to contact me.

Good luck!

Mike

Michael Littman
VP Operations
TekMetrics, Inc.
www.tekmetrics.com <http://www.tekmetrics.com>
mike.littman@tekmetrics.com <mailto:mike.littman@tekmetrics.com>
```

```
>I haven't a clue how to get started.  Any advice will be appreciated.
>Especially a book and/or examples.  The primary application is retrieving
IDMS
>data on MVS - actually OS390, from web applications.
>

Not really answering your question, but you do know (I hope) that VS COBOL
II R3.1 is *really* ancient and long out of support by IBM.  Release 4.0 is
still supported (and came after Release 3.2) - but even it will loose
support March 2001.

Bottom-Line:
   Doing "new" types of development with OLD compilers is likely to be a
dead-end on good days and a "slow-death" on others.
```

```
I understand that the C and F over punches are positive whilst the D and
```

```
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Alistair Maclean

AIUI, zones of D and B are negative, and A, C, E and F are positive.  The
B sign comes from ascii, somehow.

Someone said J thru R are negative, but that's only nine letters.
Negative zero is "}", positive sero is "{".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Overpunch, versions?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E7D261.FD6FBB2E@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7b9os6$2bf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36d89eea@news3.us.ibm.net> <QH3w3CAvYB32EwL2@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <36e6f572.65745939@netnews>`

```
The old 80-column punch cards for each column had 12 places a hole could be
punched.  The 12 punch was the top row, 11 punch was second, while the zero -
9 punches fill out the rest of the column.  After a number was punched in a
card, for example 1234, that field could be negated by punching an 11 punch
over the 4.  The 11-4 punch card code gives the letter M or x'D4'.  When the
number is packed for numeric processing, it turns into x'01234D' (on IBM
machines anyway).  A 12-zone overpunch would give the letter D or x'C4' while
no overpunch would give the digit 4 or x'F4'.  This makes sense in EBCDIC but
less so in ASCII.

Randall Bart wrote:

> 'Twas Tue, 2 Mar 1999 16:47:43 +0000, when Alistair Maclean
> <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Negative zero is "}", positive sero is "{".
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL file structure?

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dd49ca.186927657@news.enterprise.net>`

```
On Tue, 2 Mar 1999 22:32:40 -0800, "CyberSky"
<CyberSky2000@mailexcite.com> wrote:

>I need to develop an interface to MicroFocus COBOL ver. 2.5.22 data files.
>Can anyone tell me where can I find any of these:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>(If #1 & #2 aren't available).

You'll need the FD entry of the data files to interpret the record
structure correctly so the original source is required. Failing that
you may be able to get the record structure of indexed files using
Micro Focus Data File Editor. This is part of Workbench 2.5.

If your intention is that you no longer access the files using the
original COBOL application, you're better off converting to another
format. Write a program to read the data in and then write to an
intermediate flat text based format (line sequential) or if MS SQL
Server is the intended target, a BCP input file. Then write another
app in the tool you intend to use to import the data.
```

<a id="t-549887ed3a"></a>
## Thread: idcaming vsam to flat files ?? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: idcaming vsam to flat files ??

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DCA618.6ACBDFE@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<19990301224535.12259.00002204@ng99.aol.com>`

```
BFETML wrote:
> hi,
> I had previously dumped vsam files that had different minimum and maximum
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> eric

If you are talking about KSDS VSAM files, there really is no such beast
as "fixed length".  All KSDS datasets are variable length under the
covers--the average record length is a user-supplied, unenforced
"estimate".  The maximum RECSIZE is the upper bound, but all record
sizes large enough to contain the key field up to that limit are always
permitted.  To copy to a flat file you must use a utility (e.g., IDCAMS
REPRO) capable of handling variable length input files and whatever
output file format is consistent with your actual records.  If you use
some utility which attempts by default to generate an output file with
characteristics similar to the input file, it may insist on creating a
RECFM=VB output file since all VSAM KSDS appear as variable length
files.  If all logical records are really the same length, you can REPRO
to an output file defined as RECFM=FB,LRECL=actualreclength .  If
logical records have varying lengths in the VSAM file, your output file
should be at least RECFM=VB,LRECL=<maxactualreclength+4>.  Note that
LRECL for the flat file is based on the actual record lengths, not
whatever length was defined as the maximum VSAM RECSZ (which could be
larger).  If you don't know actual record lengths, LRECL=maxVSAMreclen+4
will also always work for a VB output file.  If your system supports
system-determined blocksize, then it shouldn't be necessary to specify a
BLKSIZE for the output file.  If BLKSIZE is manually specified then for
an FB file use a value of n*lrecl (want n large enough for a block size
close to 1/2 track) and for a VB file use the largest block size that
will give 2 blocks/track (minimum blocksize for VB is LRECL+4).
```

#### ↳ Re: idcaming vsam to flat files ??

- **From:** bfetml@aol.com (BFETML)
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990302223513.11208.00002474@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36DCA618.6ACBDFE@acm.org>`

```
hi Joel, 
thanks for the explanation, will try it.  Does this mean that if I wanted the

fb?

thanks again 
eric
```

- **From:** djscubed@aol.com (Djscubed)
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990303114504.09616.00002769@ng113.aol.com>`

```
If you are doing this to try to bring it down to the PC then it would be easier
to use The Solution from C-Cubed's direct edit product.  With it you can select
a VSAM file on MVS and it will create it on the PC for you with the same
properties allowing you to choose the keys to work with if you don't want the
whole file.

David Sutton
davidj@ibm.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: idcaming vsam to flat files ??

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborneTAKEOUT@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dd6967.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36DCA618.6ACBDFE@acm.org> <19990302223513.11208.00002474@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
Will you post your IDCAMS control cards and the JCL running the IDCAMS
utility, and whatever message codes IDCAMS is giving you?

 You should be able to REPRO from vsam to a recfm=fb flat file in a
single step, I do it every week.

Note, there is a ~ (tilde) character in front of the JCL identifier
slashes because Outlook Express is trying to turn the ones with text
immediately after the slashes into hyperlinks (very annoying).  Please
ignore the tildes.

Example:
~//*
~//JS010    EXEC PGM=IDCAMS
~//SYSUT1    DD DSN=your.vsam.dataset.name,
~//             DISP=(SHR,KEEP,KEEP)
~//SYSUT2    DD DSN=your.flatfile.dataset.name,
~//             DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE),
 ~//             UNIT=SYSDA,
~//             SPACE=(100,(100,50),RLSE),
~//             DCB=(DSORG=PS,RECFM=FB,LRECL=100,BLKSIZE=0)
~//SYSPRINT  DD SYSOUT=*
~//SYSIN     DD *
   REPRO  INFILE(SYSUT1) -
          OUTFILE(SYSUT2)
/*
~//

The space allocation figures and the lrecl being theory here, but the
above should work.



BFETML wrote in message
<19990302223513.11208.00002474@ng-ft1.aol.com>...
>hi Joel,
>thanks for the explanation, will try it.  Does this mean that if I
wanted the
>data in a flat file I will have to repro from vsam to vb then repro
from vb to
>fb?
>
>thanks again
>eric
```

<a id="t-154c758d57"></a>
## Thread: Problem accessing HP 4Plus lower tray from CICS/VTAM — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Problem accessing HP 4Plus lower tray from CICS/VTAM

- **From:** slider_rj@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bh95j$6bg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have a CICS program I need to change to print to an HP 4Plus Laser Printer.
My understanding is that CICS goes through VTAM (DRS,VPS), then to the HP
printer using TCPIP.  I need to pass PCL to the HP to invoke the lower tray.
Using the LaserJet 4si User's reference manual ('Don't have HP 4 Plus
Laserjet users manual) I found a PCL code: "1B266C3448" for lower tray.  Is
this correct for the HP4 Plus printer?

Anyway I was informed that this PCL HEX is in ascii and that I need to convert
it to EBCDIC HEX which is "275093F4C8" because VPS converts EBCDIC to ascii.

I tried this recommendation but the document still printed using the upper
tray source.  Could it be that VTAM(DRS/VPS) is overriding this?  Can
VTAM(DRS/VPS) be changed to invoke the lower tray?  Currently VTAM(DRS/VPS)
has been set up to pass the correct font and page orientation (landscape)
which works.  An attempted was made to set a parm in VTAM(DRS/VPS) to select
the lower paper tray as well but still continues to retrieve from the upper
tray. If VTAM can control it, maybe I can avoid using PCL code altogether.

In my cics program I used the following CICS command.  Is this the right
command?

WS-TRAY                  PIC X(5)  VALUE  X'275093F4C8'.
WS-TRAY-LENGTH    PIC S9(04)  COMP VALUE +5

EXEC CICS SEND TEXT NOEDIT
     FROM (WS-TRAY)
      LENGTH(WS_TRAY_LENGTH
      PAGING
      RESP (RESPONSE CODE)
END EXEC

Thanks

;)Kathy

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```
```

slider_rj@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<7bh95j$6bg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>I have a CICS program I need to change to print to an HP 4Plus Laser
Printer.
>My understanding is that CICS goes through VTAM (DRS,VPS), then to the HP
>printer using TCPIP.  I need to pass PCL to the HP to invoke the lower
tray.
>Using the LaserJet 4si User's reference manual ('Don't have HP 4 Plus
>Laserjet users manual) I found a PCL code: "1B266C3448" for lower tray.  Is
>this correct for the HP4 Plus printer?

1b266c3448 is upper tray.
1b266c3148 is lower tray.

Joe Hunter
```

```
userid, program-name or perhaps the infos from the ID DIVISION.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bh9l1$ano@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36DC1906.C5137F19@gmx.net>`

```
There is no feature in VS COBOL II (or the newer COBOL for this-and-that)
compilers to get this information via "native" COBOL source statements.  You
can look at DejaNews for methods of calling either an Assembler *or* COBOL
program to go searching OS/390 control blocks - to find the TIOT or similar
system resources with this information.
```

```
These new tools are driving me crazy.

Having spent some 20-30 hours designing a "File" object
for a system, testing all the lockout etc, registering it with
the system, etc., etc., etc., I now have a lovely object on
my toolbar (Fujitsu 4) that is a complete file system. I drop
it on a form, and invoke various methods, and it works like
a charm.

Step two.  Try to use it in a second program (after all the
purpose of a module is re-use).  It seems that you can only
use the tool ONCE !!!!!!!!!!

PowerCobol totally chokes on placing the object on a *second*
form, as it opens it when it puts it on the first form. Is this something
stupid that I am doing, or it is something !really stupid! that Fujitsu
have done?

I am begining to think this stuff is just a toy.
```

```
One thing you might want to try is place the work 'Dynamic' after 'Assign To'.
000060     SELECT SORTED-FILE ASSIGN TO DYNAMIC "MT25SD.DAT"

We had the same problem with WorkBench 4.0 on OS/2 and when we inserted that
word the problem went away.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bgba2$sa1$1@pollux.ip-plus.net>`

```
Sorry, my english is horrible.
us problem is:
allway we have status 98 in all data-file. We have a Windows nt4.0 server
and win95 pc's. On win95 peer to peer system, we have no problem, only with
nt server. we are dealer and have this problem very mutch on ca 10
installations.
Please help us. Write us, wath you think.
We hop, you can help us.
Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol

- **From:** Clifford E Williams <williams@southern.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-03-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DC34E9.89181FA3@southern.edu>`
- **References:** `<7bgba2$sa1$1@pollux.ip-plus.net>`

```
It would help if you told us what COBOL you are using, and described the
data file (in detail).

In general the status "98" means that there is something wrong with your
indexed file (some compilers use "98" to describe problems that have
nothing to do with indexed files).  The file structure may not be what
you told your program that it is, the index may be corrupt, the file may
be locked by some other process (the list could go on too far).

Without details we can't help you.
```

## Thread: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure — 5 this month / 27 total

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-78dad1ed75)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h5.e5.h02V3EKoRsq4iEEr4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990215172820.07685.00001563@ng156.aol.com>`

```
"RKRay" == "RKRayhawk" writes:

RKRay> Now, class, we have a pop quiz.
RKRay> 
RKRay> It would appear that we have had here in this innocent thread
RKRay> a clear recordation of the concerns, felt mutually, of two
RKRay> very informed prognosticators to the effect that should there
RKRay> be a mixture of GOTO and PERFORM of the particular variety of
RKRay> a GOTO to the top paragraph name that there could be a
RKRay> breakage, as it were, betwixt releases of the compiler.
RKRay> 
RKRay> In particular, the iterative functionality latent in PERFORM
RKRay> may be dysfunctionalized by such a jump.  There was humorously
RKRay> a disagreement as to whether this would be a break of the GOTO
RKRay> or a break of the PERFORM, (surely that is expertise
RKRay> instantiated). --- major snip --- Robert Rayhawk
RKRay> RKRayhawk@aol.com
RKRay> 
Apparently, nobody but me on this news group has worked on Burroughs
Large Systems COBOL (now Unisys).  There's a command there that is so
succint in its structure and ability to resolve the problem of going
to an EXIT paragraph.  You simply code: EXIT HERE and it is just like
GOing TO the EXIT paragraph.  IBM never implemented it, but they
borrowed other stuff from Burroughs. If you think about what you're
really doing here, you really wouldn't need a GO TO the EXIT in
almost all cases, except those of complex conditions.  You should
code the PERFORM THROUGH using UNTIL condition.  This usually
eliminates the need for a condition test inside the PERFORM.  You
also need to break the logic up into smaller blocks to avoid using
the GO TO the EXIT.

Jay
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DD47ED.23589E18@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990215172820.07685.00001563@ng156.aol.com> <h5.e5.h02V3EKoRsq4iEEr4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`

```
> Apparently, nobody but me on this news group has worked on Burroughs
> Large Systems COBOL (now Unisys).  There's a command there that is so
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the GO TO the EXIT.

I used Bourroughs (Large & small) a long time ago, and Univac more
recently - didn't know about that nice command.  I still don't like
performing more than one paragraph.  It seems that with the EXIT HERE
command there's even less reason for having THROUGH than before.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ernest.Gose@Lexis-Nexis.com (Ernie Gose)
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bm90h$6pt$1@mailgate2.lexis-nexis.com>`
- **References:** `<36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990215172820.07685.00001563@ng156.aol.com> <h5.e5.h02V3EKoRsq4iEEr4.NR@nospam.nowhere> <36DD47ED.23589E18@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Interesting topic and quite timely for me as I used a goto the other day for 
the first time in at least 10 years.  My mentor taught me to exit paragraphs - 
actually called internal modules in PL/1 - by going to xxxx-exit.  He also 
impressed upon us to keep each paragraph within a page.  I always thought this 
was about the right amount of space required to code the essance of the 
'whole' function that you are coding.  Which I think is not only structured 
but much easier to understand than many itty bitty paragaphs.  But people who 
wouldn't recognize a comp-3 data type if it bit them in their arse instantly 
branded my code as unstructured and expensive to maintain.

A few years ago I noticed that recent college graduates had adopted the 
same method that I had been taught many years before.  Somewhere along the 
line acadamia got a dose of common sense.  Good!

However, EXIT HERE would have been a great implementation and eliminated 
adopting itty-bitty paragraphs as 'ordained' structured.  

BTW: Correct me if I'm wrong - if ever there's a sure thing ... - until isn't 
the optimum solution if you've discovered you need to get 'exit' in 
mid-paragraph.

In article <36DD47ED.23589E18@NOSPAMhome.com>, brazee@NOSPAMhome.com says...
>
>> Apparently, nobody but me on this news group has worked on Burroughs
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>command there's even less reason for having THROUGH than before.
```

<a id="t-ed962e5a3c"></a>
## Thread: ISPF Tricks — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### ISPF Tricks

- **From:** "Torix" <Torixx@ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LxlD2.441$oq2.474355@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
Greetings,

I am in a fairly competitive program at work learning COBOL.   We are on an
emulated OS/390 system.  Often I find myself waiting for long periods of
time for compiles and submitted jobs to hit my hold Queue so I can see what
I have accomplished.

I know there are tricks to getting around in ISPF, and to speed up compile
and run times.   The manuals only give us the basics.

So I come to the experts, my friends here on the news group :)

Any and all shortcuts will be put to great use and much appreciated.
Thanking everyone ahead of time.

Torix
```

#### ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** "Lanny Levine" <llevine@sprint.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QdmD2.3716$SI4.731108@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`
- **References:** `<LxlD2.441$oq2.474355@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
Any batch job can be written as a CLIST or REXX exec. When they are executed
they do not end up in a batch queue but run right away. However, your
systems area may not take kindly if everyone started  using CLISTs and/or
REXX execs to avoid the batch turn around time.

Lanny
```

#### ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bm7dd$4i3$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<LxlD2.441$oq2.474355@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bnjge$n0q$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

#### ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_hmD2.705$VR3.2052768@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<LxlD2.441$oq2.474355@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
There are NO such Shortcuts.

What determines how long it takes is:
Which CLASS=  is used.  Different classes have differrent priorities.
They ALSO have different INITIATORS.  An initiator is what takes a
job from Input Queue and moves it the Active Status.  Sometimes
the Operators or Systems people FORGET to bring all the initiators
BACK UP.   ALSO, if you are remote, and going thru routers, sometimes
you'll be going thru several, and you get an occaissional lag in Interactive
response.

So, check with OPS for different classes, or just start at 1 and go thru 9
and A thru Z.  You'll get a lot where it just 'disappears' and some with
Access violations or not alloweds.

ALSO there is one 'TIP/TRICK', check your 'panels' for FOREGROUND
options, then you'll get almost instant results.
Torix wrote in message ...
>Greetings,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qe2F2.2998$bk.8227419@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<LxlD2.441$oq2.474355@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
>I know there are tricks to getting around in ISPF, and to speed up compile
>and run times.   The manuals only give us the basics.


As previously mentioned....these tips and tricks can get you fired and most
certainly will get you a call from system security. My current record is 2
days. @ days after starting a new account I received a pleasant call from
security warning me of running jobs in inappropriate job classes.

To avoid any problems get a procedure manual which lists the valid job
classes and pick out the ones which are not production/CICS/DB2 job classes.
Submit your job with the same name under as many job classes as you wish.
When one gets in cancel all of the others. This will get you in trouble in a
hurry if you use a production job class.

Converting the JCL to REXX EXECs or CLISTS can get very complicated if you
go beyond simple COBOL compiles. If you have IMS CICs or DB2 you will have
to run some kind of pre compiler. This really just complicates a semi simple
process. If you wish to convert your JCL to a REXX EXEC or CLIST I would
recommend asking the wonderful people in BIT.LISTSERV.TSOREXX or
COMP.LANG.REXX . What you will end up doing is converting each DD statement
to an ALLOC statement. Then you will CALL the compiler with a fully
qualified path such as :

CALL 'SYS1.COB2COMP(IGYCRCTL)'

There are ways around this, but they are beyond the scope of this
discussion.

I have developed several REXX EXECs which do this, but all of them create
JCL which is then submitted. Ask the people in one of the REXX newsgroups
for better detail.

When done dont forget to LINK-EDIT the object module created by the compile.

In the end it may be worth it to just wait, otherwise you will have 10
different versions of a compile EXEC like I do and only 1 works at your
current site. However if you are patient and spend the time to learn how to
do it in a REXX EXEC, especially one used as an EDIT macro, the things you
will learn will help you with many other things under TSO/ISPF/OS390.

REXX is a pandoras box. Once you learn it it will draw you away from what
you are supposed to be doing. So be careful.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2PgF2.3269$bk.8726429@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<LxlD2.441$oq2.474355@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <Qe2F2.2998$bk.8227419@storm.twcol.com>`

```
I have attached a COBSYNTX REXX EXEC which will compile in the foreground,
check listing for errors, and insert notes in appropriate position in your
source file when using ISPF EDIT or "ISREDIT". I haven't used it in quite a
while and it is rather complicated, but here it is. You will most definitely
have some problems with this as it is specialized to a particular site's
settings. One known problem is that it cannot handle CICS and DB2 programs,
only one or the other not both. You also may get some errors with CICS
programs which are simply CICS syntax embedded in COBOL using an EXEC
CICS/END-EXEC block.

This is intended to be a syntax checker, not a compiler. It does run the
actual compiler, but only to get the LISTING produced by said compiler.
Eventually you will have to submit an actual compile using JCL, but this
should save time debugging most of the syntax errors.

When you eventually do submit your compile you can try a PRTY=15 setting on
the job card. This will set your job to the highest priority and most likely
move to the front of the queue. Sometimes this option is turned off by the
systems programmers to prevent jumping to the front of the line.

It will definitely get you in trouble if you are not allowed to do this.
Even if you are, some people watch the QUEUEs and yell at you when you do
such an inconsiderate thing.

If your compile JCL does not insert the COBOL source code in-stream you can
try submitting your JCL with a TYPRUN=HOLD parameter on the JOB CARD. This
will submit your JCL as normal with no special job priorities and will not
get you more CPU time. IT WILL however eventually get your job to the front
of the QUEUE. Then when you are ready you can release the job and it will
then be the very next job to get into the QUEUE. You may want to submit
several of these at a time to have a few waiting. Otherwise everytime you
release a job you will have to wait in line again to get to the front of the
QUEUE.

As with all of my previous suggestions this may get you in trouble, but
certainly not as much as the previous suggestion. While your jobs may be at
the front of the QUEUE, they are letting everything else through, not tying
anything else up except maybe positions in line.

good luck.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00

```
In article <Qe2F2.2998$bk.8227419@storm.twcol.com>, Jeff <a@a.com> wrote:
>>I know there are tricks to getting around in ISPF, and to speed up compile
>>and run times.   The manuals only give us the basics.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>security warning me of running jobs in inappropriate job classes.

Two days?  Laggard!  Slacker!  Slug-a-bed!  Two days, indeed... *I*
usually get *my* call before noon of the first day I'm on site... and it
usually goes something like this:

Ops: 'You shouldn't be running jobs in that class.'
Me:  'No problem, I'm new here... how do I find out what classes are
permitted for my use?'
O: 'Job classes are dependent on the amount of CPU time you need and the
kind of system devices you need; class Q is for Prod and you shouldn't use
that.'
M: 'Yes, I understand... whose job is it to tell me which classes I can
and cannot use?'
O: 'That's your job to find out... ask your Project Leader.'
M: 'Of course... and if my Project Leader is new here, too, and doesn't
know?'
O: 'Then have your Project Leader call us to find out.'
M: 'What will my Project Leader do when he calls you to find out?'
O: 'He'll talk to us then and we'll tell him.'
M: 'Oh... you mean he'll talk to you then and you'll tell him then?'
O: 'Yes, that's right.'
M: 'Well, I'm talking to you now... why can't you tell me now?'
O: 'One moment, please... there, I was just putting your ID on the 'Give
These Jobs One CPU-Cycle Per Week' list'.


Next week... How To Find Out Printer Addresses and Forms!

DD
```

```
You are quite lucky, actually.  My conversations have been going like:

Is the software ready?
Me: In finall testing.  How about the hardware?
Them:Well, we are going to confirm the order this week.
Me:  Wait a minute, I am supposed to install a week Friday.
Then: They assure us it will be ready.
Me: What about the fibre optic runs?  (300 miles of custom cable)
Them: They are starting Friday.
Me:  And it will be ready a week friday?
Them: yes, do not worry about it.
Me:  And you have all the hubs, etc.?
Them:  What is a hub?
Me: Right.  The electrician has been in to install power?
Them.  Power?
Me:  Okay.  Do you have my tape with the test data?
Them:Yes.  Here it is.  You are sure that it will be ready?
Me:  I will have the computer, and the software ready to plug
into the network.
Them:  Good.  I have assured managament that we are on schedule and
everything will run when we throw the switch a week Friday.
Me: When will the network be ready to test?
Them: as soon as the cables are installed.
Me:  Good.  Isn't FM technology wonderfull?
Them: What is FM technology?
Me:  Fucking Magic.
```

#### ↳ Re: ISPF Tricks

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e53f37.7134968@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
On Wed, 3 Mar 1999 20:31:18 -0500, "Torix" <Torixx@ameritech.net>
wrote:

>Greetings,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Becareful bypassing the Batch Queue.  Shop management tend to take a
very negative view of mavericks bypassing shop institutited standards.

REXX can greatly facilitate your tool sets if used properly, but used
improperly, it can be a drain on resources.

Example:  If every programmer has and used REXX as a hot reader to
compile programs, the Batch Queue would be brought to it's knees.  

OTOH, if your shop would develop a REXX screen for submitting batch
compiles, capable of submitting multiple programs, from any location
to a range of linklibs, option to link or not, include debugging
options (ie. Intertest, Expeditor, ViaSoft), even an option to hook in
a shop program to check that your program(s) meet shop standards.
Then you wouldn't have to keep multiple compile jobstreams, and take
time to jury-rigging those jobstreams to meet your needs.  (Oh the joy
of waiting in Queue, only to get kicked out with a JCL error, when you
finally execute).

There are REXX ngs:  comp.lang.rexx, and bit.listserv.tsorexx



Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

```
operating systems.  Should be able to diagnose systems failures, perform

Operations, and Application development staffs.  Some knowledge of 3rd party
software products will also be required.

Northern New Jersey Location.  Salary is very flexible depending upon
experience.
```

```
We are writing a java application for S390.
We would like to make the java application
call an existing batch cobol application on S390.
We would also like the cobol application call the java
application. We know that the java application
can be run under the unix services of S390.

But what we do not know is how to make COBOL
and Java applications communicate with each other
on S390. Any suggestions will be highly appreciated.

Moreover, can COBOL and Java talk with each other on
a Windows NT and Unix platforms. From what I heard, JNI
is not suitable for COBOL!

Ram
```

```
Has anyone gotten the GetLastError API to work with any of their other API
called programs?

I set mine up like this

call convention 74
working storage
01  lasterror                               pic XXXX comp-5.

procedure devision.

call "cob32api".
call winapi "GetLastError"
            returning lasterror.

Lasterror returns with something but it's not in the Win32SDK list of error
codes.


Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net
```

```
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't know anything about windows, but PIC XXXX COMP-5 somehow doesn't make sense to me.
Shouldn't that be PIC S9(4) COMP-5 instead?


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GetLastError

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bmmbo$i9j$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<gneD2.662$0_.11912752@news1.i1.net> <36de206a.500810@news3.ibm.net>`

```

Volker Bandke wrote in message <36de206a.500810@news3.ibm.net>...
>On Wed, 3 Mar 1999 11:37:30 -0600, "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
wrote:
[...]
>>01  lasterror                               pic XXXX comp-5.
[...]
>>
>I don't know anything about windows, but PIC XXXX COMP-5 somehow doesn't
make sense to me.
>Shouldn't that be PIC S9(4) COMP-5 instead?
>
It isn't Windows. It is, very likely, Micro Focus.
[The vendor name was not given.]

PIC X(4) COMP-5 is equivalent to PIC S9(9) COMP-5
with no truncation.

PIC X(?) is used to allocate in terms of bytes rather than digits.

PIC X(4) COMP-X is another strange combination. It is equivalent
to PIC 9(9) COMP-5 with no truncation.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GetLastError

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bmnnv$jul$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<gneD2.662$0_.11912752@news1.i1.net> <36de206a.500810@news3.ibm.net> <7bmmbo$i9j$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <7bmmbo$i9j$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>
[...]
>
>PIC X(4) COMP-X is another strange combination. It is equivalent
>to PIC 9(9) COMP-5 with no truncation.

With apologies, it is equivalent to COMP, not COMP-5.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

## Thread: Need URGENT help on Microfocus Netexpress Screen — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Need URGENT help on Microfocus Netexpress Screen

- **From:** "Hugh Tran" <hugh_tran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bj0bi$e19$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
Hi there,

I am using Microfocus Netexpress 2.0 on windows 95/NT PCs. I ahve a "screen
section" to define my screen layout and the screen variables. This is only a
TEXT screen -- NOT a GUI.

I am not able to do:

(1) I want all input fields to be underlined. I can make them reverse-video
using Adis BUT underlining is easier on the eyes. HOW???

(2) When I use the TAB or arrow keys (down, up, left and right), the cursor
moves to the next or previous screen input fields. I would like to make it
so that when I press the down arrow key, the cursor jumps to the input field
DIRECTLY or IN-DIRECTLY below the current input field. I am able to achieve
this ONLY if the input field is DIRECTLY below the current one. HOW????

Thanks in a million


hugh_tran@hotmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Need URGENT help on Microfocus Netexpress Screen

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-U1dfTfkBJWgX@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7bj0bi$e19$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
On Wed, 3 Mar 1999 09:37:31, "Hugh Tran" <hugh_tran@hotmail.com> 
wrote:

> Hi there,
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

When I last attempted this, I too failed.  MicroFocus has the best 
screen section I have worked with, but I never could get underline 
support to work.  It's not supported for DOS/WINDOWS that I can see.

> (2) When I use the TAB or arrow keys (down, up, left and right), the cursor
> moves to the next or previous screen input fields. I would like to make it
> so that when I press the down arrow key, the cursor jumps to the input field
> DIRECTLY or IN-DIRECTLY below the current input field. I am able to achieve
> this ONLY if the input field is DIRECTLY below the current one. HOW????

Redefine the down arrow to be a TAB key with an ADIS call. It is one 
of the X"AF" calls. MAKE SURE to re-map the key upon exit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need URGENT help on Microfocus Netexpress Screen

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DE5EFF.91F6074A@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7bj0bi$e19$1@news1.mpx.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-U1dfTfkBJWgX@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 3 Mar 1999 09:37:31, "Hugh Tran" <hugh_tran@hotmail.com>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> support to work.  It's not supported for DOS/WINDOWS that I can see.

Various people have pointed out that a Windows console will not support
underline. However, if you run your Micro Focus application as a
graphical app, the text console is emulated by the COBOL Runtime System.

Within this emulation, it *is* possible to have underline support in
addition to the regular VGA set of attributes.
A caveat of this functionality is that you must be careful when using
the CBL_ calls to read/write the screen attributes, as they have a
one-byte-attribute interface which defaults to the VGA attribute byte
and thus do not have underline support. There are CBL_ calls which
will set up custom attributes in the palette if you wish to read/write
them with the CBL_ one-byte-attribute interfaces.

As an example, try running the following program first using
"run prog.int" (Windows console), then "runw prog.int" (RTS emulation).

procedure division.
  display "Reverse" at 1010 with reverse-video
  display "Underline" at 1110 with underline
  .

Cheers, Kev.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need URGENT help on Microfocus Netexpress Screen

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bj3tn$ul6@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7bj0bi$e19$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
Hugh,

1) Unfortunately the console support on Win95/NT does not provide any
support for the underline attribute so there is no way to get it.

2) The functionality of the TAB and arrow keys is configurable using ADISCF.
However, it is not clear what you mean by DIRECTLY and IN-DIRECTLY. It would
appear that the default behaviour for the cursor keys is what you are
requesting. eg if your cursor is on a field at line 1 column 1 and you press
the down arrow, the cursor will move to a field in which column 1 is valid.
If that is several lines down screen it will move to that point. Unless I
have misunderstood your note that is what I took to mean INDIRECTLY,
DIRECTLY being the next line.



Hugh Tran wrote in message <7bj0bi$e19$1@news1.mpx.com.au>...
>Hi there,
>
>I am using Microfocus Netexpress 2.0 on windows 95/NT PCs. I ahve a "screen
>section" to define my screen layout and the screen variables. This is only
a
>TEXT screen -- NOT a GUI.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

field
>DIRECTLY or IN-DIRECTLY below the current input field. I am able to achieve
>this ONLY if the input field is DIRECTLY below the current one. HOW????
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Need URGENT help on Microfocus Netexpress Screen

- **From:** scm@enterprise.net (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dd4b86.187371736@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<7bj0bi$e19$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
On Wed, 3 Mar 1999 20:37:31 +1100, "Hugh Tran" <hugh_tran@hotmail.com>
wrote:

>Hi there,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>using Adis BUT underlining is easier on the eyes. HOW???

Underline only works on monochrome screens. There isn't an attribute
bit in the standard PC attribute byte for underline. On good old DOS,
you set this up with MODE MONO but I don't know how Netexpress would
do it - I presume it runs in a text window emulation and not a DOS
shell?
```

<a id="t-54ca0983d2"></a>
## Thread: Getting Module name (under LE - on OS/390) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Getting Module name (under LE - on OS/390)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bk3o4$47r@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In response to another thread, I received the following suggestion for how
you can (with a little work) get the current module name in a COBOL program
running under LE (IBM OS/390).

"Bill,

  It is a little stretch, but you could use a condition handler to get the
program name.  Just signal a condition (CEESGL) and in the handler
call CEE3GRN to get the routine name."
```

```
RainCode's technology expands now to CSP.

After Cobol and PL/1, a CSP version of RainCode is now functional and
available.
As RainCode can be adapted to almost any kind of language, due to its
strong object structure and modularity, we are constantly creating new
versions of it, following the needs of the developers community.

If you're facing problems maintainig some huge amount of undecipherable
source code, don't hesitate to contact us and send us some samples of
your code: RainCode can help...

To have an overview of RainCode's technology, visit our website at
http://www.raincode.com
```

```
Hi,
I'm having a bit of a problem in an indexed sequential program.
I have to calculate a new salary and then update the indexed file.
I'm using a student version of RM Cobol, some early DOS based
version...
I have a repeating error when trying to compile,
If I use the variable names in the update lines of the names used for
the indexed file items of the record I get an error saying,
"User-defined word expected instead of reserved word".  This error
begins at the ACCEPT of the next line, but what is the error trying to
say?
Code is as follows,

EVALUATE MENU
  WHEN "2"
    DISPLAY "Last Name"
    ACCEPT NDX-L-NAME, LINE 20, POSITION 10
    $
E User-defined word expected instead of reserved word. (scan resumed).
I  Scan resumed.
  WHEN "3"
.
.
.

On through the cluster of this menu I have the same error for each
choice.  I'm not sure what the compiler is trying to have me do.  In
the text they use code that "accepts" into the "ndx" variable so I
thought I could also.  Is this where I need to do it differently?
I coded out an update module in which I use temporary variables for
figuring the new salary.  Using this method I didn't receive errors,
such as,

display "enter the amount of salary change:", line....
accept sal-percent-chng, line....
multiply sal-current by sal-percent-chng giving sal-chng-var....
add sal-current to sal-chng-var giving sal-new....
IF to check if correct....
if ans = "Y" then
move sal-new to ndx-sal.
This goes to an update module.

In this last segment I have no error messages in the program.
I'm stumped on that first segment of code.  I should be able to follow
that which the text says.
Can you help?
Thank you,
GH
```

<a id="t-0c58ec9513"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol85 v 3: setting dll folder — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-0c58ec9513)._

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol85 v 3: setting dll folder

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zF8D2.1$Pk.172@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36BBB915.7C67CE44@home.com>`

```
Dave,

You need to check your PATH statement in the autoexec.bat file.  It should
have a path containing c:\FSC;c:\FSC\PCOBOL32;   Use c for the drive it was
installed to.  This will allow the search to look in the path and find the
DLL's that are there in the folder.  The installation should have installed
this correctly. Sometimes it doesn't work if the Path statement isn't
correctly installed originally.   For V4 compilers use COBOL97 instead of
PCOBOL32.  Hope this helps.

Regards,
Gary Roush


dave wrote in message <36BBB915.7C67CE44@home.com>...
I recently downloaded the free version of Fujitsu's COBOL85 based on a
recommendation in one of the postings of this NG, now gone from my news
server.

The immediate problem I have is in trying to compile, link and run a
couple of the sample programs provided in the download.  The compile and
link portions work fine but when I come to run the WINEXEC from the
PROGRAMMING-STAFF window it gives me an "Error Starting Program" Dialog
Box reflecting the message "A required .DLL file, F3BIPRCT.DLL, was not
found."  Moving the F3BIPRCT.DLL to the folder in which the source file
is located resulted in subsequent similar errors for other F3BI*.DLLs.
The only resolution I have found is to move all the .dll files into the
folder which contains the source file.

Is there an Option that I can set so that I can tell WINEXEC where to
look for the required .dll files?  Or, alternatively, is there another
solution to the problem?

Thanks in advance,

Dave
```

```
retailing company, based in Hayes, Middlessex.
You will be working in a fast moving environment in an expanding team. You
will be developing new software and overseeing the maintenance of existing
products. You will come across as a mature and intelligent person who can
own problems and assist with junior A/P's development.

You need a minimum of four years experience in a IBM Mainframe environment
with strong COBOL skills. CICS and DB2 would also be an advantage. This
company offers an excellent working environment and good benefits. They
will pay up to �35k pa to the right candidate. This is also a client server
site and formal cross training will be given in this area. 

If you would like to know more about this position please give me a call on
my direct line 0181 336 6817 quoting ref:005216 or alternatively contact me
by email philip.barlow@itprospects.co.uk
```

<a id="t-e500e6965c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL word size — 10 msgs

### COBOL word size

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I just compiled a program using a different compiler and came up with a
problem which I didn't know existed.  The new compiler's word size is
slightly smaller than what I was used to.  Since our standard creates
words using a data dictionary which adds suffixes - this problem could
be an expensive one.

What sizes are out there for COBOL words on various compilers?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL word size

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
According to the glossary in ANSI X3.23-1974, "Word" is defined on page I-71
as "A character string of not more than 30 characters which forms a
user-defined word, a system-name, or a reserved word."

The same size description appears on Page IV-5 of ANSI X3.23-1985 for "COBOL
Words" in section 4.2.2.1:  "A COBOL word is a character-string of not more
than 30 characters ...".

Note that both of these are *upper* limits.

What word size were you using?  What is the new limitation that you
encountered unexpectedly?

    -Chuck Stevens

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I just compiled a program using a different compiler and came up with a
>problem which I didn't know existed.  The new compiler's word size is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>What sizes are out there for COBOL words on various compilers?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL word size: Why from 30 to 31?

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DF3C71.27C1BCB5@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com> <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
An aside: I notice the draft COBOL 2000 standard calls for increasing
the maximum word size to 31 characters. That struck me as odd. If we're
going to enlarge it, why enlarge it by just one character?

--John

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> According to the glossary in ANSI X3.23-1974, "Word" is defined on page I-71
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> >What sizes are out there for COBOL words on various compilers?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL word size: Why from 30 to 31?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bni4f$c8h@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com> <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <36DF3C71.27C1BCB5@ibm.net>`

```
John Hicks (Remove "nospam" from address before replying) wrote in message
<36DF3C71.27C1BCB5@ibm.net>...
>An aside: I notice the draft COBOL 2000 standard calls for increasing
>the maximum word size to 31 characters. That struck me as odd. If we're
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


It had to do with "compatibility" with some other ISO Standard.  My memory
is foggy about this (because we also expanded the numeric digit size from 18
to 31 - for compatibility with another standard).  I think one of these was
for compatibility with the C Standard and one with the SQL Standard - but
that is only a "vague" memory.

FYI - the reason that COBOL word length is *nice* to have the same as
another language, is that it is nice to have EXTERNAL data with the same
names across products. The draft Standard has other ways of dealing with
this, but it was part of the reason that the word size was increased to 31
characters.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL word size

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TOUD2.103$Wj6.225@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com> <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
I believe even though it is a 30 character limit, you can create larger
words. The compiler will just recognize only the first 30 characters. Some
compilers will refuse to accept it causing a severe error, and some will
give it a simple warning. The ones with the simple warnings will be the
ones that look at the first 30 characters if longer than 30. As far as I've
been programming since the start, the size is 30 characters, especially for
compilers on the mainframe.

Regards,
Gary Roush

Chuck Stevens wrote in message <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...

"COBOL
>Words" in section 4.2.2.1:  "A COBOL word is a character-string of not
more
>than 30 characters ...".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL word size

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f%D2.387$Wj6.439@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com> <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <TOUD2.103$Wj6.225@news20.ispnews.com> <36E01DBC.D37ABF1E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The type of compiler and version of it  would have a lot to do with it.
Some compilers accepted it and some didn't. I could write 40 characters and
use the names just fine. BUT if I have 2 field-names that had more than 30
characters and the first 30 characters were identical, it would cause an
error either at compile time or at run time saying identical fields found.
This was on the IBM 3090 mainframe using IBM's COBOL II.

Gary Roush

>> ones that look at the first 30 characters if longer than 30. As far as
I've

for

>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL word size

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e7190b.74841793@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com> <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <TOUD2.103$Wj6.225@news20.ispnews.com> <36E01DBC.D37ABF1E@NOSPAMhome.com> <5f%D2.387$Wj6.439@news20.ispnews.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 5 Mar 1999 18:08:13 -0700, when "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The type of compiler and version of it  would have a lot to do with it.
>Some compilers accepted it and some didn't. I could write 40 characters and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>This was on the IBM 3090 mainframe using IBM's COBOL II.

It's unlikely, but if a compiler just throws away the end of a long name,
it can introduce subtle bugs.  Eg:

 MY-FIRST SECTION.
 PAR1.
     PERFORM THIS-PARAGRAPH-NAME-IS-TOO-LONG-B
 ....
 THIS-PARAGRAPH-NAME-IS-TOO-LONG-A.
 ....
 MY-SECOND SECTION.
 THIS-PARAGRAPH-NAME-IS-TOO-LONG-B.

This will compile clean, but it will PERFORM
THIS-PARAGRAPH-NAME-IS-TOO-LONG-A not THIS-PARAGRAPH-NAME-IS-TOO-LONG-B.
The two paragraph names cannot be distinguished, so
THIS-PARAGRAPH-NAME-IS-TOO-LON is implicitly qualified by the current
section.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL word size

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KgMF2.10$ud5.140@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36DEDBF6.E73D148F@NOSPAMhome.com> <7bmobd$n2q$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <TOUD2.103$Wj6.225@news20.ispnews.com> <36E01DBC.D37ABF1E@NOSPAMhome.com> <5f%D2.387$Wj6.439@news20.ispnews.com> <36e7190b.74841793@netnews>`

```
Exactly!  Rule of thumb is making sure your names are truly unique up to 30
chars.

Gary

found.

>
```

### SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

- **From:** jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel)
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr>`

```
I'm searching for non expensive SQL precompiler wich translate
EXEC-SQL in traditional cobol statements
Thanks
:-) jm
```

#### ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
To get a "good" answer to this question, you will need to tell us
    - what operating system
    - what COBOL compiler
    - what database product

There are "easy" answers to your question, but they vary completely
depending on your answers to these 3 questions.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

- **From:** jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel)
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Hum ! In fact, i'm interested by all... else i gave these details of
course !
Why ? Because i think this is not a common software (i did not found
any reference about that on the web), and if it exists for cobol X
with OS X, and i use cobol Y on OS Y, may be it's interesting for me
to change ! And because if i cannot change, i can contact the provider
of the software and ask him if he plan to do something on 'my' OS with
'my' cobol. And because may be other people are interested. And so
on...
Well, if you have any answer, i am interested !
jm ;-)

On Thu, 4 Mar 1999 17:19:07 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>To get a "good" answer to this question, you will need to tell us
>    - what operating system
>    - what COBOL compiler
>    - what database product
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr>`

```
IBM and Micro Focus (Merant) COBOLs both have methods of handling EXEC SQL
statements in their compilers (on the Workstation - on the Mainframe you
MUST use DB2 for IBM).  There are COBOL pre-compilers for (that I know of -
and probably more exist)
 - DB2 (all platforms)
 - Oracle
 - Ingess
 - SQL Server

Therefore, as I say, if you have a real "environment" that you want to work
with, this is actually POSSIBLE for any (well almost any?) COBOL compiler on
any environment with any database - but there are NONE that are totally
portable to all environments and all compilers and all databases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel)
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e00233.31701109@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr> <7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Ok. This was not by question. Excuse me for my bad use of english.
I know the precompilers.
When i say: "wich translate in traditional cobol", i mean than all the
sentences generated are in pure cobol. I want to have:
EXEC SQL .... END-EXEC
translated by:
OPEN...
START...
READ... and so on...
and not translated by:
MOVE...
MOVE...
CALL "_SQL_something" USING...
The purpose of that is to use SQL in the source program, and to use
traditional ISAM in the generated program, without specific database
engine.
Sorry if this is still not clear. I can give more explanation if
necessary.

Best regards
jm

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>IBM and Micro Focus (Merant) COBOLs both have methods of handling EXEC SQL..............
```

```
I have heard of products that work the reverse direction but not this way.
In other words the products that I know of allow you to code OPEN, READ,
WRITE, CLOSE and still go against SQL (relational) databases.

I have never heard of a product that allows you to use EXEC SQL in your
source code but go against an ISAM file. However, now that the question is
clearer, others may have some suggestions.
```

```
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> clearer, others may have some suggestions.

I've spent most of my career working on systems with Heirarchial /
network databases. The most common one I've used is IDMS, which allows
us to use KSDS files as though they were part of the database.  Never
saw that used until my current job, and fortunately we'll be getting rid
of them soon.

Of course, IDMS also has Logical Record Facility, allowing shortcuts for
people who aren't supposed to need to know the actual database
structure.  That hasn't been used where I've worked as each shop has
come to the conclusion that we didn't want ignorant people working the
database.
```

```
I think Steve was referring to AcuSQL, which converts EXEC SQL statements to
Calls to any SQL92 compliant database.  Very similar to ProCobol.

Acucorp also has a product called AcuODBC which allows you to use SQL syntax
to access index files.

You could combine the 2 products to get your desired SQL to COBOL to index
file functionality.

But again Jean-michael, if you don't give any background on what you are
really trying to accomplish it is hard to help.

Mike

jean-michel wrote:

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> name). I'm looking for a Sql-to-native-cobol translator.
```
