wrote:
>But again Jean-michael, if you don't give any background on what you are
>really trying to accomplish it is hard to help.
Ok Mike, i will try to explain a little bit more.
We have a lot of customers who use cobol application writted with
cobol-isam syntax. This application is not expensive and sometimes,
there is just one computer. It contain a lot of programs.
We would like to use SQL syntax because it's great (:-). But if we use
proCobol or others, we have to provide DBMS engine, and we have to
upgrade customers old computers, and then it's too expensive.
If we find such a precompiler which translate SQL to NATIVE cobol (not
to a lot of CALL, with DBMS engine required), we can use SQL and
customers can stay with ISAM engine).
Somebody talk about IDMS and KSDS. Ok this WAS fine. But i cannot
imagine to give an aplication builded with KSDS engine and mainframe
platform (because this is VERY expensive).
Of course, i can imagine to write it myself; but i'm afraid it's too
much for one man alone...
Thanks everybody
jm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c2jvs$pv2@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr> <7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36e097fc.250803140@news1.ibm.net> <7brfhm$uf0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e38308.2836714@news.easynet.fr> <36E4A014.5E53F743@san.rr.com> <36e4cc59.758305@news.easynet.fr>`

```
Jean-Michael,
  What I think you are missing are two things:

1) Most precompilers do translate into "native" COBOL code - just not the
native code that you want.

2) I think you would do MUCH better looking for SQL interface programs that
allow you to code EXEC SQL statements that go against "ISAM" files.  These
are also specific per ISAM file system, but at least these DO exist (and I
think should meet your needs).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c3aab$bq7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr> <7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36e097fc.250803140@news1.ibm.net> <7brfhm$uf0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e38308.2836714@news.easynet.fr>`

```
In article <36e38308.2836714@news.easynet.fr>,
  jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel) wrote:
> On Sat, 06 Mar 1999 14:53:45 GMT, Steve Shriver
> <steves815@my-dejanews.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

jean-michel -

I believe the confusion you keep running into is partly due to your mis-use of
the term 'native-cobol.'

There are many products out there that require some sort of pre-compiler to
translate their code into "native cobol."  The ones I am most familiar with
are CICS, DB2, and IMS.  I strongly suspect that other database products,
such as IDMS, work the same way.  That is their non-cobol code -- be it a SQL
statement, a CICS command, whatever -- are enclosed in a "EXEC product ....
END- EXEC" delimiter.  The precompiler scans the source code for "EXEC"
statements that it recognizes, parses it, comments it out, and replaces it
with "native cobol."  These are invariably MOVE and CALL statements because,
ultimately, the only way for a cobol pgm to access the services of these
subsystems is by CALLing a routine that is supplied by the sub-system.	MOVE
and CALL are as "native-cobol" as you can get.

Now, as I understand it, what you are looking for is something to recognize
the EXEC SQL ... END-EXEC statements and, instead of replacing them with
calls to the realational database, replace them with standard cobol file
access methods.

Frankly, I don't think it can be done.	There are too many things in SQL that
simply don't map to standard cobol file access.  More certainly, there are
requirements of standard cobol file access that don't map to SQL.  Why would
you want to?  You mentioned "portability" but cobol is already the original
"portable" language.  I have taken several cobol programs across platforms,
and have never had to make any changes beyond the environment section.

Ed Stevens
(opinions are mine and not necessarily those of my employer)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

```
On Tue, 09 Mar 1999 14:13:39 GMT, Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:

>Now, as I understand it, what you are looking for is something to recognize
>the EXEC SQL ... END-EXEC statements and, instead of replacing them with
>calls to the realational database, replace them with standard cobol file
>access methods.
YES !!!

>Frankly, I don't think it can be done.
Why !!! At the primary level of the DBMS, you will find an ISAM !! And
it's not a technical problem to realize that. I remember now the name
of the MF product wich offered ODBC access to MF ISAM files
(Correlate). If this is possible with ODBC (writted in C or C++) it's
possible with Cobol !

My question was just: is this existing ?
(and not: "is this possible ?" or "is this good ?" or "could i call it
'native' ?")
So, if you don't know, please just don't answer, and i will assume
this is not existing !

Ok! And what do You think about such things like SQL Joins?

>My question was just: is this existing ?

I think it does not exists.
Your problem is best solved with cobol copies.
You can have the same source and embed the access to data in a separate
module like
a cobol copy or another cobol program, so if you have a database you can use
SQL
otherwise you use standard cobol to access ISAM files.
The trick is using the right tool for the job.

Yes, ports do ususally require *some* modifications.  Sometimes in some
surprising places.  My point to Jean-Michael was simply that finding a
product to allow him to code SQL in place of standard ISAM -- to access ISAM
files -- was not going to buy him anything in terms of portablility. (He had
indicated in an earlier msg in this thread that portability was the reason
they wanted to use SQL.) Perhaps I didn't make *that* as clear as I thought,
but as you can see from his reply to me, he didn't think too much of my even
asking . . .

Ed Stevens
(Opinions are mine and not necessarily those of my employer.)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:

> Yes, ports do ususally require *some* modifications.  Sometimes in some
> surprising places.  My point to Jean-Michael was simply that finding a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> asking . . .

Why not (portability)? If you had this hypothetical SQL to COBOL translator for both
platforms, you would just recompile (re-precompile?). I had a look at this subject a
while ago. Think about it - how many times you go thru the same cycle over and over
again: select, sort, transform, and show data. If you limited yourself to some
subset of the whole SQL standard, it could very reasonably be done, and it would
save a LOT of time.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

```
In article <36E6489B.8338A758@conexion.com.py>,
  Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py> wrote:
> Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why not (portability)? If you had this hypothetical SQL to COBOL translator
for both
> platforms, you would just recompile (re-precompile?). I had a look at this
subject a
> while ago. Think about it - how many times you go thru the same cycle over
and over
> again: select, sort, transform, and show data. If you limited yourself to some
> subset of the whole SQL standard, it could very reasonably be done, and it
would
> save a LOT of time.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Maybe I'm overlooking the obvious (it wouldn't be the first time!) but I
can't see where being able to code "EXEC SQL SELECT ...... FROM .... WHERE
...END- EXEC" is any more portable than "OPEN ..... MOVE .... READ" 
Especially when what you want to do is have the EXEC SQL translated into
"OPEN...READ."	(Which themselves essentially get translated into
"MOVE...CALL" to access the run-time library routines that implement the
standard cobol file i-o.)


Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer.)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c8vi2$nvr@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr> <7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36e097fc.250803140@news1.ibm.net> <7brfhm$uf0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e38308.2836714@news.easynet.fr> <7c3aab$bq7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36E53FEB.B7710AA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <7c411c$12e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36E6489B.8338A758@conexion.com.py> <7c5rq8$j0m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e77185.1164839@news.easynet.fr>`

```
Well, let me point out the opposite as well.
With the EXEC SQL you:

  Don't have to know anything much about the structure of the table(s)
      you are pulling information from or putting information in.

  Don't have to know anything about where the tables are physcially,
      and indeed, can use tables from many different locations in 
      the same statement

  Don't have to think about optimizing the code that retrieves
    the information

  Don't have to worry about managing access to the table(s)

  Don't have to worry about sorts

      and

  You can change the underlying database/strucutres with no impact
   on the application. (Emphasis on CAN, you can also change it and
   blow the application.) 


Database engines also provide some level of transactional management
as well, which is something COBOL just doesn't do all that well.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c823j$667@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr> <7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36e097fc.250803140@news1.ibm.net> <7brfhm$uf0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e38308.2836714@news.easynet.fr> <7c3aab$bq7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36E53FEB.B7710AA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <7c411c$12e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36E6489B.8338A758@conexion.com.py> <7c5rq8$j0m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e77185.1164839@news.easynet.fr>`

```
jean-michel wrote in message <36e77185.1164839@news.easynet.fr>...
   <snip>
>The portability is not the main problem.
>The problem is:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>jm

I think that the real problem is that you are looking at the solution that
you WANT rather than the data processing  PROBLEM that you are trying to
solve.  As I have mentioned (and others have mentioned as well) there are a
NUMBER or products that allow you to code EXEC SQL statements but that go
against underlying ISAM files. (These do have some restrictions on what SQL
statements they support - but some of the more sophisticated products
support MOST of standard SQL.) Therefore, if your "goal" is to allow your
customers/users to use EXEC SQL and you to support a "flat" (ISAM) type
file, then this is easily "do-able" but NOT by having he EXEC SQL statement
"translated" into native COBOL I-O.  Instead, both your users/customers
*and* your programs would be written with EXEC SQL statements - and the
"engine" (yes it is an engine) would go against the correct "ISAM" file.

If this is what you want/need, then all you need to post is what COBOL
compiler(s) you want supported and what ISAM file system you are using (as
your underlying one).  Again, this is NOT portable in some ways - but is in
others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL precompiler wich translate in cobol

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E79B93.805F24FD@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<36debbe9.8162337@news.easynet.fr> <7bn4da$e46@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36dfdcf5.22165329@news.easynet.fr> <7boqg2$50q@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36e097fc.250803140@news1.ibm.net> <7brfhm$uf0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e38308.2836714@news.easynet.fr> <7c3aab$bq7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36E53FEB.B7710AA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <7c411c$12e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36E6489B.8338A758@conexion.com.py> <7c5rq8$j0m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:

> Maybe I'm overlooking the obvious (it wouldn't be the first time!) but I
> can't see where being able to code "EXEC SQL SELECT ...... FROM .... WHERE
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard cobol file i-o.)

Well, lets have a try...

- OPEN, with all the error checking involved
- START, idem
- loop with READ, checking for end conditions, and maybe doing some fancy restarting
because of sub-key searching
- in each READ, also do foreign key checking, that is, I want all records that have
some value in a record from a different file - don't forget to include in OPEN above

- CLOSE, this is easy ;)
- select certain fields, and pass them somehow to a sorting routine (I use auxillary
files)
- get the sorted records and pass them on to whoever requested all this

OK, now lets say we have this magic program that takes this input:

SELECT a,b,c FROM file1,file2
WHERE a.file1 = b.file2
ORDER BY a,b

and churns out the above COBOL code. Now would'nt that be a time-saver? And we
have'nt even talked about a lot of other nice stuff.

I've found that roughly 50% of the code in my programs are user-interface. 25% are
searching and sorting, and 25% are report formating. Among all these are 5-10%
actual logic. If you really want to cut down on coding time, automate the first
three as much as posible.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

---

<a id="t-72f526ae03"></a>
## Thread: CobolTransformer 3.3 Announce — 11 msgs

### CobolTransformer 3.3 Announce

- **From:** vadik@siber.com (Vadim Maslov)
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<7bml2e$hgq$1@winter.news.rcn.net>`

```
	CobolTransformer 3.3 Announce
	-----------------------------

Siber Systems is pleased to announce availability of 
Version 3.3 of CobolTransformer Toolkit, Tools and Converters.

CobolTransformer toolkit provides infrastructure 
for Cobol program analyzers, converters, and generators.
CobolTransformer Tools and Converters are specific
tools built on top of this infrastructure.

To get more information on CobolTransformer, 
please direct your browser to http://www.siber.com/sct/

In case you have problems connecting to our main site,
a mirror of the main site is available http://www.siber.org/sct/


New features in version 3.3:

** Transformations:

The question of software reuse is so important for us 
that we decided to add transformations architecture to
CobolTransformer Toolkit.  

We broke all our converters into a number of small
transformations that have universal interface and run
equally well in command-line and visual environments. 
Each transformation has a well-defined functionality 
and can be easily reused in other projects.

Transformation is an object that searches and/or transforms
the Program Tree.  Transformations follow certain
conventions that allow CobolTransformer system to 
relieve a developer from many chores related to 
Program Tree transformation.

In addition to reuse transformations bring us other
important conveniences:

- Command-Line and Visual Environments.  
The same transformation can be run both from
command-line (batch) environment and from visual
(GUI) environment with no code changed in the
transformation itself.  So it is like in Java, but on
a bigger scale: Write Once, Run Everywhere, where Everywhere
spans command-line and visual world on multiple platforms!

- Configurability.
Transformation objects may contain configuration
parameters (options).  Each configuration parameter
may have an option descriptor associated with it.
This option descriptor tells the system how this
parameter is set by end user both from command-line
environment (argc, argv arguments) and from 
visual environment (tabbed dialogues in WIN32 GUI).

- Inheritance by Specialization.
If you take a transformation with configuration
parameters and specialize it by setting its
configuration parameters, the resulting
transformation can be given a separate name 
and treated like a regular transformation.

- Save and Load.
Transformation instances (specialized transformations) 
can be saved to file and loaded from file.

- Logging and Debugging.
CobolTransformer system can automatically generate a
log that lists all transformations applied to the Cobol
program. 


** Visual Tool to be announced soon.

The visual environment in which transformations are constructed and 
run is a revolutionary Windows-based visual Cobol program analyzer 
and conversion builder. Yes, you really will be able to build 
conversions in visual environment, without writing any code at all.
The visual tool will be released soon, so check our Web site often!


** Transformation Library

Often used transformations are assembled into libraries that  
from now on are sold by Siber Systems, so that our customers 
may base their conversion effort on a solid foundation 
of professionally programmed transformations.

Library of existing transformations helps you to start 
writing transformations for your conversion sooner, because:
- You can reuse the library transformations directly,
  by incorporating them into your conversions.
- The source for the library transformations is available, 
  so you can reuse programming tricks from the transformation source.
- Library transformation source provides a good example of 
  how to write transformations, so you learn faster by example.

Currently the following transformation libraries are available:
- Standard Transformation Library (STRL) that performs 
  code normalization by bringing it closer to ANSI-85 standard.
  Includes name renumbering transformations used in cbl-beau.
- SQL host variable normalization transformations.
- Year 2000 windowing transformations.
- IBM Cobol to Fujitsu Cobol transformations.
- Micro Focus Cobol to Fujitsu Cobol transformations.

CobolTransformer manual contains a complete list 
of available transformations.


** Object Configuration

In large programming projects we are dealing with
objects that need to be configurable by user.  
For instance, in CobolTransformer we have Parser object,
PrettyPrinter object and transformation objects that
all have configuration parameters, such as dialect of
Cobol to be parsed (in Parser) or indentation step 
(in PrettyPrinter).

We invented a template-based mechanism for 
describing configuration parameters that allows:

- Command-Line and Visual Configuration.
Configuration parameters, once described in our
framework, can be configured by user both in
command-line and visual (GUI) environments.

- Readable Parameter files.
Configuration parameters can be saved to text file 
in the form "name = value" and loaded from file.

- Parameter Marshalling.
Configuration parameter blocks can be sent across 
the network in platform-independent format.


** Preprocessor Integration

PrettyPrinter can now generate Cobol code containing
#FILE/#LINE preprocessor statements that connect
generated Cobol code to the original Cobol code.


** SQL parser

We added a parser and Program Tree for SQL/92
language embedded into Cobol program.


** Evaluation Version Limits Increased

By popular demand, CobolTransformer Evaluation Package 
limits were increased to:
- 1024 lines per source file (was 256),
- 1024 lines of code total (was 256),
- 512 names in a program (was 128).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CobolTransformer 3.3 Announce

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36E5CF94.EEA81DA@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7bml2e$hgq$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <36E1C72A.332522AC@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Vadim Maslov wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

Dear Ken,

We want you and everybody else to evaluate CobolTransformer
and make sure that it works. We are not afraid of customers
evaluating our product because we know that it works.

However, we have to distinguish between evaluation and 
paid-for commercial use (because we have to pay our bills).

Nature of conversion business is such that if we give
a customer 60-day trial for a full version, 
he/she can start and finish complete conversion
in these 60 days, and after the conversion is done,
some customers may forget to pay for the product.

So if Ken or anybody in comp.lang.cobol can propose
CobolTransformer evaluation scheme that works both 
for customer and for us, we would be willing to implement it.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CobolTransformer 3.3 Announce

- **From:** "Alan Maitland" <AMaitland@NBCI.Net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<7c77co$e5k$1@news-2.csn.net>`
- **References:** `<7bml2e$hgq$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <36E1C72A.332522AC@zip.com.au> <36E5CF94.EEA81DA@siber.com>`

```
Mr. Maslov,

Vadim Maslov wrote in message <36E5CF94.EEA81DA@siber.com>...
--snip--
>
>However, we have to distinguish between evaluation and
>paid-for commercial use (because we have to pay our bills).


Reasonable.

>
>Nature of conversion business is such that if we give
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Vadim Maslov

Perhaps one method you might consider is an escrow account where a
prospective customer can place the entire fee for the product for the 60 day
evaluation period.  Although such an arrangement still has risks, at least
the money is on the table and can be released at the end of a customer
conversion.

There is still some risk of the customer denying that a successful
conversion was accomplished.  Your company is at some higher risk if the
agreement is not written up properly.

Perhaps a shorter money back trial (a couple of days), with a pro-rated
arrangement up to the time of the evaluation period, where all escrow funds
become due and payable to you.  This would offer a good incentive to get the
project moving as quickly as possible.  It could also be a reasonably good
sales qualifier.

I would recommend that the details of such an agreement should be attended
to by competent counsel.

I'm sure that I have forgotten something here, so I would imagine others may
also have comments on this for you.

HTH.

Alan Maitland
President
NBCI.Net

Pay attention - stay alive
```

On Wed, 10 Mar 1999 18:47:11 -0700, "Alan Maitland" <AMaitland@NBCI.Net> wrote:

>Mr. Maslov,
>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>also have comments on this for you.

If the target machines are connected to the Inet (or at least the *evaluation*
machines can meet that requirement), it shouldn't be too difficult to work out a
comm scheme that only lets the eval run if the vendor's server allow it to run,
and if the limit is exceeded, deduct a per-use fee from the escrow funds.

A standalone alternative might be to use a dongle set to only allow a limited
number of runs.

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

Or, license the software for a limited time, or rent it even. 
There are always going to be crooks around, but stopping crooks
by muzzeling honest customers just doesn't work all that well. : )
           
Best 
-Paul


Alan Maitland (AMaitland@NBCI.Net) wrote:
: Mr. Maslov,

: Vadim Maslov wrote in message <36E5CF94.EEA81DA@siber.com>...
: --snip--
: >
: >However, we have to distinguish between evaluation and
: >paid-for commercial use (because we have to pay our bills).


: Reasonable.

: >
: >Nature of conversion business is such that if we give
: >a customer 60-day trial for a full version,
: >he/she can start and finish complete conversion
: >in these 60 days, and after the conversion is done,
: >some customers may forget to pay for the product.
: >
: >So if Ken or anybody in comp.lang.cobol can propose
: >CobolTransformer evaluation scheme that works both
: >for customer and for us, we would be willing to implement it.
: >
: >Best regards,
: >Vadim Maslov

: Perhaps one method you might consider is an escrow account where a
: prospective customer can place the entire fee for the product for the 60 day
: evaluation period.  Although such an arrangement still has risks, at least
: the money is on the table and can be released at the end of a customer
: conversion.

: There is still some risk of the customer denying that a successful
: conversion was accomplished.  Your company is at some higher risk if the
: agreement is not written up properly.

: Perhaps a shorter money back trial (a couple of days), with a pro-rated
: arrangement up to the time of the evaluation period, where all escrow funds
: become due and payable to you.  This would offer a good incentive to get the
: project moving as quickly as possible.  It could also be a reasonably good
: sales qualifier.

: I would recommend that the details of such an agreement should be attended
: to by competent counsel.

: I'm sure that I have forgotten something here, so I would imagine others may
: also have comments on this for you.

: HTH.

: Alan Maitland
: President
: NBCI.Net

: Pay attention - stay alive
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CobolTransformer 3.3 Announce

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36E8A060.1C30D121@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7bml2e$hgq$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <36E1C72A.332522AC@zip.com.au> <36E5CF94.EEA81DA@siber.com> <7c77co$e5k$1@news-2.csn.net> <7c8vmu$nvr@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Here I provdie a summary of replies and my thoughts:

- Throw in annoying comments.
Does not work, because annoying comments that follow a clear
pattern are just too easy to remove. I would write you a program
that does it in 5 minutes, be it in Perl or in C++, or even in Cobol.

- Put money/code/whatever in escrow. 
Too complicated. At the end of the day, as our Australian
friends say, only the lawyers will be to benefit,
not customers and not us vendors.

- Extend client-server arrangement to Internet.
That sounds like a good idea which had previously 
occured to me. Currently CobolTransformer is already doing 
client-server and extending it to Internet CS is quite possible.
So you will have a full version that will be somewhat slow
because it has to go to Internet CobolTransformer server
that would register all uses. Trial users will have to register,
so we will know who's using up how many lines and 
we'll be able to ask for payment when line count hits a limit,
let's say 100K lines of code.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

- **References:** `<7bml2e$hgq$1@winter.news.rcn.net> <36E1C72A.332522AC@zip.com.au> <36E5CF94.EEA81DA@siber.com> <OsA0XAAood52Ew7H@elmbronze.demon.co.uk> <36e67325.634358796@news1.ibm.net>`

```
on Wed, 10 Mar 1999 Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote
>On Wed, 10 Mar 1999 02:34:16 +0000, Dave Eastabrook
><news@elmbronze.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>- "annoyance".  :(

Whatever anyone comes up with, someone else can crack - or will organise
thousands of people planet-wide to try and crack.  Best that can be done
is to buy time, usually.  Most large companies though have annual audits
for unlicensed software, and at least pay lip service to paying fees. So
a week before the audit, everyone takes off their unlicensed editors and
cover CD screensavers - and puts them back two weeks later.

But at least "serious" software is still counted, and the extra licence
fees appropriate to unauthorised usage belatedly paid for I suppose. In
my view, Vadim would probably be better not bothering with any dongles,
as goodwill may well result in more paid-up licences, than irritation
caused by making sufficient testing possible.

I was being "superfluous" talking about multiple comment blocks.  Just a
discrete one at the front of the program would serve as a reminder.

There are ways to do it though, so as to virtually force pay-up.  He'd
need to include a tailored compiler in the product, and perhaps take a
random block of source code and render it as machine code in a listing.
That way while they could see the result source of the conversion, and
even test it, they'd not get maintainable source code.  My estimate is
that this feature could take a year to develop and fully test !!!

:Dave
```

```
In this age of databases, why in the world would any programmer in their
right mind use anything but a REAL database engine like:
Oracle, DB2, Sybase etc...  These database engines know how to 
keep track of "deleted" records.  Face it, even for COBOL, flat file
databases (and I use that term loosely) is a dead technology.
There is, of course, database maintenance, but this can usually
be done online.

Michael Austin
An Independent Database Consultant
austin@inmind.com

##### ↳ ↳ Re: deleted records

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bomvs$mvp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DF3FDD.69A6B55C@inmind.com>`

```
Do you have reasons for what you say, or are you just
talking the party line?   I've never seen the advantage of
declaring something useless just because a new method
came along.  The cases that I have seen where a small
business was given a DB2 file and told to play have never
worked worth a damn.

If a database engine is being used because it is usefull,
then fine.  However, a good number of systems that I've
seen using DB2 on the PC for small systems have used it
so the programmer can avoid writing updates, edits, etc.
Instead, they patch the database on a regular basis, and
force the user to become the database manager instead
of the computer system.

A small system using one language is simpler that mixing
two technologies.  If you are going to do a complete job,
there is often a great deal of extra overhead and work to
use a "real" (one that you already know) datebase, and
very little "real" advantage.

Michael E. Austin wrote in message <36DF3FDD.69A6B55C@inmind.com>...
>In this age of databases, why in the world would any programmer in their
>right mind use anything but a REAL database engine like:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: deleted records

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Since the original post was specific to RM/COBOL, this issue has been
explained to Dr. Carlos Garcia de Paredas and resolved via private e-mail.
But since this delicious troll, I shall explain so that Mr. Austin can face
it.

RM/COBOL indeed _does_ recover the space once occupied by deleted records.
Always has.  Golly!  Can you imagine that we figured out how to do that so
many, many years ago?

As at least one other response has pointed out, the collection of (typically
indexed) files managed by a COBOL application _is_ a database, and usually a
very good one at that.  Add on a product like Relativity (r) (see
http://www.liant.com/products/retooling/databridge/index.html) and you
discover that it has almost all the wonderful characteristics of what you
mysteriously call "a REAL database."

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standard COBOL supports real *relational* databases

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E08A14.D755B920@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DF3FDD.69A6B55C@inmind.com> <7botiv$6l$1@client2.news.psi.net>`

```
Let me add one point to Tom's excellent post:

Not only does standard COBOL support *real* databases, it supports real
*relational* databases.

Let's not forget that the term "relational database" refers to one where
each relationship in a set of data is expressed in a simple indexed
table. Being relational doesn't require either SQL or a client-server
engine. The major requirement is to follow Codd's "normal forms" when
designing your data files.

A relational table can be fully implemented as a COBOL
indexed-sequential file. A relational database can be fully implemented
as a set of such files. Moreover, in most cases, such an implementation
will be far more efficient than one using a SQL-based database engine.
(It's also cheaper and usually easier to deliver to an end-user.) 

I will grant you that a SQL engine may bring additional flexibility
(e.g. the ability to do on the fly SQL queries) and data compatibility. 

I will further grant you that when a full-blown client-server system is
required, a more elaborate calculus applies which *may* argue for an
off-the-shelf engine. (Or, more likely, the ODBC approach that Liant's
Relativity uses.)

--John



Tom Morrison wrote:
> 
> Since the original post was specific to RM/COBOL, this issue has been
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c72jb$qqu$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DF3FDD.69A6B55C@inmind.com> <7botiv$6l$1@client2.news.psi.net> <36E08A14.D755B920@ibm.net> <36e6f2b8.65049764@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <36e6f2b8.65049764@netnews>...
[...]
>
>Who is Codd?  Do you mena Dobbs?
>
E. F. Codd, prolific writer on database theory.

C. J. Date, "An Introduction to Database Systems", Second Edition,
1977, Addison-Wesley, Page 81, has this reference and comment.

" E. F. Codd, "A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data
  Banks." CACM 13, No. 6 (June 1970).

"The current interest in the ralational approach is largely due to the
  work of E. F. Codd, and this is the paper which triggered off much
  of the subsequent activity in the field. It contains an explanation of
  the relational model, definitions of some relational algebra
  operations, and a discussion of redundancy and consistency.
  A seminal paper."

-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

or overhead, or more likely, because it uses VSAM files anyway
on a mainframe. :)

In particular, VSAM (or other types of database files) managed
by a transaction monitor are essentialy just exactly what 
you would think of as a database. Very configurable databases.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: deleted records

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36df5e8c.170551511@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DF3FDD.69A6B55C@inmind.com>`

```
On Thu, 04 Mar 1999 21:22:21 -0500, "Michael E. Austin"
<austin@inmind.com> wrote:

>In this age of databases, why in the world would any programmer in their
>right mind use anything but a REAL database engine like:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>be done online.

I beg to differ.  There are MANY MANY times when a "flat" file can be
more efficient and reliable than a relational database.  In many cases
the database is overkill.  Additionally, I would not put a database in
the hands of an "end user", but I have no qualms about placing complex
indexed file system in the same hands.  They don't NEED a DBA.  Cost
is a real issue.  I'm writing applications for SMALL business.  They
can't afford, nor do they desire a database!

I have a system at work presently that is about 30 gigabytes.
Innovative techniques if file storage make it so.  If it were stored
in a relational database such as Oracle, or DB2 it would be over 100
megabytes.  The overhead would KILL response time - which must be
fast.  

There are some things, however, where the relationanal database is
superior - and in those cases, I am all for its use!
```

FIRST OFF, Michael, those products are real HOGS.
They require double to quadruple the disk space for data,
and much more memory.

SECONDLY, they are NOT always the best solution, particularly
if reports are needed often.

For the Doctor,
These records are LOGICALLY DELETED, they can be written over.
There is no real need do 'delete them'.
Michael E. Austin wrote in message <36DF3FDD.69A6B55C@inmind.com>...
>In this age of databases, why in the world would any programmer in their
>right mind use anything but a REAL database engine like:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

from
>> a database file, the deleted records are still physically present,
occupying
>> valuable disk space.  To physically remove these deleted records, we copy
the
>> file.  There must be an easier way to physically remove the deleted
records.
>> I hope somebody can help. Dr. Carlos Garcia de Paredes - Panama

<a id="t-184f3ca752"></a>
## Thread: Mixed - language help — 18 msgs

### Mixed - language help

- **From:** Paul McGougan <Paul.McGougan@braintree.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Right from the start: Please don't flame me for asking this question, I have
to for work.

I'm a C programmer and I'm being supplied Cobol source routines that I'm
supposed to call from my program. Has anyone done, or can tell me how to call
these Cobol functions from a C program.

I have AcuCobol-GT, which I have used to compile the Cobol source to object
files.

Thanks in advance,

Paul.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Mixed - language help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bnib5$cea@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Is there any chance that you SOME control over which COBOL compiler you use?
Although inter-language (C with COBOL) programs CAN be done with AcuCOBOL -
it is probably the MOST DIFFICULT Workstation (PC or Unix) COBOL for doing
this.  If you must use AcuCOBOL, hopefully one of the AcuCOBOL NG readers
will give you the "gruesome" (but well documented) requirements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mixed - language help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bolvh$r2@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7bnib5$cea@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7bo1vn$2po$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

Paul McGougan wrote in message <7bo1vn$2po$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>In article <7bnib5$cea@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>,
>  "William M. Klein" <wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com> wrote:
>> Is there any chance that you SOME control over which COBOL compiler you
use?
>> Although inter-language (C with COBOL) programs CAN be done with
AcuCOBOL -
>> it is probably the MOST DIFFICULT Workstation (PC or Unix) COBOL for
doing
>> this.  If you must use AcuCOBOL, hopefully one of the AcuCOBOL NG readers
>> will give you the "gruesome" (but well documented) requirements.
>
>I can arrange to use any Cobol compiler that will do this and will compile
for
>SCO Unix.
>
>Paul.
>


Micro Focus (Merant), IBM, and Fujitsu COBOLs (that I know of - there may be
others) all support compiling for SCO Unix environments and all make ILC
(Inter-language communication) between COBOL and C pretty easy.  I think all
3 of them have examples in their "programming guides" on how to do this.
The big difference (that I know) from AcuCOBOL is that they keep the
maintenance of your COBOL programs EASILY separate from your C programs.
You just follow their documented features, comple, and LINK the two
applications together.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mixed - language help

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C+j9OAAICX42IwKI@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IWN$kHAMJ$32IwQu@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <7bp9m2$ntk@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
In article <7bp9m2$ntk@lotho.delphi.com>, paulr
<paulr@bix.com> writes
>Gee, I hope I am just out of date, but don't you have to recompile 
>the *runtime* to include the C routines in AcuCobol? That is very much 
>harder than say, just linking two programs together in IBM Cobol, or,
>I think, MicroFocus. 
>

To call C routines (assuming they're not in DLL's on Windows)
you have to *relink* the runtime, incorporating the C routines you
wrote. To call COBOL from C you relink the C routine to
incorporate the GT runtime libraries. In both cases it's a relink,
required only when the C routines change. Changes to COBOL
objects do *not* require a relink.

Really just linking two programs together! (The Acucobol-GT
runtime and the C routine).

Best regards

Nigel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mixed - language help

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bt0jn$94e@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <C+j9OAAICX42IwKI@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
That is what the problem is I suppose. :) You have to include the
AcuCorp runtime in everything. <grin> 

IBM COBOL is very different; you can simply call a C routine,
in a DLL, or in an object file. As long as you follow some
relatively simple paramter passing rules, everything works ok.
Same on the mainframe with LE. 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mixed - language help

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bs3j6$a5n@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IWN$kHAMJ$32IwQu@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <7bp9m2$ntk@lotho.delphi.com> <7bpvs0$p6t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Uh  - no. For example, in IBM COBOL, to call a C program from 
COBOL, you can simply CALL it. To call a function or program, 
you simply LINK them together. (Real link, as in object files 
to executable...) Same for calling COBOL from C. THere are 
some calling conventions, but of course, those are present in 
any language. 

I use a C Wrapper around COBOL with the Domninoe GO Webserver, 
but that is more to handle a POST than anything else. 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mixed - language help

```
Ken,

Fujitsu IS VERY aware of Linux.  They have been watching what is going on.
The answer is that "right now" there are no plans that I've been told of.
To create a Linux compiler would require rewriting the code for a new
compiler from scratch.  Right now, they are sticking to Win 95/98/NT Sun
Solaris, and Unix platforms, all 32-bit.  The market is still very big with
these platforms. Fujitsu however has never said, "never". So who knows,
maybe in the future they will come up with one.  Anyone want to volunteer
creating the compiler in Linux for Fujitsu?  It's a tough job and a whole
different platform. They surprised us with PowerCOBOL. They could surprise
us later with Linux.  Maybe if enough people write them requesting it, it
would give them some incentive. And this is only my opinion. Just wanted to
let you all know that the developers DO know and are very aware about Linux
and its capabilities.

Regards,
Gary Roush

Ken Foskey wrote in message <36E1CD8F.3642C6F0@zip.com.au>...
>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c8a8c$4gr$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7bngjt$ktf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7bnib5$cea@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7bo1vn$2po$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7bolvh$r2@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <7bp9j1$ntk@lotho.delphi.com> <7bpah7$3lr@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36e09757.250637687@news1.ibm.net> <36E1CD8F.3642C6F0@zip.com.au> <bfMF2.9$ud5.68@news20.ispnews.com>`

```

Gary Roush wrote in message ...
[...]
>To create a Linux compiler would require rewriting the code for a new
>compiler from scratch.  Right now, they are sticking to ...
> ... Unix platforms, all 32-bit. ...

Unix, 32-bit, Intel processor, is the description of Linux. Certainly,
there are some differences between Unix and Linux; but they are
not so different as to require starting from scratch. This is because
the specification for Unix is the specification for Linux!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JHartnett" <JHartnett@jjsnack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Try this....

01 FIELD-1.
   03 CHAR-1        Pic x(1).
   03 REST-OF-FIELD        Pic x(19).

Move FIELD-TO-JUSTIFY to FIELD-1.
If FIELD-1 not = spaces
        perform until CHAR-1 not = space
                move REST-OF-FIELD to FIELD-1
        end-perform
End-if.



This works....,
```

elegant.

Tom Wymer

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be69a3$09791af0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<9ZDD2.7$5M1.1029354@news.cwi.net> <19990305084604.20123.00000791@ng-cg1.aol.com> <7bq8pc$itc$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

Jerry Peacock <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in article
<7bq8pc$itc$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...
> I think he worries about:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to do it.

Assuming he wants to get rid of all spaces (leading and intermediate),
could't this be done with an UNSTRING + a STRING statement?

INITIALIZE TEMP1 TEMP2 ... TARGET
UNSTRING SOURCE DELIMITED BY ALL SPACE INTO TEMP1 TEMP2 ...
STRING TEMP1 TEMP2 ... DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO TARGET

You must have enough TEMPn identifiers for the maximum number of words, and
each of them must be long enough to accomodate it's word.
```

## Thread: Looking for a random numbers generator routine coded in Cobol — 7 msgs

### Looking for a random numbers generator routine coded in Cobol

- **From:** "lben1" <lben1@dada.it>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36defc55.0@news.dada.it>`

```
Dear Cobol brothers,

can anyone help me find a subprogram or a utility coded in Cobol for the
generation of random numbers.
I need it badly for some work I have to urgently carry out at my company.

Thanks in advance for any useful hint concerning the subject.

Bye and best wishes to my helper

Gigiben
lben1@dada.it
Florence Italy
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for a random numbers generator routine coded in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bn44p$dvp@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36defc55.0@news.dada.it>`

```
The simplest is to use the seconds portion of FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE as input
to the FUNCTION NUMVAL which in turn is the seed value to the FUNCTION
RANDOM.  (Other simpler uses of FUNCTION RANDOM will work - but the
"pseudo-random" nature of Function Random does cause some people to question
its randomness).
```

```

the MATHPK routine.

#### ↳ Re: Looking for a random numbers generator routine coded in Cobol

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob_a@unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e02781$0$13999@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<36defc55.0@news.dada.it>`

```
I needed something like this the other day.
Try 'random' from a UNIX command line.

My cobol code has something like this.

CALL "SYSTEM" USING "random 255 > xxx"

This will generate a random number between 0 and 255.
If you only want a two digit random number then replace 255 with 99.
You can select any range not exceeding 255.

Then I read xxx for my random number that was generated.

Rob Annandale.
___________________________________________________________

## Thread: Passport and TSO — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Passport and TSO

- **From:** charlajw@netcom.com (Charla J. Williams)
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<charlajwF81nx9.Kzs@netcom.com>`

```
This is the first contract where I've used Passport as my
telecommuting software.  For some reason the PF7 and PF8
aren't working when browsing or editing in TSO.  It's rather
annoying to type 'down 20' on the command line.  Any idea
what's going on and how to fix it back to where it works again?

Thank you, Charla
charlajw@netcom.com
```

#### ↳ OT Re: Passport and TSO

- **From:** "tmj" <tmj@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bmesg$3e42@musky.state.wi.us>`
- **References:** `<charlajwF81nx9.Kzs@netcom.com>`

```
Passport has a "Power Page" feature designed for reading long lists.  It
sounds like you may have it turned on.

When you press the PF8 key the first time it only gets the page data, when
you press it a second time Passport displays the page and request the next
one.  This way you can read the first page while the second page is coming
over the wire.

You can turn this on and off via the menus. I forget which one.  My version
of Passport is at home so I can't check right now.

tmj
tjones@dor<full stop>state<period>wi<dot>us

Charla J. Williams wrote in message ...
>This is the first contract where I've used Passport as my
>telecommuting software.  For some reason the PF7 and PF8
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Passport and TSO

- **From:** charlajw@netcom.com (Charla J. Williams)
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<charlajwF83vyG.4G2@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<charlajwF81nx9.Kzs@netcom.com> <7bmesg$3e42@musky.state.wi.us>`

```
tmj (tmj@spam.com) wrote:

: Passport has a "Power Page" feature designed for reading long lists.  
: It sounds like you may have it turned on.

Thank you for the tip!  As it turns out the scroll parameter
(I normally use page but I know most people use cursor) got
changed to '0000' so when I'd hit PF7, PF8, etc. it was 
paging zero lines and appeared to be doing nothing.

I'm currently using a laptop when I telecommute and I've a cat
who likes to get on the warm keyboard when I walk away and she
must have overtyped it!

Regards, Charla
```

## Thread: calling a MF COBOL .dll (ver 3.2.50) from VB5 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### calling a MF COBOL .dll (ver 3.2.50) from VB5

- **From:** gregrogers@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bmkbo$ral$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Does anyone know if VB 5 will call a MF COBOL program.

I want VB to call a MF COBOL program that will open and read files created by
MF COBOL (isam).

I have created a simple program in MF COBOL for a test. It looks like so:

*************************************************************************
      $ set ans85 noosvs mf defaultbyte"00"

        special-names.
                call-convention 3 is WINAPI.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

       DATA DIVISION.

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 str                      pic x(10).

        procedure division WINAPI.
        entry "cobstring" WINAPI using str.

           move "10" to str
           exit program
           stop run.


It is compiled with:
cobol cobstr.obj omf"obj" litlink deffile deffiletype"win";

It is linked with:
link cobstr.obj+libinit+cblwinl,vbstr.dll,,
lcobolw+lcobol+cobw,cobstr.def/noe/nod;
*******************************************************************

The VB module looks like so:

*******************************************************************
Declare Sub Cobstring Lib "vbstr.dll" (PassString As String)
*******************************************************************

I keep getting:

Run-time error '48'
File not found: vbstr.dll


What I am tring to do is put a VB front end on a MF COBOL program. I do not
have NetExpress or Dialog.

Thanx

Greg Rogers (mailto:gregrogers@my-dejanews.com)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: calling a MF COBOL .dll (ver 3.2.50) from VB5

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bn0rt$45n$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7bmkbo$ral$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

gregrogers@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<7bmkbo$ral$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Does anyone know if VB 5 will call a MF COBOL program.
>
>I want VB to call a MF COBOL program that will open and read files created
by
>MF COBOL (isam).
>
[...]
>
>What I am tring to do is put a VB front end on a MF COBOL program. I do not
>have NetExpress or Dialog.

VB 5 cannot interact with MF 3.2.50, directly.

You have four options:

If you want a Visual Basic front-end:

1. Use VB 4 (16-bit) or earlier, or

2. Add a thunking layer between VB 5 and the MF 3.2.50 DLL.
See the Microsoft  Knowledge Base article:
HOWTO Call 16-bit Code from 32-bit Code on Windows 95
Article ID: Q155763

If what you *really* want is a GUI front-end for your COBOL:

3. Look at Flexus spII < www.flexus.com >, or

4. Upgrade or replace your 16-bit MF 3.2.50 with a 32-bit COBOL
that has GUI available.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

#### ↳ Re: calling a MF COBOL .dll (ver 3.2.50) from VB5

- **From:** jets@nbnet.nb.ca (Tony M. Mina)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36df70ae.51134255@allnews.nb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<7bmkbo$ral$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
>
>
>What I am tring to do is put a VB front end on a MF COBOL program. I do not
>have NetExpress or Dialog.

If your purpose in using VB is just to put a GUI look, take a look at
Cobol SP2 from Flexus International.  They are at   www.flexus.com

This is the product we are using heavily in all our GUI development
both on the PC and on the mainframe.

Tony M. Mina

- **From:** sl1476@momail.sbc.com

- **Message-ID:** `<7bmo0f$um6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
SBC Communications, Inc. - Southwestern Bell and its family of companies is
the second largest telecommunications company in the nation and a leader in
the global telecom industry. Through its subsidiaries, SBC provides
innovative communications including local and long-distance telephone,
wireless, paging, Internet access, cable TV, home security, and
telecommunications equipment. We currently have several mainframe programming
positions available in the St. Louis, MO. area. These positions are

- **Date:** 1999-03-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36dde23a.13515834@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com> <7b777c$35h@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Fri, 26 Feb 1999 14:26:09 -0800, "William M. Klein" wrote:

[snippage]

>Out of curiosity, does anyone know a compiler that DOES pay attention to the
>Select/Assign clause for an SD?  I am certain that there are some, so I am
>genuinely interested in hearing the answer.
>

I know most of this thread has been IBM MVS-oriented, but some other
compilers do (did) use some of the SELECT/ASSIGN information for an SD
(other than the file name in the SELECT statement, which I would
think, all must use).

Unisys V-Series COBOL compilers, both 68 and 74, use the ASSIGN
information to determine what media (disk, diskpack, tape) will hold
the sort work file.

I seem to remember that at least one of the compilers (68 or 74) on
the DECSystem 20 used at least some of this information, but I do not
remember which information or for what; it has been quite a while
since I have even seen a DECSystem 20.
```

## Thread: Deleted records — 45 msgs

### Deleted records

- **From:** "John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch>`

```
hello

I am using RM Cobol since 1988 and I am currently using RM Cobol for Windows
6.51
Since 89 RM cobol (Cobol 5.xx and Cobol 6.xx and Cobol 6.51) DID and DOES
NOT RECOVER
space once occupied by deleted records WITH ME automaticly. It seems, that I
did not read the manuals
correctly as Tim mentions IT DOES.
Therefore I would be very thankful for a tip where to find the info.
Example File 10'000 records size 2.732 MB, I delete 3000 records new (old)
size 2.732 MB
therefore
I open the file with the deleted records, open a new physical file (call it
new) and move
record 1 from old file to record 1 of new and so on.
That's the way I do it with dozens of files size more than 10 years and now
I have to
read from TIM, RMCobol did this always automaticly!

Please inform me, how RM cobol did (or does it)! Imagine all the time I
spent for writing these
simple but time consuming copy routines, all for nothing....

I use all four maximum YES levels (physical writes and so on) on my RM
Datafiles in a Network.

Thank you

Although all that work I still use the ISAM File System from RM. After
testing SQL 6.5 from MS (consulting the prices for the SQL SERVER for 50)
users and all the bywork I decided that the flat DATABASE for RM is not ONLY
faster and surer, easier to manage, but also a little bit cheaper!
Now I am going to buy some flowers for my wife, I take the bike, not a
lorry!
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleted records

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch>`

```
On Fri, 5 Mar 1999 23:08:02 +0100, "John Casagrande"
<john@casagrande.ch> wrote:

>hello
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think you misunderstand.

With RM and Realia and Fujitsu - if you have a file of say... 1000
records, that takes up... oh, say...100000 bytes. If you delete one
record, the file still takes up 100000 bytes.  BUT-- if you THEN
insert a record, it will STILL take up 100000 bytes - the space that
was used by the deleted record is recovered and used again.


To contrast, the same file with MicroFocus COBOL:  1000 records,
100000 bytes, delete a record - file size 100000 bytes - insert a
record file is 1000100 bytes - the space used by the deleted record is
never reclaimed or reused.

Your reorganization was not a waste.  If records are deleted and
others not inserted to take their place, you are recovering lost
space.  Also, it can help speed performance to reorganize the data
file.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

- **From:** "John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bsc8c$lj9$1@news.imp.ch>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Hello Thane

thank you for your information. I was surprised to hear that MF Cobol does
not reuse the space once
used by a deleted record. I did understand before the mechanism RM uses. I
just do not understand
why there is no utility from the producer of the Cobol to reorganise a file.
Before 1988 I used to work with Burroughs Cobol and I remember they had a
utility called "SQUASH"
to "delete" deleted records from a file. In these times space on hard disks
was more expensive then today and maybe therefore people did think about how
to solve space.
I imagine it would be possible to make a utility to "reorganise" such blown
up files and help us programers to avoid such routine work. In an indexed
file there is in minimum the information about the keys.

Or is there not enough information in the file to reconstruct the
content of the file?
Have a nice time
john@casagrande.ch, lucerne switzerla

with some changes
and the source became 9000 lines long, the whole thing took about 3 minutes.
I first thought, something
went wrong. Then my wife became sad. What do you do in 3 minutes?
Today the same source has exploded to 20000 lines and the compiling goes 3
seconds on a Pentium 400.

about 500 COBOL programs in our system, and I remember setting up compile
jobs which would run all day and all night. (This was before I programmed
myself; I recall that compiling seemed "awfully fast" when it only took ten
minutes for a 500-line program).

Also, I just remembered: "SQ" compressed the disk (i.e., defragment); it was
"FS" which performed the (F)ile (S)quash.

and once I will write a book

machine. And when I saw that the programer just started learning programing
I thought I better start too! And after 3 months I did not need the
programer anymore (but a lot more time of the days)!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bu44s$678$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <7bsc8c$lj9$1@news.imp.ch> <36e1e349.335628764@news1.ibm.net> <7bsros$gpl@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7bsros$gpl@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>...
[...]
>  Speaking of Micro Focus "REBUILD" - doesn't that recover the space that
>doesn't get used by added new records?  I guess I always thought of this as
>the way to "go" in an MF environment.
>
REBUILD is good choice for that purpose but it is not necessarily
the best choice.

The system I maintain has a history file that is purged every year.
I could delete the archived records from the indexed file, then run
REBUILD to recover the space. Instead, I split the data into a
sequential archive and a new indexed file. When finished, I delete
the old file and rename the new. Should the purge process fail, the
original indexed file is unchanged and the process can be rerun
without restoring files.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e31cab.415866784@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <7bsc8c$lj9$1@news.imp.ch> <36e1e349.335628764@news1.ibm.net> <7bsros$gpl@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <7bu44s$678$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
On Sun, 7 Mar 1999 09:55:32 -0500, "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:


>The system I maintain has a history file that is purged every year.
>I could delete the archived records from the indexed file, then run
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>without restoring files.

It may not be obvious - but running REBUILD without specifying  a
SEPARATE output file does not recover any space.

ie:  REBUILD INDEXED.FIL 

recreates the index portion, which is a separate file.

REBUILD INDEXED.FIL,INDEXED.NEW - creates a new file, recovering
space.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bvi87$9pj$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <7bsc8c$lj9$1@news.imp.ch> <36e1e349.335628764@news1.ibm.net> <7bsros$gpl@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <7bu44s$678$1@server.cntfl.com> <36e31cab.415866784@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36e31cab.415866784@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Sun, 7 Mar 1999 09:55:32 -0500, "Rick Smith"
><ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>SEPARATE output file does not recover any space.

Well, yes. I was thinking in terms of using REBUILD to reorganize
the file, which does recover the space.

>ie:  REBUILD INDEXED.FIL
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>space.

Which will fail due to a conflict over INDEXED.IDX ! The name of
the indexed file for both INDEXED.FIL and INDEXED.NEW.

REBUILD OLD.DAT, NEW.DAT will work.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

utility called "SQUASH"
to "delete" deleted records from a file. In these times space on hard disks
was more expensive then today and maybe therefore people did think about how
to solve space.
I imagine it would be possible to make a utility to "reorganise" such blown
up files and help us programers to avoid such routine work. In an indexed
file there is in minimum the information about the keys.

Or is there not enough information in the file to reconstruct the
content of the file?
Have a nice time
john@casagrande.ch, lucerne switzerla
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bvh5e$85t$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <7bu451$678$2@server.cntfl.com> <36e31d19.415976813@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36e31d19.415976813@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Sun, 7 Mar 1999 09:56:33 -0500, "Rick Smith"
><ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Your statement appears to be incorrect with respect to certain
Micro Focus products that have been produced since 1985.

The test program, below, was run using a Level II product. The
program creates an indexed file of 1000 records of 100 bytes
each. The program then deletes the first 500 records then adds
500 more records. I animated the program by using Perform Step
in the initial paragraph. After each PERFORM statement, I checked
the size of the data file. In each case, it was 102,000 bytes. (The
same program using MF WB 3.2 had a file size of 104,128 bytes
after each step.)

This is not the earliest MF product I have used. It is the earliest
(oldest) one that I have available. I cannot dispute whether older
Level II products reused deleted records; but, I can confirm that
this product does. I can also confirm that later products through
WB 3.2 also reuse deleted records. This means that since 1985
Micro Focus has had products that reuse deleted records.

The term "logically delete" is part of the COBOL standard and
appears in all language reference manuals. I searched through
older MF manuals and could find no information about reusing
deleted records. It is, as if, "it goes without saying." I didn't check
them all since I have about 40 or 50 MF manuals. The
MF WB 3.2 System Reference Manual has this comment.

"(Free space consists of the spaces in the data file occupied by
  deleted records. Information about these areas is normally
  retained, and the areas reused for new records as appropriate.)"

Although this comment is in the section on Rebuild, it is the
only comment that I found that actually says that space from
deleted records will be reused. The "as appropriate" could apply
to variable length records; that is, it cannot reuse space that is
too small to hold the record.

-------- Test program, ANSI 74 for MF Professional Cobol 1.1
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. IDX-TEST.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT IDX-FILE
                ASSIGN TO "IDX-FILE.DAT"
                ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
                RECORD KEY IS IDX-KEY
                FILE STATUS IS IDX-STAT.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  IDX-FILE.
       01  IDX-RECORD.
            05  IDX-KEY    PIC 9(6).
            05  IDX-DATA    PIC X(94).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  IDX-STAT    PIC XX.
       01  RECORD-SEQUENCE     PIC 9(6).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            OPEN OUTPUT IDX-FILE
            CLOSE IDX-FILE
            PERFORM CREATE-INITIAL-RECORDS
            PERFORM DELETE-SOME-RECORDS
            PERFORM ADD-MORE-RECORDS
            STOP RUN.

       CREATE-INITIAL-RECORDS.
            OPEN I-O IDX-FILE.
            PERFORM ADD-A-RECORD
                VARYING RECORD-SEQUENCE FROM 1 BY 1
                UNTIL RECORD-SEQUENCE > 1000.
            CLOSE IDX-FILE.

       DELETE-SOME-RECORDS.
            OPEN I-O IDX-FILE.
            PERFORM DELETE-A-RECORD
                VARYING RECORD-SEQUENCE FROM 1 BY 1
                UNTIL RECORD-SEQUENCE > 500.
            CLOSE IDX-FILE.

       ADD-MORE-RECORDS.
            OPEN I-O IDX-FILE.
            PERFORM ADD-A-RECORD
                VARYING RECORD-SEQUENCE FROM 1001 BY 1
                UNTIL RECORD-SEQUENCE > 1500.
            CLOSE IDX-FILE.

       ADD-A-RECORD.
            MOVE RECORD-SEQUENCE TO IDX-KEY.
            MOVE SPACES TO IDX-DATA.
            WRITE IDX-RECORD
              INVALID KEY
                NEXT SENTENCE.

       DELETE-A-RECORD.
            MOVE RECORD-SEQUENCE TO IDX-KEY.
            READ IDX-FILE
              INVALID KEY
                NEXT SENTENCE.
            DELETE IDX-FILE
              INVALID KEY
                NEXT SENTENCE.
--------
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

"Deleted records are certainly reused in MF indexed files.  They may not be
reused immediately, but they are added to a free space list and made
available to future updates."

I think this corresponds with Rick's empirical evidence.

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c19hi$905@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>...

>
>I'm a curious mainframe guy.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>periodic defrag of the file?

When I first moved from IBM mainframe environments to PC COBOLs it came has
a HORRIBLE shock to me that the "operating system" did NOT have any type of
indexed files.  Each COBOL product on Workstations (OS/2, Windows, or UNIX)
needs to EITHER "invent their own" or use 3rd party products (such as
C-isam).  For an IBM mainframe, this is a "real eye opener" - especially if
you are interested in "portability".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1a9o$s7a$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Indexed files.  The reason that it is that the Cobol needs a facility
to rebuild an Isam file is because the file format is proprietary, and
the structure is part of the Cobol package.  I would expect that VSAM
probably has some sort of repair facility as well ... though since VSAM
is a one-vendor type of thing, whether it is part of Cobol or part of the
OS is just semantics.

Defrag is completely different.  The rebuild facility that is being
discussed deals with logical structure within the file only.  The physical
location of the file on the drive is solely within the OS, and a standard
defrag works on Cobol files.


Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>> With RM and Realia and Fujitsu - if you have a file of say... 1000
>> records, that takes up... oh, say...100000 bytes. If you delete one
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>periodic defrag of the file?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Indexed files.  The reason that it is that the Cobol needs a facility
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> defrag works on Cobol files.

I wasn't thinking of a fragmented disk with different parts of a file in
different locations, I was thinking of a file with different sized null
records in it.  You don't care too much if the records are the same size
- when you add a record it will always fit where a deleted one was
removed (it may be at an inconvenient spot, but that's a different
problem).  But if you have variable length records, you could have a 50
byte hole and a 100 byte record.   Of course this can be fixed by
whatever maintenance is being done to move records around to make the
key search more efficient - maybe an unload-reload.

> Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>...
> >> With RM and Realia and Fujitsu - if you have a file of say... 1000
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> >periodic defrag of the file?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BMWE2.943$Tw3.148552@client.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>...
[snip]
>I'm a curious mainframe guy.
>What kind of files are you talking about, indexed, relative?

Indexed, in this discussion, relative having fallen into 'relative' disuse.

>Why is it COBOL's function to organize file reclamation instead of the
>OS's?

Simply because no OS (including MVS and VMS), save a very distinct few (two
of which I helped write), provides indexed IO at its very core.  The indexed
IO methods  of MVS and VMS are so entrenched that it seems as if their
indexed methods are part pf the OS.

So, for those nonproprietary platforms (Windows (r), UNIX (r)) where there
has been no single dominant indexed method (or, more to the point, COBOL
implementation) available, it falls to the vendors of COBOL systems to
provide some very essential services if COBOL is to be usable.  Exact
decimal arithmetic, for example, is not supported by the hardware or OS;
decimal arithmetic support must be supplied by the COBOL vendor.

And so it is with indexed, relative, AND COBOL STANDARD CONFORMING
sequential IO.

As far as I am aware, all the major COBOL vendors on these nonproprietary
platforms reclaim and reuse the physical space claimed by records that have
been logically deleted from their indexed file system implementations.


>What happens when you have variable length records?

Since most (all?) nonproprietary COBOL vendors provide on-the-fly
compression of the data in indexed records, virtually all data is stored in
structures that are well aware of variable length records.  The issue is not
variable length records, but intelligent reuse of space relinquished by
deleted records.

>Do you need to do a
>periodic defrag of the file?

Won't hurt.  Again, most (all?) nonproprietary COBOL vendors provide
rebuild/recovery utilities that help to reoptimize a file.

The original subject of this thread, RM/COBOL, has such a utility that will
shuffle much of the unused space to the 'physical' end-of-file and then
relinquish the unused space to the OS (assuming that the OS has a means of
taking back part of a file).

I hope that this has been helpful.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```
