###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1p3g$7m4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com> <BMWE2.943$Tw3.148552@client.news.psi.net> <36E439BE.AAB448F7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message

>I think I would skip indexed files altogether on most PC environments,
>opting instead to some type of DBMS - just for universality.  Java seems
>to do this.

I think that if I were writing code for general bussiness applcations, in
effect inhouse code, I might do the same.  However, I do not.  I am writing
self contained, standalone systems. In that type of application (keep in
mind Cobol is used on PC's mainly by VARS), there is far less neccessity to
be able to "code on the fly" in several languages.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleted records

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e4a048.514823750@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com> <BMWE2.943$Tw3.148552@client.news.psi.net> <36E439BE.AAB448F7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Mon, 08 Mar 1999 13:57:34 -0700, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:


>It has, thanks.   I wonder why relative files aren't used much in PC
>environments.  It does have several advantages, especially where you
>don't already have a well developed indexed system.
>

I use relative files for some things - but interestingly, different
platforms offer different support.  For example:  With Realia I can
access a sequential file using relative access *IF* I define it as a
bytestream file.

With MicroFocus I can access a sequential file (not line sequential)
as if it were a relative file, simply by saying it is in the select.

With Realia, and NON bytestream files, there are added bytes in each
record to allow Realia to identify the relative key. The same is true
of Fujitsu.  

Most of the advantages of a relative file can be acquired by useing an
indexed file with a record number for the key - and I think this is
easier for most programmers to understand.  Also, some implenentations
of relative access are FAST while others are dismally slow.  (ie RSDS
file access with VSAM).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Usefullnes of Relative Files (was: Re: Deleted records)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c3dr7$nvf$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com> <BMWE2.943$Tw3.148552@client.news.psi.net> <36E439BE.AAB448F7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E439BE.AAB448F7@NOSPAMhome.com>...
[...]
>> Howard Brazee wrote in message <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>> >I'm a curious mainframe guy.
[...]
>[... ]  I wonder why relative files aren't used much in PC
>environments.  It does have several advantages, especially where you
>don't already have a well developed indexed system.

Firstly, I think indexed file management in COBOLs on PCs is
well-developed.

Secondly, it seems to me, the primary advantage of relative files
is the fast direct access for data that cannot be held in memory.
With virtual memory and disk caching there is little use for relative
files. (I do use a relative file to off-load a large table to disk; but the
application is still restricted to 640K. I off-loaded three other tables
as an indexed file since the lookup was random rather than relative.
Furthermore, these files tend to reside in disk cache.)

Any other comments?
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Usefullnes of Relative Files (was: Re: Deleted records)

```
>Secondly, it seems to me, the primary advantage of relative files
>is the fast direct access for data that cannot be held in memory.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

I am starting to wonder about using files at all.  I am converting a Cobol
system that ran for ten years with a 40 meg disk drive to run on a 128
megabyte machine.  A customer name address file of 25000 records is probably
in the 6-7 megabyte range.  Perhaps just loading the whole thing into a
table on startup is the way to do it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Usefullnes of Relative Files (was: Re: Deleted records)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-V1Pmvdc2EVgD@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com> <BMWE2.943$Tw3.148552@client.news.psi.net> <36E439BE.AAB448F7@NOSPAMhome.com> <7c3dr7$nvf$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
On Tue, 9 Mar 1999 15:13:05, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> 
wrote:

>> Secondly, it seems to me, the primary advantage of relative files
> is the fast direct access for data that cannot be held in memory.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I've done this two ways, when using compilers restricted to 64K 
tables.

First - only offload to a relative file when the table size is 
exceeded.

Second - make 64K "images" of the table, so it's in chunks, and then 
decide which chunk to search based on it's first and last entries.  
The files are not relative - they are "flat" and placed enmass in the 
table area of working storage when required.
```

```
I ask this every few months (and this comment certainly brings it to mind),

Are there any IBM mainframe COBOL programs out there that actually USE "Data
in Virtual"?  (For non-IBM mainframe people, this is a system that lets you
store "large chunks of data" in a file system - that can then be read -
sort-of - into your program as a table - or whatever. This is a cruddy
explanation of what it is, but then again, I don't know many COBOL
programmers who actually use it.)
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36e671a8.633977103@news1.ibm.net>...
  <snip>
>>
>>Are there any IBM mainframe COBOL programs out there that actually USE
"Data
>>in Virtual"?  (For non-IBM mainframe people, this is a system that lets
you
>>store "large chunks of data" in a file system - that can then be read -
>>sort-of - into your program as a table - or whatever. This is a cruddy
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>product.  There is/was such a beast?

Thane,
  That depends on what you mean by "manually".  There is a documented CALL
interface for "data in virtual".  There is no native COBOL syntax for this -
but the CALL interface is fairly reasonable for IBM COBOL mainframe
programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Usefulness of Relative Files (was: Re: Deleted records)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** SimonLElliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E6BD73.EEBA4096@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<7bpkes$pte$1@news.imp.ch> <36e09983.251193672@news1.ibm.net> <36E4031C.9250754@NOSPAMhome.com> <BMWE2.943$Tw3.148552@client.news.psi.net> <36E439BE.AAB448F7@NOSPAMhome.com> <7c3dr7$nvf$1@server.cntfl.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-V1Pmvdc2EVgD@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7c4545$1vu@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```

With my CICS Sysprog hat on.

It's possible that as an applications programmer in an IBM mainframe environment
you may be using (if I understand the term correctly) Data in Virtual without
knowing it.

MVS and CICS quite merrily buffer data in storage whenever possible (one of my
jobs is to tune CICS file buffering). Add to that, controller and disk cache,
solid state disks, as well as the use of 'Dataspaces' for a really big files and
there's a good chance that reading a record doesn't necessarily result in a
'traditional'  physical I/O (head movement over spinning disk or a few more tape
revolutions) at all.

Referring to the subject of PCs and indexed records. I would guess that one
reason for the poor implementation would be that it's not required (or
demanded). A box capable of holding a 16M file in storage is going to be pretty
fast at finding a record, keyed access or not. Presumably this means that all
these wonderful PC database systems are using 'flat' files - even DB2?
```

```
John Casagrande wrote in message <7c3v4u$7ra$1@news.imp.ch>...
[snip]
>I am using RM Cobol for Windows 6.51 under Windows 98.
>Is there such an utility in this RM version and what is the NAME of the
>Utility?

It was introduced in v6.6.  A new recovery utility (described in Appendix G
of the User Guide) has made the previous recovery utilities obsolete.  (But,
in the tradition of COBOL, we continue to ship the obsolete modules for
compatibility.)

>
>I am quite new in Newgroups and I am very INSPIRED  of the way you guys and
>girls handle such a simple question "deleted records"!
>Compliments to all of you!


I extend thanks from all of us at Liant!

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
```

## Thread: What does this statement mean. — 5 msgs

### What does this statement mean.

- **From:** thomas@vdb.de
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bosf2$p0e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hello

Did anyone knows what this statment means.

pic f 999 comp -3

Thanks

Thomas

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: What does this statement mean.

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36DFFD17.772DA25@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7bosf2$p0e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
thomas@vdb.de wrote:

> Did anyone knows what this statment means.
>
> pic f 999 comp -3

The short answer is "NO" <g>, but you might not have copied the statement
correctly.
  f pic 999 comp-3     would have defined a three digit numeric field
contained in 2 bytes
                                named "f".  This is only part of a
statement.
    pic s999 comp-3    is part of a statement defining a three digit SIGNED
field contained
                                in two bytes.  The level number, field
name, and any value clause
                                were not shown.  HTH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What does this statement mean.

- **From:** steve casey <stevencasey@ustrustboston.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E052D2.5C503DA@ustrustboston.com>`
- **References:** `<7bosf2$p0e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36DFFD17.772DA25@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
It probably can be interpreted as

05(or whatever the level number) filler pic x(02).

> thomas@vdb.de wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rainy Days OR Mondays Always Get Me Down.
```

#### ↳ Re: What does this statement mean.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7bpb1t$3ua@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
thomas@vdb.de wrote in message <7bosf2$p0e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Hello
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I actually think I *might* know what this means (although the lower-case
letters AND a reasonability check both make it pretty unlikely).  With the
OLD IBM mainframe compilers (documented in ANS COBOL Vn - and still
supported but not documented with OS/VS COBOL) there was native language
support for the "olde" British monetary system (pre-decimal) with pounds,
shillings, and pence.  Most often you would see this with USAGE DISPLAY-ST,
but I think (and this is really racking my brain) you could use it with
other numeric usages.  As I recall (and again this is pretty foggy), the
PICTURE symbols did include an "f" (as well as "d" and "l" - maybe?) and
even though the rest of your source code had to be upper case, I do remember
that "D" and "d" represented different values/uses.  Again, this is all
QUITE vague and probably NOT what you are running into - but it should be an
interesting diversion if nothing else.
```

#### ↳ Re: What does this statement mean.

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E435B4.9FDEA41E@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7bosf2$p0e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I suppose you put the space between the "f" and the "999" by accident as
you did betweren "comp" and "-3".
Than I read: ... pic F999 comp-3

This could be part of a complete field definition like:

01  variable picture is f999, usage is computational-3.

I first thought about an alternative edit character F i.s.o $, which is
possible by stating CURRENCY SIGN IS "F" in the special-names paragraph.
but that conflicts with the usage. May be you should post more code.

The COBOL Frog

thomas@vdb.de wrote:

> Hello
>
```

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990306122540.26584.00004134@ng152.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36E061C4.FE1708DB@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Dale,

Check out our website, www.trainersfriend.com, and select Topics, then COBOL;
this page has a good narrative discussion of the courses we offer for COBOL. Of
particular interest in your case would be the third course in the list, what we
call Beyond COBOL II. This two-day course is an extensive coverage of features
introduced with various COBOL compilers: COBOL II, COBOL/370, COBOL for MVS &
VM (and COBOL for VSE), COBOL for OS/390 & VM, along with introductions to LE
and Millennium Language Extensions (MLE).

The course is designed to be taught in OS/390 and VSE shops, just skipping
irrelevant topics for the site we are teaching at. IBM purchased this course
two years ago and is teaching it all over the world. 

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol{csp

- **From:** "Jon Santarelli" <jsant@interacess.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wfnagvagrenprffpbz.f85h3y0.pminews@nntp.interaccess.com>`
- **References:** `<7bp446$l2h$1@news.cybercity.dk>`

```
Docdwarf, in a later post did get it correct.  CSP stands for Cross
Systems Product.  It is an IBM SAA specific language it runs on all of
their platforms from PC-DOS to MVS and everything in the middle.

I'm going to guess that you *DO* know what COBOL is and you *do not*
know what CSP is.

CSP is a rather simple language to learn, provided you know another
programming language.  I never heard of it until I took a contract with
a company that used it.  Within a week or so, I was productive in it.

It has COBOL-like syntax, however with some C overtones, as most of it
has been written in C (from what I have been told).  Here in the USA it
did not do to well but apparently in Europe it is much more popular.

If you can do a DejaNews search, do one for this newsgroup as I
answered this question just a few weeks ago.

Jon Santarelli
jsant at inter access dot com 
(remove the blanks and substitue as necessary to reply)

On Fri, 5 Mar 1999 18:26:10 +0100, Jette Dahl Bauer wrote:

>Does anyone know the difference between Cobol and CSP?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: MF: S9(n) representation — 2 msgs

### MF: S9(n) representation

- **From:** Stephan Eichenlaub <stephan@uni-koblenz.de>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E09095.425CB692@uni-koblenz.de>`

```
Hi out there,

I'm trying to convert data from the S9(n) respectively S9(n)V99 format.
The data is lying on an old HP-UX 3000 machine, the database TurboImage
and the program written with MicroFocus Cobol.

I found out so much:
- It is an ASCII format, one byte per decimal digit. '1' stands for 1 -
and so on.
- The number of decimals before and behind the dot is merely
interpretion by
  code, it is not contained in the data itself.
- The last byte represents both a number and the sign.

Question: How does that last byte work?

Thanks, Stephan
```

#### ↳ Re: MF: S9(n) representation

```
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm not sure about the ASCII codes but generally speaking the high order half
byte is the number 0 - 9 and the low order half byte is the sign F unsigned or
D negative  C positive, at least that's how it works in EBCDIC
```

## Thread: "Nice to have" Enhancement in draft/next Standard (was Exit program vs. Goback — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: "Nice to have" Enhancement in draft/next Standard (was Exit program vs. Goback

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bqueg$84@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<japC2.8497$jf2.3109245@hme2.newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <36dae4be.29987780@news.enterprise.net> <36DAEC47.AEC0DEDB@NOSPAMhome.com> <36daff7d.6644584@news3.ibm.net> <36db151e.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <36dbc94f.88500967@news.enterprise.net> <7bh9dr$aks@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <36DEB2CF.A11C1B72@ms.com> <7bn4v4$ehd@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36E04E56.31E459C6@ms.com> <7bpshc$ap5@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <36e09afa.251569626@news1.ibm.net> <7bqeta$lsl@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I checked the rules and my last example is NOT valid.  A "good" (valid)
example of the use would be:

Evaluate A
    When > B
        Perform X
    When < C
        Perform Y
    When Positive
        Perform Z
    When Other
        Display "Here we go again"
End-Evaluate

This is valid because the Selection OBJECT begins with a relational operator
or starts a class test - but the Selection SUBJECT is still an identifier.
(My original error was having the "dangling" relational operator - which I
think at one time was allowed but not by the current draft.)
```

<a id="t-2243644f44"></a>
## Thread: BASIS, INSERT, DELETE — 2 msgs

### BASIS, INSERT, DELETE

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e00f17.2128881@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Hello,

does (or has) anybody out of this distinguished group ever used the BASIS, INSERT, and
DELET compiler directing statements?  And if yes, why not <g>


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: BASIS, INSERT, DELETE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bs2t1$87j@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36e00f17.2128881@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote in message <36e00f17.2128881@news3.ibm.net>...
>Hello,
>
>does (or has) anybody out of this distinguished group ever used the BASIS,
INSERT, and
>DELET compiler directing statements?  And if yes, why not <g>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>(BSP GmbH)

For those who don't understand this question, this refers to an IBM
extension that is quite old - but still supported in the latest IBM S/390
compilers.  It is similar to Panvalet and Librarian (should I say Endeavor)
library management system.  It allows you to do "virtual" updates to your
COBOL source code during a compile.

In answer to the question, both when I worked with the vendor of Xpediter
and when I worked for Micro Focus, we provided "support" for this IBM
feature - however, I never found any customers who actually used it.  I
think the basic reason was that the 3rd party products (Librarian and
Panvalet in their PRE-CA days) were considered superior and provided greater
functionality. I do remember there being 1 or 2 IBM APARs against this, so
someone must have been using it - but I sure question any common usage.
```

## Thread: Passing variables to called pgrms — 2 msgs

### Passing variables to called pgrms

- **From:** "John Dwyer" <johndwyer@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fido.ger.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gzjE2.4628$px5.6164@news.rdc1.ct.home.com>`

```
None of my reference materials discuss in depth the procedure to pass a
variable to a called program.
Can someone suggest a web reference, or briefly explain it

Also, I'm having problems with multiple indexed files in my programs.  I
can't find any references for this either.

thanx
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing variables to called pgrms

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fido.ger.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bshpi$e9o$2@news-2.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<gzjE2.4628$px5.6164@news.rdc1.ct.home.com>`

```
John Dwyer wrote in message ...
>None of my reference materials discuss in depth the procedure to pass a
>variable to a called program. Can someone suggest a web reference, or
briefly explain it.

Over-simplified:

Call-ING program:

    CALL "MYPROG" USING  Var-1, VAR-2, VAR-3.

Call-ED program

LINKAGE SECTION.
   01 VAR-1    PIC whatever.
   01 VAR-2    PIC whatever.
   01 VAR-3    PIC whatever.


PROCEDURE DIVISION USING VAR-1, VAR-2, VAR-3.


The CALL-ed program may either be a completely separate program, or can be
an embedded program; if separately compiled, make sure the CALL-ed
object/load module is available to the linker. (When embedded, that usually
takes care of itself.)

>Also, I'm having problems with multiple indexed files in my programs.  I
>can't find any references for this either.

Huh? What do you mean by "multiple indexed?"  If you mean indexed files with
multiple keys, check the ALTERNATE KEY phrase of the SELECT (these may vary
by compiler).

Note: if the alternate keys are non-unique, you can get some funky file
statii depending on the specific compiler in use.

MCM
```

<a id="t-968e72b8ed"></a>
## Thread: RMCOBOL and Copy Replace — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-968e72b8ed)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RMCOBOL and Copy Replace

- **From:** boblorenze@aol.com (BobLorenze)
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990307104907.15819.00005337@ng141.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7av951$qth@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Many compilers are very case senstive. Try BY instead of by. Also, if you are
doing my favorite trick of using the same COPY statement for both an input and
output file (for example), try puting the REPLACING on the first occurance of
the COPY, not the second (this has tripped up some older compilers I've
used....
i.e. try
COPY INPUTFD REPLACING ==INFILE== BY ==OUTFILE==.
COPY INPUTFD.
instead of
COPY INPUTFD.
COPY INPUTFD REPLACING....
```

#### ↳ Re: RMCOBOL and Copy Replace

- **From:** helpless@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7clorv$4mv$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36d31a8c.20857062@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```

  davidbm@ix.netcom.com (David) wrote:
> I am running RMCOBOL v. 6.1 and I am attempting to use the  COPY
> REPLACING option.  The command I use is:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

this is what we use
           COPY "PRCTLFIL.PRF" REPLACING
                ==REDEFINES MCBA-CONTROL-RECORD.== BY ==.==.

the only difference is you show replacing and by in small
all ours does is removes the redefine.
>
```

```
I'm not exactly sure of the full syntax as I have just started with tables
(called so in Cobol). I hope this will help you somewhat:

Variables
Table occurs 10 times : Num [4]   {So there are 10 fields in the array which
can hold 4 numeric characters each)
T-Sub : Num [2]

Now, every field in the array is written as Table(T-Sub).

"Arlene B. Call" wrote:

> Can anyone give me an example of a simply array using Cobol. I'm trying to
> learn this language and it would help to see an example,
> thanks
> abcall@sisna.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: array

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E27815.E2EFA79D@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36cedcf0.0@news.sisna.com> <36E26CD3.A95F81B6@hotmail.com>`

```
This example demonstrates assign values to a table by using a
REDEFINES.  ("FILLER" is optional and has been left out.)

01  ws-end-of-month-list.
     02          PIC X(24)  VALUE '312831303130313130313031'.
01              REDEFINES ws-end-of-month-list.
     02          OCCURS 12 TIMES.
         03 ws-eom-dd            PIC XX.

******************************************************************

This is a example of using a SEARCH verb against a one-dimensional
table. Note that the table elements have values assigned when the
program starts to execute, and that you will need to write code to load
data into the table

 01  Suspense-table.
     02  suspense-element           OCCURS 48 TIMES
                                                INDEXED  susp-index.
         03 suspense-code            PIC X        VALUE HIGH-VALUES.
         03  suspense-key             PIC X(27) VALUE SPACES.
..
..
..

SET susp-index TO 1.
SEARCH suspense-element
        AT END
            MOVE ZEROS TO file-key
        WHEN vjs-index NOT < ws-limit
             MOVE ZEROS TO file-key
         WHEN suspense-code  (susp-index) = some-field
             MOVE suspense-key (vjs-index) TO file-key.

Atta wrote:

> I'm not exactly sure of the full syntax as I have just started with
> tables (called so in Cobol). I hope this will help you somewhat:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-82834abc83"></a>
## Thread: from cobol to c — 1 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-82834abc83)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: from cobol to c

- **From:** boblorenze@aol.com (BobLorenze)
- **Date:** 1999-03-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990307105843.15819.00005345@ng141.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990227034238.17155.00000407@ngol07.aol.com>`

```
Been there, done that.... I was a 10 Year COBOL veteran who bought into a C
language software house. Now I switch back and forth daily (sometimes hourly
when the fires come).
 
Anyway, I'd also suggest a good data structures book. C stores things and
represents data in pretty much very different manners than COBOL does. And
knowing how the machine is handling your data and what it is going to do next
is important. A base knowledge of non-COBOL data structures and representation
will make your C experience a lot nicer.
 
Don't worry about C++. Just another layer of stuff on top of C. C++ is really
cute, and makes life a lot nicer. By don't worry, I don't mean forget about it,
C++ is here to stay and a career programer shoud not ignore it. But learn C at
a basic level first, then branch out.
 
And don't worry about "object oriented programming" - an 01 level (record, for
example) is an "object" when you read past the fluff. You've been using objects
since you first read a file.... just have to adjust your thinking....
 
And remember, these machines only count to 1. So you are still in charge
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-1 conversion

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990309083436.12463.00000035@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7c1sto$ds5$2@news-1.news.gte.net>`

```
comp-1, or comp, is useage BINARY and is not a floating point storage area
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-1 conversion

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c38fp$8rt@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<7c1sto$ds5$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <19990309083436.12463.00000035@ng-fi1.aol.com>`

```
In IBM mainframe land, there are a number of ways that data can be stored:

DISPLAY -- the default, character mode in EBCDIC
COMP -- binary, length is 2/4/8 bytes depending on number of significant
digits
COMP-1 -- full-word length (4 bytes) floating point
COMP-2 -- double-word length (8 bytes) floating point
COMP-3 -- "packed decimal" with 1/2 byte (a nibble) per digit and a trailing
sign in the final nibble

In 30+ years of programming, I have had the opportunity to use COMP-1/COMP-2
only a few times, although the last one was only two years ago as (believe
it or not) part of our Y2K project.  Nonetheless, it is sometimes a valuable
tool in selected instances.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

Twymer wrote in message <19990309083436.12463.00000035@ng-fi1.aol.com>...
>comp-1, or comp, is useage BINARY and is not a floating point storage area
```

```
If COMP-1 and COMP-2 are IEEE singles and doubles, using BASIC:

Numericval! = CVS(four-byte-comp-1-string)
NumericVal# = CVD(eight-byte-comp-2-string)

For complete description, I know the format is public domain; you might
inquire at IEEE;
OR;
if you have a friend who uses PowerBASIC/DOS, there are pictures of the bit
patterns, text and and everything in the manual.

MCM
Special Contributor, Basically Speaking magazine
```

```
Krister H�ger�s wrote in message <36eadd6b.0@d2o94.telia.com>...
>Well, the problem is easily solved with the CVS / CVD functions in QB, but
>my problem is that i want to convert the strings to numbers without having
>those functions, with a program that runs throug the bytes and calculates a
>value.
>
Why not write the functions? (You had mentioned Basic, C, Pacal,
and XBase earlier.)

The equivalent C code should be:
-----------
#include <string.h>

float cvs ( void * s )
{
    float f;
    memcpy ( s, &f, 4 );
    return (f);
}

double cvd ( void *s )
{
    double d;
    memcpy ( s, &d, 8 );
    return (d);
}
-----------
These functions are useful for aligning data on systems that do not
automatically allign data. (Most / All ?) COBOL implementations
will move unaligned data if required.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

## Thread: Question on NetExpress running in NT — 3 msgs

### Question on NetExpress running in NT

- **From:** "Juan Rodriguez" <juanr@wilwel.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1tBF2.15$FF.1117@news.ntr.net>`

```
We are in the initial stage of converting our Cobol product to NetExpress.
The programs and data will reside in an NT Server and the client
workstations will be running Win95/98.  We are unsure as to whether to use
the NetExpress Indexed Files or a DBMS such as SQL Server as the Data
Warehouse.

If anyone has had any experience with NetExpress addressing indexed files
residing on an NT Server or NetExpress addressing a SQL DataBase residing on
an NT Server, we would really appreciate your comments.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on NetExpress running in NT

- **From:** jack_b@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cbh2f$kc1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<1tBF2.15$FF.1117@news.ntr.net>`

```
In article <1tBF2.15$FF.1117@news.ntr.net>,
  "Juan Rodriguez" <juanr@wilwel.com> wrote:
> We are in the initial stage of converting our Cobol product to NetExpress.
> The programs and data will reside in an NT Server and the client
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I developed an application for a client in 1997 which does exactly what you
describe using MF indexed files and no DBMS.  It is a Direct Marketing
application that maintains several million member and purchase records as well
as many associated files necessary for running a business.

Our results have been excellent.  Of course, system design related to the
indexes is all important for a fast and user friendly outcome.

Keep in mind the 1GB file-size limitation.  I have not had to deal with that
and cannot advise you of the complications of the MF fix.

A good UPS will also keep you out of trouble.  Also the MF rebuild program is
useful.  Good luck.





-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
Juan,

Both options are possible. We use SQL Server, with some success. NetExpress 2.0
has a nasty ODBC-bug when using null-indicators on updates and inserts, I
recommend v3.0.

Good luck.

The COBOL Frog
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e8e915@news1.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36e3bd3b.0@207.227.203.4>`

```
Hey!  I found this in the Netexpress on-line help file, under "Mixed
language programming" topic...  Hope it helps!

COBOL programs can call and be called by programs written in other
languages. However, some languages handle calls differently from COBOL. For
example, some language pass parameters in the opposite order from that
expected by COBOL programs. To enable COBOL and non-COBOL programs to call
each other, you can adjust the calling mechanisms that the COBOL program
uses or that it expects to be used.
To specify the calling mechanisms you require, you first construct the call
convention number that produces the required mechanisms. The call convention
number is a 16-bit number defined as follows:

Bit Meaning

0 0 Process parameters from right-to-left
1 Process parameters from left-to-right
1 0 Parameters removed from stack by calling program
1 Parameters removed from stack by called program
2 0 RETURN-CODE is updated on exit
1  RETURN-CODE is not updated on exit

Any RETURNING phrase is not affected by this bit
3 0 Normal linking behavior
1 Call is resolved at link time
4 Reserved (always 0)
5 Reserved (always 0)
6 0 The call does not conform to the Windows stdcall calling convention
1 The call conforms to the Windows stdcall calling convention

This has no effect on dynamic calls, but alters the call name on
static-linked calls
7 Reserved (always 0)
8 0 The parameter-count of individual entry-points is checked
1 The parameter-count of individual entry-points is not checked
9 0 The case of the program or call-name is disregarded
1 The case of the program or call-name is significant
 This call-convention is an instruction to the generator on how to treat the
text of a quoted program name or call-name when litlinking. If this bit is 0
the case of the program or call-name might still be significant depending on
the CASE generator directive.

10-15 Reserved (always 0)

When you have constructed the call convention number, you convert it to a
decimal number, and then assign this decimal number to a name, by defining
it the Special Names paragraph. You then use the call convention name in the
CALL statement.

For example, if you define the following call conventions:

SPECIAL-NAMES.

CALL-CONVENTION 0 IS Microsoft-c
 CALL-CONVENTION 3 IS Pascal.

you can then use one convention when calling a C program as follows:

CALL Microsoft-c "routine-name" USING parameter-1,parameter-2

and use the other convention when receiving a call from a Pascal program,
such as:

PROCEDURE DIVISION Pascal USING parameter-1,parameter-2

If you do not specify a call convention, the standard COBOL convention
(call-convention 0) is assumed.


Gati Amir wrote in message <36e3bd3b.0@207.227.203.4>...
>Hi,
>How do I build a project on the NetExpress for MFC which contains a cobol
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: COMP-3 conversion — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COMP-3 conversion

- **From:** Paul Jeremy <NA@NA.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM>`

```
Hi,

I have a file written by a COBOL program on an HP3000. Some of the data
I can read with a text file. However I have COMP-3 fields which I can't
read. Is there anyway I can either, by hand, algorithmically, or
whatever (not using COBOL), convert this data into something readable?

Thanks,

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3 conversion

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<920906417.8321.0.nnrp-03.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM>`

```
Basically if you can read the fields in hex you can translate them  (if they
are really comp-3).

Packed decimal fields only contain numeric hex values apart from the
rightmost half byte which is a sign.  Normally  F or C denote positive
values and D denotes negative values.  Eg X'01234F' and X'01234C' are 1234
whereas X'01234D' is 1234-.

Regards
Rick

Paul Jeremy wrote in message <36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM>...
>Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3 conversion

- **From:** "Krister H���ger���s" <krhag@telia.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e43e08.0@d2o94.telia.com>`
- **References:** `<36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM>`

```
You can try this code. It is written in FoxPro, but I guess that it is
understandable. You pass two parameters to the function, the comp-3 string
and the number of decimals. It returns a string with decimal point.

Krister H�ger�s


The function is not tested, so take care....

func comp3
 parameter m_str, antdec
 private m_str, m_data, m_cnt, m_luku, m_ub, m_lb, m_uc, m_lc
 m_ub  = 0
 m_lb  = 0
 m_uc  = ""
 m_lc  = ""
 m_cnt = len(m_str)
 m_data = ""
 for i = 1 to m_cnt-1
  asctkn = asc(subs(m_str,i,1))
  m_ub = int(asctkn/16)
  m_lb = asctkn-(m_ub * 16)
  m_uc = chr(48+m_ub)
  m_lc = chr(48+m_lb)
  m_data = m_data + m_uc + m_lc
 next
 asctkn = asc(subs(m_str,i,1))
 m_ub = int(asctkn/16)
 m_lb = asctkn-(m_ub * 16)
 m_uc = chr(48+m_ub)
 m_lc = chr(48+m_lb)
 m_data = m_data + m_uc

 if asctkn % 2 = 0    && even
  m_data = '+' + m_data
 else
  m_data = '-' + m_data
 endif
return left(m_data, len(m_data)-antdec) + '.' + right(m_data, antdec)
```

```
Comp-3 is really simple.  Here are some examples


PIC 9(3) comp-3 value 123   = x'123f'
PIC s9(3) comp-3 value 123  = x'123c'
pic s9(3) comp-3 value -123 = x'123d'
pic s9(4) comp-3 value 1234  = x'01234c'
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3 conversion

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.unixware.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<7c6inf$4t2$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM> <36E526CB.E0BC1316@zwg.zicl.co.uk>`

```

Nige wrote in message <36E526CB.E0BC1316@zwg.zicl.co.uk>...
[,,,]
>By coincidence, someone has been asking on comp.unix.unixware.misc
>about using data files written on DOS using PACKED COMP-3.  In his case,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Because COMP-3 is non-standard, its implementation is defined
by the implementor. The most common form is that defined by IBM.
This is the format you learned in "the dim and distant past."

As for translating the above, I have not worked out all the details
since there is an insufficient range of examples. For example,
no negative numbers or decimal fractions. For the examples
given, here is the code. The rest is up to you! :-)

------------
/* fltpnt.c */

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <math.h>

int main ( void );
void sftostr ( unsigned char * sf, char * s );

int main ( void )
{
 unsigned char a_float[] = { 0x00, 0xb0, 0x01, 0x8e };
 char s[10];

 sftostr ( a_float, s );
 printf ( "%s\n", s );
 return 0;
}

/* strange float to string */
void sftostr ( unsigned char * sf, char * s )
{
 double d = 0.0;
 int exponent;
 d = (double) sf[2] * 65536;
 d += (double) sf[1] * 256;
 d += (double) sf[0];
 d /= 4096.0;
 if (sf[3] & 0x80)
  d += 2048.0;
 exponent = ( sf[3] & 0x7F ) - 12;
 d *= pow ( 2.0, (double) exponent );
 d /= 100.0;
 sprintf ( s, "%f", d );
}
------------
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-3 conversion

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.unixware.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<7c6t5o$jgq$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM> <36E526CB.E0BC1316@zwg.zicl.co.uk> <7c6inf$4t2$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <7c6inf$4t2$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>
>Nige wrote in message <36E526CB.E0BC1316@zwg.zicl.co.uk>...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
[....]
>
>As for translating the above, I have not worked out all the details
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is probably more accurate. It certainly makes more sense.
-----------
/* fltpnt.c */

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <math.h>

int main ( void );
void sftostr ( unsigned char * sf, char * s, double scale );

int main ( void )
{
unsigned char a_float[6][4] = {{ 0x00, 0xb0, 0x01, 0x8e },
        { 0x00, 0xa0, 0x3e, 0x8d },
        { 0x00, 0x60, 0x06, 0x8e },
        { 0x00, 0x10, 0x3d, 0x8e },
        { 0x00, 0xd0, 0x04, 0x8e },
        { 0x00, 0x40, 0x1c, 0x8f } };
size_t x;
char s[10];
for ( x = 0; x < 6; x++ )
{
  sftostr ( a_float[x], s, 100.0 );
  printf ( "%s\n", s );
}
return 0;
}

/* strange float to string */
void sftostr ( unsigned char * sf, char * s, double scale )
{
double d = 0.0;
int exponent;
/* extract fraction */
d = (double) sf[2] * 65536.0;
d += (double) sf[1] * 256.0;
d += (double) sf[0];
d /= 16777216.0;
/* adjust */
d += 0.5;
/* adjust for sign of number - magnitude */
if (!(sf[3] & 0x80))
  d *= -1.0;
/* get exponent */
exponent = ( sf[3] & 0x3F );
/* adjust for sign of exponent */
if (sf[3] & 0x40)
  exponent *= -1;
/* adjust the number */
d *= pow ( 2.0, (double) exponent );
/* scale the number */
d /= scale;
/* convert to string */
sprintf ( s, "%f", d );
}
-----------
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3 conversion

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VCHF2.3771$Ek.6341056@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36E3D42E.525D@NA.COM>`

```
Reply is to group as I've seen similar on other sites as well as here.

VALUE +12345.
01  data     pic s9(4) comp-3 value +0.    HEX value now is X'00000C'
move 12345 to data.                              should be
X'12345F' maybe (wrongly)
X'12345C'
(as unsigned is ASSUMED positive therefor  'F', 'D' is -, 'C' is +

move +99999 to data.                            should be
X'99999C'.
COMP-3 is ALWAYS SIGNED, therefor the physical size is computed as
follows:   count 9s + 1 for sign (even if missing) if odd Add 1.
DIVIDE BY 2.
s9(4) = 4 + 1 = 5 (5/2 = 2r1, so + 1) = 6
6/2 = 3 bytes.



Aloha
```

## Thread: COBOL Question — 9 msgs

### COBOL Question

- **From:** "Lord Soth" <spawn69SPAM@ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NZUE2.23110$oA1.2135317@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
Is there a way to make MF COBOL read non-numeric data into a numeric field?
I have tried everything I know to get it to do this but so far no go.  What
I am attempting is to read data with spaces inserted into a numeric field.

           IF SALE-PRI IS EQUAL TO SPACES AND
              SALE-PRI <= LIST-PRI
              MOVE SALE-PRI TO SALE-PRI-OUT
           ELSE
              MOVE SALE-PRI TO SALE-PRI-OUT
           END-IF.
           MOVE SPACES TO INPUT-RECORD.


I have also tried IF SALE-PRI IS NON NUMERIC AND
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Question

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1oen$7bm$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<NZUE2.23110$oA1.2135317@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
If it is leading space only that is the problem, the easiest way
is to inspect it replacing all " " by "0".  If it is trailing space, it
should work with just a move.  If it is imbedded, you will have to
play with it character by character to decide what you want.

Lord Soth wrote in message ...
>Is there a way to make MF COBOL read non-numeric data into a numeric field?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Question

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2WLF2.6$ud5.187@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<NZUE2.23110$oA1.2135317@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7c1oen$7bm$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Lord,

Donald answered correctly. To add to what he said, it would be done by
using this command in the redefines field defined as alphanumeric of the
numeric field and then your numeric field would be able to calculate.

Another way that works just like the inspect command would be to create a
perform routine to look at the field byte by byte using FIELD-A (X1:1) and
replace any space found with a zero.  X1 is numeric starting at 1 and
incrementing it by 1 until hitting the length of the numeric field OR until
you hit a numeric byte.

Gary Roush
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Question

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E42248.D942E68C@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<NZUE2.23110$oA1.2135317@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
05 SALE-PRI-X.
    07  SALE-PRI          PIC 9(04)V99.
would give you the option of addressing SALE-PRI-X if you wanted to treat it as
alphanumeric, but there are things you simply can't do with numeric fields.

Lord Soth wrote:

> Is there a way to make MF COBOL read non-numeric data into a numeric field?
> I have tried everything I know to get it to do this but so far no go.  What
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>               SALE-PRI <= LIST-PRI

You didn't give the format of LIST-PRI, but if it isn't numeric, compare it to
spaces as well.  Also note that when numeric variables or literals are
compared, generated code will pay attention to where the decimal point and what
the sign is while alphanumerics are compared left to right with trailing spaces
assumed on the shorter one if need be.  99.00 and 100.00 will compare
differently if they are alphanumeric for example (with no leading space).

>
>               MOVE SALE-PRI TO SALE-PRI-OUT
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Surely you have a list of COBOL reserved words?  Words such as NO, NOT, and
NUMERIC are in there but not NON.  You could say IF NOT SALE-PRI NUMERIC ...

>
```

```
>would give you the option of addressing SALE-PRI-X if you wanted to treat
it as
>alphanumeric, but there are things you simply can't do with numeric fields.
>
>Lord Soth wrote:
>
>> Is there a way to make MF COBOL read non-numeric data into a numeric
field?
>> I have tried everything I know to get it to do this but so far no go.
What
>> I am attempting is to read data with spaces inserted into a numeric
field.
>>
>>            IF SALE-PRI IS EQUAL TO SPACES AND
>>               SALE-PRI <= LIST-PRI
>
>You didn't give the format of LIST-PRI, but if it isn't numeric, compare it
to
>spaces as well.  Also note that when numeric variables or literals are
>compared, generated code will pay attention to where the decimal point and
what
>the sign is while alphanumerics are compared left to right with trailing
spaces
>assumed on the shorter one if need be.  99.00 and 100.00 will compare
>differently if they are alphanumeric for example (with no leading space).

SALE-PRI is defined as 9(5)v99 value Zero.
Current statement:
           IF SALE-PRI IS NOT NUMERIC AND
              SALE-PRI IS EQUAL TO SPACES
      *       SALE-PRI <= LIST-PRI (I remarked this statement out)
              MOVE SALE-PRI TO SALE-PRI-OUT
           ELSE
              MOVE SALE-PRI TO SALE-PRI-OUT
           END-IF.


>
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>NUMERIC are in there but not NON.  You could say IF NOT SALE-PRI NUMERIC
...

NON was a typo I meant NOT

>
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: OT: Humor only — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Humor only

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1alh$n4u@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
It is a cold and snowy day in the Chicago area and it has been a while since
I posted a completely "off-topic" piece of humor.  My sister just sent me
the following which I thought I would share.  (Some, I have heard before -
but not all.)

Actually, I am not certain that Number 4 (and possibly some others) are
completely off-topic - when you have to deal with vendors OR users!

***

Life's Laws

1.  The two most common elements in the universe are hydrogen and
stupidity

2. If at first you don't succeed, skydiving is not for you.

3.  Money can't buy happiness, but it sure makes misery easier to live
with.

4.  Deja moo:  The feeling that you've heard this bull before.

5.  Psychiatrists say that 1 of 4 people is mentally ill.  Check three
friends.  If they're OK, you're it.

6.  Nothing in the known universe travels faster than a bad check.

7.  A truly wise man never plays leapfrog with a unicorn.

8.  It has recently been discovered that research causes cancer in rats.

9.  Always remember to pillage BEFORE you burn.

10. If you are given an open-book exam, you will forget your book.

11.  Corollary:  If you are given a take-home test, you will forget where
you live.

12.  The trouble with doing something right the first time is that nobody
appreciates how difficult it was.

13.  It may be that your sole purpose in life is simply to serve as a
warning to others.

14.  TJ's Law:  You can't fall off the floor.

15.  The average woman would rather have beauty than brains because the
average man can see better than he can think.

16.  Clothes make the man.  Naked people have little or no influence on
society.

17. Vital papers will demonstrate their vitality by moving from where you
left them to where you can't find them.

18.  Law of Probability Dispersal:  Whatever it is that hits the fan will
not be evenly distributed.
```

```
It means it compiled with no fatal errors.

cyberon01 wrote in message <920918614.688053@eole>...
>what does means this statement :
>STATISTICS: HIGHEST SEVERITY CODE=I, PROGRAM UNIT=1
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: error...

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1biv$mgt$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<920918614.688053@eole>`

```
In article <920918614.688053@eole>,
  "cyberon01" <cyberon01@infonie.be> wrote:
> what does means this statement :
> STATISTICS: HIGHEST SEVERITY CODE=I, PROGRAM UNIT=1
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It means that the "HIGHEST SEVERITY CODE" returned (while compiling the
program) was of severity level "I"

Severity level "I" - "Informational"
Severity level "W" - "Warning"
Severity level "E" - "Error"
Severity level "D" - "Disaster"

Ed Stevens

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-3921b69a72"></a>
## Thread: Help with Microfocus personal cobol and Microfocus Level II cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with Microfocus personal cobol and Microfocus Level II cobol

- **From:** john@smithjd.freeserve
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e4399b.2979504@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Help:-

I am currently having a problem with microfocus personal cobol and
wonder if anyone can through any light on it.

I have a client who is running a number of cobol program compiled
under Microfocus level II cobol on a machine using real 32 as an
operating system ( A version of Concurrent cp/m I believe). I have
taken a copy of a number of files and restored them  onto my own
machine running windows NT. I have then written a program to read the
data part of the file (It was an indexed file) as a relative file and
write it out as an indexed file.

The process works well on most file but I occasionally get a run time
error 018 Read part record error : eof before eor or file open in
wrong mode. this normally seems to coincide with the true end of file
and has not been a problem. I now however have one file that this
appears to happen midway through and is giving major problems. I have
checkd my conversion program and the record lengths are correct as
arey those used in the program on his machine which  creates the data
file.

Any ideas ?

Regards

John
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Microfocus personal cobol and Microfocus Level II cobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1pla$809$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36e4399b.2979504@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Yes.  It is cause by a blocking factor on the disk that is not an exact
multiple of the record size. The Isam leaves a space, and synchronizes on
the next physical block with the next record.  I have not seen that since
MPM/86 on an Altos, circa late 70's, but I can sortof remember encountering
it during a recovery.

You are probably going to have to explore the file with a binary editor, but
I think the end of file will have a control Z.  The mid-point problem was
solved, if I remember rightly, by the fact it always happened at fixed
intervals.  You can probably tell if this is the case by file size. It
should only happen if the file exceeds a certain fixed cluster size that is
a power of two.

john@smithjd.freeserve wrote in message
<36e4399b.2979504@news.demon.co.uk>...
>Help:-
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL for Macintosh

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z5.w5.m02dF3h1ycq46c9s4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<19990308145808.05057.00004878@ng-da1.aol.com>`

```
"PASDA" == "PASDAN" writes:

PASDA> I am looking for a Macinosh COBOL Compiler, Editor and
PASDA> whatever other tools are needed to create applications.
PASDA> 
PASDA> Dan Tompkins
PASDA> pasdan@aol.com

I don't know of any.  Have you considered running SoftWindows, the
Windows emulator for the Macintosh?  Which Mac do you have, a 68k or
a PPC?  If you can get the Windows emulator running, you should be
able to instal any Windows-compatible COBOL.  I've got an Amiga here
and I emulate both MS-DOS & Windows 95 with PC- Task 4.4 and
Macintosh OS ver. 8.1 with Shapeshifter 3.10.  Obviously, if you run
something under MS-DOS, as I do with MicroFocus COBOL, it runs fairly
fast, but under Windows, it will run slower.  Lots of big-endian -
little-endian translations with instruction between CPUs, even though
my PC-Task is a dynamic emulator.  I've also got NRCOBOL for the
Amiga.  Since the Amiga is a 68k CPU, maybe its author might be
interested in compiling a version for the 68k Mac if no COBOL
compiler exists.  But, that's a *big* maybe, 'cause there's
differences between Amigas & Macs.

Jay
```

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00

```
Synkronix has a Cobol compiler for the Java environment.  Although Cobol
programs compiled with PERCobol will run on the Mac, we do not support
Macintosh as a development platform.  This could change if there is enough
demand for PERCobol on this platform.  Please e-mail us if you are
interested in PERCobol for the Mac:

info@synkronix.com

Regards,
David Sanders
Synkronix, Inc.
```

## Thread: Java calling COBOL — 1 msgs

### Java calling COBOL

- **From:** vsikri@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c1kar$ufp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Problem : I got to call COBOL (microfocus) programs from Java on SunOS. I know
there was an earlier posting by Don Braffitt regarding this but this problem
deals with using the same programs BUT buidling a  shared library for SunOS.
Components :
1. Class jni5.java
2. jni5c.c
3. jni5cob.cob
4. jni5.h

Steps 1. javac jni5.java 2. javah -jni jni5 3. /opt/bin/gcc -c
-I/usr/java1.1/include/ -I/usr.java1.1/include/solaris jni5c.c 3. cob -c 
jni5cob.cob 4. ld -G jni5c.o jni5cob.o -o libjni5.so 5. setenv
LD_LIBRARY_PATH /local/users/vishal/javatest  If I remove the COBOL call from
the C program and have Java call C without the cob compile then the program
runs. But if I include a call to the COBOL program and compileand load it
then when I run the program I get the foll. error - 
java.lang.UnsatisfiedLinkError : no jni5 in shared library path  at
java.lang.Throwable.<init>(Compiled Code)  at java.lang.Error.<init>(Compiled
Code)  at java.lang.LinkageError.<init>(Compiled Code)	at
java.lang.UnsatisfiedLinkError.<init>(Compiled Code)  at
java.lang.Runtime.loadLibrary.<init>(Compiled Code)  at
java.lang.System.loadLibrary.<init>(Compiled Code)  at jni5.main(Compiled
Code)  I think I ma not able to link the C and COBOL  together. Can anyone
please tell me how to create a shared object for SunOS using the above as a
framework.

Thanks Vishal Singh

```

<a id="t-f0f789a60e"></a>
## Thread: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** Harish Murthy <worm@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
Hi,

The problem is that i have variables

01 VAR-A      PIC 9V9(4) VALUE 3.2222

01 VAR-B      PIC 9V9(4) VALUE 1.6235

01 VAR-C     PIC 9V9(4)

MULTIPLY VAR-A BY VAR-B GIVING VAR-C ROUNDED.

The result we get is 5.2312

but the desired result is 5.2300

Are there any method of getting this done at the MULTIPLY /ROUNDED verb
itself ,
without MOVEing to variable of lesser decimal places ?

Thanks

- Harish
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** terrywin.nospam@spectra.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e68fe0.2058728@supernews.spectra.net>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
On Tue, 09 Mar 1999 18:07:42 +0200, Harish Murthy
<worm@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Change variable Var-C to -->  pic 9v9(2)

HTH
Terry
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c6je2$5nb$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```

Harish Murthy wrote in message
<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not with MULTIPLY but with COMPUTE

    compute var-c =
        ( function integer ( var-a * var-b * 100 + 0.5) ) / 100

should do it.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith at aiservices dot com >
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F87070A02213DDDD.3306B880A7663FCC.8ABA30E6681339F0@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
On Tue, 09 Mar 1999 18:07:42 +0200, Harish Murthy
<worm@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> enlightened us:

>Hi,
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Since all of your variables are 9V9(4), the rounding will occur on the
4th decimal place.  The actual multiplication of the variable a and b
is 5.2312417.  If you only want precision to the 100ths, then define
your result variable as 9v99.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Only Dead Fish Go With The Flow.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION -- NO

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SqHF2.3748$Ek.6332444@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
No there isn't.

You specified 4 digit precision after the decimal place for EACH FIELD,
what makes you think you can throw that away?

either change VAR-C to  PIC 9V99
OR multiply AGAIN by 100
or move to 9V99 and BACK to 9V9(4).


Harish Murthy wrote in message
<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** "Rob Kemmer" <rob@tobin-kemmer.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ces4c$63v$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```

Harish Murthy wrote in message
<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>without MOVEing to variable of lesser decimal places ?


Simplest to use a redefinition.

eg

01   var-c-group.
        02   var-c              pic 9v99.
        02                          pic xx value "00".
 01   real-var-c redefines   var-c-group       pic 9v9(4).

do the same    mutltiply but use  GIVING VAR-C
then use real-var-c as your result


Rob Kemmer
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36EB2381.D9B0BCF5@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <7ces4c$63v$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
To get the desired result, you need to change the picture clause for var-c
to  PIC 9V9900.  The  two zeros of the pic clause  are place holders where
COBOL will use a zero.  No other changes, redefines, or move statements are
required.

Regards,
John

Rob Kemmer wrote:

> Harish Murthy wrote in message
> <36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rob Kemmer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ch4ne$7vs$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <7ces4c$63v$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <36EB2381.D9B0BCF5@ix.netcom.com>`

```
I like your answer; but, it has the effect of changing the class
of VAR-C from NUMERIC to NUMERIC-EDITED. Whether
that is a problem depends upon the other uses of VAR-C.
----------------
Rick Smith

John Trifon wrote in message <36EB2381.D9B0BCF5@ix.netcom.com>...
>To get the desired result, you need to change the picture clause for var-c
>to  PIC 9V9900.  The  two zeros of the pic clause  are place holders where

[...]
>> >01 VAR-C     PIC 9V9(4)
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** "ThinkAgain" <1day@atime.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<922209880.068.35@news.remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
Try changing  the picture on VAR-C   to Pic 9v99.


Good luck.


Harish Murthy wrote in message
<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990323134442.01431.00000223@ng-cg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<922209880.068.35@news.remarQ.com>`

```
define 
01  VAR-C Pic 9V(2)
01  VAR-D redefines VAR-C Pic 9V9(4).

COMPUTE ROUNDED VAR-C =
        VAR-A * VAR-B

DISPLAY VAR-D.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36FDFF42.9F9BAE32@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<922209880.068.35@news.remarQ.com> <19990323134442.01431.00000223@ng-cg1.aol.com> <7d94ki$1l9$1@news.igs.net> <36F950F9.6973D51@IMN.nl> <7dc4lk$q2o$1@news.igs.net>`

```
ll x redefines y ...

When the level-number (ll) of X is other than 01, it must specify fewer or the
same number of character positions that the data item referenced by Y contains.

When the level-number (ll) of X is equal to 01, this restriction does not exist.

[partially quoted from Micro Focus on-line help (not one of their extensions)]

The Frog
```

<a id="t-98801f3e7b"></a>
## Thread: Cobol analyzer tool available? — 3 msgs

### Cobol analyzer tool available?

- **From:** Michel Dufour <mimi2@netcom.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E5D02E.FCE8DA51@netcom.ca>`

```
Hi

Does someone know some good program tool to analyze cobol program: to
read cobol program more efficaciously than using a normal editor.

Best regards, MD
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol analyzer tool available?

- **From:** jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel)
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e62d8d.5112932@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<36E5D02E.FCE8DA51@netcom.ca>`

```
On Tue, 09 Mar 1999 20:51:43 -0500, Michel Dufour <mimi2@netcom.ca>
wrote:
>Does someone know some good program tool to analyze cobol program: to
>read cobol program more efficaciously than using a normal editor.
This is a main good reason to use Microfocus.
On Windows, you have a very good editor/animator/analyzer wich is
provided with all the versions (called Workbench, or NetExpress, but
near the same)
On Unix, there is also good tools: editor/anim/analyze with CSI (cobol
source information) facilitie. It is included in tbox, a part of OCDS
(object cobol dev suite).
All these tools include interactive cross reference facilities and
various fonctions very nices when your job is cobol.
This answer is not an advertisement. There is a lot of problems with
Microfocus tools... but edit/anim et so on are nices.
jm
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol analyzer tool available?

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E86682.FEE1ADE6@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<36E5D02E.FCE8DA51@netcom.ca>`

```
Michel Dufour wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Best regards, MD

We can send you a beta version of Visual CobolTransformer.

Not only it gives you a nice view of the program
with all the gory language-sensitive details,
you also can write visual macros that transform program 
at the language level. Things like creating macro
(in 1 min) that transforms TRANSFORM (OSVS) statement
to INSPECT CONVERTING (ANSI) statement.

Visual CobolTransformer is based on CobolTransformer
available at http://www.siber.com/sct/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-61b9cc10b0"></a>
## Thread: 2 or 3 things I wonder about: MF and Fujitsu Cobol — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### 2 or 3 things I wonder about: MF and Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e4e186.1086233@news.skynet.be>`

```
Now that MF has bought Intersolv, I hope that they will develop and
put on the market their own ODBC driver for MF-ISAM files.  I have
always been disoriented by the ODBC strategy og MF: Correlate (too
expansive IMHO) then nothing then transfer to Transoft...

I have heard from some MF people that a Linux Object Cobol suite is
under development or at least that they are thinking of developpint
such a suite. We should all write to MF to ask them to port their Unix
suite to Linux. At least, good news.

I talked to some Fujitsu representative to know if they plan to be
able to read MF-ISAM files directly from their own FJ-Cobol or
PowerCobol with a compiling option like they have for C-ISAM files for
example. The answer was : no! I wonder why FJ doesn't want to
introduce such a option which could increase their selling potential.
I think that we are many developpers using MF products under Unix.
These files, residing on a Unix server, can be accessed from Windows
95-98-NT clients easely by mounting disks. The FJ environment for
Win95 is relatively cheap compared to others packages(!) and we could
thus develop some application under Win95 accessing native MF files.
Am I the only one in that situation? If not, I propose that we all
write to FJ to ask them such a possibilty. What can one programmer do?
Many can...

Have a nice day,

Alain Reymond
CEIA
```

#### ↳ Re: 2 or 3 things I wonder about: MF and Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e51faf.547443225@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36e4e186.1086233@news.skynet.be>`

```
On Tue, 09 Mar 1999 09:06:52 GMT, areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
wrote:


>I talked to some Fujitsu representative to know if they plan to be
>able to read MF-ISAM files directly from their own FJ-Cobol or
>PowerCobol with a compiling option like they have for C-ISAM files for
>example. The answer was : no! I wonder why FJ doesn't want to
>introduce such a option which could increase their selling potential.

I think they don't want to get into a nasty lawsuit with MicroFocus!

On the Linux subject, I would rather use a Fujitsu version than a MF
version simply because of cost, speed and runtime license
considerations.  The ONLY reason I have not seriously looked at
AcuCOBOL for Linux is because of the runtime charges.  If they would
drop the runtime fee for the Linux version I would give them serious
consideration.  However, my preferece would be a Fujitsu version for
Linux.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 2 or 3 things I wonder about: MF and Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e533e3.22174328@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<36e4e186.1086233@news.skynet.be> <36e51faf.547443225@news1.ibm.net>`

```
>I think they don't want to get into a nasty lawsuit with MicroFocus!
I thought the same. But Parkway Software (editor of an ODBC driver for
MF files) or Transoft or Liant have ODBC products that can read the MF
files. The EXTFH is perfectly described in the MF Unix Cobol Suite.
Even the structure is defined in the documentation. I see no reason
why Parkway and other should be able to access the files and FJ not.

>
>On the Linux subject, I would rather use a Fujitsu version than a MF
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are perfectly right!

Alain Reymond
```

```
David
```

```
for Linux available at:

http://www.synkronix.com/educational/educational.html

Note:  The Educational version does not come with the SlickEdit IDE or many
of the features of the Professional version, including speed improvements.
We do offer the Professional version for Linux for $1250 per user.  No
runtime fees or royalties!

Regards,
David Sanders
Synkronix, Inc.
```

<a id="t-4a86e18ef0"></a>
## Thread: (Relatively) new IBM COBOL compiler option — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### (Relatively) new IBM COBOL compiler option

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c4743$po3@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Partially, this note is in reply to the thread on ISPF options and
compiling/scanning your COBOL source code.)

I don't think it has received lots of publicity but do you know about the
IBM (mainframe and PC - I don't think it is on COBOL/400) feature that lets
you "syntax scan" EXEC CICS and EXEC SQL statements during your compile step
(without using the preprocessor or translator)? According to the latest APG,

"Use ANALYZE when you want the compiler to check the syntax of imbedded SQL
and CICS statements in addition to native COBOL statements."

For more detail, see

http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYPG201/2.3.3
..2
```

#### ↳ Re: (Relatively) new IBM COBOL compiler option

- **From:** Roland Schiradin <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E5C011.4C5E84F6@tap.de>`
- **References:** `<7c4743$po3@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill,

> "Use ANALYZE when you want the compiler to check the syntax of imbedded SQL
> and CICS statements in addition to native COBOL statements."

thanks for that info. Apart form that I like to expand my Cobol-Analyzer to
support
this Cobol-Version and the new option. Do u or somebody can send me a load-lib
which contains at least one Cobol-Load compiled with that Cobol-version

Roland
```

---

<a id="t-8f4a2b06ad"></a>
## Thread: Resolving ABENDs under LE — 1 msgs

### Resolving ABENDs under LE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c4pso$526@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I don't think that this is off-topic - or certainly not completely.  FYI, I
just put the following information out on the IBM-MAIN list server and
thought that it might be of interest to some readers of this NG as well.

If you aren't aware of it, the SHARE handouts from the session,

 "How to Solve Abends using Language Environment Dumps"

are now available (in Acrobat PDF format) at

 http://www.s390.ibm.com/le/conference/s8209.pdf

Obviously, the foils don't cover everything that BEING there would do (End
Plug for SHARE) - but I think these will help a lot of the common questions
on IBM-MAIN.

P.S.  These foils do NOT cover the new TERMTHDACT options:
        - UAONLY
        - UAIMM
        - UATRACE

For information on those, see
 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/ceea306/2%2e1%2e53

or for the web-less, the section, "2.1.53 TERMTHDACT" in
 Title: OS/390 V2R7.0 Lang Env for OS/390 & VM Prog Ref
 Document Number: SC28-1940-06

but to "wet your appetite" the Summary of Changes describe these as,

"Three new TERMTHDACT sub-options that will allow the supression of CEEDUMP
while obtaining a SYSxDUMP. This will allow the capture of a system dump
prior to Language Environment condition management processing. ..."
```

---

<a id="t-6583e6d6a8"></a>
## Thread: Cobol data file — 2 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-6583e6d6a8)._

#### ↳ Re: Cobol data file

- **From:** Philippe Palau <philipp.palau@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-03-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E5244F.D4FCA222@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<7b6q3g$vfk$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
For what I know, you have to do it "by hand". Generally, Cobol data file
don't have an header with the structure of the records nicely stocked.
There are some utilities (I think of one from Futjisu for example) who
can help, but mostly if you don't have the sources, it will be a lot of
tests and trials.

xpto a ï¿½crit :
> 
> I would like to know if anyone have or knows a tool that, giving a cobol
> datafile, prints the structure of the file.
> 
> Josï¿½ Oliveira
```

- **From:** Rajagopal Krishna Raj <krishnaraj@softhome.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E69EFE.1587A565@softhome.net>`
- **References:** `<7b6q3g$vfk$1@duke.telepac.pt> <36E5244F.D4FCA222@wanadoo.fr>`

```
Hi,

From whatever I have worked on, if u have 'file description' as copybook for
the data file and the data file, you can get a formatted output (Tabular with
headers).
This can be done by means of FILE-AID tool in mainframe.
And in PC, I know 'Data file editor' in MF Workbench can do it. But I have not
worked much on it.
Eg.
If you have 'master file' data file and you have 'master' as copybook
containing the file description (declaration), then these two are sufficient
in generating a formatted output using FILEAID.
Note: You can print data files in formatted mode (as above), Character mode
and Hex mode.
Cheers,
Krish.


Philippe Palau wrote:

> For what I know, you have to do it "by hand". Generally, Cobol data file
> don't have an header with the structure of the records nicely stocked.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Josï¿½ Oliveira
```
