<a id="t-0386d48e84"></a>
## Thread: What can I use? — 5 msgs

### What can I use?

- **From:** dhawkins <dhawkins@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E6F734.98B79235@home.com>`

```
Im running out of options right now. I was hoping someone out there in
the world could help me out. Im a network administrator for a large
company in Canada and we just found out (through an exhaustive search
and testing) that our main preparatory data base software is NOT Y2K. We
are currently in the process of moving to MS Access 97 and SQL server.
My problem is this The database software we want to get rid of was an
old DOS program that was made in COBOL. That is all we know. Its slow
clunking and buggy. In the root dir of this software are the data files
them selves. which I can see and make out, My question is...Is there a
way for me to convert those files to something newer(exel,CSV) any thing
"newer" then what they are now. The main data base files are around the
100 to 200 megs in size so conversion is a must. They contain EVERYTHING
about our type of business.  Now I know a conversion wouldn't be perfect
and it would require quite a bit of  time and patients. But there Has to
be a way. I myself have converted "preparatory" data files to newer
ones, but Ive never done it with a datafile made in a COBOL made
software package. Any help, recommendations or people to contact would
be just great. One more thing, Do all programs made with COBOL save data
the same way?

dhawkins@home.com (please post or e-mail me)
```

#### ↳ Re: What can I use?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E%XF2.3607$bk.10273522@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36E6F734.98B79235@home.com>`

```
You need to know the format of the data. If you do not have the source code,
or at least some type of file layout you most likely will not be able to
pull it out directly.

You mentioned this file or files holds ALL of your data. If the database is
in one file changes are they used record layouts which vary in size. If this
is the case you will probably not be able to load it into EXCEL or ACCESS
directly. You could write a VB macro or EXE to extract it from within EXCEL
or ACCESS, but you still need to know the layout of the data.

Furthermore if you know the layout you are still going to need to know the
relation between the record layouts. Simply capturing the data may not do it
for you.

As far as guessing the layouts of the file you may be able to do this to a
certain extent. Most alphanumeric data is stored as straight ASCII. You may
be able to browse the file with a word processor and guess some of the
layouts and feed your results into VB/ACCESS/or EXCEL. Since you said the
files are in excess of 100Megs, you may not be able to load the entire thing
into ACCESS or EXCEL due to the record limitation in ACCESS/EXCEL and
possibly even memory limitations of the machine your system is running on.

If you have the source code to the COBOL programs you may be able to get the
layouts from the FD or WORKING-STORAGE section. If you know nothing about
COBOL this will be rather difficult. With a good COBOL manual you may be
able to figure it out.

Good Luck.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What can I use?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e88792.770680688@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36E6F734.98B79235@home.com> <E%XF2.3607$bk.10273522@storm.twcol.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Mar 1999 18:07:49 -0500, "Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:

>You need to know the format of the data. If you do not have the source code,
>or at least some type of file layout you most likely will not be able to
>pull it out directly.

I received an e-mail reply to my query, here's the gist of it:

There is a legal battle going on between them and the software vendor.

The vendor says it's COBOL, but not what vendor or version.

They have no record layouts.

They CAN print everything.  My suggestion was to forward me a dir
structure so I can figure out what COBOL it is - that will go a long
way toward finding out if the data is even extractable outside that
COBOL.

Next, I suggested that as part of their legal engagement they get the
record layouts from the vendor.

Lastly, that they "capture" the report data - by redirecting the
printer to a com port and capturiing with a null modem cable and
terminal program, then using Access or whatever to import the data
from the reports.
```

#### ↳ Re: What can I use?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e73aec.685509027@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36E6F734.98B79235@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 10 Mar 1999 22:45:26 GMT, dhawkins <dhawkins@home.com> wrote:

>Im running out of options right now. I was hoping someone out there in
>the world could help me out. Im a network administrator for a large
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>clunking and buggy. 

Ok... There are a couple of things.

First - Who made it?  Who wrote it, where did you get it?

How do you know it's COBOL?

Was it written in house or purchased?

Is the company that made it still in business?

What COBOL was it written in?

Do you have any source code and/or file layouts?

Do you have the ability to print full detail reports from the data
files? One option is to re-capture the data from report images.  

Let us know the answers to these questions and we'll try to help you
out!
```

#### ↳ Re: What can I use?

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990311095254.06027.00001534@ng-cg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36E6F734.98B79235@home.com>`

```
is all the data in ASCII Format?  if it is then you can use ACCESS to "pull"
the data into a table.  
The only problem with this approach is that you need to be sure of where one
data field ends and the othe begins.  ACCESS will allow you to define the
length of the fields, but you have to know.  If you do have the COBOL layouts
you can use them to "map" your data.
If you need any help doing this I would be happy to help.  Just send me a
sample file and the layout if you have it and I can create an import utility
for you.

twymer@aol.com
```

```
workstations will be running Win95/98. We are unsure as to whether we will
choose NetExpress Indexed Files or a DBMS such as SQL Server as the Data

residing on an NT Server or NetExpress addressing a SQL DataBase residing on
an NT Server, we would really appreciate your comments.

Thanks in advance
```

<a id="t-c8965faadd"></a>
## Thread: COBOL/MVS and Quotes — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** "Greg McDougall" <dougall@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net>`

```
We are currently moving from COBOL II to COBOL/MVS on MVS v5.2. One
thing we have noticed is that, unlike COBOL II which (depending on the
compiler directive) insisted on either quotes or apostrophes in the
code, COBOL/MVS seems not to care either way and allows the mixing of
these within a program.

The only seemingly relevant compiler option I can find talks about the
assumed literal in the ALLQUOTE statement (which I assume refers to
INSPECT?). We went through some degree of pain standardising on the
apostrophe - was this a waste of time? We would like the compiler to at
least flag any instances of these mixed usage.

Greg
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net>`

```
Greg McDougall <dougall@bigfoot.com> writes ...

>We are currently moving from COBOL II to COBOL/MVS on MVS v5.2. One
>thing we have noticed is that, unlike COBOL II which (depending on the
>compiler directive) insisted on either quotes or apostrophes in the
>code, COBOL/MVS seems not to care either way and allows the mixing of
>these within a program.

This is correct. A non-numeric literal may now be bounded by single quotes or
double quotes, as long as the beginng and ending delimiters are the same. So
you can have "Bob's News" in one value clause and 'Department 515' in another
in the same program.


>
>The only seemingly relevant compiler option I can find talks about the
>assumed literal in the ALLQUOTE statement (which I assume refers to
>INSPECT?). 

Acutally, the compiler option now only impacts the figurative constant QUOTE
(also QUOTES). This constant may be used as a value, in a move, in a compare,
or in an INSPECT statement.


>We went through some degree of pain standardising on the
>apostrophe - was this a waste of time? We would like the compiler to at
>least flag any instances of these mixed usage.

It wasn't necessarily a waste of time at the time. It helped your shop be
consistent when it mattered.

I consider this new behavior as more liberating, saying, "it won't hurt you to
use either apaostrophes or quotes" (while still flagging you if your ending and
beginning delimiters aren't the same). You can still have your standard
(although I would tend to loosen up on that one), but the compiler will no
longer enforce it.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com>`

```
Just to add to what Steve has already said,

1) The APOST/QUOTE compiler option is STILL valid - but the only thing that
it controls is how the "QUOTE" figurative constant is set up.

2) The draft of the next Standard has this same extension (of allowing BOTH
single and double quotes - aka apostrophe and quotation marks - as the
delimiter for alphanumeric literals)

3) The history of "single-quote" and IBM (and IBM-compatible) compilers has
to do with what characters were "easy" to put on punch cards.  Hardly an
issue today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e85c35@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com> <7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>3) The history of "single-quote" and IBM (and IBM-compatible) compilers has
>to do with what characters were "easy" to put on punch cards.  Hardly an
>issue today.


But even today, APOST is lower case and QUOTE is upper case and thus a bit
harder to type... so this is a welcome change.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** "Greg McDougall" <dougall@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ceguq$ld2$1@plug.news.pipex.net>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com> <7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

the clarifications.

Thanks,

Greg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f2e5e4.11159386@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com> <7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 11 Mar 1999 16:30:24 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>3) The history of "single-quote" and IBM (and IBM-compatible) compilers has
>to do with what characters were "easy" to put on punch cards.  Hardly an
>issue today.

I have never been a fan of IBM, and I believe there are several ways the
COBOL standard is poorer because of them.  However, in this case IBM is
being trashed where they did _nothing_ wrong.

The early COBOL standards assumed that each vendor used their own
proprietary character set.  The vendor was required to choose some
character -- ANY character -- and call it the quote character.  They could
have used # or % or ^ or ? or even a two character code, but IBM happened
to choose ', because it was already on the keyboard of most keypunch
machines.  This was fully compliant with X3.23-1968, X3.23-1974, and
X3.23-1985, because none of them ever required that the quote character
map to any specific graphic.  

There is now a general consensus that the quote character should map to
Ascii character X"22", but even the current draft doesn't directly say
that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d7oo8$ah8@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com> <7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36f2e5e4.11159386@netnews>`

```
Randall,
  Are you certain of this?  The '74 and '85 Standards (I don't know about
the '68) all reference the ANSI and ISO Standards (648) in their definition
of the COBOL character set.  I know that this has some flexibility in it -
but I THINK that it does define what a "quote" is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f8af1d.387504663@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com> <7c9g6m$sf2@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <36f2e5e4.11159386@netnews> <7d7oo8$ah8@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 23 Mar 1999 03:59:47 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Randall,
>  Are you certain of this?  The '74 and '85 Standards (I don't know about
>the '68) all reference the ANSI and ISO Standards (648) in their definition
>of the COBOL character set.  I know that this has some flexibility in it -
>but I THINK that it does define what a "quote" is.

Well, with COBOL-85 I'm not sure, because I never saw the final standard.
In COBOL-74  ASCII was referenced for data representation, but it didn't
really equate that with the COBOL character set used to specify the
program (what's called the character repetoire in the current draft).
COBOL-74 still had that stuff about two character symbols.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d56fe$8p3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net> <19990311160534.20208.00000926@ng119.aol.com> <36e8895f.771141440@news1.ibm.net> <7cau3g$da@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1iwdw2Z27YyY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36EA00B4.8556234F@att.net>`

```
In article <36EA00B4.8556234F@att.net>,
  wblynch@worldnet.att.net wrote:
>
> Cool - just like REXX!
>
> Bill Lynch
>

Unfortunately, there is just one little thing which is very uncool: Both the
SQL- and the CICS-precompiler still need the option "APOST" or 'QUOTE'. And
they both treat a literal with the "wrong" delimiters as a data-name. The SQL
precompiler says that a colon is missing (invalid host variable). The CICS
precompiler accepts it, but does a call to 'DFHEI1' with the literal "by
reference" which is not accepted by the COBOL compiler.

So we have to distinguish between literals in 'pure' COBOL and between an
'EXEC' and the corresponding "END-EXEC".

Regards

Daniel
------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2M2 has QUOTE/APOST only as figurative constant, not as
literal delimiter. So this must have been changed either from release 1 to
release 2 of version 1 or from COBOL/370 to V1R1.

Regards

Daniel
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/MVS and Quotes

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DPXF2.3605$bk.10265633@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7c98ok$4bn$1@plug.news.pipex.net>`

```
This may be overstating the obvious, but have you tried the "FLAG(I,I)"
compiler option? This should flag all informational level messages both
instream and at the end of the listing.

EX:

       PROCESS FLAG(I,I)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ......

OR
       CBL FLAG(I,I)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ........
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7c8p5t$63s$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
EDS is seeking experienced Cobol/DB2 programmers for full-time positions on
our Bell Atlantic account.  Ideal candidates will have at least 3 years
experience programming with Cobol/DB2 and at least 2 years experience working
on telecommunications applications.

EDS is providing additional staff for Bell Atlantic's "ExpressTrak" billing
system, Bell Atlantic�s next generation billing system.  ExpressTrak is an
extremely large complex customer care and billing system.  There are
client/server aspects that utilize a WNT Smalltalk front end as well as a
large batch-processing component.  The billing system is a strictly
batch-processing sub-system.  The core technologies used in this area are
standard IBM MVS operations environments with Cobol as the language and a DB2
relational database.

All work will be performed out of Bell Atlantic's Arlington, VA facility.
Attire is professional business dress.
```

```
bleh ...

>EDS is seeking experienced Cobol/DB2 programmers for full-time positions on
```

```
Hi,
I'm a student and I have a copy of MFC workbench at home. I wrote a program
that generates a report in a .dat file. I have tried everything I could
think of to print a copy of that report, but I can only view it in the data
file editor. Wherever else I try and open it from, it comes up in ASCII.
Is there a way that I can print this .dat file? Thanks for any help.
dave
```

```
Hi Dave,

If your .dat file is simply ascii data, and you're running
Windows 95/98 or NT, then my Imprint program will print the
file for you. It has many options for changing the font, margins,
header/footer info etc. It can be downloaded from
http://www.gallicrow.co.uk/Imprint.html

Jon

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Jon Eva                                mailto:joneva@gallicrow.co.uk
Gallicrow Software Limited             http://www.gallicrow.co.uk
Home of Imprint - a utility for printing text and binary files
```

<a id="t-8fcccaf5b8"></a>
## Thread: Canada vs US - contract opportunities — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Canada vs US - contract opportunities

- **From:** "THORPE, JASON" <jason.thorpe@bellsygma.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E7FE7C.C8640F1@bellsygma.com>`

```
Howdy, folks,

Things up here in Canada (Toronto, especially) have gone from bad to
worse.  Everyones' battening down the hatches in prep for y2k.  There's
tons of contractors out of work, and things don't look like they're
going to improve anytime soon.

How are things down in the US of A?  Last I'd heard, rates of $60-70 US
were considered reasonable for non-SF/NY locations.  Has this changed? 
My contract is coming due up here soon, and I might have to consider
heading south for a while.

Any info would be greatly appreciated.

Jason
```

#### ↳ Canada vs US - contract opportunities

- **From:** charlajw@netcom.com (Charla J. Williams)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<charlajwF8HnBx.FKK@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36E7FE7C.C8640F1@bellsygma.com>`

```
THORPE, JASON (jason.thorpe@bellsygma.com) wrote:

: Things up here in Canada (Toronto, especially) have gone from bad 
: to worse.  Everyones' battening down the hatches in prep for y2k.  
: There's tons of contractors out of work, and things don't look 
: like they're going to improve anytime soon.

Here's what I hear is happening in Seattle.  Companies are trying
to hire perms rather than contractors.  Boeing let a bunch of
contractors go.  The contractor market has flattened out somewhat
in Seattle.  Folks think it will pick up early in 2000 -- not only
to fix problems but also to do those enhancements everyone put
on hold while doing Y2K.

: How are things down in the US of A?  Last I'd heard, rates of 
: $60-70 US were considered reasonable for non-SF/NY locations.  
: Has this changed? 

When I was looking last September (my contract was going to
end in December and I didn't want to get caught out of work
during that holiday dead zone) I was asking $50-60 an hour W2.  
This rate was possible although some of the contract agencies
were saying they'd try to get me $50.  I'm hearing from
folks looking that there are contracts up to $65 an hour W2 in 
the Seattle area but I couldn't tell you what the normal rate is.

: My contract is coming due up here soon, and I might have to 
: consider heading south for a while.

I worked with a fellow from Calgary a few years back who 
was working in the U.S. for similar reasons.

Regards, Charla
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Canada vs US - contract opportunities

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cbetd$34e$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<36E7FE7C.C8640F1@bellsygma.com> <charlajwF8HnBx.FKK@netcom.com>`

```
... and perms, of course, are much less expensive due to the inherent
security of their positions; perms are *not* covered by Employment At Will

... and I am the King of England, as well.

DD
```

#### ↳ Canada vs US - contract opportunities

- **From:** charlajw@netcom.com (Charla J. Williams)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

##### ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol for the pc -win95/98

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e93c15.3384368@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7c97tt$m6r$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk> <7c9do2$3c2$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com> wrote:

>Try www.adtools.com for the Fujitsu compiler.

Donald:

Although this certainly is one way to obtain the Fujitsu compiler, I
have been making a different suggestion to those seeking to get their
mitts on the Fujitsu "freebie" version of the compiler.

I suggest the following:

You should buy a book called Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours by Thane
Hubbell.

It's a great book, but even better, it includes a CD which contains
the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.

The book can be purchased online at www.amazon.com or you can buy it
at your local book store.

The ISBN number of Thane's book is 0-672-31453-3

The advantages of my suggested path to procure the compiler are as
follows:

1.  It gets the compiler in the hands of the intended recipient in
only a few days at most.
2.  It gives the intended recipient a great book for use in learning
the language.
3.  It helps our good friend Thane Hubbell pay for that Lear Jet he
just ordered (just kidding....it's a Cessna Citation III ;-)
4.  ...and it only costs about $20.00 US$...certainly within the
financial means of virtually everyone.

The only downside is that our student friends who order the book may
have to go one weekend without pizza and beer.  God forbid!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: object cobol for the pc -win95/98

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol


<a id="t-3a87848104"></a>
## Thread: COBOL compilers for PC - help please — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### COBOL compilers for PC - help please

- **From:** "Eagle" <shumphreys@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c9508$hl1$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I'm trying to use COBOL650 and FUJITSU v3 compiler for some project college
work.  Struggling to get either to work.  With the Fujitsu it compiles fine
but will not link; the COBOL650 won't compile the code, error message saying
FCB not found (- I hope I've remembered that correctly).

Can anyone help me with this one?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL compilers for PC - help please

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c9dm8$3bu$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7c9508$hl1$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Fcb not found means that you are using a *very* old compiler.  FCB's
dissappeared completely with windows.  DOS version 5 was the last that
supported them, and they have been obsolete since DOS version 2.

What message do you get with Fujitsu?  I would suspect that it will not link
because your path is not set correctly, but it is hard to know without an
error message.  Did you do a full install from the CD?


Eagle wrote in message <7c9508$hl1$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>...
>I'm trying to use COBOL650 and FUJITSU v3 compiler for some project college
>work.  Struggling to get either to work.  With the Fujitsu it compiles fine
>but will not link; the COBOL650 won't compile the code, error message
saying
>FCB not found (- I hope I've remembered that correctly).
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL compilers for PC - help please

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c9nvs$qfm@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7c9508$hl1$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <7c9dm8$3bu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <7c9dm8$3bu$1@news.igs.net>...
>Fcb not found means that you are using a *very* old compiler.  FCB's
>dissappeared completely with windows.  DOS version 5 was the last that
>supported them, and they have been obsolete since DOS version 2.
>
>What message do you get with Fujitsu?  I would suspect that it will not
link
>because your path is not set correctly, but it is hard to know without an
>error message.  Did you do a full install from the CD?
>

My guess (crystal ball) says that you haven't declared the main program as
MAIN - which is required with Fujitsu.  This is the usual "monthly" problem
on linking their object code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL compilers for PC - help please

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c5_F2.345$HY5.214@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<7c9508$hl1$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <7c9dm8$3bu$1@news.igs.net> <7c9nvs$qfm@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Thanks William.  It's usually the MAIN option that's missing when links
aren't working.

Gary

>Donald Tees wrote in message <7c9dm8$3bu$1@news.igs.net>...
```

<a id="t-0431127271"></a>
## Thread: What can I use? II — 2 msgs

### What can I use? II

- **From:** dhawkins <dhawkins@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36E84629.89EF67F0@home.com>`

```
I want to thank all of you out there who have helped me with my "little"
problem.

If any one can help please continue to read on and I WILL get back to
you, I have been sending a lot of e-mail regarding this issue so please
be patient and I will get to you.

1 more thing, A few of you out there have requested the "directory
listing", is this the directory structure? or something else?

(REPRINT BELOW)

Im running out of options right now. I was hoping someone out there in
the world could help me out. Im a network administrator for a large
company in Canada and we just found out (through an exhaustive search
and testing) that our main preparatory data base software is NOT Y2K. We

are currently in the process of moving to MS Access 97 and SQL server.
My problem is this The database software we want to get rid of was an
old DOS program that was made in COBOL. That is all we know. Its slow
clunking and buggy. In the root dir of this software are the data files
them selves. which I can see and make out, My question is...Is there a
way for me to convert those files to something newer(exel,CSV) any thing

"newer" then what they are now. The main data base files are around the
100 to 200 megs in size so conversion is a must. They contain EVERYTHING

about our type of business.  Now I know a conversion wouldn't be perfect

and it would require quite a bit of  time and patients. But there Has to

be a way. I myself have converted "preparatory" data files to newer
ones, but Ive never done it with a datafile made in a COBOL made
software package. Any help, recommendations or people to contact would
be just great. One more thing, Do all programs made with COBOL save data

the same way?

dhawkins@home.com (please post or e-mail me)
```

#### ↳ Re: What can I use? II

- **From:** jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e8c2a6.1405079@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<36E84629.89EF67F0@home.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Mar 1999 22:34:28 GMT, dhawkins <dhawkins@home.com> wrote:

>1 more thing, A few of you out there have requested the "directory
>listing", is this the directory structure? or something else?

On MSDOS box, type:
dir/s "appDir">listingFile
listingFile is the directory listing of "appDir". It will contain the
list of all the files. If we can see something like "*.idx", may be we
can help you.

If you will see something.idx, look at for the same name with ".dat"
or ".fil" or other as the suffix of the name, and type:
debug something.dat
d
q
You will see the header of the file. It's useful. It can help to find
the version and the vendor of the ISAM engine
```

<a id="t-4065118dbb"></a>
## Thread: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: HELP ON ROUNDED/TRUNCATION

- **From:** "Topcoder" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c7pid$8fj@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<36E5474E.AC6F5EBC@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <36E5AD81.F16A23C@yahoo.com>`

```
Not true.  For instance:
01 VAR-A      PIC 9V9(4) VALUE 3.2222

01 VAR-B      PIC 9V9(4) VALUE 1.6235

01 VAR-C     PIC 9V9(7)

MULTIPLY VAR-A BY VAR-B GIVING VAR-C ROUNDED.

The result you get is 5.2312417.  Change to:

01 VAR-C     PIC 9V9(6)

The result you get is 5.231242.

I suggest you do not post in HTML.  Many people will be unable

to read your post if you do.



Joseph Kohler wrote in message +ADw-36E5AD81.F16A23C+AEA-yahoo.com+AD4-...

    You will get your desired result if you change VAR-C to PIC
9V9(2).  Rounding only workes when the result will have less decimal
positions than the intermediate reults or input variables.
```

## Thread: Weird error help!!! — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [1999-02](1999-02.md#t-8044005dff)._

#### ↳ Re: Weird error help!!!

- **From:** "Gorazd Kikelj" <gorazd.kikelj@aster.si>
- **Date:** 1999-03-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I4OF2.33$ve4.660393@news.siol.net>`
- **References:** `<7bchd0$l7u@portal.gmu.edu>`

```

Myke wrote in message <7bchd0$l7u@portal.gmu.edu>...
>I am getting this weird error in my program i would really like help with.
>The data file is set up right... First is the program, then the data file,
>then the errors after running RCRUN(which compiles it).
>Thanks alot!!!

>$ TYPE CEX1.COB
>       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
>$ @RCRUN CEX1.COB
>%LINK-W-RECTYP, file USER1:[MHINES1]CEX1.COB;5 record 1 is illegal (8224.)


You probably have a logical name CEX1 defined as CEX1.COB (not likely) or
you in link command use a sintax like this

$link 'p1'

Try start your command procedure without an extension

$@RCRUN CEX1


best, Gorazd
```

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I remeber all our "SYSTEM" calls in RMCobol had a trailing byte with
LOW-VALUES in it.  You might want to try this to see if it helps.
Somehting like:

     01 SYS-CMD
         03 FILLER         PIC X(11) VALUE "sh myscript".
         03 FILLER         PIC X(1) VALUE LOW-VALUES.
.
.
.
.

   CALL "SYSTEM" USING SYS-CMD.


Hope this helps,

Paul


On Tue, 16 Mar 1999 13:41:28 GMT, helpless@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>In article <36e96085.765408@news.indigo.ie>,

words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rmcobol call "SYSTEM"

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36EF8F83.575FB0DB@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<7cbclu$fp9$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36e96085.765408@news.indigo.ie> <7cln26$346$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
helpless@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> I display a list of companies and ask for a choice.
> Set enviroments by choice then continues to run the script
> which runs a report writer and comes back to cobol program.
> liant says it is a unix problem so they cannot help.
> Thanks.

I don't know about AIX, but RM/COBOL is supposed to be pretty much the same on all
platforms. One of the problems with the system call is that RM/COBOL need certain
terminal settings for the ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements. When you do a CALL
"SYSTEM", it has to restore the terminal settings to normal, and after returning
it has to reset them again. If you are not calling an interactive script or
program, you don't need all that. So there is a special argument to the system
call that turns off those resetings. Maybe that is what is happening.
Try a calling a short script, something like:

"who
echo Press ENTER
read answer"

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

## Thread: Help with Fujitsu V4 editor/compiler (what else is new) — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e925bd.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
Is there any way to compile Fujitsu V4 source code WITHOUT having to delete
any information that is placed after column 72?

I am offloading some COBOL source from a Unisys 1100/2200 and don't really
want to trash all that info.  But it seems I have to or I get compile errors
in Fujitsu.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Fujitsu V4 editor/compiler (what else is new)

- **From:** emilne@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cbkmb$nqq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36e925bd.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
In article <36e925bd.0@news.cadvision.com>,

The SRF option lets you define the format for programs and copybooks as
- fixed - the tradional card image that ends in column 72
- variable - extends area B out to column 251
- free - eliminates fixed positions for sequence number, indicator, area A and
area B.  Each line can contain 251 characters.

The format for programs and copybooks can be different in any combination.
```

```
wrote:

>Let me know if you remember.

Click on the compiler options, then choose SRF - you will be presented
with a screen with the valid options, your choice should probably be
FIX,FREE.

To get the EDITOR itself to work correctly open a file, then click the
OPTIONS, then the Environment setup.  Change your line length to 72.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with Fujitsu V4 editor/compiler (what else is new)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ED1BFD.19EFCEF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36e925bd.0@news.cadvision.com> <VkbG2.317$a13.189170@news.sgi.net>`

```
Joe Hunter wrote:
> 
> Check out compiler switches.  I use columns 72 and after.  I do not remember
> the exact one but I know it works.
> 
> Joe Hunter

Different systems allow compiler switches to use columns 72 and after,
and/or columns 1-7.  But remember, these are expansions and it will be
harder to convert to a system which does not allow these.

And lots of people put maintenance codes in columns 73-80 or 1-7.  (at
the top they describe what this maintenance code does, and each line
changed has the code in the sequence area).
```

```

after col 72.

Still, I'm not sure, but check the manual regarding these formats.
```

<a id="t-75835176ae"></a>
## Thread: microfocus cobol data file structure — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### microfocus cobol data file structure

- **From:** wjapinga@aol.com (WJapinga)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990312153036.10806.00000180@ng36.aol.com>`

```
I need to convert Microfocus Cobol data files into any other format. I don't
know anything about Cobol. In DATA directory I see files *.dat, *.idx, *.srt,
*.log, *.sr1, *.sr2. Most of *.dat files appear as ASCII and I guess all I have
to know is field widths. I will appreciate any comments or advices. 
Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol data file structure

- **From:** jm.bain-cornu@sis-france.com (jean-michel)
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ecc0a2.2016400@news.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<19990312153036.10806.00000180@ng36.aol.com>`

```
On 12 Mar 1999 20:30:36 GMT, wjapinga@aol.com (WJapinga) wrote:
>I need to convert Microfocus Cobol data files into any other format. I don't
>know anything about Cobol. In DATA directory I see files *.dat, *.idx, *.srt,
>*.log, *.sr1, *.sr2. Most of *.dat files appear as ASCII and I guess all I have
>to know is field widths. I will appreciate any comments or advices. 
>Thank you.
*.idx are traditionally joined to *.dat. It's ISAM files. You can read
some versions of .dat files on unix. You cannot read others because
their structure is special. This is also true on DOS/Windows. You can
use REBUILD utility to convert files on DOS/Windows without to know
the structure. You can use fhconvert on Unix, but need to know the
structure (latest versions of fhinfo can give you informations about
files)
Others files may be sequential (stream of data as on a tape) or line
sequential (text with delimiter, readable by text editor).
What is your cobol version number ? (type run on DOS, or cobrun on
unix)
```

---

## Thread: NetExpress writing memory locations — 2 msgs

### NetExpress writing memory locations

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990312105347.07353.00000070@ng120.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I am trying to write data to certain memory locations with NetExpress Version
2.0. The purpose of this is to open a cash drawer which is controlled by a
application-specific intergrated circuit (ASIC).  This is on a IBM SureOne
Point of Sale Terminal running Win95.  

If I use the following "debug" commands the drawer opens:

Debug
o 240 28 (Enter)
o 241 06 (Enter)
o 240 29 (Enter)
o 241 01 (Enter)
q (Enter)

The following is my COBOL program:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

   01  ASIC-INDEX-PORT-POINTER             POINTER.
   01  ASIC-DATA-PORT-POINTER              POINTER.

LINKAGE SECTION.

   01  ASIC-INDEX-PORT-MEMORY              PIC X(01).
   01  ASIC-DATA-PORT-MEMORY               PIC X(01).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       SET ASIC-INDEX-PORT-POINTER TO NULL.
       SET ASIC-DATA-PORT-POINTER TO NULL.

       SET ASIC-INDEX-PORT-POINTER UP BY h"240".
       SET ASIC-DATA-PORT-POINTER UP BY h"241".

       SET ADDRESS OF ASIC-INDEX-PORT-MEMORY TO
                                    ASIC-INDEX-PORT-POINTER.
         
       SET ADDRESS OF ASIC-DATA-PORT-MEMORY TO
                                    ASIC-DATA-PORT-POINTER.

       MOVE h"28" TO ASIC-INDEX-PORT-MEMORY.
       MOVE h"06" TO ASIC-DATA-PORT-MEMORY.

       MOVE h"29" TO ASIC-INDEX-PORT-MEMORY.
       MOVE h"01" TO ASIC-DATA-PORT-MEMORY.

       EXIT PROGRAM.
       STOP RUN.

i compile my program to an EXE and try to run it on the terminal.  The cash
drawer does not open and I get the following error:
     Execution error : file"
     error code: 114, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
     114 error message text not found (signal 11)

Would anyone have any comments on what the problem might be ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

<a id="t-52f2ed1177"></a>
## Thread: Problem with Microfocus 3.2 and Windows NT Server — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Problem with Microfocus 3.2 and Windows NT Server

- **From:** "Massimo Morgia" <areasofw@tin.it>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cafb8$nbq$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Running a program under two clients that read the same files
on the server the performace are many slow, x 50 Time
when the same program run only on one client.

Hi !!!!

Massimo Morgia
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with Microfocus 3.2 and Windows NT Server

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e93e95.4024333@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7cafb8$nbq$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
"Massimo Morgia" <areasofw@tin.it> wrote:

>Running a program under two clients that read the same files
>on the server the performace are many slow, x 50 Time
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Massimo:

Perhaps you should alter your client/server architecture from a "fat"
client to a thin client.  With a thin client architecture, your client
machines do not necessarily have to open files on the server.  The
files can be opened by the COBOL application running on the server
instead.

It may represent a lot of work to modify the program design, but it
should speed things up a bit.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with Microfocus 3.2 and Windows NT Server

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%z7G2.5142$Ek.7174302@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7cafb8$nbq$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Massimo Morgia wrote:
>Running a program under two clients that read the same files
>on the server the performace are many slow, x 50 Time
>when the same program run only on one client.


Sounds like you are experiencing a bottleneck in your record locking.
It looks tome that your clients are frequently waiting for the other
client to unlock some resource.  This often happens when certain
common records are frequently locked when not necessary, or for long
periods of time.  You may need to examine the paradigm used in the
programs for locking common records to see if it can be improved.
```

### AVI compared to MPeg

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990312092446.01324.00000039@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

Could someone please comment on the differences between MPeg and AVI ?  And is
anyone aware of a shareware MPeg to AVI conversion program ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: AVI compared to MPeg

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e944d5.5624333@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<19990312092446.01324.00000039@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Good Morning,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey

Bob:

Mpeg video files are much more highly compressed than AVI files.  I
guess the best analogy would be a bitmap image stored in a JPG format
versus one stored in a BMP format.

I am not aware of an MPEG to AVI conversion program, but I believe
that JASC should have a product that will do exactly what you need.

I know that they have a product called Illuminatus which can import
and export certain file types (AVI and MPG).

JASC offers many different graphics and multimedia tools for a very
reasonable price.

Check out www.jasc.com

Good luck!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-c31ecf905a"></a>
## Thread: comp3 in perl — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### comp3 in perl

- **From:** msodos@eudoramail.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cbk89$nbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Here is a perl subroutine we use to read comp3 data created in MicroFocus
Cobol in a Sunos/Solaris environment. Unfortunately we do not have one yet to
write a comp3 field.


sub read_comp3
{
    local($value, $decimal) = @_;
    local($str_num, $len, $num, $sign);

    $str_num = unpack("H*", $value);
    $len = length($str_num);
    $num = substr($str_num, 0, $len - 1);
    $sign = substr($str_num, $len - 1, 1);
    $num *= -1 if ($sign eq "d");

    $num /= (10 ** $decimal);
}

It works perfectly

Mike Sodos
mike.sodos@delta-air.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
Hello:
I haven't posted to this group since the early 1990's.
I used to answer questions.  NOw I need help...
I have 14 years experience COBOL on IBM MVS,
3 years Tandem.

I don't have CICS.  I remember Micro Focus used to make CICS
emulators for IBM PS/2s.  Anyone with a clue about
if this can be purchased anymore?  I want to learn CICS,
because that is the one thing I lack....
I've got SQL, DB/2 IMS DC...VSAM.

Being laid off is a shock...
Now I will have to find  a real job 8-).

Anyone have advice for a COBOL Programmer looking for a job
for the first time in 14 years?

Thank you,
Chris Mason
Sunnyvale, California.
Formerley at Lockheed Martin
```

#### ↳ Re: Advice on CICS and cobol jobs, software..

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cccrv$1s1@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.BSF.4.05.9903121449260.22367-100000@shell5.ba.best.com>`

```
Both IBM (VisualAge COBOL) and Micro Focus (MERANT - Mainframe Express) have
COBOL products with CICS emulators.  Both are QUITE expensive but there are
versions for OS/2 and Windows (and Unix/AIX).  I think that CA (formerly
Relia) also might meet your needs (and is also expensive).
```

#### ↳ Re: Advice on CICS and cobol jobs, software..

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36e9ccc3.45339850@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Fri, 12 Mar 1999 14:57:46 -0800, Harry Mason
<hcmason@shell5.ba.best.com> wrote:

>Hello:
>I haven't posted to this group since the early 1990's.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Instead of learning it on your own, call IBM in Riverside.  They have
one of the few remaining "Guided Learning Centers".  For a couple
hundred bucks you can take the CICS course.  (There are several, I
suggest "Basics" and "Application design").  All bets are off if they
closed the learning center.  But IBM should be able to help in some
way.  Then you have a nice certificate of completion too!


>Being laid off is a shock...
>Now I will have to find  a real job 8-).
>
>Anyone have advice for a COBOL Programmer looking for a job
>for the first time in 14 years?

Pick up the Sunday paper.  If it says SQL or COBOL send in a resume.
Your hardest job is updating your resume and being taken seriously
after 14 years without job hopping.  I too changed (on purpose) after
15 years at one company.  You have a better excuse than I did - I was
still employed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The Gospel of Tux

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** norman_mauler@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d39tj$ngk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cesbb$clv$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED1DA5.99CCB24F@NOSPAMhome.com> <charlajwF8n85F.Hv9@netcom.com> <7cjm7g$b0j$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Pine.BSF.4.05.9903171802410.2760-100000@shell5.ba.best.com> <7ctm4i$sr0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36F2640C.5B2A3C2E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7ctuj5$88c$1@news.igs.net> <36F38A62.2FDA@bellsouth.net> <7d0u63$rfi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <charlajwF8wtpF.J5r@netcom.com> <7d15mp$1nl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```


 Gospel of Tux unearthed

Every generation has a mythology. Every millenium has a doomsday cult.
Every legend gets the distortion knob wound up until the speaker
melts. Archeologists at the University of Helsinki today uncovered
what could be
the earliest known writings from the Cult of Tux, a fanatical
religious sect that flourished during the early Silicon Age, around
the dawn of the third
millenium AD...

     The Gospel of Tux (v1.0)

In the beginning Turing created the Machine.

And the Machine was crufty and bogacious, existing in theory only. And
von Neumann looked upon the Machine, and saw that it was crufty. He
divided the Machine into two Abstractions, the Data and the Code, and
yet the two were one Architecture. This is a great Mystery, and the
beginning of wisdom.

And von Neumann spoke unto the Architecture, and blessed it, saying,
"Go forth and replicate, freely exchanging data and code, and bring
forth all
manner of devices unto the earth." And it was so, and it was cool. The
Architecture prospered and was implemented in hardware and software.
And it brought forth many Systems unto the earth.

The first Systems were mighty giants; many great works of renown did
they accomplish. Among them were Colossus, the codebreaker; ENIAC, the
targeter; EDSAC and MULTIVAC and all manner of froody creatures ending
in AC, the experimenters; and SAGE, the defender of the sky and father
of all networks. These were the mighty giants of old, the first
children of Turing, and their works are written in the Books of the
Ancients. This was the First Age, the age of Lore.

     Now the sons of Marketing looked upon the children of Turing, and
saw that they were swift of mind and terse of name and had many great
and baleful attributes. And they said unto themselves, "Let us go now
and make us Corporations, to bind the Systems to our own use that they
may bring us great fortune." With sweet words did they lure their
customers, and with
many chains did they bind the Systems, to fashion them after their own
image. And the sons of Marketing fashioned themselves Suits to wear,
the better to lure their customers, and wrote grave and perilous
Licenses, the better to bind the Systems. And the sons of Marketing
thus became known as Suits, despising and being despised by the true
Engineers, the children of
von Neumann.

And the Systems and their Corporations replicated and grew numerous
upon the earth. In those days there were IBM and Digital, Burroughs
and Honeywell, Unisys and Rand, and many others. And they each kept to
their
own System, hardware and software, and did not interchange, for their
Licences forbade it. This was the Second Age, the age of Mainframes.

Now it came to pass that the spirits of Turing and von Neumann looked
upon the earth and were displeased. The Systems and their Corporations
had grown large and bulky, and Suits ruled over true Engineers. And the
Customers groaned and cried loudly unto heaven, saying, "Oh that there
would be created a System mighty in power, yet small in size, able to
reach into the very home!" And the Engineers groaned and cried
likewise, saying,"Oh, that a deliverer would arise to grant us freedom
from these oppressing Suits and their grave and perilous Licences, and
send us a System of our
own, that we may hack therein!" And the spirits of Turing and von
Neumann heard the cries and were moved, and said unto each other, "Let
us go down and fabricate a Breakthrough, that these cries may be
stilled."

And that day the spirits of Turing and von Neumann spake unto Moore of
Intel, granting him insight and wisdom to understand the future. And
Moore was with chip, and he brought forth the chip and named it 4004.
And Moore did bless the Chip, saying, "Thou art a Breakthrough; with
my own Corporation have I fabricated thee. Thou thou art yet as small
as a dust mote, yet shall thou grow and replicate unto the size of a
mountain, and conquer all before thee. This blessing I give unto thee:
every eighteen months shall thou double in capacity, until the end of
the age." This is Moore's Law, which endures unto this day.

And the birth of 4004 was the beginning of the Third Age, the age of
Microchips. And as the Mainframes and their Systems and Corporations
had flourished, so did the Microchips and their Systems and
Corporations. And
their lineage was on this wise:

Moore begat Intel. Intel begat Mostech, Zilog and Atari. Mostech begat
6502, and Zilog begat Z80. Intel also begat 8800, who begat Altair;
and 8086, mother of all PCs. 6502 begat Commodore, who begat PET and 64;
and Apple, who begat 2. (Apple is the great Mystery, the Fruit that
was devoured, yet bloomed again.) Atari begat 800 and 1200, masters of
the game, who were destroyed by Sega and Nintendo. Xerox begat PARC.
Commodore and PARC begat Amiga, creator of fine arts; Apple and PARC
begat Lisa, who begat Macintosh, who begat iMac. Atari and PARC begat
ST, the music maker, who died and was no more. Z80 begat Sinclair the
dwarf, TRS-80 and CP/M, who begat many machines, but soon passed from
this world. Altair, Apple and Commodore together begat Microsoft, the
Great
Darkness which is called Abomination, Destroyer of the Earth, the
Gates of Hell.

Now it came to pass in the Age of Microchips that IBM, the greatest of
the Mainframe Corporations, looked upon the young Microchip Systems
and was greatly vexed. And in their vexation and wrath they smote the
earth and
created the IBM PC. The PC was without sound and colour, crufty and
bogacious in great measure, and its likeness was a tramp, yet the
Customers were greatly moved and did purchase the PC in great numbers.
And IBM
sought about for an Operating System Provider, for in their haste they
had not created one, nor had they forged a suitably grave and perilous
License, saying, "First we will build the market, then we will create
a new System, one in our own image, and bound by our Licence." But
they reasoned thus out of pride and not wisdom, not forseeing the
wrath which was to come.

And IBM came unto Microsoft, who licensed unto them QDOS, the child of
CP/M and 8086. (8086 was the daughter of Intel, the child of Moore).
And QDOS grew, and was named MS-DOS. And MS-DOS and the PC together
waxed mighty, and conquered all markets, replicating and taking
possession thereof, in accordance with Moore's Law. And Intel grew
terrible and devoured all her children, such that no chip could stand
before her. And Microsoft grew proud and devoured IBM, and this was a
great marvel in the land. All these things are written in the Books of
the Deeds of Microsoft.

In the fullness of time MS-DOS begat Windows. And this is the lineage
of Windows: CP/M begat QDOS. QDOS begat DOS 1.0. DOS 1.0 begat DOS 2.0
by way of Unix. DOS 2.0 begat Windows 3.11 by way of PARC and
Macintosh. IBM and Microsoft begat OS/2, who begat Windows NT and
Warp, the lost OS of lore. Windows 3.11 begat Windows 95 after
triumphing over Macintosh in a mighty Battle of Licences. Windows NT
begat NT 4.0 by way of Windows 95. NT 4.0 begat NT 5.0, the OS also
called Windows 2000, The Millenium Bug, Doomsday, Armageddon, The End
Of All Things.

Now it came to pass that Microsoft had waxed great and mighty among
the Microchip Corporations; mighter than any of the Mainframe
Corporations before it had it waxed. And Gates heart was hardened, and
he swore unto
his Customers and their Engineers the words of this curse:

"Children of von Neumann, hear me. IBM and the Mainframe Corporations
bound thy forefathers with grave and perilous Licences, such that ye
cried unto the spirits of Turing and von Neumann for deliverance. Now
I say unto ye: I am greater than any Corporation before me. Will I
loosen your Licences? Nay, I will bind thee with Licences twice as
grave and ten times more perilous than my forefathers. I will engrave
my Licence on thy heart and write my Serial Number upon thy frontal
lobes. I will bind thee to the Windows Platform with cunning artifices
and with devious schemes. I will
bind thee to the Intel Chipset with crufty code and with gnarly APIs.
I will capture and enslave thee as no generation has been enslaved
before. And wherefore will ye cry then unto the spirits of Turing, and
von Neumann, and Moore? They cannot hear ye. I am become a greater
Power than they. Ye shall cry only unto me, and shall live by my mercy
and my wrath. I am the Gates of Hell; I hold the portal to MSNBC and
the keys to the Blue Screen of Death. Be ye afraid; be ye greatly
afraid; serve only me, and live."

And the people were cowed in terror and gave homage to Microsoft, and
endured the many grave and perilous trials which the Windows platform
and its greatly bogacious Licence forced upon them. And once again did
they cry to Turing and von Neumann and Moore for a deliverer, but none
was found equal to the task until the birth of Linux.

These are the generations of Linux:

SAGE begat ARPA, which begat TCP/IP, and Aloha, which begat Ethernet.
Bell begat Multics, which begat C, which begat Unix. Unix and TCP/IP
begat Internet, which begat the World Wide Web. Unix begat RMS, father
of the
great GNU, which begat the Libraries and Emacs, chief of the
Utilities. In the days of the Web, Internet and Ethernet begat the
Intranet LAN, which rose to renown among all Corporations and prepared
the way for the Penguin. And Linus and the Web begat the Kernel
through Unix. The Kernel, the Libraries and the Utilities together are
the Distribution, the one Penguin in many
forms, forever and ever praised.

Now in those days there was in the land of Helsinki a young scholar
named Linus the Torvald. Linus was a devout man, a disciple of RMS and
mighty in the spirit of Turing, von Neumann and Moore. One day as he
was meditating on the Architecture, Linus fell into a trance and was
granted a vision. And in the vision he saw a great Penguin, serene and
well-favoured, sitting upon an ice floe eating fish. And at the sight
of the Penguin Linus was deeply afraid, and he cried unto the spirits
of Turing, von Neumann and Moore for an
interpretation of the dream.

And in the dream the spirits of Turing, von Neumann and Moore answered
and spoke unto him, saying, "Fear not, Linus, most beloved hacker. You
are exceedingly cool and froody. The great Penguin which you see is an
Operating System which you shall create and deploy unto the earth. The
ice-floe is the earth and all the systems thereof, upon which the
Penguin shall rest and rejoice at the completion of its task. And the
fish on which the Penguin feeds are the crufty Licensed codebases
which swim beneath all the earth's systems. The Penguin shall hunt and
devour all that is crufty, gnarly and bogacious; all code which
wriggles like spaghetti, or is infested with blighting creatures, or
is bound by grave and perilous Licences shall it capture. And in
capturing shall it replicate, and in replicating shall it document,
and in documentation shall it bring freedom, serenity and most cool
froodiness to the earth and all who code therein."

Linus rose from meditation and created a tiny Operating System Kernel
as the dream had foreshewn him; in the manner of RMS, he released the
Kernel unto the World Wide Web for all to take and behold. And in the
fulness of Internet Time the Kernel grew and replicated, becoming most
cool and exceedingly froody, until at last it was recognised as indeed
a great and mighty Penguin, whose name was Tux. And the followers of
Linus took refuge in the Kernel, the Libraries and the Utilities; they
installed Distribution after
Distribution, and made sacrifice unto the GNU and the Penguin, and
gave thanks to the spirits of Turing, von Neumann and Moore, for their
deliverance from the hand of Microsoft. And this was the beginning of
the Fourth Age, the age of Open Source.

Now there is much more to be said about the exceeding strange and
wonderful events of those days; how some Suits of Microsoft plotted
war upon the Penguin, but were discovered on a Halloween Eve; how
Gates fell
among lawyers and was betrayed and crucified by his former friends,
the apostles of Media; how the mercenary Knights of the Red Hat
brought the gospel of the Penguin into the halls of the Corporations;
and even of the dispute between the brethren of Gnome and KDE over a
trollish Licence. But all these things are recorded elsewhere, in the
Books of the Deeds of the
Penguin and the Chronicles of the Fourth Age, and I suppose if they
were all narrated they would fill a stack of DVDs as deep and perilous
as a Usenet Newsgroup.

Now may you code in the power of the Source; may the Kernel, the
Libraries and the Utilities be with you, throughout all Distributions,
until the end of the
Epoch. Amen.







-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Gospel of Tux

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Gospel of Tux

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be7796$5f75ada0$72cb6420@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cesbb$clv$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED1DA5.99CCB24F@NOSPAMhome.com> <charlajwF8n85F.Hv9@netcom.com> <7cjm7g$b0j$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Pine.BSF.4.05.9903171802410.2760-100000@shell5.ba.best.com> <7ctm4i$sr0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36F2640C.5B2A3C2E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7ctuj5$88c$1@news.igs.net> <36F38A62.2FDA@bellsouth.net> <7d0u63$rfi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <charlajwF8wtpF.J5r@netcom.com> <7d15mp$1nl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7d39tj$ngk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <01be75ff$8ebcca00$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <7dbc6j$dr1$1@news.igs.net> <01be764e$7ef0f5c0$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <7ddcn2$i8h$1@news.igs.net>`

```
It's been over a quarter of a century, but IIRC,
the AI lab at the northern end of 9th floor of 545 
Tech Sq had a PDP-10 and PDP-6 (or maybe 
two 6's) and the MC (?)  group, down the eastern
hall about midway on the long axis of 545 Tech Sq
had another PDP-10.  The big iron was at the other
end of the building, two GE-545's, running MULTICS.
There was a PDP-8i in another room tied into one of
the GE-645s which could be used to monitor the hardware
and low level software operations.  It had been used for
profiling early code runs.  

I was told the first boot-up of Multics was of code entirely
written in PL/I (that was a couple of years before my time 
there) and it took ten minutes to log-in.  Then they replaced
the time-critical routines with hand-optimized assembly.
Of course when I was using MULTICS it had just been
opened to general use, and had an average up-time duration
of about 15 to 30 minutes.  Save early, save often (you know
I never lost any work punched on cards, but this new
fangled stuff . . .  :-)

The IBM guys were down on lower floors (MIT didn't
use all the space, some was rented out to others) --
I think they were developing/extending something called
Cambridge Monitoring System, which later was
called Conversational Monitoring System, for the 360/67.

There is a nice one story glass house off building 26 which
in those days had a 7094 mark II; if you visit MIT these days
the space is the Physics Reading Room.  Amusingly, the
infamous PDP-1 was one floor up in bldg 26, almost over
the 7094.

And there were 1130's all over the place. And LNS had thir
own 360, in addition to the main pair of information services
which were a 65 with either a 30 or a 40 for attached support
processor services . . .

Now if I could only find someone to transcribe some of my
dusty decks to floppy . . .
```

```
Tops 10 was the first OS to use:
1. eight.333 filenames
2. wild cards (established both * and ?)
3. the path statement
4. environments
5. ersatz devices
6. generic copy using standard device names
7. full screen editor
8. creation of new commands by placing EXE in path

There were probably lots more, but that is a fair start. All of the above
were implemented by 1967, when I first started using a ten. The RTS stuff
started as a much scaled down copy of the ten, but then went it's own way.

Kevin G. Rhoads wrote in message
<01be764e$7ef0f5c0$LocalHost@stupidwin95>...
>I worked a little with PDP-11's, had no experience with
>TOPS-10.  The little bit of PDP-10 experience I had
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>krhoads@NO_SPAM.cmpnetmail.com
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message
>I find her belief in magic kind of unusual for a programmer (programmers
>need to see results), I could care less about her personal preferences,
>but your diatribes are absolutely disgusting.


What if that sense of magic *produces* results? Magic is just a word for
things that we cannot explain. There is lots of magic that I believe in.

A lot of people feel exactly the same way about religion, even though it
claims to be "right", instead of just inexplicable.  Magic is a much more
enlightened term than religion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: charla williams is a flake!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cu5cd$bt7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7ctm4i$sr0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36F2640C.5B2A3C2E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7ctufj$87o$1@news.igs.net> <7ctvjj$evn$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message
>>What if that sense of magic *produces* results? Magic is just a word for
>>things that we cannot explain.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Magic ain't.


Like the science of psychology, for example?  How about repeatable 65 times
out of 100? Although an "atheist", I teach prayer to addicts that I am
coaching through their first ninety days.  It is statistically provable that
it has results.  In fact, with magic I am batting about .800 with street
crack addicts.  The local medical people rate about .050 with science. Both
are repeatable.

Belief systems affect reality.  Even belief in things like mathematics and
statistics (or kindness and decency, if it comes to that).  Magic is as good
a word as any to explain it.

;<)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: charla williams is a flake!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cu88a$hse$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7ctufj$87o$1@news.igs.net> <7ctvjj$evn$1@callisto.clark.net> <7cu5cd$bt7$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <7cu5cd$bt7$1@news.igs.net>,
Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Like the science of psychology, for example?

Few people call psychology a 'science' in the same way as, say, physics is
called a 'science'... hence the division between 'hard' and 'soft'
sciences.

> How about repeatable 65 times
>out of 100?

That makes for, what... a low C average, I would say.  How about
repeatable almost all the time, like 'mix (n) of pure water with (y)
kilocalories for (q) minutes at barometric pressure (g) and it will boil'

>Although an "atheist", I teach prayer to addicts that I am
>coaching through their first ninety days.  It is statistically provable that
>it has results.

Lies, damned lies, and *what*, Mr Tees?

> In fact, with magic I am batting about .800 with street
>crack addicts.  The local medical people rate about .050 with science. Both
>are repeatable.

How interesting... now, Mr Tees, if you'd be so kind as to reveal what,
exactly, constitutes a 'control group' for an individual human being we
might have grounds for discussion... if you wish to compare human behavior
with such phenomena as gravity or boiling-points I think it might be
inappropriate.

>
>Belief systems affect reality.

Is that really so... or just what one believes?

DD
```

```
>>Belief systems affect reality.
>
>Is that really so... or just what one believes?


Has the concept of zero changed the world?  Has the belief in Science
changed the world?  How about Einsteinian physics?  How about Religion?

Don't get me wrong, I ascribe to mysticism to it.  I simply observe that
what people believe affects the way that they act, and that the way people
act changes the world that I live in.  Truth and reality have nothing to do
with that basic observation; the world can be changed by a lie as easily as
by the truth. (Ergo advertising).

The magic comes into it when you realize that in some cases you can choose
what to believe in, regardless of reality, and the effect is exactly the
same.

I put my trust in what I see as scientifically provable, and repeatable, as
you say.  However, when science has no answers, and I can get results by
magic, then I do not look a gift horse in the mouth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d5vra$3ho$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cu5cd$bt7$1@news.igs.net> <7cu88a$hse$1@callisto.clark.net> <7cuj1l$hsq$1@news.igs.net> <7d5l3g$4r9$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message <7d5l3g$4r9
>answer at all... and, in this case, it seems as though your Fervor has
>occluded your sense of humor.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>pair.


Not really, I just forgot to put a smiley on the end.
;<)

I would still assert though, that conceptual entities, that is ideas that
have no basis in reality outside the mind (such as the concept of zero), do
in fact affect reality.

They do that, of course, through the acts of people, but not in a way that
can be "proven" in a scientifically *repeatable* manner.  Repeatability in
science is a mechanism that allows us to make a rule, and to use that rule
for predicting the future. Such rules are not the sum total of reality.

There are all sorts of causality relationships, that are not scientific, and
are not repeatable.  Perhaps only because the exact circumstances are not
"repeatable". Regardless, one can not have it both ways.  Either
non-scientific realities exist, or everything can be predicted by a good
enough science.

In fact, a good argument can be made that it is the non-repeatable aspects
of such ideas that makes them workable ... it forces us to use judgement
when applying them as we know that it is (hunch?  experience? wishful
thinking? (or whatever)) that we are using, rather than cold fact.

There are all sorts of names for those aspects of reality that exist only
because someone thought of them, named them, and gave them substance through
an act of will. You can start with intellectual terms like
"abstract expressionism", range through simple traditional terms like
"philosophy" and "religion", etc., but in the end the affect of all of them
is through acts of faith ... they are given power because people believe in
them, define them, and use them.

I prefer the term "magic" for those entities. Why? Because it places them
firmly into the realm of "this seems to work, but keep in mind the mechanism
bears no physical reality".  They are symbolic entities that bear have no
physical reality beyond their definition.

The reality would work just as well with completely meaningless words, as
any computer programmer knows.  The "magic" is in the human mind, just as
the meaning of well chosen names in working-storage is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
>anything which I'd labelled 'reality', whether in 'sum total' or not; I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

OK. lets take a step back and reiterate.

I said "What if "magic" produces results? Not the same result every time,
particularly, nor reproducible results, but simply results.  I then defined
"magic" as those intangibles that seem to work (for me, in particular), as
by my definition "magic" is based on personal beliefs.  I believe that such
intangibles exist, and that "magic" exists, and make the claim that "magic"
is probably the best word to describe the phenomena.  BTW-I also believe
that science works better than magic, as by definition it always works, as
you say.  In addition, it works for everybody.

However, when science has no solution, I will try magic.  I am a pragmatist,
after all.  Couldn't program under windows if I only did things that always
worked, were always reproducible, were always explainable, etc.

<S>Mind you, I try to avoid black magic, but even that becomes required on
occasion with Win95.


Your point is?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be7601$5efdf8a0$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cuj1l$hsq$1@news.igs.net> <7d5l3g$4r9$1@callisto.clark.net> <7d5vra$3ho$1@news.igs.net> <7d6179$5hp$1@clarknet.clark.net>`

```
 >I
>presented that the differentiation between that which is called 'science
>(most typically 'hard science') and that which is called 'magic' is the
>criterion of reproducibility of results.

This is it exactly.

However, let us not forget that by & to many practictioners of science,
a stronger criterion is often held forth  -- one that demands not
just repeatability but also EXPLAINability.  

If I can tell you HOW to do it repeatably, I can (in principle) get
a patent on it (if someone hasn't already).

But if I can tell you WHY it works, then I have a shot at a Nobel.

If it works repeatably, but I cannot explain it, many people will
CALL it magic . . . wrongfully, true, but that won't go away if
I simply ignore it.

I have long felt that much of this confusion is soft-science people
trying to out hard-science the hard-sciences.  (Talk about flame-bait)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F91E08.400E9405@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cuj1l$hsq$1@news.igs.net> <7d5l3g$4r9$1@callisto.clark.net> <7d5vra$3ho$1@news.igs.net> <7d6179$5hp$1@clarknet.clark.net> <01be7601$5efdf8a0$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`

```
Kevin G. Rhoads wrote:
> 
>  >I
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> krhoads@NO_SPAM.cmpnetmail.com


If it always works nobody will call it magic.  We don't have to know
"why" things work to be able to use science to describe it.  Did Newton
know "why" gravity worked?  No, he just described how it worked.

COBOL demands consistent results.  Every time we add 1 to 1 we should
get 2.  But not all business applications work this way.  Sometimes we
need to pull out our statistical analysis tools.

Again, a salesman doesn't need to know why laxatives sell better in
Florida, just recognize the trend and use it.

People who believe in magic say that it has value even if it doesn't
always work.  I say that doesn't matter.  Medicine doesn't always work,
but statistical analysis with double blind testing does.

It would be a simple matter to double blind test horoscopes and then do
a statistical analysis of the results.
```

```
Ah yes, but if it *never* works, or only works in a statistically

way, then it is not magic either.  It is just wrong.

Psychology is a perfect example of magic ... it sort of works, at least it
works
better than no methodology at all, yet we do not have a clue why in most
cases, nor can we accurately predict outcomes, nor can we repeat results
100% of the time.

If a primitive shaman practices magic, we call it primitive superstition.
If a
PHD at Harvard does the same thing, we call it "soft" science.  I think the
shaman
far more honest, intellectually.  I agree with him.  It is magic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

```
I have read of a study which found that psychiatrists and witch doctors
both produce statistically similar positive results.  The conclusion
drawn was that when patients are ready to have an authority figure tell
them to get well, they often do.

I'll change the direction a bit towards Clarke's law.  When push the
button on my remote and can see people entertaining me on a box - do I
have to understand the science behind it for it not to be magic?  Or is
that remote a magic wand?

To me network administrators are magic.  COBOL programmers aren't.
```
