>I have read of a study which found that psychiatrists and witch doctors
>both produce statistically similar positive results.  The conclusion
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>To me network administrators are magic.  COBOL programmers aren't.

My point exactly.  Let me use another example.  Most people would consider
mental telepathy "magic".  Yet I will predict it as a reality within fifty
to one hundred years.  All it would take is a cellular phone small enough to
be implanted, a computer chip big enough to dial and know the numbers of a
few million people, and some sort of "mumble interface".  It is almost
possible now.  Of course, as soon as it is a reality, people will claim it
is not magic anymore.

To a wizard, there is no magic, simply learning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be764e$43d1c460$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cuj1l$hsq$1@news.igs.net> <7d5l3g$4r9$1@callisto.clark.net> <7d5vra$3ho$1@news.igs.net> <7d6179$5hp$1@clarknet.clark.net> <01be7601$5efdf8a0$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <36F91E08.400E9405@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>If it always works nobody will call it magic.

Oh? Really?

How about the reverse transcription of RNA patterning to
DNA -- there was considerable evidence -- repeatable
(and repeated!) experiments -- demonstrating just that --
but many bio- and med- people said it wasn't so  ---
until reverse transcriptase was discovered.  Then, of
course, it "could" happen.

I agree that "if it always works, it isn't magic" BUT many
people (incorrectly) hold that if it doesn't have an explanation
then it is magic.  Many technical professional have demonstrated
this attitude, even though they should know better.

I don't like it, but I've seen it happen often.  Ignoring what I've
seen occur because I don't like it would be believing in a form
of magic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: magic

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ddefu$j6h$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7ceeti$1jq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7cuj1l$hsq$1@news.igs.net> <7d5l3g$4r9$1@callisto.clark.net> <7d5vra$3ho$1@news.igs.net> <7d6179$5hp$1@clarknet.clark.net> <01be7601$5efdf8a0$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <36F91E08.400E9405@NOSPAMhome.com> <01be764e$43d1c460$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`

```
Kevin G. Rhoads wrote in message

>I agree that "if it always works, it isn't magic" BUT many
>people (incorrectly) hold that if it doesn't have an explanation
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>of magic.


I always think of it as incomplete, or perhaps too complex to fully
understand "rational" thinking.

I remember a mentor I had back in the 70's.  Ted was a mining engineer, and
chief executive officer of one of Canada's largest gold mines.  He was
responsible for the selling of about 3 million ounces of gold per year on
the futures market.

Ted was a chartist, and had three PHD mathy's on his staff that used a
DEC-system-10 eight hours per day to analyze trends.  He was a legend in the
gold markets, and had an uncanny ability to forecast gold prices.  One day I
asked him to explain how he interpreted those thousands of charts that
covered his walls/desk/floor etc.

He said "Oh, there is no way to interpret them.  None of them work".

I said "then why spend all that money, and do all that work?"

He said "Well, it gives me a methodology to examine all the data.  Once I do
that, then I follow my gut feel. It is all just magic".

Smart man.
```

## Thread: Flow Charts — 28 msgs

### RE: Flow Charts

- **From:** SS <sstrozza@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```

   I was just curious about something and thought I would throw it
out...

   Does anyone here (or HAS anyone EVER) actually written flow charts
when designing/developing
   new applications?  Does anyone work for a company that requires flow
charts as part of documentation
   for projects/systems? I have created system flow charts using Visio
for complex systems (more for my
   own use so I would be able to understand how the system worked) but I
would think they would help immensely
   when learning/maintaining complex applications. Having worked for
quite a few companies, I found it 
   interesting to note that none of them had any flow charts for their
systems nor did any of their programmers
   even consider developing any. In fact, they all looked at me quite
strangeley when I mentioned this.

   Sherry
```

#### ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "JC" <john@jeclark.globalnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cegc4$jgi$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```
I've worked as a contractor for quite a few companies in the UK, all of
which required the construction of flowcharts for the initial design of the
programs....

It's what happened afterwards that was interesting, most of them suddenly
found good reasons for dropping the charts as...'the programmer can
determine the structure of the program from the source code'.

There are some packages around that draw program structures..McCabe Toolkit
springs immediately to mind. I downloaded something called Legacy from
www.shareware.com a few days ago, but haven't looked at it yet...

John

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "Peter Midgley" <midgley@midgleypr.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cejl3$se$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <7cegc4$jgi$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <36EAF805.5D4C44F@yahoo.com>`

```
I worked as a contractor at AA Insurance for a couple of years and found
that they had a policy of designing/documenting programs with flowcharts.  I
found this very hard, having been used to designing and writing
simultaneously, to be forced to draw all these boxes and allocate jsp
actions to them.  In favour, it certainly made me try to decide up front
exactly what was to happen, and the overall logical structure, but against,
I found myself feeling that while I was doing all that, I could be writing
the actual program.  After all, who needs to know what the program is doing
IN MINUTE DETAIL apart from a programmer who should be able to see it in the
code (assumes meaningful data names, not too many strange GO TOs etc), in a
form which is 'language independent'?. To answer my own question, the
'analyst' (where an installation splits the function of analyst and
programmer.  Conclusion: I guess it's horses for courses, depending on where
you are working.  I find that it also depends on the complexity of the
program.  For example a simple file-read, update or print is pretty easy to
understand however it's documented (ergo why bother with boxes) , whereas a
complex program such as a till program is pretty hard to take in, once you
start moving beyond the simplistic 'initialisation, main-process,
termination type of documentation.
As they say, HTH!!
--Pete Midgley

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F95261.29EDD116@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <7cegc4$jgi$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <36EAF805.5D4C44F@yahoo.com>`

>    I guess the general consensus is that flow charting is a waste of
> time..lol.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    in a short period of time! [been there - done that]

>   Sherry

So flow charts are useful for maintenance.  That means that as soon as
you get done writing a program or maintaining a program, the flow chart
needs to be updated.

I have done this with system flow charts, and it works quite well.  But
I've never seen a program flow chart maintained.
```

#### ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36eadb5b.114622449@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```
On Sat, 13 Mar 1999 14:14:44 -0800, SS <sstrozza@yahoo.com> wrote:

>
>   I was just curious about something and thought I would throw it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   new applications?  

I can create a flow chart.  It does nothing for me as far as helping
design.  I just don't do them.  I DO Pseudocode, however.  I might
spec out a whole program (in comments) and then code around the
comments.  

I did do one flowchart for a process I designed where I worked before
- AFTER the program was written, only to help those who later worked
in the program to understand it.

Flow charts do not work for Event Driven or OO programs.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "S R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00

>I did do one flowchart for a process I designed where I worked before
>- AFTER the program was written, only to help those who later worked

>
>Flow charts do not work for Event Driven or OO programs.


I agree with you that flowcharts do not help in developing complex
solutions.  I am more in favour with PseudoCode, and structure charts.  I am
currently developing a very complex system for a company in OO, and i have
to produce some form of logical structure anaysis to show employers, which
in your opinion would be the best method,when dealing with OO logic?

SrHart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7crl28$28q$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <36eadb5b.114622449@news1.ibm.net> <921440452.26247.2.nnrp-08.c1ed9803@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

S R Hart wrote in message
<921440452.26247.2.nnrp-08.c1ed9803@news.demon.co.uk>...
>>I did do one flowchart for a process I designed where I worked before
>>- AFTER the program was written, only to help those who later worked
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>solutions.  I am more in favour with PseudoCode, and structure charts.  I
am
>currently developing a very complex system for a company in OO, and i have
>to produce some form of logical structure anaysis to show employers, which
>in your opinion would be the best method,when dealing with OO logic?
>
There is no *best* method for OO or any other logic. There are
methods that seem to work well for describing different portions
of a system. Use whatever tools you have at your disposal.

Here are some suggestions:

Data Structure Diagrams: Used to show system wide data and
to show the relationship between data entities. When used within
a network model to describe data, the resulting diagram can be
used to distinguish classes, or to identify the various records
(entities) that exist within a network, heirarchical, or relational
data base; or the records for indexed sequential files.
(I discovered this, on my own, after finding other methods to be
unsatisfactory.)

Warnier-Orr Diagrams: Used to show the temporal structure of
an application; in particular, the sequential processes and data
necessary for an application. It is derived from JSP and is used
with output-oriented design.

Data Flow Diagrams: Though originally developed to show the
flow of data through a complete system, they may still be useful
for describing the data flow within a sub-system.

State Transition Diagrams: Useful for describing the parsing of
inputs to a process. Simple parsing can be described in
psuedo-code or a flow chart; but for complex input, a diagram
may be significantly clearer.

Decision Tables: Useful for listing the actions required for
numerous or complex coditions. May translate directly to
EVALUATE.

Flow Charts: Most useful for describing a complex process with
numerous intermediate calculations or variables.

Psuedo-code: To describe any process for which there is not
another, more satisfactory means.

Finally, the "method du jour" for OO is UML. This is, yet, one more
attempt to create a comprehensive means for designing systems.
I have looked at it, played with it, but I don't like it. You might think
more highly of it. It is backed by many big name software vendors.
< www.rational.com >

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

#### ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cerfi$b8c@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```
For Sherry and others regarding flow charts.

Early on - 1960-65, flow charts were used extensively. One reason was that
between compiles, there was much down time to desk check code. What I'm
saying is that there were few tools to do otherwise.  At any rate, "we" had
conventions for charts that had a number system for nodes on the chart that
reflected page number as well as node number, and a node occurred between
each statement.  At one site, the program was "written" on a chart, handed
off to a teletype operator where it was typed and made into paper tape
before loaded on the computer for compiling.

One of the "utilities" that Grace's sailors did was a COBOL program to read
COBOL source, and draw a flow chart on the printer.  This helped to move the
process to after a compile instead of before a compile.  And it allowed this
form of documentation to stay current when program changes were made.  I
believe the move to structure code helped reduce the presumed need for the
charts, too.

Warren Simmons
w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net

SS wrote in message <36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>...
>
>   I was just curious about something and thought I would throw it
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   for projects/systems?
```

#### ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cgf9o$9bo$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```
Flow charts only work well for unstructured programs.  The flow chart for
structured code is always exactly the same. You do one thing, or you do a
list of things.  Then you have another list of flowcharts.

The technique has been obsolete for twenty-five years.

SS wrote in message <36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>...
>
>   I was just curious about something and thought I would throw it
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Sherry
```

What about taking this point one step further.....

Structured programming is based on merging the Input & Output Data
structures together with the list of Conditions & Operations to produce the
program structure.

How many organisations allow programmers the time to go back to the original
structures & modify them properly??

Then again, there's the age-old problem of Structure Clashes in report
programs.

Formal methodologies propose producing an intermediate file & then inverting
the printing module wrt the main processing module.  How many organisations
implement that??

John
(Getting back into COBOL after years of 4GL work)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cgnn8$e9i$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <7cgf9o$9bo$1@news.igs.net> <7cgfoj$3jn$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
JC wrote in message

>Structured programming is based on merging the Input & Output Data
>structures together with the list of Conditions & Operations to produce the
>program structure.


I do not think I agree with that. (I am also not sure I understand what you
are saying<S>).  Structured code is produced by taking a complex process,
and subdividing it into numerous less complex processes.  That process is
continued on with the sub-processes, until all processes are simplified to
the point that they are trivial to code.

Flow charting is designed to give a graphical representation to a complex
process, so that one can take it in at a glance.  If you have no processes
that complex, then what need for a flow chart, beyond that of a tool to do
the analysis?  The output of the design stage should be simplified to the
point that a flowchart is not needed.

That leaves another question, mind you.  Is there a graphical method for
representing a well designed top down structure?  The design is going to be
a tree-structure, which flow charts do not handle well.  However, you can
graphically represent a tree.  A tree view, with nodes representing
decisions might very well be a usefull tool, but it would not be a
traditional flowchart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> JC wrote in message
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> traditional flowchart.

What would such a graphic tell us?   A structured program can show us in
one screen, what it does - the main paragraph with a series of
PERFORMs.  If we want more detail, we page down to the PERFORM in
question and look at it.  Sure we could make a flow chart and print it
out, but do we gain any understanding which is easier to see than a well
written program?   And which is more trustworthy - the code itself, or
the translation of that code into a different medium?
```

#### ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cgk4j$c6m$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```

SS wrote in message

<Snippage asking about flowcharts>

After posting to your last message, I thought perhaps the answer was a bit
curt, and decided to write a bit more.

Flow charts are designed to map out program flow.  While that observation
may seem trite, it bears examination.  Top down methodology is designed to
eliminate, or trivialize flow control.  It does so by forcing the programmer
to design simple procedures with one entry point, and one exit point.  No
"GOTO"'s is only a rule to enforce that; when gotos are allowed, they are
present because the language forces it. The real rule of structure is
isolation of sub-modules for flow control.

Picture a flow chart where every single box contains one entry point, and
one exit point.  There are no triangles, representing GOTO statements, and
absolutely no arrows that do not procede directly to the box immediatey
below.  That is structured code. The flow chart is absolutely identical to
the list of paragraph names in the program.  The flow chart says nothing; it
documents nothing that is not documented better by well chosen procedure
names.

Flow charting may be a usefull pencil-type methodology for thinking about a
system at the design stage.  I often doodle with a pencil with little boxes
and arrows.  It may also be a useul method for figuring out a badly written
program. However, DESIGNING the code consists of eliminating all that
complexity, and organizing it into a structured.  Every arrow on the
flowchart has to be eliminated.  By the time you actually start coding,
design should be well beyond the flowchart stage.

In summary, flowcharts are not used because modern design technique renders
every flowchart trivial. Top down programming methodology is based on the
idea of dividing a project into thousands of tiny, absolutely trivial
flowcharts, each standing in isolation.  A non-trivial flowchart is a mark
of poorly designed code.
```

#### ↳ RE: Flow Charts

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)

seeing how you have received many response to your query let me put my 2 cents
in.  Flowcharts were required when I was attending classes (in the early 70's)
to show us how to document.  Since then pseudocode has since replaced
flowcharts as that portion of the required documentation. Data flow diagrams
and structure charts round complete the "package".   Hope this helps.

Tom Wymer
```

#### ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ECF05C.54BB1F06@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com>`

```
SS wrote:
> 
>    I was just curious about something and thought I would throw it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  company that requires flow charts as part of documentation

Yes I have defined a flowchart once.  It even seemed silly to do it
then.

> 
> for projects/systems? I have created system flow charts using Visio
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

For defining a system diagrams are great, for detail on a program they
are next to useless and go out of date almost instantly.  Have you ever
written a significant program from scratch and got it right first time?
Have you then gone back and updated the doco?

Pseudo code came in and went out fairly quickly.  It is not a bad idea
to code your program structure first but using a non-language just
confuses everyone.  Show pseudo code to a user one day and what them
fade out of the conversation.  Sorry Sherry...

Any large system needs a diagram to see how it interacts:

Data flow diagrams have been round a while and are very good at showing
the flow of data.  Getting these right can help your project structure,
it will help you minimize your interfaces.  It will help you to
construct a significant functional specification as your FS will follow
the diagrams:  define the top layer and how it works then drill a little
into each area, then drilling again as required.  To much detail (in
english) is a mistake I am still trying to avoid.

Entity relationship diagrams are great for seeing how the various parts
of a database interacts.  This tells a programmer how to get one piece
of information with another piece.  Required for any significant
database.  (Tip use access to draw the diagram it is simple and a lot of
PC's already come with it installed).

Use cases (from OO land) are great for collecting user requirements.  It
focuses the interviewer and interviewee on the system and the goals of
the system.  Worthwhile looking into this, great to talk to users
without glazing on their eyes.

Other object diagrams:  Has anyone tried these?  Probably not useful
until OO cobol is in use.  When Rational (or whoever) supplies cobol
generating code same as it does for C I might consider using OO cobol.

Thanks Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dJwbqi8MPHv5@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <36ECF05C.54BB1F06@zip.com.au>`

```
On Mon, 15 Mar 1999 11:34:52, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

> Pseudo code came in and went out fairly quickly.  It is not a bad idea
> to code your program structure first but using a non-language just
> confuses everyone.  Show pseudo code to a user one day and what them
> fade out of the conversation.  Sorry Sherry...

Ken,

I'm not sure about the others here, but when I say I "Pseudocode" I 
don't mean the "official recognized" Pseudocode syntax.  I mean 
something more like this:

* Open the input file
* read records until end
* Close the input file

Then I code around these comments.  The actual syntax of the 
pseudocode is unimportant - what matters is that by doing this you 
have thought through the process. 

As Donald does, the method I use involves breaking down each 
"operation" into smaller and smaller pieces conceptually, until the 
code to perform the operation becomes trivial.

Re: your comments on Data Flow Diagrams - I have only seen these in 
use in one shop - and it was great to have.  I wish I had TIME to do 
these more often.

-----------------------
Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ED4A55.FE44A296@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <36ECF05C.54BB1F06@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dJwbqi8MPHv5@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
> I'm not sure about the others here, but when I say I "Pseudocode" I
> don't mean the "official recognized" Pseudocode syntax.  I mean
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> have thought through the process.

And what I do is the following:

OPEN INPUT-FILE.
PERFORM 900-READ-RECORDS UNTIL EOF-READ.
CLOSE INPUT-FILE.

It's just as obvious that I've thought through the process, and if the
process changes, so will my statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flow Charts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-M713t3Doh6W6@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<36EAE354.3077ACC4@yahoo.com> <36ECF05C.54BB1F06@zip.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dJwbqi8MPHv5@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36ED4A55.FE44A296@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Mon, 15 Mar 1999 17:58:45, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> 
wrote:

> > I'm not sure about the others here, but when I say I "Pseudocode" I
> > don't mean the "official recognized" Pseudocode syntax.  I mean
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> process changes, so will my statements.

Yes.. of course.  My example was simplified.  For complex processes, 
before you have distilled them down to actual code, directly coding it
won't work.

Try something like:

* Open the input file and output file, also the cross reference 
database table.
* establish a cursor on the intermediate update file that allows 
update of 
* the status field from the cursor.
* read sequentially through the input file, looking up the actual 
descriptive data
* for the record in the cross reference file.  Define and read from a 
cursor based 
* on the returned information, creating output records for each 
returned item.

****
For each of the major tasks here I code a paragraph.  Within that 
paragraph I write similar comments (pseudocode if you will) for each 
task within a task - until I get down to the point where the COBOL is 
the same as the comments.  


-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

## Thread: URGENT, two types of input — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** "domTHEstom" <domTHEstomr@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi I have a real problem,

I'm writing a program to read in info a bout customers and their shopping
orders!
Two inputs, a customers file and a orders file.

I can easilt enough read in the customers but the problem is the orders.

The orders input file is fixed, ( i cannot change it, unfortunetly).

The orders file is as follows:

Record Type OrderID    CustID   OrderDate
                    /StockNo /Amount /Price
1 Byte          6 bytes    4 bytes   6 bytes

H         000001 0001   201294
I         000956 0025   0006.00
I         012897 0010   0005.50
H         000002 0002   311294
I         000956 0010   0006.00
H         000003 0005   010195
I         000956 0010   0006.00

There is a H (Header) line for each Customer Line, (in the Customer File).
And there are a set of I (Items) for each Header.

I need to do some calculations with Price, (cost)

The problem is how do I read the each line into it own structure depending
ont the Record Type, i.e.
H or I.


If anyone can help, i'd be most grateful.

cheers domTHEstom
```

#### ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** terrywin.nospam@spectra.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ea8a68.15220312@supernews.spectra.net>`
- **References:** `<7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sat, 13 Mar 1999 15:28:56 -0000, "domTHEstom"
<domTHEstomr@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Hi I have a real problem,
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Something like this is what you could use:

FD  Input-File.
01  Hdr-Record.
    05  Hdr-Id         pic x(01).
    05  Hdr-OrderId    pic x(06).
    05  Hdr-CustID     pic x(04).
    05  Hdr-OrderDate  pic x(06).

01  Dtl-Record.
    05  Dtl-Id         pic x(01).
    05  Dtl-StkNo      pic x(06).
    05  Dtl-Amount     pic 9(04).
    05  Dtl-Price      pic 9(04)v99.

Then read each record and make a decision based on the
ID field.  Having two 01 levels allows you to redefine
the record two ways.

if Hdr-Id = 'H'
    perform Header-Logic-Using-Hdr-Fields
else
    if Hdr-Id = 'I'
        perform Item-Logic-Using-Dtl-Fields
    else
        perform Error-Logic
    end-if
end-if.

Not elegant, but effective.

HTH
Terry
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** "domTHEstom" <domTHEstomr@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ce4is$ie2$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <36ea8a68.15220312@supernews.spectra.net>`

```
do i read it into a temp structure of wot?

i.e.

01 TEMP-RECORD.
    02 TEMP-TYPE PIC X.
    02 FILLER PIC X VALUE SPACES.
    02 TEMP1 PIC 9(6).
    02 FILLER PIC X VALUE SPACES.
    02 TEMP-O-CUSTID PIC 9(4).
    02 FILLER PIC X VALUE SPACES.
    02 TEMP2 PIC 9999V99.

.
.
.
 READ ORDERS-FILE INTO TEMP-RECORD AT END MOVE 'Y' TO
    ORDERS-EOF-FLAG.
.

    MOVE TEMP-TYPE TO RECORD-TYPE

    IF HEADER-TYPE
       MOVE TEMP-TYPE TO HEAD-TYPE
       MOVE TEMP1 TO ORDERID
       MOVE TEMP-O-CUSTID TO O-CUSTID
       MOVE TEMP2 TO ORDERDATE
    ELSE

       MOVE TEMP-TYPE TO IT-TYPE
       MOVE TEMP1 TO STOCKNO
       MOVE TEMP-O-CUSTID TO AMOUNT
       MOVE TEMP2 TO PRICE
    END-IF.

but the problem with this it the Price field I lose the decimal point????

wot way around this, plz

Terry Winchester wrote in message
<36ea8a68.15220312@supernews.spectra.net>...
>On Sat, 13 Mar 1999 15:28:56 -0000, "domTHEstom"
><domTHEstomr@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

```
No.  Get rid of temp-record altogether. Use the coding sample from Terry
Winchester and change your  READ to
READ ORDERS-FILE
    AT END
        MOVE 'Y' TO  ORDERS-EOF-FLAG.


domTHEstom wrote:

> do i read it into a temp structure of wot?
>
…[115 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

I think I would set up the FD to include the line Data Records are Header-Rec,
Detail-Rec

Then depending on the Record-ID I would process accordingly, that way you would
not be losing your decimal point.

Tom Wymer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cj834$h00$1@news-2.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<36EAA619.5FD02D8@ix.netcom.com> <19990315085209.11906.00001333@ng-cg1.aol.com>`

```
This problem has been lurking her for almost a week and I can't believe I
it's taken this long for someone (Twymer) to come up with something using
the implicit REDEFINES of multiple '01' records in the FD.. Fleshing it out
a tad, we have:

FD  INPUT-FILE
01   INPUT-BUFFER.
    05 RECORD-TYPE              PIC X(01).
      88 HEADER-RECORD VALUE IS "H".
      88 DETAIL RECORD    VALUE IS "D".
    05 FILLER                           PIC X(79).     << whatever it takes
to fill record
01 HEADER-RECORD.
    05 FILLER                           PIC X(01).
    05 REST-OF-HEADER-RECORD.
       10  CUSTOMER-NO         PIC X(10).
       .....
01 DETAIL-RECORD.
    05 FILLER                          PIC X(01).
    05 REST-OF-DETAIL-RECORD.
        10 PART-NUMBER         PIC X(12).
        ....

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       OPEN INPUT INPUT-FILE.
       PERFORM UNTIL 1 NOT EQUAL 1
          READ INPUT-FILE
               AT END
                   EXIT PERFORM
              NOT AT END
               EVALUATE TRUE
                 WHEN HEADER-RECORD
                    PERFORM PROCESS-HEADER-RECORD END-PERFORM
                 WHEN DETAIL-RECORD
                    PERFORM PROCESS-DETAIL-RECORD END-PERFORM
              END-EVALUATE
          END-READ
     END-PERFORM.
    CLOSE INPUT-FILE.
    GOBACK.

        Twymer wrote in message
<19990315085209.11906.00001333@ng-cg1.aol.com>...
>I think I would set up the FD to include the line Data Records are
> Header-Rec,Detail-Rec
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ED1CD5.4B77FA18@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <36ea8a68.15220312@supernews.spectra.net> <7ce4is$ie2$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I wouldn't.  If your shop MANDATES a READ...INTO, I would try to use a
redefines, allowing you to READ INTO the same location all the time.

If your shop doesn't mandate a READ...INTO, I would put your TYPE in the
record description under the FD.  Then do a simple READ then MOVE.

domTHEstom wrote:
> 
> do i read it into a temp structure of wot?
…[116 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

#### ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ED1B44.4D1AA3CF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
This is a very common file organization.  One solution is to redefine
the working storage where you READ INTO.  Another solution is to have
two working storage spots.  Read a record, and pick which 01 to move the
data two depending on your record type.  This second lets you still see
your header data while processing detail records.

domTHEstom wrote:
> 
> Hi I have a real problem,
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> cheers domTHEstom
```

#### ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** "ThinkAgain" <1day@atime.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d8jdp$dvk$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Try redefining your input with your first 01 level representing the header
record and your redefine 01 representing your detail.
Interrogate the record type to know which fields to access.



domTHEstom wrote in message <7ce05q$far$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>...
>Hi I have a real problem,
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: URGENT, two types of input

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990323133850.01431.00000222@ng-cg1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7d8jdp$dvk$1@remarQ.com>`

```
have your input record the max length of you biggest record with just the first
character being defined and the rest defined as filler.

Then define 2 working storage areas defining a "H"eader and a "I"nput. Read
your record and depending if it is a header or customer input move it into the
appropriate working storage area and go on from there.

TW
```

Re: Magick and Sexual Attraction
Author:

L303303 (l303303@aol.com) wrote:

: I have been considering using magick to increase sexual
: attractiveness. I have in the past used visualisations and
: affirmations for this, but without results.  Can anyone tell
: me how to go about doing this, and if anyone has had any
: experience with using magick for this purpose?

With a large stellium in the 8th house, this has never been an
issue for me.

In my 20's I started drinking damiana tea as it had a reputation
for toning the generative organs.  (I think that it has since been
found to be detrimental to the liver under long term usage.)
Within only a day or two I literally had strange men I didn't know
knocking on my apartment door.  I stopped taking it immediately :-)

Most noticeable to me is the fact that certain planets in
transit situations bring attention my way.  I've got to admit
to being of the mind that if you're doing what you're supposed
to be doing that the rest will fall in place.

Personally I don't do magick to bring lovers to me per se,
partially because I figure I may not like what shows up.

Regards, Charla

This address is from the early 80's so it may not be current.
I used to use their 'professional chart no. 3' to draw astrological

directly by mail.  They may still do so.

Re: Magick, Wicca and Praxis

                                                             alt.magick...




 more headers
            author posting history





Answering these questions from a Wiccan perspective ...

lorax (tyagi@houseofkaos.abyss.com) wrote:
: [from personal email, notified individual that I would be posting,
: no objection]

: #How do I close a circle after it has been cast?

Personally I undraw it and stamp my foot.  Others symbolically
cut it with a dagger.

: #What is meant by cakes & wine?

: in Wiccan tradition typically any form of bread, cake or cookie and
: some juice or alcoholic beverage, both utilized as receptacles for
: the charge of a symbolic 'Great Rite'.  they are also symbols of
: the sexual juices if you want to do the Great Rite in a literal
: and carnal sense. ;>

Hm.  A symbolic Great Rite would involve dipping the athame into
the chalice full of wine.  The cakes would then be annointed
with the charged wine using the athame.

Within a Wiccan coven, the sharing of cakes and wine is often a relaxed
more social part of a ritual, a grounding after concentrated
magical work.

I leave an extra cake uneaten on the pentacle in the North as well
as pour a libation from the chalice prior to drinking.  This is
similar to setting an extra table for unseen guests at the feast after
a ritual.

: #How do I prepare an area before I cast a circle or build an altar?

If you really want to be fastidious, wash the floors with something
like hyssop or camphor.  Ritually sweep it out.  Banish.  etc.

: #What do I focus on while casting  spells?

I primarily focus on raising the energy until I'm ready
to direct the charge.  I wouldn't say that I 'cast spells' though.

: #How do I invoke spirits?

Read a good book on this subject.  ;)

: #What protects me from harm from them after invocation?

If they're asking basic question about circle castings, they're
not ready to invoke much beyond the Quarters and some benign
God/Goddess figures IMO.

Regards, Charla

Greetings,

I attended the very first Twilight Covening in western Mass. and
thought that it was a very good concept that for the most part
went well.  I ended up in the Stag clan which was fitting for the
mythology I was working with at the time.

I do wish that Andras and Diedre would rethink the fact that they
moved it to Columbus Day which conflicts with another excellent
gathering in the Northeast, Panthea.

I attended the 1rst and 2nd MAPPCAF (Mid Atlantic Pan Pagan
Conference & Festival) which was the forerunner of Panthea.

Unfortunately I arrived too late Friday night at MAPPCAF-2 to
catch Herman Slater's opening ritual, but was treated to imitations
of his solemn 'Darksome night and shining moon' for the rest of
the weekend.  ;)

Regards and Blessed Be,

Charla

L.F. (sac41940@saclink.csus.edu) wrote in reply to Jon Browne:

: It's also supposed to be an aphrodisiac, though I wouldn't know.
: From what Jon said above it might be a slight sense-enhancer.

What the hay, I haven't packed either the herb or sex sections of
my library yet.

Richard Alan Miller in his book "The Magical and Ritual Use of Herbs"
says the primary effect is as a mild aphrodisiac and marijuana-like
euphoria which lasts about 1-1/2 hours.  He then gives a recipe for
an aphrodisiacal cordial of damiana and another recipe for a herbal
smoking mixture he calls Yuba Gold.  He also gives a reference to
Louis J. Culling's book "A Manual of Sex Magick."

I'm assuming he means Louis T. Culling's book "Sex Magick" which
has an appendix on the herb damiana.  Culling gives a recommended
dosage and includes diary entries of his own experience with the herb.

The book "Legal Highs" by Adam Gottlieb lists the same effects
as Richard Alan Miller's book does.  This book suggests simmering
2 tbsp. leaves in 1 pt. water and drinking the tea at the same time
as smoking a pipeful of the leaves.

Both herbal books suggest that damiana has a tonic effect on
the sexual organs and that excessive long-term use may be
toxic to the liver.

Regards, Charla

A guy brought datura to a pagan gathering in the Jemez Mountains
in New Mexico at the full moon / Lammas in 1982.  He passed it around
at night after the main ritual.  I did not ingest it but did talk to
a couple of folks who did.

they made it through to the morning.  An older gentleman had physical
repurcussions for the next 6 months.

It's definitely not for everyone!

This thread has too many responses for me to figure out who posted
the original question:

:  >> Cast a spell to cure an Aids patient.

I am reminded of someone (who I respect greatly!) who I attended
weekly classes under for a year saying he's tried everything he knows
against this disease without success.

I applied to his class (one must apply and only 13 are accepted
each year) after hearing him talk at a round robin sponsored by COG.
When someone in the audience asked the panel about how Death is
viewed, he offered that he is a Priest of the Lord of Death and Rebirth
and as part of that he works with people with AIDS.  My ego fancied that
with my stellium in the 8th house, that perhaps I had some work to
do in this helping the dying stuff also.  It turns out that a lot of
help in that area is with real earthly things.  I've since decided
that my 8th house work lies elsewhere, but would not ignore the
subject should it surface within my vision again.

Regards, Charla

<a id="t-296453af76"></a>
## Thread: Copy Replacing Syntax? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Copy Replacing Syntax?

- **From:** charlajw@netcom.com (Charla J. Williams)
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<charlajwF8IJIx.n0@netcom.com>`

```
I know that I used to see this done but danged if I can
figure out the syntax and get a clean compile.  I've tried
the online manuals.

Development is COBOL 2, Production is COBOL for MVS.  IBM Mainframe.

Record Layout is:
    01  Emp-Record.
        05  Emp-Number
        05  Emp-Name

I want to do a copy replacing of the copylib member and end up with:
    01  WS-Record.
        05  WS-Number
        05  WS-Name

which should be a very simple thing to do.  I just want to change
the prefix on every field name and put this in Working Storage.

Thanks for any help,
Charla
```

#### ↳ Re: Copy Replacing Syntax?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cd7t6$q0c@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<charlajwF8IJIx.n0@netcom.com>`

```
The always "exciting" partial word expression.  The following works - and is
ANSI conforming.  (However, do remember that once you have a COPY member
like this, you canNOT then use it without the REPLACING phrase and get valid
syntax).

Your copylib member should be:

 01  :Emp-:Record.
        05  :Emp-:Number
        05  :Emp-:Name

Your COPY statement (for Working-Storage) then becomes,

   Copy Whatever
        Replacing ==:Emp-:== by ==WS-==.

but you will also need to have the following in your File Section (if that
is where the original file layout was)

   Copy Whatever
        Replacing ==:Emp-:== by ==Emp-==.


You can use parenthesis "(" and ")" instead of the colons, but there are
some long term maintenance reasons that I usually suggest the colons.

NOTE:  Insert plug for next ANSI/ISO COBOL -
    The next Standard will actually (finally) provide "self-documenting"
ways of replacing prefixes and suffixes!!!
```

I am looking for a good source of info on converting plain COBOL2 programs
into the newer LE370 COBOL.
Wasn't sure if this is just a runtime library pointer change or if it will
require some rewriting.
Can anyone assist in pointing me to an info area on this and any
differences which exist between this two versions?
```

- **From:** George Young <gmyoung@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

think the manual you are looking for is the Migration Guide. The other

George

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7chhs8$d0e@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon>`

```
There are SEPARATE migration guides for both the compiler (COBOL) and the
run-time (LE).  However, the bottom-line is that all "valid" VS COBOL II
code is supported without change with the newer COBOL for this-and-that
compilers.  (Actually, there are a few minor new reserved words - but not
many.)  Moving from the VS COBOL II run-time to the LE run-time is, however,
a bit more difficult but not nearly as bad as moving from OS/VS COBOL to VS
COBOL II.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f2ca2e.4075215@netnews>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon> <7cigl1$9vh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED2860.BB10A241@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 15 Mar 1999 08:33:52 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>> Are you just asking about the compiler? AFAIK, there is just one difference:
>> The 'IBM COBOL' compilers don't accept the period in area A as COBOL II does.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>73, creating a subtle bug.

You just set off my BS detector.  I have seen a missing period in the
PROCEDURE DIVISION cause a subtle bug (many times).  In COBOL-85 it is
possible for a missing period in the DATA DIVISION to cause a subtle bug
(though I've never seen it happen).  But in COBOL-68 or COBOL-74 there is
no way for a missing period to cause a subtle bug, unless a compiler bug
caused some invalid syntax to be accepted.

Can you give an example of how the lost period caused a subtle bug in the
DATA DIVISION, or the story actually that it caused a syntax error, and
you were _worried_ it might cause a subtle bug?
```

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)

…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>l    |/ MS^7=6/28/107     http://users.aol.com/PanicYr00/Sequence.html

Believe it or not, I once encountered a "Column 73" error on a sorted table
definition that kept the IBM compiler folks baffled for about 6 weeks.

Wasn't obvious since the omitted period still printed on the compiler listings
and the TSO editor of the day (FSE for you older geeks) allowed more than 72
columns to be displayed for line numbers that didn't require a full 6 columns.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00

```
Theo DP wrote:
> 
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> columns to be displayed for line numbers that didn't require a full 6 columns.

How about a program that abended in production and not in development
all files were sequential and we read directly from the production
datasets.  The cause:  There was a table overrun and the optimizing
compiler reordered the working storage for efficiency.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F663DB.52A158D4@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon> <7cigl1$9vh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED2860.BB10A241@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f2ca2e.4075215@netnews>`

```
> >I remember once when we needed to convert " pc " to " pic " (small
> >Burroughs to large Burroughs).  One program moved a period into column
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> you were _worried_ it might cause a subtle bug?

      You're right, in this case it was part of a value clause which got
moved into column 73.  I never write code with value clauses continued
on multiple lines, but I have maintained code which does this.  I prefer
multiple value clauses myself.  In the same project we had periods moved
into column 73 in the procedure division, and I wrote my message too
quickly.

I suppose it's possible though if a period moved into column 73,
converting a decimal point into a period.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f8a86b.385796275@netnews>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon> <7cigl1$9vh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED2860.BB10A241@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f2ca2e.4075215@netnews> <36F663DB.52A158D4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 22 Mar 1999 08:38:03 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>      You're right, in this case it was part of a value clause which got
>moved into column 73.

After my post I started thinking about it and realized your subtle bug
probably related to a VALUE clause.  It could even be an OCCURS clause,
eg:
              15  SILLY-LONG-LONG-DATA-NAME    PC 9(10) OCCURS 100
                                      COMP.

As long as you were changing PC to PIC I'm sure you were changing VA to
VALUE (which could easily truncate the value) and OC to OCCURS (I can't
think of a subtle bug that could cause). 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f8b7ca.389718799@netnews>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon> <7cigl1$9vh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED2860.BB10A241@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f2ca2e.4075215@netnews> <36F663DB.52A158D4@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f8a86b.385796275@netnews>`

```
'Twas 24 Mar 1999 09:25:51 GMT, when Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>              15  SILLY-LONG-LONG-DATA-NAME    PC 9(10) OCCURS 100
>                                      COMP.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>think of a subtle bug that could cause). 

Brain cramp:  I was thinking that changing OC to OCCURS would push the
argument over four colums and this couldn't cause a bug unless it was OC
10000 or more.  Then I realized two more things:

1)  OC 10000 was valid in Medium Systems COBOLV (and probably COBOLL), and
2)  Shifting by four columns might cause loss of only one digit (and three
spaces)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F8F837.DFC629B8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon> <7cigl1$9vh$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ED2860.BB10A241@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f2ca2e.4075215@netnews> <36F663DB.52A158D4@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f8a86b.385796275@netnews>`

```
>               15  SILLY-LONG-LONG-DATA-NAME    PC 9(10) OCCURS 100
>                                       COMP.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> think of a subtle bug that could cause).

Your occurs example could happen here, with say a variable length table
either filled with high values for a binary search or a range of
lengths.  If it normally reads in 500 records, but was written:
       05 MY-TABLE                          OC 10000
                indexed by my-index.
          10   MY-VALUE-1         PC X(5).
          10   MY-VALUE-2         PC X(2).

It is possible for this to compile with OCCURS 1000 (the trailing 0 is
in column 73).
Then the program will run until you read in a table with more than 999
records.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 2 to LE370 conversions

- **From:** Roland Schiradin <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ED9E15.4B140E59@tap.de>`
- **References:** `<01be6e2b$afc49e20$a7741ad1@jlaydon>`

```
Jeff,

> I am looking for a good source of info on converting plain COBOL2 programs
> into the newer LE370 COBOL.

look at the migration guide. Another helpful tool (my cbt contribution) can
be found on www.cbttape.org File #321. This "freeware" program is helpful to
identify RES/NORES applications which can cause problem in some cases.


Roland
```

Hi,

quick question,

I've written a program and I want a little message printed
when I run the program saying that the thing is being run:

as follows:

Program Executing.

then I want to add another full stop to it, several times, and end up
with...


Program Executing.....

each dot appearing every second.

Is this possible with cobol, it being a file oreinted language!


Any takers?

domTHEstom

p.s. thanks to everyone who helped me out with the previous problem. :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Counter In COBOL

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ckidb$jip$2@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7cjqaj$4cc$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
It is a good idea to let the user know that the program is actually
doing something useful. Assuming your program is doing
something, during the processing insert a counter that triggers
the message. Such as:

01  TICKER            PIC 99 VALUE 0.
01  DOT-LINE.
       02  FILLER        PIC X(10) VALUE 'Working'.
        02 DOT-LINE-DOTS       PIC X(70).
01  DOT-COUNTER    PIC 9(2) VALUE 0.



(code here)
ADD 1 TO TICKER
IF TICKER = 0
    PERFORM DOT-MAKER.
(continue with processing code)


DOT-MAKER.
    ADD 1 TO DOT-COUNTER.
    IF DOT-COUNTER > 70
        MOVE 'Working' TO DOT-LINE
        MOVE 1 TO DOT-COUNTER.
    MOVE '.' TO DOT-LINE-DOTS(DOT-COUNTER:1).
    DISPLAY DOT-LINE.

Each 100 iterations of the code gets another dot.
You can adjust TICKER to be, say, 9(3) and get a dot
every 1000 iterations.

Or, you could use:
ON 1 AND EVERY 100
    PERFORM DOT-MAKER.

Or, if you really wanted a dot every "x" time interval,
you could query the clock - but the overhead would
be ferocious.
domTHEstom wrote in message <7cjqaj$4cc$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>...
>I've written a program and I want a little message printed
>when I run the program saying that the thing is being run:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is this possible with cobol, it being a file oreinted language!
```

```

you a little of what is possible.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7clras$coa$1@news.igs.net>`

```
01 current-time.
    02 current-hour        picture 99.
    02 current-minute     picture 99.
    02 current-second    picture 99.
01 last-second             picture 99.

display-and-stuff.
      display "I am running" with no advancing.
      perform do-a-loop-for-a-while.

do-a-loop-for-a-while.
     accept current-time from time.
     if current-second is not equal to last-second
          move current-second to last-second
          display "." with no advancing.
     perform something-usefull-here.

## Thread: I need a little help.... — 10 msgs

### I need a little help....

- **From:** "imfzyufzy" <gofuzzylogic@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hello all,

I'm a Visual Foxpro programmer (o please, no flames), who was just asked to
do a conversion on some old Cobol files. The files as far as I can tell are
unreadable without Cobol. (I'm not even sure what version they were compiled
in.)

The company that used to support them has dropped the product. It's not Y2K
compliant.

Is it possible (through ODBC, ActiveX, COM, DLL's, Anything) to take Cobol
data files and convert them into a readable (ASCII) format without Cobol
installed? I don't care about the source code, screen sets, reports or any
other files - just the data itself.

I'm semi-proficient in Visual C++ as well as VB, if that helps.

Any help would be much appreciated.

Thanks in advance,

Tod McKenna
Opal Data Technologies
Tod@opaldata.com
```

> The company has refused to give out any information to third parties
> (including us). They insist that everything we need is contained on site.
>

So this means you have the source code?  .COB, or .CBL or something 
similar?  Given the source your task is doable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need a little help....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** frederico@memosis.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36efe7a3.736332@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WFb5ieQyTdtW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7cjtcq$gkt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WjJdRW0o4y66@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
On 16 Mar 99 00:48:47 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>On Mon, 15 Mar 1999 21:17:14, "imfzyufzy" <gofuzzylogic@att.net> 
>wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>So this means you have the source code?

Even without the source it may be possible to get the layout 
of the fields. Even the PICTURE.

Although it may take some time depending on the record structure.
For those who know RM/PANELS and that did had a look at the 
structure of the .LIB files they know it is hard to get it, although I

have done it. and it has a lot of COMP-4 on it.


Frederico Fonseca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need a little help....

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36edddaa.311851250@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WFb5ieQyTdtW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7cjtcq$gkt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On 15 Mar 1999 21:17:14 GMT, "imfzyufzy" <gofuzzylogic@att.net> wrote:

>The company has refused to give out any information to third parties
>(including us). They insist that everything we need is contained on site.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tod

With those .COB files you should be able to figure out the files and
their structures.   We should be able to point you in the right
direction - I'm not a UNIX expert and I know there are some
differences in the file structure (at least for MicroFocus) possible
under Unix.  But given the source, and the associated FD you should be
able to figure out whats in there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need a little help....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter Midgley" <midgley@midgleypr.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cmejc$q9c$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WFb5ieQyTdtW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7cjtcq$gkt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <36edddaa.311851250@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Guys,
COB files are the RM cobol equivalent of an 'interpretable', being read by
the runtime system 'runcobol'; as such they are not source.
Pete Midgley.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36edddaa.311851250@news1.ibm.net>...
>On 15 Mar 1999 21:17:14 GMT, "imfzyufzy" <gofuzzylogic@att.net> wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>able to figure out whats in there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need a little help....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** frederico@memosis.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36eee6ce.523569@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WFb5ieQyTdtW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7cjtcq$gkt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <36edddaa.311851250@news1.ibm.net> <7cmejc$q9c$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Tue, 16 Mar 1999 20:21:21 -0000, "Peter Midgley"
<midgley@midgleypr.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Guys,
>COB files are the RM cobol equivalent of an 'interpretable', being read by
>the runtime system 'runcobol'; as such they are not source.
>Pete Midgley.
I have to disagree with this.

Under Unix the RM/COBOL objects can have what ever extension you want
it to have.

It does not have to be .COB

In fact it does not need any extension whatsoever.

The source CAN have the extension .COB if you want.


Frederico Fonseca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I need a little help....

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0NHI2.396$NB5.1014702@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WFb5ieQyTdtW@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7cjtcq$gkt@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
>The company has refused to give out any information to third parties
>(including us). They insist that everything we need is contained on site.


Sounds like some good old COBOL research. They want you to track down each
file through JCL to see which COBOL program accesses it. Then research each
COBOL program to find the record layouts of each file. Then, having the
records layouts, theoretically you should be able to write your own program
to convert the data to a usable format.

Have fun.
```

---

<a id="t-6869efa145"></a>
## Thread: Pentium III Chips Released with IDs - Intel won't budge — 16 msgs

### Pentium III Chips Released with IDs - Intel won't budge

- **From:** amdchips@rbetter.com (Intel no one)
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.python,comp.lang.tcl
- **Message-ID:** `<36eff3d3.281855@news.earthlink.net>`

```

	Pentium III chip with the individual serial number that can
track your web surfing and buying habits can now have the ID number
turned on and off by software.  Following some links I found the
www.fightdivx.com website and noticed that they have a Intel Boycott
page with links, quotes and info on why you should boycott the
invasion of privacy Pentium III chips. Just like everyone suspected,
the ID number can be taken without a customers knowledge. Just like
cellular phone fraud, once someone has your unique ID number, they
could pose as you on the internet.  Do not be fooled by reports that
this problem is fixed because Intel disabled this feature by software
on their up coming chips.  Information is power. They want to know
your surfing and buying habits. That is what this is all about. Here
is the link to the page with the boycott info and links. 

http://www.fightdivx.com/intelboycott.htm

Also you will find a Boycott Intel screen saver and banner on their
page above. Spread it around.
```

#### ↳ Re: Pentium III Chips Released with IDs - Intel won't budge

- **From:** Stephan Wilms <Stephan.Wilms@cwa.de>

In <01be6f1a$1298f700$2a01a8c0@arc7>, "Richard Heathfield" <binary@eton.powernet.co.uk> writes:

>Subject line says it all - we've been through this subject ad nauseam
>already, after all.

The latest spam came from earthlink:  complain to abuse@earthlink.net.

The website is hosted by value.net:  abuse@value.net.

Send them a copy of the full posting (including headers),  along with a 
*courteous* request that they persuade their customer(s) to either:

   a. play nice,  or
   b. get the *#$% out of the Usenet sandbox.

Unfortunately,  it may take a while to run them out of ISPs:  the 
earthlink posting appears to have come from Santa Clara,  where POPs are
almost as common as phone booths.

Ran
```

### file status error

- **From:** "Sydney" <sydney@i-france.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>`

```
Hi

I use personal cobol from Microfocus.
I have a sequential file defined like this
select fcomplement assign to nomfic file status fs1.
fd fcomplement.
01 r.
   02 d pic 99/99/9999.
   02 t pic +99.

77 fs1 pic 99.

after a
read fcomplement
I have 39 and 12 in fs1. This values are in hexadecimal.

It causes an error Illegal character in a numeric field
because I have this instruction
        perform until fs1 not = 00


Can you help me??

Pardon for my English but I'm french
A+
Sydney
```

#### ↳ Re: file status error

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be6f37$c13282f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
Sydney <sydney@i-france.com> wrote in article
<7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>...
> I have a sequential file defined like this
> select fcomplement assign to nomfic file status fs1.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have 39 and 12 in fs1. This values are in hexadecimal.

Why should read fcomplement change fs1? There must be something you haven't
told us.

If 39 is the file status key it means a conflict between the file
attributes and what is specified in the program.  At least for Cobol for
MVS, I don't know about Microfocus Cobol.

Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: file status error

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990315204444.13551.00001521@ng97.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>`

```
unless you initialize fs1 with some type of numeric value there is "junk" in
the field.  So if you try to compare something numeric with "junk" you will get
an error everytime.

Tom WYmer
```

#### ↳ Re: file status error

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<921573749.14207.0.nnrp-11.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>`

```
You should specify the file status as PIC XX not PIC 99.  In fact in a lot
of compilers, including AS400, fs2 does not have to be numeric.  In
microfucus cobol whene fs1 = 9 then fs2=b where b is a one byte binary
value.

In your case you have fs 9/018 which is described in the manual as 'Read
part record error: EOF before EOR or file open in wrong mode.' whatever that
may mean.  It sounds as though the file does not match the definition.

Regards
Rick



Sydney wrote in message <7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>...
>Hi
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: file status error

- **From:** "T Brown" <tom.brown@clara.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pPzI2.959$mi3.190@nnrp3.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>`

```
add
SET COBSW=-F
to autoexec.bat
stops illegal character in numeric field from being reported as error.

File status fields in Microfocus can be defined as follows
01 file-status   pic xx.
     88 file-success  value "00".
01 fs redefines file-status.
    03 fs-1   pic x.
         88 run-time-error value "9".
    03 fs-2   pic x.
    03 fs-2-bin redefines fs-2  pic x comp-x.

Then process as follows after any file operation
 if not file-success
   if run-time-error
       move fs-2-bin to w-pic-999
       display "Run time error " w-pic-999
   else
       display "File error: " file-status
   end-if
end-if.


Your file error would turn out to be Run-time error 018

<from NxtExpress V3 Help> - Probably, but not guaranteed to be, the same as
MF personal COBOL

"018 Read part record error: EOF before EOR or file open in wrong mode
Recoverable
A part record has been found at the end of a file. Consequently your
run-time system treats the data file as a record, and not finding a full
record, reports this error.
Resolution:
Ensure that the record size you give when you read from or WRITE to a file
is consistent."

You could try adding the clause "Organization line sequential" - otherwise
it may default to record sequential, which will read an exact number of
bytes per read, regardless of the position of newline characters.

Regards Tom

Sydney wrote in message <7cjutq$d9$3@platane.wanadoo.fr>...
>Hi
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Newbie requiring help. — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Newbie requiring help.

- **From:** Rodney Thompson <rodney@timaru.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36EDDEEF.2505F90B@timaru.com>`

```
I havn't been able to find resources on the net so have come here for
help. Could any one tell me how to call a DOS command from COBOL-85?
I tried SYSTEM "type output.dat |more"
and it spat back at me, not knowing what SYSTEM was.
Your help appriciated in advance.
Ta.
Rodders.
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie requiring help.

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7clpj5$59d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36EDDEEF.2505F90B@timaru.com>`

```
In article <36EDDEEF.2505F90B@timaru.com>,
  Rodney Thompson <rodney@timaru.com> wrote:
> I havn't been able to find resources on the net so have come here for
> help. Could any one tell me how to call a DOS command from COBOL-85?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It depends on your compiler, but you might try looking at something like
"DISPLAY ... UPON COMMAND-LINE"

What lead you to believe that SYSTEM would work?  Did you try looking up the
syntax for use of SYSTEM?

Ed Stevens
Opinions are mine and not necessarily those of my employer.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie requiring help.

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36EE8819.C2F87FFA@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<36EDDEEF.2505F90B@timaru.com>`

```
Rodney Thompson wrote:

> I havn't been able to find resources on the net so have come here for
> help. Could any one tell me how to call a DOS command from COBOL-85?
> I tried SYSTEM "type output.dat |more"
> and it spat back at me, not knowing what SYSTEM was.

Perhaps that should have been:
01  CALL-PARM.
       05 FILLER      PIC X(22) VALUE "type output.dat |more".
       05 FILLER      PIC X(01) VALUE LOW-VALUE.
-------------
       CALL "SYSTEM" USING CALL-PARM.

Check the documentation for the compiler on the system you're using to
see if you need to specify "$SYSTEM" instead or if you can specify your
command line after USING instead of setting up another variable in
working-storage.  HTH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie requiring help.

- **From:** "S R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<921605691.16170.0.nnrp-09.c1ed9803@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36EDDEEF.2505F90B@timaru.com> <36EE8819.C2F87FFA@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
>> I havn't been able to find resources on the net so have come here for
>> help. Could any one tell me how to call a DOS command from COBOL-85?
>> I tried SYSTEM "type output.dat |more"
>> and it spat back at me, not knowing what SYSTEM was.


..or Micro Focus extensions enable you to

               move 22 to name-len
               move 35 to function-code
               call x"91" using result
                                function-code
                                parameter

where parameter holds :

  01  parameter.
           05 name-len               pic x comp-x.
           05 command-n           pic x(65) value 'type output.dat | more'.

SrHart
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie requiring help.

- **From:** "Peter Midgley" <midgley@midgleypr.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cmec1$q3m$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36EDDEEF.2505F90B@timaru.com> <36EE8819.C2F87FFA@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
RM Cobol Certainly allows for call "system" using "whatever dos command is
appropriate" or substitute a data name for the literal.  Remember that RM
cobol is platform independent in this respect, and achieves this by using a
platform specific runtime.
TWH
Pete Midgley.

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Newbie requiring help.

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ldGI2.382$NB5.970281@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Obviously your SYSTEM, old QBASIC syntax, has been answered.

Have you tried a standard COBOL method of calling the executable directly?
Not sure in DOS environment, but in mainframe environment it would be
something like:

           CALL 'SUBPROG' USING WS-PARMS

Where "SUBPROG" is in the search PATH. I assume in DOS you can use a fully
qualified executable name.

Just my 2 bits.
```

#### ↳ Re: SEQ search

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36eda20a@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990315164107.15560.00001119@ng66.aol.com>`

```

Dupavoy wrote in message <19990315164107.15560.00001119@ng66.aol.com>...
>Does anyone know the algorithm for
>Sequential search?
>If so, please post it here or E-mail me.
>thanks.


01  SIMPLE-TABLE.
    02  SIMPLE-SIZE             PIC S9(3)  VALUE ZERO COMP.
    02  SIMPLE-MAX              PIC S9(3)  VALUE +100 COMP.
    02  SIMPLE-ITEM                        OCCURS 100 TIMES.
        03  SIMPLE-ITEM-KEY     PIC X(...).
        03  SIMPLE-ITEM-DATA    PIC X(...).

SEARCH-SIMPLE-TABLE.
    PERFORM LOOK-AT-SIMPLE-TABLE-ITEM
      VARYING ITEM-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
        UNTIL ITEM-NBR > SIMPLE-SIZE
           OR SIMPLE-ITEM-KEY (ITEM-NBR) = ITEM-KEY

    IF ITEM-NBR > SIMPLE-SIZE
         MOVE "ITEM NOT FOUND" TO DISPLAY-DATA
         PERFORM RAISE-EXCEPTION
    ELSE
          MOVE SIMPLE-ITEM (ITEM-NBR) TO ITEM
    .

 LOOK-AT-SIMPLE-TABLE-ITEM.
    EXIT
    .
```

#### ↳ Re: SEQ search

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990315204730.13551.00001522@ng97.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990315164107.15560.00001119@ng66.aol.com>`

```
open file for input

do  this until end of file:
read 
process

close file

Tom WYmer
```
