## Thread: Conversion — 5 msgs

### Conversion

- **From:** "Paul Jacobs" <pjacobs@efg.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com>`

```
I am getting a tape with packed signed and unsigned data on it which I am
working with in VB.

I have some fields that are unpacked  PIC S9(03) but the data actually takes
3 bytes.  How does the sign work?  You've got a 3 digit number but
also the sign fitting into only 3 bytes.  Is it some sort of bitwise thing
with one
just the highest order bit?

It's been a while since I've worked with data like this, so please forgive
the
ignorant question.

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ede041.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com>`

```
The "COBOL Unleashed" authors are the best ones to ask about this.

But if I recall correctly, data in plain old numeric format (ex: S9(03))
uses only the last four bits for the 0-9 digit.  The leftmost four can be
used for other things (like a sign).  So, for example, if you write -68 to a
file as pic S99 and look at it in Fujitsu's file editor you'll see two 8 bit
numbers: 36, 58 (00110110, 01011000).   The last two nibbles are obvious (6
and 8), but notice the sign is in the last byte as a 5 (0101).  If it was
+68 it would be 36, 38...

In IBM I remember looking at hex Fs in the leftmost nibble (1111) for
positive and something else (C or D?) for negative.  Either way, that gives
you some idea of what's going on.

However, I think you have a different problem if you're trying to get the
sign from an ASCII number in VB after the fact.  ASCII uses the full 8 bits
to signify a digit and therefore the sign is separate.  If the conversion
process didn't put it in there somewhere, you're SOL.  If you're somehow
reading EBCDIC or something similar, then my explanation above should give
you some clue.  Just look for a known negative number and examine the first
4 bits in the last byte.






Paul Jacobs wrote in message <54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com>...
>I am getting a tape with packed signed and unsigned data on it which I am
>working with in VB.
>
>I have some fields that are unpacked  PIC S9(03) but the data actually
takes
>3 bytes.  How does the sign work?  You've got a 3 digit number but
>also the sign fitting into only 3 bytes.  Is it some sort of bitwise thing
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ee101a.4534540@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com> <36ede041.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
On Mon, 15 Mar 1999 21:39:52 -0700, "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com> wrote:

>
>However, I think you have a different problem if you're trying to get the
>sign from an ASCII number in VB after the fact.  ASCII uses the full 8 bits
>to signify a digit and therefore the sign is separate. 

It is????  Well, I'll be struck!  X'3n' = 'n' for n = 0,1,2,...,9  which (imo) means that
only the right nibble is "really" being used....

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ED560F.5A088B6D@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com>`

```
Paul Jacobs wrote:

> I am getting a tape with packed signed and unsigned data on it which I am
> working with in VB.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> just the highest order bit?

Each byte has 8 bits but in unsigned data, the value of the digit is only in the
last 4 bits, called nybbles.  With origins in the punched card era, the first 4
bits are the zone with the last 4 being the digit.  In signed numeric (unpacked)
data, the sign will be in the zone of the low order byte.  On platforms that
must pack the data (comp-3) to work with it, the zone and digit of the low order
byte are reversed while the zones of other bytes in the field are ignored.

Usually x'F' means unsigned, x'C' is positive and x'D' is negative.  E.g.:
+123 = 12C
-123 = 12L  In both cases, the byte containing the sign appears as an alphabetic
character.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ck0m2$kdn$2@news-2.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com>`

```
In your case, the sign is bit-flagged in the third (units) byte.

But, if you want to learn how COBOL data types are constructed, try my
(free) text and graphics tutorial on COBOL data types at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html ; follow the page to
COBDATA.ZIP.

MCM

Paul Jacobs wrote in message <54cH2.96$AM3.358949@news2.randori.com>...
>I am getting a tape with packed signed and unsigned data on it which I am
>working with in VB.

takes
>3 bytes.  How does the sign work?  You've got a 3 digit number but
>also the sign fitting into only 3 bytes.  Is it some sort of bitwise thing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Paul

```
On Mon, 15 Mar 1999 21:46:30, dhawkins <dhawkins@home.com> wrote:

> Well Ive gotten more info for those who are still following this post..
.. Snippy...

> Will this help me extract my data files??
> 

You are further along, but without the actual layout of what is in the
data, you are still not far.  From this we can tell you how long each 
record is, and we can tell you what the "key" fields length and 
positions are - looks like 3 keys on that file - one primary and two 
alternate.  But you still don't know the actual content of each field 
- you can't get it without the source code.  Given the "record layout"
for each file, however, you could  use rebuild to create a level II 
indexed file - which will output the data portion in "flat" ascii, and
you can use that (once you know what is in it) to get where you need 
to go for data conversion.  But you need to pressure the vendor for 
the layouts.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

unititialized subscript, particularly in light of the fact that it does not
always happen.

## Thread: VisualAge or MicroFocus COBOL mainframe access — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### VisualAge or MicroFocus COBOL mainframe access

- **From:** wtsquid@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cmijm$tr8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Has anyone used VisualAge COBOL or MicroFocus COBOL LAN developed
applications to access VSAM files on an IBM mainframe and other file types on
a file server?	From the IBM ads I'm not sure whether VisualAge can do it,
they either state or imply that it can but do not emphasize the fact. That
leads me to believe that it can but not well enough to develop apps with the
method. MicroFocus states emphatically that it can access files on multiple
platforms, including the IBM mainframe, but their verbage does not make it
sound like an easy process.

I would appreciate any experiences with these products anyone is willing to
share.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: VisualAge or MicroFocus COBOL mainframe access

- **From:** "Walter Willis" <wwillis1@uswest.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<osdJ2.672$%u2.2712@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<7cmijm$tr8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
To answer your question, Yes it is possible.  I am working on a project now
that essentially does this.  But it uses mainframe programs for the data
retrieval and related computations and sends the results back.  I believe it
uses a CICS type transaction to call the mainframe program from the GUI
interface.  I was not involved in this aspect of development.

One of the 'now' Microfocus products is XDB gateway which you can use to
test an application on the PC that access DB2 data on the mainframe.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: VisualAge or MicroFocus COBOL mainframe access

- **From:** Eric Andersen <eric.no.andersen@merant.spam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7B4E1.6AD8CEB6@merant.spam.com>`
- **References:** `<7cmijm$tr8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <osdJ2.672$%u2.2712@news.uswest.net>`

```
Micro Focus Fileshare, with the Micro Focus Mainframe Access (MFA) Product for
VSAM access may be what you're looking for. Fileshare provides transparent
access to remote files using standard COBOL I/O syntax.  Redirection of I/O
requests is controlled outside of the program through an external
configuration file.  The redirector allows selection of the destination server
on a file-by-file basis, and fileshare servers run on a variety of platforms
so your need to access "other file types on a file server" may be covered here
too.  MFA evolved from the old Fileshare for Host Systems, and for your
purposes would provide transparent access to VSAM files on the host.  Whether
Fileshare servers on other platforms will fill the bill probably depends on
the target files.

Some product information is available at
http://www.merant.com/ads/md/products/mfa.asp and you can find some additonal
material by running "mainframe access" and "fileshare" through the site's
search engine.

Note that I'm not directly involved with the Fileshare team, so some of the
terminology may have changed, but in general the preceding should be accurate
- I hope this helps...

Eric


<wtsquid@my-dejanews.com> wrote in message
news:7cmijm$tr8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com...
> Has anyone used VisualAge COBOL or MicroFocus COBOL LAN developed
> applications to access VSAM files on an IBM mainframe and other file types on
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
 

----------------------------------------------

```

#### ↳ Re: VisualAge or MicroFocus COBOL mainframe access

- **From:** djscubed@aol.com (Djscubed)
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990324211228.01606.00000725@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cmijm$tr8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Check out www.c-cubed.net their solution product has been running at FDR, DST,
ABBOT Labs, Flemming Foods, State of Nebraska, Bank of De Credito, Bank of
Lima, Ross Labs and many other companies doing much more that just easy file
access.  The performance is more than 8 times faster than the Micro Focus File
share product and is much less expensive.
```

#### ↳ Re: Learning COBOL on a PC

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-R6FuE23mDCn5@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<WrxH2.501$Gy1.375@news7.ispnews.com>`

```
On Tue, 16 Mar 1999 18:31:36, "Jesse Olson" <jesseolson@triton.net> 
wrote:

> Here's all of my questions:  Would it be possible for me to learn COBOL on a
> Win 95 PC? 

Yes!

>Approx. how long would it take?

Would you beleive 24 hours?  (24 1 Hour lessons!) -- keep reading.  
>Could I learn it quick enough
> to be of any help on the Y2K problem, or is this just wishful thinking?

You could be proficient enough to be of some use, but getting you "in 
the door" with no real world experience to work on Y2K here at "crunch
time" will be difficult. 

> Where would I get a COBOL compiler for a PC?  Any good books, internet
> sites, etc. for learning COBOL?

Go to my site listed below and click on the books Link.

You can order "Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours, by Thane 
Hubbell" (That name sure sound familiar) from there.  It comes with a 
compiler (full featured, not crippled or restricted) for Windows 95 - 
plus some nifty third party software.  It's a book designed for a 
reader such as yourself: one who wants to get hands on and learn the 
language with a compiler on his PC.

> 
> Thanks for any and all help you can give!


-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

<a id="t-6fb67b9b36"></a>
## Thread: TekMetrics offering free certifications to COBOL programmers through March... — 2 msgs

### TekMetrics offering free certifications to COBOL programmers through March...

- **From:** Michael Littman <mikelittman@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36EE5C5A.57C6A07E@erols.com>`

```
Dear COBOL Programmer,

Through the end of March, TekMetrics is offering free 'COBOL II'
certifications through it's internet-based CAT engine.  Simply go to
http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/certtop.html to order your free
certification.  You can either elect to take the test right away, or
come back later, using the PIN you are provided when you order.

To qualify as a Certified COBOL II Programmer, you must pass the
examination with a score of 2.75 or higher.  To be certified as a Master
COBOL II Programmer, you must obtain a score of 4.00 or higher.  At the
end of the test, your score will be provided (with a scoring range of
1-5).  Once certified, you can add the logo associated with the test
(standard or master logo, depending on how well you do) to your resume
or website.  Furthermore, those that pass will be mailed a certificate,
once again, at no cost.  There's no catch.  We're simply trying to prove
a concept during a beta trial period.  Thus far, the feedback has been
extremely positive!

Why bother getting certified by TekMetrics?

1)  More and more corporations recognize TekMetrics Certifications as an
indication not only of the technical workers knowledge level, but also
as an indicator that they were willing to demonstrate their skills to
them.

2)  It's free and it's fun.  They won't be free forever, but they are
now.  So take advantage of them.  Furthermore, those who have completed
the tests generally indicate that they enjoyed the experience and
considered both the delivery of the test and the items themselves to be
of high quality.

3)  Over 4000 technical workers from around the world are ordering our
certifications each week, and that number is steadily rising.

4)  Strut your stuff.  See if you are good enough to make the top 10
list.

5)  Find out both your strengths and weaknesses in 'COBOL II'.  At the
end of the test, you can request specifics on how you did, by subtopic.
And yes, that too is free.

All we ask for in return is your feedback at the end of the exam.  We
will be continually focusing on the improvement of the delivery of the
test and quality of the questions.  Any feedback you can provide,
positive or negative, will help us greatly.

Another FAQ is, "How do you know the person who supposedly took the test
was actually the same person?  What if it was his hotshot 'COBOL II'
Programmer brother, sister or friend?  In other words, what is to
prevent him from cheating?"  Our solution:  we allow employers to
spotcheck the technical workers with a short test at no cost at their
corporate locations, if they so choose.  Corporations have unanimously
applauded this solution.

Lastly, data you provide in the certification process is not used
without your permission other than to store your certification record
and verify to valid employers at their request.  We do, however, publish
names on our site in a Top Ten Scores list for each category, but no
addresses, phone numbers, email, etc.. are shown.  For more information,
see our privacy statement at
http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/privacy.html

Well, that's pretty much it!  We're trying to establish a new
cost-effective and convenient alternative to today's certification
environment.  During this beta trial period, you will be the benefactor
of this fun and challenging experience.

If you have any questions/concerns regarding the 'COBOL II'
certification, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Good luck!

Mike

Michael Littman
VP Operations
TekMetrics, Inc.
www.tekmetrics.com <http://www.tekmetrics.com>
mike.littman@tekmetrics.com <mailto:mike.littman@tekmetrics.com>
```

## Thread: Amusing comment — 30 msgs

### Amusing comment

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
How do you guys like this for a comment?  I found it while doing some Y2K
conversions for a client...

* if not one of these codes letter is not created and it is rejected
     IF C-BR-CODE NOT = 1 AND 2 AND 3 AND 4 AND 7
         MOVE 'NOT LETTER CODE '      TO W40-REASON
         MOVE SPACES                  TO C-BR-RECORD
         MOVE 1                       TO W30-REJECT-IND
         GENERATE REJECT-LINE.

I'll spare you the scary GOTO code...
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

On Wed, 17 Mar 1999 22:29:24 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
wrote:

You are being sarcastic, right?  This really is standard code. 

Personally, I would not use an Evaluate either - I'd make it an 88
level and just have a single condition.

05  C-BR-CODE Pic 9.
      88  Invalid-BR-Code Value 1, 2, 3, 4, 7.


If Invalid-BR-Code
    Move ......
End-if

>I am astonished that it compiled !
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cr7ou$jvf$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <7cpqn1$onn$1@news.igs.net> <36f0f6cd.201170501@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Yes, but only partly.  I do not trust the compiler to do something that I
cannot understand myself, and since I have never written such an abortion, I
tend to assume it will not do what I think it might.  I use brackets a lot
on any compound conditional ... I have been burnt too many times.
I would change:
     IF C-BR-CODE NOT = 1 AND 2 AND 3 AND 4 AND 7
to
     IF (C-BR-CODE  is NOT equal to 1
          AND
          c-br-code is not equal to  2
          AND
          c-br-code is not equal to 3
          AND
          c-br-code is not equal to 4
          AND
          c-br-code is not equal to 7)
...

I *READ*
     IF C-BR-CODE NOT = 1 AND 2 AND 3 AND 4 AND 7
as
     IF (C-BR-CODE  is NOT equal to 1
          or
          c-br-code is not equal to  2
          or
          c-br-code is not equal to 3
          or
          c-br-code is not equal to 4
          or
          c-br-code is not equal to 7)

which is obviously wrong, and obviously would not work.  However, that is
what it says, if one reads English as a first language.  I certainly would
not trust it as it sits, in spite of the fact I understand order of
prcedence rules.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36f0f6cd.201170501@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Wed, 17 Mar 1999 22:29:24 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
>wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Ah, much better. Nine lines of code replacing one. The single line is straight
forward: C-BR-CODE is not part of the set of (1,2,3,4,7) . Why do you read an
'OR' when the code says 'AND' ?

…[70 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be7170$2374de10$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <7cpqn1$onn$1@news.igs.net> <36f0f6cd.201170501@news1.ibm.net> <7cr7ou$jvf$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees <donald@wilmack.com> wrote in article
<7cr7ou$jvf$1@news.igs.net>...

> I *READ*
>      IF C-BR-CODE NOT = 1 AND 2 AND 3 AND 4 AND 7
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> what it says, if one reads English as a first language.  I certainly
would
> not trust it as it sits, in spite of the fact I understand order of
> prcedence rules.

I do not understand how you could interpret it that way, but then English
is not my first language.  I see this as a matter of transitivity rather
than presedence.  You seem to regard the 'NOT' and the '=' as two separate
operators in this case, and you assign transitivity to the '=' operator but
not to the 'NOT' operator.  OK, it looks like I took your point after all.

If 'NOT =' is taken as a single compound operator, the original meaning is
obvious.

Not long ago I found a statement like this in a program I debugged:

IF A NOT = 1 OR 2 OR 3

and a little investigation revealed that the programmer obviously had
meant:

IF ( A NOT = 1 ) AND ( A NOT = 2 ) AND ( A NOT = 3 )

which could have been written in the form that amuses (some of) us:

IF A NOT = 1 AND 2 AND 3

The funniest thing is that the program had been in production for several
years and nobody had noticed anything unusual.

The most elegant solution, as one poster demonstrated, is to use an 88
level symbol having all the relevant values.  But guess what the compiler
produces from that!  In plain English:

IF C-BR-CODE = 1 GOTO FALSE.
IF C-BR-CODE = 2 GOTO FALSE.
IF C-BR-CODE = 3 GOTO FALSE.
...
true clause.
FALSE.

which is correct, of course, and spelled out in clear, like some posters
preferred.

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cs23l$4ob$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <7cpqn1$onn$1@news.igs.net> <36f0f6cd.201170501@news1.ibm.net> <7cr7ou$jvf$1@news.igs.net> <01be7170$2374de10$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```

Gunnar Opheim wrote in message <01be7170>I do not understand how you could
interpret it that way, but then English
>is not my first language.  I see this as a matter of transitivity rather
>than presedence.  You seem to regard the 'NOT' and the '=' as two separate
>operators in this case, and you assign transitivity to the '=' operator but
>not to the 'NOT' operator.  OK, it looks like I took your point after all.


The problem for me, in simply reading the code, is that English does do the
latter. For example, If I say
it is not black and white, the mathematical sense is
not (black and white).  It does not mean ((not black) and (not white)).
Computer languages, of course, follow more stringent rules of precedence.
However, I read code almost as fast as I read English ... about 250 pages an
hour.  That is why I would write out more complex conditionals with
brackets.
```

#### ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F10A62.DFD0A571@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
Let me see:
1.  It doesn't tell us anything about the reason for the code.
2.  It does tell us what the intent is if we want to debug his
intentions as to what the "NOT" applies to (I always use parentheses
instead of relying on the default - simply to make my intentions obvious
to maintenance programmers).
3.  If you're using a system where caps are important, it could have the
effect of turning caps off, causing errors (MVS/TSO).

I am a strong believer that comments should not tell us what we already
know, skip the obvious and be useful!!!!!

Of course, this means code should be written to be obvious (my comment
#2 above).

R. Hayes wrote:
> 
> How do you guys like this for a comment?  I found it while doing some Y2K
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll spare you the scary GOTO code...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<05E80BCEDD9BB525.B494228EC1403ACF.837BDEE2271601D3@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <36f10f06.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
On Thu, 18 Mar 1999 07:36:13 -0700, "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
enlightened us:

>Well, it looks like I not started a short thread that is longer than I
>thought and it is long.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While the code may produce the desired results, I agree with everyone
who said it is hard to read.  I have two rules.  If you can avoid
using NOT, do so; always code the IF so that it tests the most likely
condition to occur.  Hence, I would rewrite the code as:

IF C-BR-CODE EQUAL 1 OR 2 OR 3 OR 4 OR 7
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
         MOVE 'NOT LETTER CODE '      TO W40-REASON
         MOVE SPACES                  TO C-BR-RECORD
         MOVE 1                       TO W30-REJECT-IND
         GENERATE REJECT-LINE.

Cheers,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Only Dead Fish Go With The Flow.

 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be70de$032109a0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <36f071db.1432169@news.pond.com>`

```
Ray Greenberg <raygberg@pond.com> wrote in article
<36f071db.1432169@news.pond.com>...
> >
> >* if not one of these codes letter is not created and it is rejected
> >     IF C-BR-CODE NOT = 1 AND 2 AND 3 AND 4 AND 7
> 
> AARRRRRRGHH!!  It makes so MAD when I see things like that!!  

Why?  This was the shorthand in old Cobol.
Now you could code:

EVALUATE C-BR-CODE
WHEN 1 THRU 4
WHEN 7 CONTINUE
WHEN OTHER
   reject
END-EVALUATE


Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Walter Willis" <wwillis@foxinternet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f14833.0@news.prostar.com>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <36f071db.1432169@news.pond.com> <01be70de$032109a0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36f071e3@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Rule #2 of Cobol programming: Thou Shalt not use not
   You can always code an if statement to be 'positive' as in
       IF C-BR-CODE = 1 OR 2 OR 3 OR 4 OR 7
           CONTINUE
       ELSE
            DO WHATEVER
       END-IF

Yes, evaluate works as well.  This is just another way of writting it.

Walter L. Willis; C.C.P.
wwillis@foxinternet.net

- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cqumo$i0h$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <36f071db.1432169@news.pond.com> <01be70de$032109a0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36f071e3@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
In article <36f071e3@news3.us.ibm.net>,
  "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:
> so you replace 1 line by 5, thus boosting your
> productivity (LOC) by a factor of five !
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There's more to productivity than how many lines of code one uses to solve a
problem.  If the 30 seconds it take to key in the extra 5 lines of code saves
30 minutes of debugging and thinking through the complexities of a compound
NOT, then writing the extra lines is more efficient.

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F10E3F.62262547@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <36f071db.1432169@news.pond.com> <01be70de$032109a0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
Two things wrong with it:
1.  The coder does not know how to make useful comments.
2.  It costs nothing to put in parenthesis, and makes the maintenance
programmer's job easier - knowing that you meant what you got.  I would
tend to use NEXT SENTENCE to do this logic, with various choices on
different lines (easier to comment out).  (Or a CONTINUE if we don't
choose to use EVALUATE (I would probably use an 88 level and an IF for
this - but I have to look at the requirements to be sure))



Gunnar Opheim wrote:
> 
> Ray Greenberg <raygberg@pond.com> wrote in article
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gunnar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cqucr$9k3@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36f03de8.0@news.cadvision.com> <36f0f941.7374624@news3.ibm.net>`

```
For those who are (or were) on IBM Mainframe compilers - OS/VS COBOL or
DOS/VS COBOL,  this is the very close to (the same as?) the case that
LANGLVL(1) versus LANGLVL(2) give different semantics to - although both
accept it as valid source code.  (This reflects either  a "true" or
interpretation difference between the '68 and '74 COBOL Standards. - The
issue concerns whether the "NOT" was transitive or not across the rest of
the combined abbreviated relational condition.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F24AA8.17D3342C@zip.com.au>`

Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
..trimmed for clarity...
> >* if not one of these codes letter is not created and it is rejected

> trial....  But I see nothing wrong with this syntax, nor with the comment
> 


If not not???  I ain't going to accept no double negatives!

The comment adds confusion as much as the code.  I think the code is OK
as is but it might be better written as:

	88 valid-letters values 1,2,3,4,7
	if not valid-letters

Alternatively (I assume this compiles correctly)

	if CODE not = (1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and 7)

This would make the intent of a set of values clearer.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Amusing comment

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f38931@news3.us.ibm.net>`

news:36F24AA8.17D3342C@zip.com.au...
> The comment adds confusion as much as the code.  I think the code is OK
> as is but it might be better written as:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> This would make the intent of a set of values clearer.

Actually not, as it should be:
if code not = (1 or 2 or 3 or 4 or 7)

which however is a syntax error.
I often write it like this:

if code = 1 or 2 or 3 or 4 or 7
       next sentence
else
       do something
.

The 'best' solution is the 88-condition
88 valid-values values 1 thru 4, 7.
```

<a id="t-17f81667d2"></a>
## Thread: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!! — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cp1ku$l9b@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au>`

```
By now, Moose, you have had a lot of answers. The closest one had a
reference to water cooled computers.  Even that reference was just a jab and
missed the mark.

1. Early computers did not come with an operating system as we know of
today.  For almost a decade, the programmer was responsible for keeping
track of the number of tapes in a file, the beginning and ending of a file,
block counts, and anything else needed. We in the Univac I and II crowd (who
had water cooled computers) had to write all of our I/O, including an
interface to the "run to run" locator on the drive that held the program
library.

When you started an application, the operator (and often the programmer
responsible for the application) would mount a program library tape, and a
tape carrying variables shared by the chain of runs that were in that
library.  The first program run was one that made a "working tape library"
for production. An example of the function of this run is that each "word"
of the library tape was
inspected for the value "TODAY_______" and replaced with a value
representing the date to be used for the production run.

2. Tape jockeys would find the grand father tape(s) for the first program in
a chain, and mount it on the drive the operat;ing instructions specified,
and then mount blanks everywhere else needed. Note: other input files might
be included.

3. Each program was responsible for checking a label record on the first
block on the tape to determine if it was the correct file, and if not rewind
the tape, and notify the operator to mount the correct tape.

4. Each program that created output tapes was responsible for labeling each
output correctly, and that included labels for the beginning and ending of
each tape. The ending label carried unique information that told the next
program if this was a final tape, or an intermediate tape.

5. When we got smart enough to provide recovery points, the
program would also write a memory image on the end of the tape so that the
program could be restarted anywhere in the process.

While I may have missed something, and my memory of it may be somewhat
skewed, that's the sum and substance of standard tape labels.  They became
the audit trail of a highly manual computer operation.  Twenty years later,
many sites had operating systems that did it all including staging tapes and
disk files.  COBOL reflects it's roots in these two statements.

I hope that computer operations still has some way of insuring their files
are what they think they are and ways to check this before removing a file
from a system for use elsewhere.

Warren Simmons
Moose wrote in message <36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au>...
>
>I have been trying to find out about the LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED /
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F1EC19.22CF@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au> <7cp1ku$l9b@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> By now, Moose, you have had a lot of answers. The closest one had a
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> from a system for use elsewhere.

And we young programmers think we're so clever.  I'm not sure how I would have
managed back in those days!

Anyway, reminds me of an interesting program we have in our shop.  Basically, it's
pretty simple.  The program reads an input tape which contains some records for the
month, then reads in today records and writes both to today's output tape.  At the
end of the month a report is produced based on the final tape, which contains an
entire months worth of records.  (At the beginning of the month the input tape is
ignored.)

Problem...On Tuesdays after a Monday holiday the operators would often load the Monday
tape as the input, rather than the correct Friday tape.  Since Monday was a holiday it
actually contained data from the Monday eight days ago.

So one of our (old time, 20+ years programming) programmers came up with a solution.
Each of the five (Monday thru Friday) tapes was given a label which contained the day of
the week.  Both the input and the output tape files were given *two* file control entries 
and *two* file section entries, one with LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD and one with LABEL 
RECORDS ARE OMITTED.  He would open the "label omitted" files as input and read in the
first records, which just happens to *be* the label.  He would check today's day of
the week, check for a holiday the previous day, and make sure the correct day of the
week tapes were loaded, both for the input and the output dates.  If they were wrong
he'd give an error and abort.  If they were correct he'd close both files and then
open the "standard labels" tapes as input and output respectively and proceed with
processing.

On the same note, since this terminology seems to have gone away in COBOL II and later
(or has it?), I wonder how I could accomplish this same thing if I convert this program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7csond$g8k@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I had to look it up, but "Label Records are Omitted" is still valid syntax
according to the latest IBM LRM for their latest compiler.  It does indicate
that it is not valid for "line sequential" files and is otherwise ignored in
their Workstation (OS/2, NT, and Unix) products.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <kerrye@ms.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F2A1D5.787913CF@ms.com>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au> <7cp1ku$l9b@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <36F1EC19.22CF@sprynet.com>`

```
> On the same note, since this terminology seems to have gone away in COBOL II
>   and later
>   (or has it?), I wonder how I could accomplish this same thing if I convert this
>   program.
>

Although "Label Records Omitted" might still compile successfully, it would
have no effect.  However, if you get some clever bits-and-bytes Systems
Programmer-type to help you out, you could get him/her to write you an
Assembler subroutine to call using the file-name as as input parameter
(which is still legal in the IBM COBOL for MVS & OS/390 world provided
that the file is for a non-VSAM sequential (i.e.: QSAM) dataset.  The
routine would be called after the file has been opened in the COBOL prog-
ram; it would issue the RDJFCB macro and return the contents of the
JFCBCRDT (CReation DaTe) field. QED

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F4F778.27B0CC54@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au> <7cp1ku$l9b@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <36F1EC19.22CF@sprynet.com> <alGI2.384$NB5.974347@storm.twcol.com> <36F47491.791E@sprynet.com>`

```
GenerationDataGroup. For instance, to reference the current version of the GDG you
could code:
//FILEA DD DSN=GLD.AUDIT.HISTORY(0) ....
To create a new one:
//FILEA DD DSN=GLD.AUDIT.HISTORY(+1) ....
Prior:
//FILEA DD DSN=GLD.AUDIT.HISTORY(-1) ....
The operating system converts the ([+/-] n) to GnnnnV00 where the nnnn is a sequence
number.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F58A73.60A92F59@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au> <7cp1ku$l9b@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <36F1EC19.22CF@sprynet.com>`

```


Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> Warren Simmons wrote:
...
> > While I may have missed something, and my memory of it may be somewhat
> > skewed, that's the sum and substance of standard tape labels.  They became
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> (or has it?), I wonder how I could accomplish this same thing if I convert this program.

The LABEL RECORDS clause in COBOL is obsolete not because labels have
disappeared but because they are managed via JCL (Job Control Language)
and their use is so ubiquitous that allowing their presence or absence
to be specified at the program level is no longer reasonable.  Standard
tape labels are essential both to automated tape management systems and
to security management systems designed to manage and protect data
residing on tape media.

At least in the case of IBM operating systems, all DASD datasets have
"labels" which define the external dataset name and characteristics of
the dataset.  Also all shops of any reasonable size use standard tape
labels (with the occasional exception of data interchange with some
other installations with conflicting standards).  Tape labels involve
volume header label records (uniquely identifies the physical volume and
hopefully agrees with any external label on the tape), dataset header
records and dataset trailer records, which contain the dataset name and
various dataset characteristics. There are also standard label
conventions for indicating multi-file tape volumes and multi-volume tape
files.  The JCL directing program execution establishes the relationship
between program "file" names, and an external dataset name and media
used to store the dataset.  Some kind of tape management system (TMS) is
invariably used to track which tapes are scratch vs those with current
datasets.  For output tapes, the TMS guarantees that the tape mounted
for output is really a scratch tape.  For input tapes, either the TMS,
the operating system, or both determine the physical tape(s) that should
contain the requested dataset, and the label records on the tape are
used to verify that the correct tape was actually mounted by the
operator and that the tape actually contains the requested dataset name.

If you really wanted to handle a weekday master-in/master-out job using
unique labels (i.e, unique dataset names) for each day of the week, you
would use unique JCL for each day of the week (with different dataset
names) to drive the program execution, with a special version to use
after a holiday.  If what you really want is to always use the previous
run's output as input and only run the job on nonholiday weekdays, then
much simpler solutions are available.  Most TMS's will allow you have
several active datasets with the same name, and there are techniques
that can be used to have an input request be satisfied by the most
recent version while an output request for the same name goes to an
available scratch tape.  In the MVS arena, you also have the choice of
using a Generation Dataset Group (GDG), where the JCL contains a fixed
dataset name, but the real dataset name is forced unique by the
operating system adding a unique sequentially-assigned part to the end
of the name (called a "goo-voo" number because they are values like
G0001V00, G0002V00, G0003V00, etc.).  GDG's have the advantage that JCL
need only specify "relative" generation numbers (like -1, 0 , +1), and
multiple generations other than the most current and next (new) can also
be easily referenced in JCL.
```

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <kerrye@ms.com>

The latest, greatest and fully supported version of this utility
has been folded into standard ISPF/PDF (version 4) as options
3.12 and 3.13, the SuperCompare functions.  3.13 supports
background submission; i.e.: a batch job is submitted whose
guts one can examine after the job has run its course.  The
program name has been changed into a "standard" IBM form
and is now "ISRSUPC."

<a id="t-5697c0f5c0"></a>
## Thread: COBOL/400: searching info on NO LOCK clause for START — 1 msgs

### COBOL/400: searching info on NO LOCK clause for START

- **From:** Rob Schluter <rob@loi.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7coi82$ino$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm writing some generic COPY members to handle LOCK situations, and found
that the START statement in ILE COBOL has a NO LOCK clause. This will cause
the START not to lock a record.

In OPM COBOL the documentation doesn't mention the NO lOCK clause, but the
compiler seems to handle it the same as the ILE compiler. I've looked at
several versions of the documentation on IBM's AS/400 online library but all
versions I checked (v3r7 - v4r3) point to the same  COBOL/400 reference.

Can anyone tell me if there's a more up to date  COBOL/400 reference available
somewhere, or if the NO LOCK clause is an undocumented feature?

Thanks,
Rob

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: New easy to use & powerful Cobol Analyzer

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F0E9FF.367DE048@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36EF1724.298D383@netcom.ca> <36EF9EBD.2E9E6559@zip.com.au> <36f0abc4.1644504@news.easynet.fr>`

```
jean-michel wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 17 Mar 1999 23:23:25 +1100, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, i do !

OK for my first step basic concept:

The parser will contain a basic syntax parser  It will find every
division statement and have a parser for each.  If the parser des not
understand the code then it gives up, it assumes that the program
compiles so does not need to be smart, and you have the code and can
easily fix it.

release 1 will ignore anything between EXEC and END-EXEC.  Special case
later we might make it a bit smarter for SQL especially.

The parser for identification is simple: program id only (MVS extension
is handling the with debug and with recursion).  All else is obsolete.

The next is the environment division:

the data division has three sections

configuration section, object computer:   obsolete

Special-names:  ??

Input-output section:

File control paragraph:  File details marking obsolete bits and
recording others.

I-O-CONTROL.   ??? never user it ???  first pass of code ignores
entirely.

Data division:  has file section, working storage section, linkage
section (and obsolete communication where does this go)

The file section contains:  FD (with obsolete bits) and data definition.

Data definition is number, fieldname, PIC, + keywords= COMP-3, COMP,
BINARY, VALUES, etc.  (Oh look my cobol for MVS manual has 'justified
right' as a keyword - cool).

That's far enough for now:

In order to accurately parse the cobol code we use a directed parser. 
It looks at the first word and then assumes that it is that sort of
statement. (This gets harder in procedure with no dot to separate it.)

The parser will accept a list of keywords that are assigned a magic
number e.g.:  ZERO, ZEROS, ZEROES are all value zero, PIC and PICTURE
are value 1, etc.   The numeric value is defined by the cobol source
code but the load able table makes it slightly more extensible.

When we parse a variable name (defined by definition context) then the
variable text is stored internally to the program and defined as a
number.  When a variable is read in code it is parsed to VARIABLE,
VARIABLE-NUMBER.  This make the parser a bit easier to break out.  If a
variable is defined as the same value as a reserved word then it will
never be located in code as the reserved word look up is first.

The variable definition will have a fixed number of fields in it:

Internal reference number (implicit by index)

Level number.

text Pic x(32)  (any advances...)

pic descriptive.

Comp type  11=display, 12=comp, 13=Binary, 14=packed, 15=comp-3. 
(values defined from the reserved words table but again specific to the
parser program.  This means (again) that computational-3 and COMP-3 have
the same value.  Note that 88 get a special value of boolean.

Blank-when-zero clause (blank token assumes it is followed by when token
and then zero token, if it does not get it it dies with a message).

Value clause:  (String parsing is an issue here, reformatting it back
out in a pretty printer program will be interesting as well).

Justification clause:

Occurs clause, index, order by, depending:  Note that the depending
clause may 'predefine' a variable so there is a special case to handle a
later definition of this.

Redefines:

Sign clause:  trailing/leading, separate/not defined

Synchronized:  obsolete.

.....

Over to you, comment, rip apart, make better, or just bag it.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New easy to use & powerful Cobol Analyzer

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F10DB7.849C83A2@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36EF1724.298D383@netcom.ca> <36EF9EBD.2E9E6559@zip.com.au> <36f0abc4.1644504@news.easynet.fr> <36F0E9FF.367DE048@zip.com.au>`

```
I don't know if I will ever use a Cobol analyser, but I often change my
compile options to include MAP, to verify that all of my record sizes
are what I want.  A parser could check all moves (including READ INTO)
and make sure record sizes are constant.

I tend to include a RECORD CONTAINS clause in my FD to get compiler
warnings.  Or better yet to make sure future changes have this check.
```

Everest-Tools wrote:
> 
> Why never use a Cobol analyser.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I don't know if I will ever use a Cobol analyser, but I often change my
...

No I don't.  But this may be partially due to my editor.  I've used
various editors with different languages and different environments. 
One feature I have never found in a PC editor I use extensively in TSO. 
That is the ability to exclude lines from view.  TSO is based upon 80
column cards and has line commands including exclude.  I can exclude as
many lines as I want, coming up with data which looks like this:

sssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssssss
 EDIT       UMSDEV.R1ABC.LIB(SIPR624) - 01.02               4 CHARS
'COUNT-103'
 Command ===>                                                  Scroll
===> CSR
 ****** ***************************** Top of Data
******************************
 - - -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   169 Line(s) not
Displayed
 000170 017000     05  COUNT-103       PIC S9(09)   COMP-3 VALUE ZERO.
 - - -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   125 Line(s) not
Displayed
 000296 029600         ADD 1              TO COUNT-103
 - - -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   258 Line(s) not
Displayed
 000555 055500     MOVE COUNT-103         TO DISPLAY-COUNT1.
 - - -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  1 Line(s) not
Displayed
 000557 055700     DISPLAY ' COUNT-103 = ' DISPLAY-COUNT1
 - - -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  - 12 Line(s) not
Displayed
 ****** **************************** Bottom of Data
****************************
(I typed in the command X ALL;F COUNT-103 ALL)

Combined with split screens and structured methodology (and decades of
experience), COBOL is very readable.

I wonder what RPG editors look like nowadays.  The last time I used RPG
was RPGII which was very column dependent - with different columns
meaning different things on different lines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New easy to use & powerful Cobol Analyzer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36FE0396.2664EB41@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<36EF1724.298D383@netcom.ca> <36EF9EBD.2E9E6559@zip.com.au> <36f0abc4.1644504@news.easynet.fr> <36F0E9FF.367DE048@zip.com.au> <36F10DB7.849C83A2@NOSPAMhome.com> <36F178F2.C0A8D51D@netcom.ca> <36F25B52.5D56A6E7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36F6B4AD.30BF7C80@IMN.nl> <36f9b85b@199.186.16.51>`

```
In earlier years, when I wordked with MF's work bench, I indeed used SPF/PC.
It's editor could be integrated in the workbench (replacing the standard
WrkBnch editor)) by replacing the standard EDIT.LBR with the specially written
EDIT.LBR provided by the builders of SPF/PC.

The other way around was a bit more tricky but possible: From the command line
of SPF/PC the MF-compiler was callable, compiling the source being edited. The
embodying of the resulting messages had to be done by issuing the command-line
command MSGFILE xxx.MSG, if memory serves me well. That was the tricky part:
one had to setup some compile option (directive) to produce a .MSG file and
after each compile one should thus tell the editor to read it.

Now-a-days I use NetExpress. Allthough it has still lots of workbench-behaviour
in it (those guys heavily re-used their software: the compiler directives, the
possible settings using environment variables, lots of other tricks and
features), NE is certainly not the same as the workbench. ... Now I come to
think of it, I'll look if there still is and "EDIT.LBR".
(looking around on my C: ...)
Nope. No EDIT.LBR

Besides, the NE source code editor has functionality enough for me so I no
longer feel a strong need to use others. And, what has SPF/PC more than source
code colourization and some elementary column handling? (I know the product up
to version 3). NetExpress uses the edit window also while debugging and has put
a lot of specific COBOL-oriented and own-product-oriented features in it. I
doubt if SPF/PC has that as a replacement-editor or makes it worthwile to use
their editor outside the NE IDE.

Anybody out there who knows if it is still possible to plug in another editor
in NetExpress?

The Frog

gee wrote:

> why not use ISPF-PC?
> COBOL Frog (Huib Klink) wrote in message <36F6B4AD.30BF7C80@IMN.nl>...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

Not exactly responsive to Huib's question - but I think that the "theory" at
MERANT is that if you want an ISPF "look-alike" editor, then you would be
using Mainframe Express.  If you want NetExpress - then you don't want to
work in an ISPF type environment.  I won't swear to this distinction - but I
think it fits in with their general "world view".
```

On Sun, 28 Mar 1999 12:25:26 +0200, "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)"
<H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:

>
<Snip>
>
>Besides, the NE source code editor has functionality enough for me so I no
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<Snip>
>
From the IDE:

1. Options
2. Customize
3. Tools Tab
4. New Tool Button
5. Enter Menu Text
6. Program Button
7. Locate the .EXE for the tool and select it


This will add the selected tool to the Tools option on the IDE.  A
couple of options also available for when the tool is started.  I
didn't investigate them too much since I am very happy with the
NetExpress editor.  I tried this with UltraEdit32 (which I use at work
quite happily) and it works.

Hope this helps.
```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36F10DB7.849C83A2@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I don't know if I will ever use a Cobol analyser, but I often change my
>compile options to include MAP, to verify that all of my record sizes
>are what I want.  A parser could check all moves (including READ INTO)
>and make sure record sizes are constant.

I have taken to coding in error messages that compare record sizes.  I know
that once the code runs, they are a no-op and a total waste of computer
time, but it is saving me grief, hardware is cheap and the overhead
miniscule.

For example:
IF LENGTH OF FILE-RECORD
    NOT EQUAL TO LENGTH OF WS-FILE-RECORD
      DISPLAY UPON CONSOLE
         "Please notify programming that copy book x needs updating".

I would never have considered such a thing five or ten years back, but cost
and time changes all.

I am finding that Fujitsu, with both a screen AND a console on the PC, is
changing my habits.  The console is ideal for that sort of thing, and I
reserve it for that type of function and that type of function only.



>
>I tend to include a RECORD CONTAINS clause in my FD to get compiler
>warnings.  Or better yet to make sure future changes have this check.
```

### This doesn't add up

- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000

```
Have you noticed that all the the talk about Y2K is catastrophic?
Billions of lines of Cobol code?
Shortage of technical staff?

Yet, Cobol programmers are struggling to get 50/hr?

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: This doesn't add up

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36f0f807.201484528@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7cqpvl$eav$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Thu, 18 Mar 1999 12:01:59 GMT, random01@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>Have you noticed that all the the talk about Y2K is catastrophic?
>Billions of lines of Cobol code?
>Shortage of technical staff?
>
>Yet, Cobol programmers are struggling to get 50/hr?

Well, I can understand why.  If I have a car, and I don't have
collision insurance.  Suddenly a truck runs it over.  The estimate for
labor, excluding parts is ... oh, say ... 20 hours.  I can afford,
$600.00 - thats $30.00 an hour.  But all the body shops are charging
$40.00 an hour.  Guess what?  I'm going to either fix it myself, or
drive around in a dented car until I can find someone to work for
less.  Eventually I will too.  The problem with Y2K is that on a fixed
date the car will blow up.  I'm stuck.  If I can't find the funds, or
someone to fix it on time, I better abandon it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This doesn't add up

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-8wGRXjZ7nWuN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7cqpvl$eav$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36f0f807.201484528@news1.ibm.net> <36F2670D.3957740A@ie.ibm.com>`

```
On Fri, 19 Mar 1999 15:02:37, Joan Green <joan_green@ie.ibm.com> 
wrote:

> I don't see all those COBOL houses suddenly dumping all that code!
>

I don't LIKE to hear it, but lets be frank.  I have seen a lot of 
posts here from people with turnkey COBOL systems that HAVE to replace
them with something other than COBOL - why?  They are not Y2K 
compliant, they can't GET a Y2K compliant version - for whatever 
reason - and the systems being sold now, or developed in house are 
clicked up in some DB application or some such.  Heck, they might 
replace it with a COBOL turnkey ap if they find one - but they still 
have to deal with the legacy data.

It's made me think.  I have customers running my stuff. (they don't 
have the source).  I think I'll give it to them (at least the layouts 
and some way to extract the data into something useful).  It's THEIR 
DATA, not mine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This doesn't add up

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cu85e$cms$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7cqpvl$eav$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36f0f807.201484528@news1.ibm.net> <36F2670D.3957740A@ie.ibm.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-8wGRXjZ7nWuN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
I always write two routines into every system.  One is to dump all data into
flat ASCII line sequential files, and the other is to rebuild all data from
flat ASCII line sequential files.

Since I have started to do that, I have found it incredibly useful.  Not
only does it give you a really fast field patch tool (combined with a text
editor), but it pretty well trivializes file conversions from one version to
another. It also covers the customer's butt.


Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Fri, 19 Mar 1999 15:02:37, Joan Green <joan_green@ie.ibm.com>
>wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: This doesn't add up

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Dave Eastabrook <news@elmbronze.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k7hOCAA6Cq82Ewml@elmbronze.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7cqpvl$eav$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36f0f807.201484528@news1.ibm.net> <36F2670D.3957740A@ie.ibm.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-8wGRXjZ7nWuN@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
on Fri, 19 Mar 1999 Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote
>
>It's made me think.  I have customers running my stuff. (they don't 
>have the source).  I think I'll give it to them (at least the layouts 
>and some way to extract the data into something useful).  It's THEIR 
>DATA, not mine.

Object Only is a great concept - keeps control and makes pirating more
difficult.  But there should be some sort of central repository where
software vendors can deposit their source code - securely - in case of
their demise.  For which they'd be able to display and advertise some
form of approval - not that the code was tested or approved, but just
that they'd taken steps to protect their customers for just such a
situation as now.  Such a repository would have to be very strongly
protected and trustworthy, and preferably totally international.

Perhaps there already is one.  If so - please let me know :)

:Dave
```

<a id="t-d61f85d75a"></a>
## Thread: Whats Happening with Y2K? — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Whats Happening with Y2K?

- **From:** grobertsjr@aol.com (Grobertsjr)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990318092357.11705.00000007@ng55.aol.com>`

```
Two years ago I took a course in COBOL because there was supposed to be all
kinds of opportunity to do Y2K work. Afterwards, I went on a few interviews,
but no one was interested in hiring anyone without experience.

Today, you see Y2K in the news daily, but the COBOL job situation has remained
the same. Is the problem not as bad as the media portrays, has Big Business
just buried its head, or what's the story?

George
```

#### ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990318184755.14874.00000161@ng-ft1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990318092357.11705.00000007@ng55.aol.com>`

```
yes and yes
```

#### ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pIrI2.9443$Ek.11623938@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990318092357.11705.00000007@ng55.aol.com>`

```
George wrote:
>Two years ago I took a course in COBOL because there was supposed to be all
>kinds of opportunity to do Y2K work. Afterwards, I went on a few interviews,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>just buried its head, or what's the story?


Right now I am neck deep in Y2K remediation, and have been for about
a year.  But there is something I have noticed in all the fracas.  Not
one of the people I can remember crying wolf over Y2K was a programmer
directly involved in the Y2K effort.  I even started a thread recently
asking Y2K programmers in this newsgroup to say if their company/clients
were in good or bad shape, and every single response was that their
company/clients Y2K efforts were either complete or in good order on
conservative schedules.  I don't have a clue how the rest of the world
is doing, or how embedded systems are doing.  But for mainline business
in the U.S., the people who are making the noise are not the ones in
the best position to know.  I would be much more concerned if the ones
actually doing the work seemed worried.  My clients are in good shape.
If anybody here has *direct* knowledge of a different story, I would
like to hear it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F25EF7.23AFF0A7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990318092357.11705.00000007@ng55.aol.com> <pIrI2.9443$Ek.11623938@news1.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Right now I am neck deep in Y2K remediation, and have been for about
> a year.  But there is something I have noticed in all the fracas.  Not
> one of the people I can remember crying wolf over Y2K was a programmer
> directly involved in the Y2K effort.  

I can see that.  But I wonder how many people have turn-key software and
no programmers who will be in trouble.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kqNI2.4685$q82.3077258@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<AdvI2.9837$Ek.11751403@news1.mia> <19990319233314.00429.00000261@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote:
>
>I, for one, am "hands on" and see and hear lots of scary things.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>in this situation.

You are correct.  None of my clients are 'really big' companies.  As
I said in my first post of the thread, I was making a point that none
of the people I had heard 'crying wolf' are actually programmers who
are remediating Y2K.  A few weeks ago I started a thread in the COBOL
newsgroups asking specifically of the programmers who are working on
Y2K what the status of their companies/clients were.  Every single
response was that their company/clients were either completed with
Y2K or very close to it.  This is the consistent pattern I have seen
for some time.  Perhaps the scared ones aren't talking.  But if your
experience is different, I for one would love to hear about it, if
you are free to tell us (no names, of course). :-)

When you say "see and hear lots of scary things", I assume you mean
that you are directly aware of the "scary things", and you are not
going by rumors?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d1tj0$44q$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<AdvI2.9837$Ek.11751403@news1.mia> <19990319233314.00429.00000261@ng-ca1.aol.com> <kqNI2.4685$q82.3077258@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...

>When you say "see and hear lots of scary things", I assume you mean
>that you are directly aware of the "scary things", and you are not
>going by rumors?


Here's what's scary: NOT ONE SINGLE COFFEE BEAN IS GROWN
IN THE USA. If many foreign systems have not even started on Y2K
remediation (as the US Senate reports), what becomes of my coffee?

On a more serious side, 80% of the ingredients in pharmaceuticals
come from outside the US. Venezuela can't even SPELL Y2K (let alone
fix it) and provides us with 10% of our petroleum needs. (Again,
courtesy of the   US Senate report.) There are many, many products
that must come from overseas inasmuch as they are not manufactured
in the US (TVs, VCRs, Microwave ovens, etc.).

Woe is us.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f476a9.26274857@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<AdvI2.9837$Ek.11751403@news1.mia> <19990319233314.00429.00000261@ng-ca1.aol.com> <kqNI2.4685$q82.3077258@news3.mia>`

```
On Sat, 20 Mar 1999 13:30:56 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>When you say "see and hear lots of scary things", I assume you mean
>that you are directly aware of the "scary things", and you are not
>going by rumors?

The higher ups (really high) in our company probably don't KNOW what's
been going on.  I have prevented some Y2K failures already.  As it
sits right now, I know of only one thing that must be done in the
systems that I have responsibility for, to prevent any Y2K problems.
There were a handfull of items that I already fixed.  Some of these
would have gone unnoticed until after the rollover, but would have
started happening in January this year.  We have some billing records
that are once a year.  When we bill, we roll them into the next year.
The very next step deleted any records from this recurring billing
file (It does monthly also) that are over 3 months old.  In January
the yearly items would have been rolled into year 00 (it's YYMM) and
then would have been quietly deleted.  I changed the delete to only
delete if YY > 90, so as not to roll off any of these items.  But very
few people even know it would have happened.  I suspect this type of
thing IS happening in some places, and they won't find out till they
wonder where those yearly billing records went!

 We have the misforture of having to incorportate some purchased
business into our own, and it was NOT compliant in the least.  I have
fixed that as I have moved it.  I do not think that Y2K will go over
for us without a hitch, but I don't see it being any worse than the
daily struggles I encounter just keeping all the disparate peices from
beating each other to death.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Whats Happening with Y2K?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
It's super that you were agressive in correcting these date-related
flaws.  Unfortuantely, for most organizations, "higher up" management is,
at best, reluctant to even deal with the Y2K problem.  They do not want to
admit there is a problem, because 1) there is no monetary return for the
money they would need to spend to fix the problem, and 2) their jobs would
be on the line.  This attitude should not be surprising, as most
management decisions are reactice, instead of proactive.

In article <36f476a9.26274857@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

> On Sat, 20 Mar 1999 13:30:56 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
> <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> beating each other to death.
```

problem and then if they think the solution to the problem will cost more than
their annual salary (less bonus) without doing an analysis of their own will
generally reject your analysis for one that better fits the company "fortune". 
Instead of Fixing the problem they may elect to band-aid it or reject it
outright.  And yes I have worked for the Govt, and for private sector...and the
problem is pervasive.

TW

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is learning Computer Programming hard?

- **From:** DLPolzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F1B770.7BAD@coredcs.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
 > 
 > On Thu, 18 Mar 1999 18:00:29 GMT, "The Real."
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
 > Something I should have relied on just a few posts ago!

Math for programming?  All you need is a year of high school geometry
for logic skills and a year of high school algebra to understand the
relationship of positive and negative numbers.  For business
programming, some courses in accounting will be more beneficial than a
lot of high powered math.

================================
Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
================================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thanks a lot, you kind people.....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x7sJ2.149$1h3.65591@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<IQmI2.9139$Ek.11504682@news1.mia> <19990321042415.27254.00000743@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
CarlosS940 wrote:
>programming game application is much harder than programing business
>application.  Anyone agree?

Games take a half dozen programmers several months to complete.  Some
business applications take fifty or a hundred programmers *years* to
complete.  See a pattern?  You think a game program is more difficult
than a payroll/personnel system for a city with 100,000 employees and
seventy-five unions to deal with?  Some business logic is so convoluted
by illogical laws, regulations and rules that it is almost impossible
to even *define* the correct logic, let alone program it.  Sometimes
you can't even find out what the correct logic is because it is so
complex and obscure that no one can tell you.  Then you have to program
by 'best guess' and correct errors as they crop up.  I and an associate
had to design and program a system like that a couple of years ago.
The client loves the system, but there is a certain small part of it
which may never function completely as they want.  A totally correct
logic simply does not exist to satisfy all the conflicting rules and
exceptions and exceptions to the exceptions in the boneheaded state
regulations.

A game takes the form the designers want, and they are free to modify
their design to fit the situation.  In business programming the system
must take a form dictated from outside, often by non technical people
who have no clue what the ramifications are of one design decision vs.
another, and do not care, or even want to know or hear.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thanks a lot, you kind people.....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F5954A.57A32A32@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<1bbI2.1975$q82.1947416@news3.mia> <36f1ab97.247459642@news1.ibm.net> <36F1B770.7BAD@coredcs.com> <IQmI2.9139$Ek.11504682@news1.mia>`

```
"The Real." wrote:
> Thanks.
> DLPolzin wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>

Analysis and design of complex systems really requires much more math
sophistication than basic algebra.  Yeah, he will be able to write code,
but when he unknowingly implements an exponential-time or factorial-time
algorithm for a large problem he will be clueless if you try to explain
why just because his test data for n=20 ran OK he shouldn't try a
production run for n=1,000,000!  Also, the concept that response time
may approach infinity as resource usage of a DASD drive approaches 100%
is a difficult concept for the mathematically challenged.

If you allow someone with such minimal skills to do systems analysis and
design, you will eventually pay the price.
```

## Thread: Please Do My Job/Homework? — 76 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Please Do My Job/Homework?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<7cr32c$l2c$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```

Alright, gang.. I've got a moderately juicy one upon which I would
appreciate a modicum of assistance.

What I've been doing lately is only tangentially COBOL-related... but it
beats the heck outta Y2K remediation for holding my interest!  The company
I've contracted with has Stuff 'n Processing on the mainframe and Stuff 'n
Processing on the PC (actually networked NTs) and data need to be bounced
from one to the other.  I've set up a series of four mechanisms which
kinda sorta maybe boil down to these steps:

The geepo in the warehouse figures 'Hey, time to download the latest set
of orders that were entered on the mainframe!'... and double-clicks on an
icon I've set up for this purpose.

The icon invokes a batch file.

The batch file deletes the previous-previous order file, renames the
previous order file 'OldOrd' (or something like that) and invokes ftp
using the -s option and the mainframe's IP address as the target.

The file specified by the -s option logs onto the mainframe and submits a
job to the internal reader.

The job submitted to the internal reader executes a proc (one of those
plain ol' proc-type procs, in SYS.PROD.PROCLIB)

The executed proc consists of two steps: an IEFBR14 (don't ask me why but
the guys in Ops said this 'dummy step' was necessary) and an invocation of
UCC7 (PGM=U7SVC)... and the PARM of this invocation specifies some JCL
which UCC7 is supposed to submit.

(note: this is done so a record of the JCL's run will be kept by UCC7)

The JCL submitted by the invocation of UCC7 by the proc executed by the
JCL submitted to the internal reader by the ftp invoked by the batch file
executed by the operator's clicking on the NT icon (still with me so far?)
invokes another proc which does the actual work... for the sake of clarity
I'll call this proc FRED.

FRED, at heart, consists of two steps... a COBOL job (which I wrote... see
where the 'lang.cobol' fits in?) and another ftp.  The COBOL job runs
against the mainframe databases (DataComm), extracts the orders that the
warehouse-gang needs and dumps these to a flat-file.  The flat-file is
then ftp'd by the next step back down to the PC so the warehouse crew can
take care of their own jobs.

Now that I've explained all that I can get to the guts of my request.  The
ftp step in the proc FRED uses a standard sort of SYSIN member from
SYS.PROD.CARDLIB.  This member directs the ftp to the target system... in
this case, the IP address of the NT workstation which originated the
request.

The latest installation I'm working on has *2* NT workstations, Primary
and Backup.  Under normal circumstances everything goes to Primary, of
course... but when (*not* 'if', 'when') the Primary drops the Backup gets
used.  Everything about the processing initiated by either Primary or
Backup is identical EXCEPT... except for the one line in the CARD member
which directs the mainframe ftp back to the originating workstation.  In
other words, when Primary is used the first line of the CARD member will
be, say, 100.100.100.101 (Primary's IP address) and when Backup is used
this needs to be 100.100.100.102.

I'm looking for as elegant a way to deal with this as possible.  The most
ready solution would be to have two versions of FRED, FREDPRIM and
FREDBACK, and the only difference between the two would be in the SYSIN
for the ftp step.. FREDA would pull in SYS.PROD.CARDLIB(FTPPRIM), FREDB
would pull in SYS.PROD.CARDLIB(FTPBACK) and the only difference in these
CARD members would be the IP address mentioned above.

The more elegant solution would be to be able to supply, via override,
either the DD for the ftp step in FRED or the suffix of the member-name
via symbolic... but I'm not sure if such a thing can be preserved through
the layer upon layer of JCL and proc and UCC7 and JCL and proc that I've
described.

Any suggestions?

DD
```

- **References:** `<7cr32c$l2c$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
My JCL..very bad.

But couldn't you also have the NT ftp the SYSIN card
for FRED, identifying itself with its own IP address?

Hoffmeister
