<a id="t-0eff65ba0f"></a>
## Thread: Evaluate Question — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Evaluate Question

- **From:** "Martin Ruston" <martin@nisie-1.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<921782408.3125.0.nnrp-07.c1ed7479@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Hello,

I`m new to programming and have been studying Cobol for the last few months now,
I am currently working on a problem which requires a table recording the number
of passes, fails, etc, for an exam to be filled.

I`ve used the following piece of code to evaluate the results, repeated for
paper-2 and paper-3;

EVALUATE PAPER-1
    WHEN 0 THRU 39
        ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (1, FAIL)
    WHEN 40 THRU 49
        ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (1, PASS)
    WHEN 50 THRU 69
        ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (1, CREDIT)
    WHEN 70 THRU 100
        ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (1, DISTINCTION)
END-EVALUATE

EVALUATE PAPER-2
    WHEN 0 THRU 39
        ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (2, FAIL)
    WHEN 40 THRU 49

etc.

Although this is a simple solution, it does not seem a very elegant one.  Would
it be possible to evaluate each of the three exam papers and fill the relevant
sections of the table from the one EVALUATE statement, and if so how?

The file holding the exam results is as follows;

FD    RESULTS-FILE.
01     RESULTS-REC.
         03 CAND-NO
         03 S-NAME
         03 RESULTS
              05 PAPER-1
              05 PAPER-2
              05 PAPER-3

I hope this problem is not to trivial for everyone, and that some of you can be
of some help.

thanks in advance

Martin.
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate Question

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-jbBicDRi1E8v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<921782408.3125.0.nnrp-07.c1ed7479@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
On Thu, 18 Mar 1999 18:40:07, "Martin Ruston" 
<martin@nisie-1.demon.co.uk> wrote:

> Hello,
> 
> I`m new to programming and have been studying Cobol for the last few months now,
> I am currently working on a problem which requires a table recording the number
> of passes, fails, etc, for an exam to be filled.

Three possible methods:

EVALUATE TRUE 
------
EVALUATE Paper-1 Also Paper-2 Also Paper-3
------
Make Paper-1, 2 and 3 a table and then use one Evaluate 3 times.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Evaluate Question

- **From:** edwardw355@aol.com (EdwardW355)
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990319023332.03192.00000346@ng20.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-jbBicDRi1E8v@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Put the papers in an indexed table, then put the evaluate in a perform loop.

01  grades.
    03  grade
        occurs 100 times
        indexed by g        pic 9(02).

move spaces to grades.
perform 0000-load-grades.
perform 0000-evaluate-grades.
.
.
..
0000-evaluate-grades.
    perform
        varying g from 1 by 1
        until (g > 100)
        or (grade(g) not numeric)
            evaluate grade (g)
                when ....
                when....
                end-evaluate
        end-perform.
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate Question

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G4HI2.392$NB5.994060@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<921782408.3125.0.nnrp-07.c1ed7479@news.demon.co.uk>`

```

FD    RESULTS-FILE.
01     RESULTS-REC.
         03 CAND-NO
         03 S-NAME
         03 RESULTS
              05 PAPER-1
              05 PAPER-2
              05 PAPER-3
         03 filler redefines results.
              05 paper occurs 3 times pic......

...

perform varying counter
        from 1 by 1
        until counter > 3
   EVALUATE PAPER (counter)
      WHEN 0 THRU 39
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, FAIL)
      WHEN 40 THRU 49
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, PASS)
      WHEN 50 THRU 69
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, CREDIT)
      WHEN 70 THRU 100
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, DISTINCTION)
   END-EVALUATE
end-perform
.

and don't forget to use conditionals.....


FD    RESULTS-FILE.
01     RESULTS-REC.
         03 CAND-NO
         03 S-NAME
         03 RESULTS
              05 PAPER-1
              05 PAPER-2
              05 PAPER-3
         03 filler redefines results.
              05 paper occurs 3 times pic......
                 88 is-fail           values 0 thru 39.
                 88 is-pass           values 40 thru 49.
                 88 is-credit         values 50 thru 69.
                 88 is-distinction    values 70 thru 100.
...

perform varying counter
        from 1 by 1
        until counter > 3
   EVALUATE PAPER (counter)
      WHEN is-fail (counter)
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, FAIL)
      WHEN is-pass (counter)
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, PASS)
      WHEN is-credit (counter)
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, CREDIT)
      WHEN is-distinction (counter)
         ADD 1 TO W-RESULT-TABLE (counter, DISTINCTION)
   END-EVALUATE
end-perform
.

FD    RESULTS-FILE.
01     RESULTS-REC.
         03 CAND-NO
         03 S-NAME
         03 RESULTS
              05 PAPER-1
              05 PAPER-2
              05 PAPER-3
         03 filler redefines results.
              05 paper occurs 3 times pic......
...
working-storage section.
01  filler.
    05 grade-table-data.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '1111111111'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '1111111111'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '1111111111'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '1111111111'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '2222222222'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '3333333333'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '3333333333'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '4444444444'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '4444444444'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '4444444444'.
       10 filler pic x(10) value '4'.
    05 FILLER REDEFINES GRADE=TABLE-DATA.
       10 GRADE OCCURS 101 TIMES PIC 9(01).

perform varying counter
        from 1 by 1
        until counter > 3
   add 1 to W-RESULT-TABLE (COUNTER, GRADE( PAPER (COUNTER) ) )
end-perform
.


Is that simple and elegant?
```

<a id="t-731792acc2"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu line sequential (V4) — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I know this sounds like a beginner's question, but does anybody know how to
get Fujitsu to put a formfeed into a line sequential file? The following
standard Cobol does not work:

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020 PROGRAM-ID. "TESTIT".
000030 AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
000040 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000050 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000060 FILE-CONTROL.
000070     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "\TEMP1\X"
000080         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000090 DATA DIVISION.
000100 FILE SECTION.
000110 FD PRINT-FILE
000120     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
000130     DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
000140 01 PRINT-LINE  PICTURE X(80).
000150 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000160 MAIN-LINE.
000170     OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.
000180     MOVE "THIS IS A TEXT LINE" TO PRINT-LINE.
000190     WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER PAGE.
000200     CLOSE PRINT-FILE.
000210     STOP RUN.

If I assign the file to "printer" then it will *print* the formfeed.
Unfortunately, the computer that it is going to run on does not have a
printer.  The file is written to disk, then printed later.  It also appears
to be impossible to use a network printer. As soon as the file is written to
anything but "printer" then all the logic changes, and and the form control
simply stops working.
```

```
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
does do form control corectly, but only if you let everything default.

IE:
Select print-file assign to "\temp\x".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cs43m$5qp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net> <7crn9u$rbo$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-2UVRu4d2lBui@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-NMdRe30I7rnP@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <36f4780e.10440576@news.indigo.ie>`

```
But it does.

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020 PROGRAM-ID. "TESTIT".
000030 AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
000040 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000050 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000060 FILE-CONTROL.
000070     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "\temp1\x".
000080 DATA DIVISION.
000090 FILE SECTION.
000100 FD PRINT-FILE
000110     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
000120     DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
000130 01 PRINT-LINE  PICTURE X(72).
000140 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000150 MAIN-LINE.
000160     OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.
000170     MOVE "THIS IS A TEXT LINE" TO PRINT-LINE.
000180     WRITE PRINT-LINE BEFORE ADVANCING 5.
000190     WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER PAGE.
000200     CLOSE PRINT-FILE.
000210     STOP RUN.

When run, produces the file \temp1\x. When that file is examined with a text
editor, there is the line "THIS IS A TEXT LINE", followed by 5<CR,LF>
combinations, followed by a formfeed (ASCII FF, control L), followed by a
second line of "THIS IS A TEXT LINE" .

That is what a file with "ACCESS IS LINE SEQUENTIAL" should be,
if I am not mistaken. However, if LINE SEQUENTIAL is mentioned in any of its
several invocations,
it seems to turn off all the advancing.

No matter.  The above code works, and when I copy it to just about any
printer in the world on just about any system in the world, it will print
what it is supposed to print.



Frederico Fonseca wrote in message <36f4780e.10440576@news.indigo.ie>...
>On 18 Mar 99 23:10:45 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Frederico Fonseca
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7crp0i$lrd$1@client2.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net>...
>I know this sounds like a beginner's question, but does anybody know how to
>get Fujitsu to put a formfeed into a line sequential file? The following
>standard Cobol does not work:

Good thing you said "standard" and not "Standard"  :)

[snip]
>000060 FILE-CONTROL.
>000070     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO "\TEMP1\X"
>000080         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.


Donald, I think you need a PROCESSING MODE clause in the SELECT sentence.
See chapter 8 of the User Guide, Tables 8-10 and 8-11.

Good luck!
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Coporation
ww.liant.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

- **From:** frederico@memosis.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f273ec.9381989@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net> <7crp0i$lrd$1@client2.news.psi.net>`

```
>
>
>Donald, I think you need a PROCESSING MODE clause in the SELECT sentence.
>See chapter 8 of the User Guide, Tables 8-10 and 8-11.
>
Hi Tom.

PROCESSING MODE is only for PRESENTATION FILES.
This is not the case.

In this particular case if the organization is LINE SEQUENTIAL it
will strip the "^L" and "^M" from the file.
If the organization is SEQUENTIAL it will keep the"^M" but it will 
remove the "^L" if this is the first char of the file.
IF we do a
WRITE AFTER 1 and then a WRITE AFTER PAGE then we will have a "^L"
on the file.

This is one of the many thinks that makes Fujitsu COBOL hard to work
with.


Frederico Fonseca
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Bk0Pedim1FFq@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Thu, 18 Mar 1999 18:16:20, "Donald Tees" <donald@wilmack.com> 
wrote:

> I know this sounds like a beginner's question, but does anybody know how to
> get Fujitsu to put a formfeed into a line sequential file? The following
> standard Cobol does not work:

My experience has been that if you direct the output to a file the 
"advancing, page, etc" items are lost.  This is because when you send 
it to PRINTER you are talking to a Windows PRINTER.

Solution?  Try report writer and see if it works better, but I bet it 
won't. 

Second solution - manually do it with CBL_WRITE_FILE routines.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu line sequential (V4)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cs2ng$551$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7crf7g$o5h$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-Bk0Pedim1FFq@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>My experience has been that if you direct the output to a file the
>"advancing, page, etc" items are lost.  This is because when you send
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>won't.


Third solution, which is what I meant.

Select filename assign to data-name.
01 data-name            picture x(10) value "myname.rpt".

or
Select filename assign to "myfile.rpt".

both work fine, with the advancing options.  They also both can be copied
fine to a windows printer after the fact.
```

## Thread: MF Compiler version question — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Compiler version question

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f22879.4484636@news.enteract.com>`

```
Hello,

Is it possible to run programs compiled using the MF Unix compiler
version 3.0.5  under the run-time supplied with new MF Unix compiler
version 4.1???

It would be nice not to have to re-compile everything!

Thanks,

Paul
--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Compiler version question

- **From:** "T Brown" <tom.brown@clara.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VaAI2.973$mi3.257@nnrp3.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<36f22879.4484636@news.enteract.com>`

```
There's a compiler directive INTLEVEL"x"  - for your case compile your new
stuff with INTLEVEL"3" and mixing old and new .INTs should work (in
theory) - What it does is force the new compiler to make code compatible
with the old. - Obviously you can't take advantage of any of the new
features added to the compiler.
I did try this using Object cobol 4.1 and cobol/2 V1.3 (1998 compiler with
1990 INTs) - not surprisingly it didn't work.  But you may have a better
chance since your 2 versions of cobol are closer.
Regards Tom

PAL3000 wrote in message <36f22879.4484636@news.enteract.com>...
>Hello,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Compiler version question

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f20a3a.2069069@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<36f22879.4484636@news.enteract.com>`

```
If yours programs are linked, they are native exe and you don't need
to recompile.
If you use int or gnt, it will work. But be careful. You'd better
recompile the code because if you make any modification with the code,
you'll have a mix of v 3 and v 4 int and/or gnt. And the result might
be unpredictible. 
Recompiling is easy under Unix. A for ... do ... done loop will do the
job in just a few minutes. If the code is error free....

Yours faithfully

Alain Reymond

(PAL3000) wrote:

>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
It looks like Microsoft is making a new attempt business programming market
with Biztalk. It sounds more like an alternative to Javascript or XML, but it
might expand to encompass some of the thing COBOL does. The name (by my
parsing) at least implies it could compete with OO COBOL, Biz for business
and talk a from the OO language Smalltalk.

Here's a link for more information:
http://www.infoworld.com/cgi-bin/displayIcommerce.pl?/prophet.htm

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Another possible assault on COBOL and it's not Java

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7crnrn$ljs$1@client2.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<7crd30$u94$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
MS have been making noises about this topic for some time; this has just
provided the marketing name.

This is actually a competitor (enhancment?) for EDI, based upon (not an
alternative to) XML.  It is a _data_ markup language, designed as a means
for compatible data interchange, in much the same way HTML allows compatible
document interchange.

The COBOL Committee immediately should begin working on:

      SELECT ELECTRONIC-INVOICE-FILE ...
            ORGANIZATION IS BIZTALK...

which presumably would be a sequential file which does the XML
automagically, based upon the record definition used in the WRITE statement.
;>)

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

DACatHome@aol.com wrote in message <7crd30$u94$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>It looks like Microsoft is making a new attempt business programming market
>with Biztalk. It sounds more like an alternative to Javascript or XML, but
it
>might expand to encompass some of the thing COBOL does. The name (by my
>parsing) at least implies it could compete with OO COBOL, Biz for business
>and talk a from the OO language Smalltalk.
[snip]
```

#### ↳ Re: Another possible assault on COBOL and it's not Java

- **From:** "David Sanders" <dsanders@synkronix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f169e0$0$228@nntp1.ba.best.com>`
- **References:** `<7crd30$u94$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
    If a language is useful enough to replace Cobol as a business language,
then may the best language win.  I don't believe that Java is an assault on
Cobol because it isn't a business oriented language.  I do believe however
that some companies are trying to replace their Cobol with other languages
like Java, but I don't necessarily agree that that is an assault on Cobol.

Our company (www.synkronix.com) has a product called PERCobol which compiles
Cobol for use in a Java environment.  We ALWAYS recommend that companies
keep their business logic IN COBOL, but with our product they get all of the
benefits of Java.  Their Cobol is compiled to what we call a "Cobol Bean" (A
Java Bean that was written in Cobol) rather than to native code.  (They have
the option of compiling to native code as well, of course.)

David Sanders
info@synkronix.com
Synkronix, Inc.
```

```
Well, gang, I cannot resist... even though it is in bad taste *guess* who
just sent me an email in reply to a standard 'Please do your own homework'
that read:

--begin quoted text
```

<a id="t-e855e2fa8c"></a>
## Thread: Initialize... — 38 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Initialize...

- **From:** chris@sssltd.co.uk
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Can anyone tell me what the INITIALIZE verb does to a variable that has a
VALUE clause.  For example:

WS-COUNT    PIC 9    VALUE 5

MOVE 7 TO WS-COUNT.
INITIALIZE WS-COUNT.

What value would you get?  A book we've got says 7, as INITIALIZE is supposed
to ignore variables with VALUE clauses.  But other people reckon it's zero,
where others reckon it's initialised to the original value of 7.

Is it different between COBOL 2 and 370?

Cheers.

Chris.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ctjqb$qru$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  chris@sssltd.co.uk wrote:
> Can anyone tell me what the INITIALIZE verb does to a variable that has a
> VALUE clause.  For example:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It really doesn't matter what "a book" says.  What does your compiler say?  I
tried it using MF Cobol 4.x I found that the INITIALIZE verb always set the
numeric field to a value of zero.  This held true for the following language
options: OSVS, ANS85, VSC2, DOS/VS, and COB/370.


Ed Stevens

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
> Can anyone tell me what the INITIALIZE verb does to a variable that has a
> VALUE clause.  For example:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

What book is that?  It ignores FILLER items entirely but does NOT set 
the value to that on the VALUE clause.  The answer is zero.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990319230859.13806.00000216@ng-fr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WjO2jdqm6zW6@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
inilialize statement for numeric will give you zeroes and for alphanumeric -
spaces, regardless of initial value. try to display field before and after.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Initialize does not nor has it ever referenced a VALUE clause. A VALUE
clause does not make a field in COBOL a CONSTANT never changing value.
ITIALIZE only looks at the type of variable, NUMERIC or ALPHANUMERIC, to
determine which values to move to the referenced fields. NUMERIC gets zeros,
and sometimes LOW-VALUES depending on the USAGE clause. ALPHANUMERIC fields
get SPACES or hex '40'. What a field contains is irrelevant to the
initialize clause. If you look into the assembler/machine code generated by
an INITIALIZE clause it would be the same as several separate moves moving
the appropriate ZEROS or SPACES to the initialized fields.

INITIALIZE has been frowned-upon by most programmers I have worked with. It
is less efficient than doing the moves manually. In one case I have seen a
record containing several thousand bytes initialized by saving off the
initial state filled in by value clauses. Then the initial state is simply
moved off to a large filler working storage field. Then moved back when the
record needs to be initialized. Seems crazy, but the CPU usage for the later
operation as opposed to an initialize statement were extremely significant.
Considering such a  thing may be performed several million times for a large
database, it would be impractical to use an initialize statement.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com>`

```
(*sigh*)          
Initalize here fills a block of memory with either zeros or
spaces. You can't get much more efficient than that. 

And besides which, the value of using initialize vs. hand moving a 
ton of initialization code should be rather obvious... the cost of a
program is a complex thing.         
=Paul


Jeff (a@a.com) wrote:
: Initialize does not nor has it ever referenced a VALUE clause. A VALUE
: clause does not make a field in COBOL a CONSTANT never changing value.
: ITIALIZE only looks at the type of variable, NUMERIC or ALPHANUMERIC, to
: determine which values to move to the referenced fields. NUMERIC gets zeros,
: and sometimes LOW-VALUES depending on the USAGE clause. ALPHANUMERIC fields
: get SPACES or hex '40'. What a field contains is irrelevant to the
: initialize clause. If you look into the assembler/machine code generated by
: an INITIALIZE clause it would be the same as several separate moves moving
: the appropriate ZEROS or SPACES to the initialized fields.

: INITIALIZE has been frowned-upon by most programmers I have worked with. It
: is less efficient than doing the moves manually. In one case I have seen a
: record containing several thousand bytes initialized by saving off the
: initial state filled in by value clauses. Then the initial state is simply
: moved off to a large filler working storage field. Then moved back when the
: record needs to be initialized. Seems crazy, but the CPU usage for the later
: operation as opposed to an initialize statement were extremely significant.
: Considering such a  thing may be performed several million times for a large
: database, it would be impractical to use an initialize statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F4DAF9.8073A36A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> (*sigh*)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> program is a complex thing.

Paul

Think about this in assembler terms:

If you have a comp numeric, followed by a display numeric, followed be a
alpha, repeated many times: the (fast) assembler fill instruction will
be repeated many, many times.  This is especially the case in many
tables.

The next problem is basically lazy programming.  Initialize followed by
individual moves to every field that is initialized or a slightly less
lazy some fields are moved under certain conditions.  The last I would
prefer each if has an else that moves zero (or space) to make it more
explicit.

It is a tool to be used not abused.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dlnod$j0n@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <36F4DAF9.8073A36A@zip.com.au>`

```
I have been off c.l.c most of the last week with flu - and thought I would
"summarize" a few things about INITIALIZE (from MY perspective):

1) Almost (but probably not all) INITIALIZE statements *can* be coded as
"MOVE" statements - with better run-time efficiency.  HOWEVER, depending on
the application design and record lay-outs, INITIALIZE *may* be a more
efficient "self-documenting" and easier to maintain structure.

2) INITIALIZE *defaults* to moving spaces to named alphanumeric items and
zeroes to named numeric items.  It *never* (currently - see below) changes
filler fields or uses the VALUE clause.

3) The current INITIALIZE statement fully supports using OTHER than spaces
or zeros for initialization - but this is not the default (and requires the
use of a REPLACING phrase).  This allows using separate values for each type
(category) of elementary item to be initialized.

4) In the next Standard (and I think some vendors have started adding the
syntax as a current extension) there WILL be ways to:
   -  INITIALIZE filler fields (when requested by a key word)
   - INITIALIZE using VALUE clause (again with an explicit keyword)

Bottom-Line:
  INITIALIZE is a powerful verb (and getting more so in the future).  It
probably is NOT your best choice for repeatedly done object-code sensitive
application logic - but does provide a significant advantage for maintenance
and self-documenting code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dok36$ea3@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <36F4DAF9.8073A36A@zip.com.au> <7dlnod$j0n@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <922706268.7331.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Yes, that is what (will) be supported.  (There are also extensions to allow
VALUE clauses in Linkage Section and File Section.  These are ignored during
initial "memory allocation" but ARE paid attention to when you use the new
format of the INITIALIZE verb. FYI - many vendors already allow VALUE
clauses in these places - as an extension - to make use of COPY statements
easier.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f47122.24859602@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On 20 Mar 1999 19:11:02 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

>(*sigh*)          
>Initalize here fills a block of memory with either zeros or
>spaces. You can't get much more efficient than that. 

Lest it be forgotten, Initialize can do more than move space.  For
Example, le't say you have this construct:

01  Detail-Line.
      03  Detail-Desc    Pic X(20).
      03  Filler               Pic X.
      03  Detail-Dollars Pic $$,$$$.99-.

Move "Desc" To Detail-Desc
Move 500 To Detail-Units
Initialize Detail-Line 
              Replacing Numeric Edited by zeros


---- What is the result?

Initialize can target specific fields - initializing counters etc
without disturbing other fields.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
>Initalize here fills a block of memory with either zeros or
>spaces. You can't get much more efficient than that.


Yes you can. Moving from one location to another can be more efficient than
moving individual bytes and determining which bytes to place in which
location, which is what INITIALIZE does. Not to put it down completely, but
I never recommend it be used in repeated initializations. A first INITIALIZE
then a move to a filler is what I recommend for repeated, complicated group
structures. Assuming the version of COBOL you are using properly initializes
group structures and their associated sub-fields.

>
>And besides which, the value of using initialize vs. hand moving a
>ton of initialization code should be rather obvious... the cost of a
>program is a complex thing.


I agree with you here, but most programmers only spend 1% of their time
programming and the rest testing and debugging.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:
> 
> >Initalize here fills a block of memory with either zeros or
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> group structures and their associated sub-fields.

In this case, why do an initialize at all?  Why not just use VALUE
clauses the way you want them and make it compile time instead of run
time?  You can still copy the record to a filler if you want.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RHzJ2.965$NB5.2782680@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>In this case, why do an initialize at all?  Why not just use VALUE
>clauses the way you want them and make it compile time instead of run
>time?  You can still copy the record to a filler if you want.

Several reasons:
- personal preference (not mine)
- the structure size may prove too time consuming to place values on all
fields
- the structure may be in a copybook which you do not have authority to
change

these are a few that come to mind.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
INITIALIZE is very useful when you need to write many records to a file;
it's the fast way to get a clean record before EACH new record you MOVE data
to.

MCM

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Jeff wrote:
>>
>In this case, why do an initialize at all?  Why not just use VALUE
>clauses the way you want them and make it compile time instead of run
>time?  You can still copy the record to a filler if you want.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net>`

```
Only when you want it initialized in the particular way INITIALIZE
initializes.  Moving spaces to the field, or moving the first field to
the whole record is faster.  I'm not seeing much advantage in moving
zeros to each numeric field before moving real data there.  

More useful is saving the compiler initialization (value is) in a temp
field and moving it back to initialize.

Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> INITIALIZE is very useful when you need to write many records to a file;
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >time?  You can still copy the record to a filler if you want.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f99e9d.5850452@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 Mar 1999 07:10:03 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

> Moving spaces to the field, or moving the first field to
>the whole record is faster.
 But has two problems:

1)	The compiler produces a warning that an overlapping move will take place
2)	The results will be unpredictable:
	With /370 and /390 systems a length of about 768 bytes and smaller, this will work
3)	For larger records, an MVCL will be generated which does NOT allow overlapping
moves
4)	For non /370 or /390 systems, like NT , or OS/2, this does not work at all



with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)

- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f99e9d.5850452@news3.ibm.net> <36F9A132.A3FA8B@ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Wed, 24 Mar 1999 21:36:34 -0500, John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Re item#2 - Is not the length 256 ?
>Re item #3: Can someone verify that the  compiler will generate  a MVCL and not multiple MVC's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
John,

I learned this fact (on MVS and VSE) the hard way.  One of my customers complained that
the commarea in his CICS program would not always be around....

They had the following in their programs

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01   MY-COMMAREA PIC X(2048).

LINKAGE SECTION.
01 DFHCOMMAREA PIC X(4096).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO MY-COMMAREA.


Every so often the date was NOT moved at all, the MY-COMMAREA var remained in its initial
state.

Only while looking at the disassembled code I found the MVCL.  Decreasing the length to
768 (3 * 256) showd that the compiler generated 3 consecutive MVC instructions

Of course, I haven't chaecked this with the latest and greatest COBOL for This and
That....
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

```
:>I learned this fact (on MVS and VSE) the hard way.  One of my customers complained that

Volker, I am missing something.

overlaps or is overlapped by the WORKING-STORAGE section I would suggest a
compiler bug. ANd other bad things happening.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36fa5f3c.471958@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f99e9d.5850452@news3.ibm.net> <36F9A132.A3FA8B@ix.netcom.com> <36fadf55.1500036@news3.ibm.net> <370927a2.7658296@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
On Thu, 25 Mar 1999 12:45:37 GMT, postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen) wrote:

>On Thu, 25 Mar 1999 07:06:46 GMT vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke) wrote:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>example use 4096 even though it was 2048? 

Binyamin,

yes, you ARE missing something ... because I quoted from memory (whichone shouldn't do in
my age) and omitted some details.  Here the full picture (and all the customer's programs
were written that way, because they used a skeleton).

MAIN Program
Working-Storage Section.
01 MY-COMMAREA PIC X(512).           !!!!

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

EXEC CICS LINK PROGRAM('B') COMMAREA(MY-COMMAREA) LENGTH(2048) END-EXEC



Sub Program
 Working-Storage Section.
01 MY-COMMAREA PIC X(2048).                !!!!

Linkage Section.
01 DFHCOMMAREA PIC X(4096).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

MOVE DFHCOMMAREA TO MY-COMMAREA

Now everything is in place.  MY-COMMAREA in the MAIN program is located at, let's say,
X"00060000" and is 512 bytes long

This is the address of DFHCOMMAREA in the sub program.  Working Storage of the sub program
may as well begin at X"00060300", e.g.,  and then the move (which generates an MVCL for
2048 bytes) will be an overlapping one, an dwill not be done.  This WAD....
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dg426$nij$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f99e9d.5850452@news3.ibm.net> <36F9A132.A3FA8B@ix.netcom.com> <36fadf55.1500036@news3.ibm.net> <370927a2.7658296@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
In article <370927a2.7658296@news.netvision.net.il>,
  postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen) wrote:
<snip>
> If the LINKAGE section
> overlaps or is overlapped by the WORKING-STORAGE section I would suggest a
> compiler bug. ANd other bad things happening.
>
<snip>

Not necessarily.  I've seen linkage overlap either working-storage or
procedure -- or someone elses pgm -- due to programmer error.  All you have
to do is define your linkage section larger than the area being passed to it
by the calling pgm.

PGM-A.
WORKING-STORAGE.
01 PASSED-TO-PGM-B  PIC X(50).
PROCEDURE.
CALL PGM-B USING AREA-TO-BE-PASSED.

PGM-B.
LINKAGE.
01 PASSED-FROM-PGM-A PIC X(1024).

or the same principle in CICS.

WORKING-STORAGE.
01 WS-COMMAREA  PIC X(50).
01 WS-COMMLEN   PIC S9(8) COMP.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 DFHCOMMAREA  PIC X(1024).
PROCEDURE.
MOVE LENGTH OF WS-COMMAREA TO WS-COMMLEN
EXEC CICS RETURN TRANSID(PGMA)
            COMMAREA(WS-COMMAREA)
            LENGTH (WS-COMMLEN)
END-EXEC.


In either example, anything beyond the first 50 bytes of linkage is 'no-mans
land.'  The complier has no way of knowing the calling program is passing a
pointer to a storage block that is smaller than what was defined by the
receiving program. It could be just about anything, and if you move anything
into it (past the first 50 bytes, in this example), you are almost guarenteed
to crash something -- and it could be just about anything.  I discovered this
years ago when I had to track down why a CICS program I was working on was
crashing in a random manner.


Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f99e9d.5850452@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:

> On Tue, 23 Mar 1999 07:10:03 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> moves

Perhaps, but MVCL requires a length of source and destination, their addresses, and a padding
character.  Specifying a source length of zero will result in ALL padding character being
moved to the destination field.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F8FAAC.C7F241A1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d6gm1$fn1$2@news-1.news.gte.net> <36F7A0BB.84DAC0C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36f99e9d.5850452@news3.ibm.net>`

```
I'm sorry I wasn't clear.  In COBOL I don't try to move the first field
of a record to the record  (that's C or assembler thinking).   I meant
moving the data which I would move to the first field to the record
instead.

Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 23 Mar 1999 07:10:03 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> (BSP GmbH)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d7u7j$arv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message

>In this case, why do an initialize at all?  Why not just use VALUE
>clauses the way you want them and make it compile time instead of run
>time?  You can still copy the record to a filler if you want.

I am re-thinking this one, as I have been fixing programs all week with this
particular bug.

All the code was initialized with value statements.  The code was all in
subroutines, laid over a menu mainline.  With large memories, the new
versions became linked subroutines in a 2.5 meg program, rather than
canceled overlays.  Of course every program crashes on the second call in
the new version.

There are other fixes (like the initial statement), but I am now converting
them all to use moves in the startup.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7A2CD.46E2B1C6@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d7u7j$arv$1@news.igs.net>`

```
One trouble with doing moves to initialize is that it is subject to
maintenance bugs if the record size changes and the maintenance
programmer forgets to change the temporary field sizes.  I suppose that
the initialization routine could check these lengths if you wanted to be
safe.   In fact, I would mandate that if this technique were to be used.

Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> them all to use moves in the startup.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d8f0d$kkf$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <gAGI2.387$NB5.981059@storm.twcol.com> <7d0rs7$kju@lotho.delphi.com> <jL%I2.586$NB5.1626430@storm.twcol.com> <36F66BE4.3C7DF5F4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7d7u7j$arv$1@news.igs.net> <36F7A2CD.46E2B1C6@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36F7A2CD.46E2B1C6@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>One trouble with doing moves to initialize is that it is subject to
>maintenance bugs if the record size changes and the maintenance
>programmer forgets to change the temporary field sizes.  I suppose that
>the initialization routine could check these lengths if you wanted to be
>safe.   In fact, I would mandate that if this technique were to be used.


<?>It does not matter with numeric literals, and with non-numeric ones they
are going to be padded with spaces.  If I wanted an entire field set to a
fixed character, I would use the ALL modifier, so I cannot see how the above
would happen.

About the only time that I can see a need for the length to be checked is if
you had a temporary record defined in WS, initialized with values, then
moved the temporary record into the record to be initialized (which I have
never done).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7C44A.8857F15B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
That's the technique which I was warning against.  It was suggested
earlier in the thread and I was thinking in terms of having to clean-up
after maintenance.

Donald Tees wrote:
>
```

```
COBOL lives...
chris@sssltd.co.uk wrote in message <7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Can anyone tell me what the INITIALIZE verb does to a variable that has a
>VALUE clause.  For example:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>What value would you get?  A book we've got says 7, as INITIALIZE is
supposed
>to ignore variables with VALUE clauses.  But other people reckon it's zero,
>where others reckon it's initialised to the original value of 7.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f47355.25422672@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <921968359.427933@woody.hotkey.net.au>`

```
On Sun, 21 Mar 1999 09:24:14 +1100, "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd"
<wlodge@hotkey.net.au> wrote:

>Initialize will give you zeros for numeric and spaces for alphanumerics.
>
>And that's that.
>

My last posting was not quite complete, let me piggy back on here.
You can initialize to anything - not just spaces and zeros.  You could
initialize alphanumeric fields to all Z's and numeric fields to 1 all
in a single initialize statement, by using the often overlooked
replacing clause.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F73CDE.3A9A@zag.si>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
chris@sssltd.co.uk wrote:
> 
> Can anyone tell me what the INITIALIZE verb does to a variable that has a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
All numeric fields will be ZERO, all character fields would be SPACE.
If you have character field called FILLER and record is part of file,
then such FILLER will be filled with X"00" (ascii value zero or NUL
char).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7A20A.671E9763@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7cth7k$ora$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36F73CDE.3A9A@zag.si>`

```
> Own
> All numeric fields will be ZERO, all character fields would be SPACE.
> If you have character field called FILLER and record is part of file,
> then such FILLER will be filled with X"00" (ascii value zero or NUL
> char).

Yeah, as if this method of initialization is what we want.  Moving one
value into the record is more efficient and at least as likely of being
useful.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic call to C Library from COBOL 390

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d4vdk$3cu$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<TNyI2.12709$EF1.55423514@WReNphoon1>`

```
In article <TNyI2.12709$EF1.55423514@WReNphoon1>,
  michael@mathcorp.com (Michael T. Shohoney) wrote:
> We are calling a C library out of COBOL/390.  We are successful when we call
> using a static call but we cannot make it work with a dynamic call.  We get
> system abends.  Can anyone shed any light on what we are doing wrong?

What's the meaning of 'C library'? Do you try to use a DLL? If yes, have you
used the prelinker to resolve the external entries? If yes, was is the right
definition side deck, that one the prelinker produced when prelinking the C
DLL?

If you can give us more details about the abend codes that occurred and about
the JCL you used to compile, prelink, link and go of both C and COBOL, I am
pretty sure we can help you more!

Daniel
------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```


Rick Price wrote:

> I don't know if any one else agrees but I think it would be nice and also
> good manners if users who raise a query acknowledged which answers worked.

Looks like you might be talkin' to me.

When I asked about finding optional COMP-3 fields a few weeks ago (changing the
compiler was not an option), I moved the data in question to a long COMP-6
field (unsigned packed), then to numeric display items, redefined as an array.
We checked specific bytes for x"3C" (ASCII platform) and the rest for the
numeric class test.

> I don't know if we can set up a standard reply code showing valid answers.

Would CA work?
```

```
'Twas Fri, 19 Mar 1999 23:16:09 +0000, when Philip A Colbert
<philip@colbert.u-net.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Anyone know how to start batch or executable code from cobol,
>(We are thinking about building a GUI front end for an old system)

What compiler and what operating system?  If you happen to be using Unisys
A Series the syntax is 

CALL SYSTEM WFL USING <param>
```

#### ↳ Re: Command line

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZHGI2.390$NB5.984535@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36F2DAB9.CBD7875D@colbert.u-net.com>`

```
There is a command you can use which is pretty much similar on every system.
It is called HELP or in some strange dialects it can be referenced as a
single '?'. I have even seen it assigned to that weird row of keys at the
top of your keyboard with 'f' and numbers on them. In the United States they
usually assign a HRLP command or some English derivative to this command.
Navigation through the screens presented usually produces something of use.
```

```
microfocus cobol
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Command line

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qW%I2.589$NB5.1632551@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36F2DAB9.CBD7875D@colbert.u-net.com> <ZHGI2.390$NB5.984535@storm.twcol.com> <36F42BEE.883FB148@colbert.u-net.com>`

```
>Sorry about that,
>                        I have not been programming for long.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One guess would be:

           CALL 'C:\COMMAND.COM' 'C:\COBOLPRG.EXE' 'PARM VALUES'

Of course substituting double quotes for single quotes for necessary. This
example was a derivative of a primitive BASIC compiler which from the
documentation was the way it had to be done to allow for DOS commands such
as DIR and other commands to be executed.
```

<a id="t-b89c34cd22"></a>
## Thread: Control bytes — 5 msgs

### Control bytes

- **From:** Tim Altmann <usatfcal@pop.mpls.uswest.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F4361F.AE428D07@pop.mpls.uswest.net>`

```
My understanding is that in a sequential file (MVS/QSAM),
there are four "hidden" control bytes at the beginning of
each record that include a reference to the length of the
given record (provided the file is defined as variable
block?).  I am wondering if it is possible to access that
length value.

This is all fairly new to me, so I could be completely
missing the boat.  Here's my dilemma.

I am reading from a variable record length sequential file
and would like to write selected records (of various
lengths) out to a second sequential file for later
processing.  When I try to just read into, and write out of,
a record of max. length, it either includes the next 'n'
bytes (from successive records) to fill the record (01 level
definition) or inserts blanks (read into working storage).
It seems to me that a feasible way of achieving the desired
results would be to read in a record of the max. length,
look at its actual length in the control bytes and then move
the first 'length' bytes to a record with length defined as
occurs depending on ... length, finally writing that record
out to the new file.

Any assistance would be greatly appreciated.

Tim
```

#### ↳ Re: Control bytes

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d1fi6$bnc$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<36F4361F.AE428D07@pop.mpls.uswest.net>`

```
Tim,

    If you are executing COBOL2, just define an unsigned working-storage
integer and include it in your FD as 'RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM nnnn TO
nnnn DEPENDING ON working-storage integer. After the READ, the integer will
contain the length of the record just read. Remember, it must be an UNSIGNED
integer (preferably a halfword or fullword binary). Before you issue the
WRITE, populate the integer with the appropriate value.

HTH....

WOB

Tim Altmann wrote in message <36F4361F.AE428D07@pop.mpls.uswest.net>...
>My understanding is that in a sequential file (MVS/QSAM),
>there are four "hidden" control bytes at the beginning of
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Tim Altmann <usatfcal@pop.mpls.uswest.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F46B83.BA983687@pop.mpls.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36F4361F.AE428D07@pop.mpls.uswest.net> <7d1fi6$bnc$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Tim
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7d2q1f$4ls$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36F4361F.AE428D07@pop.mpls.uswest.net>`

```

rather than sequential.  Are the records <CR,LF> terminated?

>there are four "hidden" control bytes at the beginning of
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Control bytes

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990320191620.20115.00000612@ng30.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36F4361F.AE428D07@pop.mpls.uswest.net>`

```
it depends on the varible portion of the record...if it is beause of an occurs
clause, set the record depending on the value of the occurs.  This will write
out records with lengths either equal to max or less than, and you reallydon't
have to worry about the length bit
```

<a id="t-282d980e42"></a>
## Thread: I need a little help.... — 1 msgs

### Re: I need a little help....

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_WHI2.397$NB5.1020461@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7cjfb3$2rr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7cn3qm$m21$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
>"I have a file created by a program written in C or C++,
>but I don't have access to the source code or even know
>for sure the compiler that generated the executable.
>(How) can I extract the data into something usable?"


Thats like saying I have a pile of fertilizer, can you reassemble it into
the terds which came out of the cow. Then can you further reconstitute it
into the food which the cow ate and tell me where in the world the food was
created and what type of fertilizer was used on it?

If you don't have the source code you are, forgive my French, you are
fucked.

There are several formats in COBOL which are valid across format types, for
instance any sequence of bytes can be trnsformed into a valid number. If
formats can be interchangeable you would have to do some serious context
checking to tell the format and even then you won't be 100% right.

You will never know the correct format until you look at the COBOL layouts
of each file. If you can't get them, then I suggest you take a
leave-of-absence for a couple months and hope the problem goes away by the
time you get back. Sick relatives are always good excuses for
leave-of-absences.
```

<a id="t-35d0612c26"></a>
## Thread: Check Printing-Dollars to Spanish — 1 msgs

### Re: Check Printing-Dollars to Spanish

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 1999-03-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990319231549.13806.00000218@ng-fr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36efa6a9.0@news1.jps.net>`

```
        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. MONEY.
      *****************************************************************
      ** THIS PROGRAM WAS CREATED TO ACCEPT ANY NUMERIC VALUE / UP
TO *
      ** 8 CHARACTERS AND WILL CONVERT THEM TO THE ALPHANUMERIC
STRING*
      *****************************************************************
        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  RECORD-10.
            05 DIG1  PIC X(09) VALUE 'ONE '.
            05 DIG2  PIC X(09) VALUE 'TWO '.
            05 DIG3  PIC X(09) VALUE 'THREE '.
            05 DIG4  PIC X(09) VALUE 'FOUR'.
            05 DIG5  PIC X(09) VALUE 'FIVE'.
            05 DIG6  PIC X(09) VALUE 'SIX '.
            05 DIG7  PIC X(09) VALUE 'SEVEN '.
            05 DIG8  PIC X(09) VALUE 'EIGHT '.
            05 DIG9  PIC X(09) VALUE 'NINE'.
            05 DIG10 PIC X(09) VALUE 'TEN '.
            05 DIG11 PIC X(09) VALUE 'ELEVEN '.
            05 DIG12 PIC X(09) VALUE 'TWELVE '.
            05 DIG13 PIC X(09) VALUE 'THIRTEEN '.
            05 DIG14 PIC X(09) VALUE 'FOURTEEN '.
            05 DIG15 PIC X(09) VALUE 'FIFTEEN '.
            05 DIG16 PIC X(09) VALUE 'SIXTEEN'.
            05 DIG17 PIC X(09) VALUE 'SEVENTEEN'.
            05 DIG18 PIC X(09) VALUE 'EIGHTTEEN'.
            05 DIG19 PIC X(09) VALUE 'NINETEEN '.
      * 01  TABEL-RECORD-10  VALUE
      *    'ONE      TWO      THREE     FOUR     FIVE     SIX       SEVE
      *    'N    EIGHT    NINE     TEN      ELEVEN   TWELVE   THIRTEEN F
      *    'OURTEEN FIFTEEN SIXTEEN SEVENTEENEIGHTEEN NINETEEN '.
        01  TABEL-RECORD-10 REDEFINES RECORD-10.
           05 MONEY-10 OCCURS 19 TIMES.
                10 MONEY-WORD-10  PIC X(09).
        01  TABEL-RECORD-20  VALUE
           'TWENTY THIRTY FOURTY FIFTY  SIXTY  SEVENTYEIGHTY NINETY '.
           05 MONEY-20 OCCURS 8  TIMES.
                10 MONEY-WORD-20  PIC X(07).
        01  F-DOLLARS.
            05 F-MONEY      PIC 9(06) VALUE ZEROES.
            05 F-CENT       PIC V99   VALUE ZEROES.
        01  F-SUB           PIC 9(02) VALUE ZEROES.
        01  F-COUNT         PIC 9     VALUE ZEROES.
        01  F-GO-TO         PIC 9     VALUE ZEROES.
        01  MONEY-STRING.
            05 F1           PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F1A          PIC X(08)  VALUE 'HUNDRED'.
            05 F2           PIC X(07)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F3           PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F3A          PIC X(09)  VALUE 'THOUSAND'.
            05 F4           PIC X(07)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F4A          PIC X(08)  VALUE 'HUNDRED '.
            05 F5           PIC X(07)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F6           PIC X(09)  VALUE SPACES.
            05 F6A          PIC X(07)  VALUE 'DOLLARS'.
            05 F7           PIC .99    VALUE '.00' .
            05 F7A          PIC X(05)  VALUE 'CENTS'.
        01  MONEY-LINE      PIC X(80) VALUE SPACES.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            MOVE SPACES TO MONEY-STRING.
            ACCEPT  F-DOLLARS.
            INSPECT F-MONEY TALLYING F-COUNT FOR LEADING ZERO.
            DISPLAY 'F-COUNT:  ' F-COUNT
            ADD 1 TO F-COUNT GIVING F-GO-TO.
            DISPLAY 'F-GO-TO:  ' F-GO-TO.
            GO TO
                  PARA-1
                  PARA-2
                  PARA-3
                  PARA-4
                  PARA-5
                  PARA-6
                  DEPENDING ON F-GO-TO.
              PERFORM PARA-FINAL.
       PARA-6.
           MOVE  F-MONEY(6:1) TO F-SUB
           MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F6.
           MOVE  'DOLLARS'  TO F6A.
           GO TO PARA-FINAL.
       PARA-5.
           MOVE F-MONEY(5:2) TO F-SUB.
           MOVE  'DOLLARS'  TO F6A
           IF F-SUB = 00
              MOVE SPACES TO F5 F6
           ELSE
             IF F-SUB <= 19
               MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F6
           ELSE
               IF F-SUB(2:1) NOT = 0
                  PERFORM PARA-6A
                  PERFORM PARA-6
               ELSE
                  PERFORM PARA-6A
               END-IF
             END-IF
            END-IF.
           GO TO PARA-FINAL.
       PARA-4.
           MOVE  F-MONEY(4:1) TO F-SUB
           IF
              F-SUB = 0
              MOVE SPACES TO F4 F4A
           ELSE
              MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F4
              MOVE  'HUNDRED'  TO F4A.
           PERFORM PARA-5.
       PARA-3.
           MOVE F-MONEY(3:1) TO F-SUB.
           MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F3.
           MOVE  'THOUSAND' TO F3A.
           PERFORM PARA-4.
       PARA-3A.
            MOVE F-MONEY(2:1) TO F-SUB
            MOVE MONEY-WORD-20(F-SUB - 1) TO F2.
       PARA-2.
           MOVE F-MONEY(2:2) TO F-SUB.
               MOVE  'THOUSAND' TO F3A
           IF F-SUB = 00
               MOVE  SPACES TO F3  F2
               PERFORM PARA-4
           ELSE
           IF F-SUB <= 19
               MOVE  MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F3
               PERFORM PARA-4
           ELSE
               IF F-SUB(2:1) NOT = 0
                  PERFORM PARA-3A
                  PERFORM PARA-3
               ELSE
                  PERFORM PARA-3A
                  PERFORM PARA-4
               END-IF
            END-IF.
       PARA-1.
            MOVE F-MONEY(1:1) TO F-SUB
            MOVE MONEY-WORD-10(F-SUB) TO F1.
            MOVE  'HUNDRED'  TO F1A.
            PERFORM PARA-2.
       PARA-6A.
            MOVE F-MONEY(5:1) TO F-SUB
            MOVE MONEY-WORD-20(F-SUB - 1) TO F5.
       PARA-FINAL.
            IF F-CENT  = .00
               MOVE '00' TO F7(2:2)
               MOVE 'CENT' TO F7A
            ELSE
             IF F-CENT = .01
               MOVE 'CENT' TO F7A
               MOVE '01' TO F7(2:2)
             ELSE
               MOVE F-CENT      TO F7
               MOVE 'CENTS' TO F7A
            END-IF
            END-IF.
            MOVE SPACE TO F7(1:1).
            STRING
                 F1  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F1A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F2  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F3  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F3A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F4  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F4A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F5  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F6  DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F6A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 'AND' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 ' ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F7  DELIMITED  BY SIZE
                 '/100 ' DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 F7A DELIMITED BY SPACE
                     INTO MONEY-LINE
           END-STRING.
           DISPLAY MONEY-LINE.
           STOP RUN.
```

---

<a id="t-134fed9651"></a>
## Thread: Problem with the string fonction — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Problem with the string fonction

- **From:** "Sydney" <sydney@i-france.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d30tb$g98$2@platane.wanadoo.fr>`

```
Hi

I'm french, and I have a problem with the string fonction.

I have an indexed file.
fd freleves.
01 releve.
   02 dat pic 9999/99/99.
   02 temp pic +99.

I use this to fill the dat variable
01 plg-insertion.
   02 line 5 column 38 value "Insertion".
   02 line 7 column 3 value "Date du releve: ".
   02 line 7 column 20 pic 99/99/9999 to dat auto.
   02 line 9 column 5 value "Temperature relevee: ".
   02 line 9 column 27 pic +99 to temp.

I have also a
77 daterev pic 9999/99/99 variable

Here is the problem

display plg-insertion
accept plg-insertion
I enter 21/03/1999
So in dat we have
2103/19/99 due to the pic 9999/99/99

I want in dat : 1999/03/21

In order to this, i use
string dat(6:2) dat(9:2) "/" dat(3:2) "/" dat(1:2)
delimited by size
into datrev *The error is here *
end-string.
move datrev to dat

But there is an error
Edited field not allowed

What the problem?

I use Personal Cobol

A+
Sydney
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with the string fonction

- **From:** Harry Nauta <hna@unizone.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F52748.23EB4360@unizone.nl>`
- **References:** `<7d30tb$g98$2@platane.wanadoo.fr>`

```
I often deal with date-formatting and I use to handle it in
a different way than you tried to do. I respect personal 
COBOL styles, so I was hesitant just to present my own 
approach to you. But I think you will have to change
more than one feature.

Let us see.
Output : pic 9999/99/99
Input  : user types "dd/mm/ccyy"     (c = century).

I don't know what type of COBOL compiler you use, but I
cannot find anything about a print-format with slashes,
so /-signs. So I don't know whether it will work.
And what do you intent to do with "freleve - dat"?
Do you want to print it or is it the format for
saving data on disk or tape?
If you want it printed with slashes, then it is okay to
try it this way. If you keep it on magnetic storage, you
might leave out the slashes.

Never string or unstring numerics, because only pic X is
fit for (un)stringing. That is for sure.

I would recommend you to handle it this way.

In your Working-Storage Section:
01  plg-insertion     (accepts date from screen)
    02  plg-dd        pic xx.
    02  filler        pic x.
    02  plg-mm        pic xx.
    02  filler        pic x.
    02  plg-ccyy      pic x(4).

In your File Section:
fd  releves.
01  releve.
    02  dat.
        03  dat-ccyy  pic x(4).
        03  filler    pic x value '/'.
        03  dat-mm    pic xx.
        03  filler    pic x value '/'.
        03  dat-dd    pic xx.

This is the interaction with the user as you described it:
- accept plg-insertion
- I enter 21/03/1999
- So in dat we have 2103/19/99 due to the pic 9999/99/99
Well, in plg-insertion we now got 21/03/1999! Due to the
picture I just described. Now we move it to releves in
such a way that it fits. You code in your procedure division:
    move plg-dd    to dat-dd
    move plg-mm    to dat-mm
    move plg-ccyy  to dat-ccyy

Assez simple, n'est-ce pas?

- Harry -

Sydney wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sydney
```

- **From:** "Jason Stine" <jasonstine@mindspring.com>

```
if anyone has got the chance to reply before noon on Tuesday, 23 March, then
I'd be grateful:

1.  Wouldyou please give an example in COBOL code of Y2K error?
2.  Would you please give an example of how to fix it -- re-write that line
of code?

interaction with another program or database that only accepts 2-digit
dates?

Thank you very much,

jasonstine@mindspring.com
```

```
01  TODAYS-DATE.
    02  TODAY-YY    PIC 9(2).
    02  TODAY-MM    PIC 9(2).
    02  TODAY-DD    PIC 9(2).
01  LAST-PAYMENT.
    02  LAST-YY    PIC 9(2).
    02  LAST-MM    PIC 9(2).
    02  LAST-DD    PIC 9(2).


COMPUTE DAYS-DELINQUENT = (TODAY-YY * 365  +  TODAY-MM * 30
    + TODAY-DD) - (LAST-YY * 365 + LAST-MM * 30 + LAST-DD).
IF DAYS-DELINQUENT > 180
    DISPLAY 'Terminate life support for patient: '  PATIENT-NAME.

Rewrite:

IF DAYS-DELINQUENT > 180
    DISPLAY 'Investigate life support for patient: ' PATIENT-NAME.

then
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K ERROR QUESTIONS

- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00

```
"Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
>01  TODAYS-DATE.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi all;

Would it not be better if you resize the variables as well???

01  TODAYS-DATE.
    02  TODAY-YY    PIC 9(4).
    02  TODAY-MM    PIC 9(2).
    02  TODAY-DD    PIC 9(2).
01  LAST-PAYMENT.
    02  LAST-YY    PIC 9(4).
    02  LAST-MM    PIC 9(2).
    02  LAST-DD    PIC 9(2).

Of cause this will probably require a rewrite of any files used, which
should be fairly easy, if the dates are below 2000.
--Regards :                David. E. Goble
                           goble@kin.on.net
           http://www-mugc.cc.monash.edu.au/~degob1/goble
      Po Box 648 Kingscote Kangaroo Island South Australia 5223
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K ERROR QUESTIONS

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mrrK2.18093$Ek.15594784@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7d3aj5$sfc$1@samsara0.mindspring.com> <7d4cme$k5j$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36f9f7a2.0@duster.adelaide.on.net>`

```
David. E. Goble wrote:
>"Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>    02  LAST-DD    PIC 9(2).


1. You should make sure the size of DAYS-DELINQUENT is large enough to
   hold well over 2000 * 365 = 730,000, say 6 digits, which should work
   until sometime in the 29th century.

2. The two characters in DD, MM and YY imply two digit fields.  So data
   names TODAY-YY and LAST-YY should be changed to TODAY-YYYY and
   LAST-YYYY or TODAY-CCYY and LAST-CCYY for clarity.  You can change
   all the fields to -YEAR, -MONTH, -DAY, but that often conflicts with
   a similar field set up for the same date in month, day, year format.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K ERROR QUESTIONS

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dg2jt$mas$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7d3aj5$sfc$1@samsara0.mindspring.com> <7d4cme$k5j$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36f9f7a2.0@duster.adelaide.on.net>`

```
In article <36f9f7a2.0@duster.adelaide.on.net>,
  goble@kin.on.net wrote:
> "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
> >
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Of course, resizing the variables in the files is the "best" and most solid
way of fixing the problem, even though it doesn't address all the issues.  It
is also deceptively difficult to implement.  You have to convert the file and
EVERY program that touches it all at once.  This can turn out to be a LOT of
programs, JCL, CICS tables, files and databases that all have to be converted
at once.  This is why logic (code) is continually evolving, but data
structures live forever.  A lot of shops will go to extraordinary lengths to
avoid changing a data structure.

Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **From:** hodori@shore.net (Yaung-Moon Cho (Doug))

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Trying to close a CICS TDQ from within an online program, added the
following code and I'm running into a translation error.

2      OPENSTATUS('CLOSE')
3      RESP(ws-resp)
4 END-EXEC.

The error message is:

I'm new to CICS and COBOL programming, and would appreciate your help.
Much thanks in advance.

Cheers,
DOug
```

```
On 21 Mar 1999 23:39:53 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
enlightened us:

>Yaung-Moon Cho (Doug) <hodori@shore.net> wrote in article
><hodori-2103991434130001@netwlls-s01-182.port.shore.net>...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>SPI commands must be translated with the SP translator option".

Right, my memory (see previous post) has been jogged.  The SP is an
option that must be specified when running the CICS/COBOL translator.
It is an option for the translator and is not put in your EXEC CICS
command.  You may have to talk to your system administrator in order
to change translator options.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

friends.  If they're OK, you're it.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: exec cics set tdqueue -- SP option?

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d6itj$gb3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<hodori-2103991434130001@netwlls-s01-182.port.shore.net> <01be73f3$15551fe0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <E90DD5DE6941EFC7.D162C3D2F536CC56.069DC7F18B7728E9@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
In article <E90DD5DE6941EFC7.D162C3D2F536CC56.069DC7F18B7728E9@library-
proxy.airnews.net>,
  swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB) wrote:
> On 21 Mar 1999 23:39:53 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
> enlightened us:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> >SPI commands must be translated with the SP translator option".

If you can't have your Translator Options changed to support 'SP', then try
issuing a CICS 'LINK' to the CEMT-Programmable Interface Entry Point
'DFHEMTP', with the following:

03  WS-COMMAREA PIC X(21) VALUE 'SET TDQ(XXXX) OPE ENA'.

EXEC CICS LINK
          PROGRAM ('DFHEMTP')
          COMMAREA(WS-COMMAREA)
          END-EXEC.

You won't need the 'SP' option. However, you may get a NOTAUTH response when
you attampt the LINK. You won't need to specify the LENGTH as COBOL2 (or
greater) will build it for you. The one drawback is that 'DFHEMTP' does not
return a response-code, indicating success or failure. The other programmable-
interface entry point is 'DFHEMTA', but I'll save that for another time.

HTH....

WOB



-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: exec cics set tdqueue -- SP option?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f72d77.859135@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<hodori-2103991434130001@netwlls-s01-182.port.shore.net> <01be73f3$15551fe0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <E90DD5DE6941EFC7.D162C3D2F536CC56.069DC7F18B7728E9@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7d6itj$gb3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Mon, 22 Mar 1999 23:15:09 GMT, WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>If you can't have your Translator Options changed to support 'SP', then try
>issuing a CICS 'LINK' to the CEMT-Programmable Interface Entry Point
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>You won't need the 'SP' option. 

Great, and the next thing he tries is 

MOVE 'PERFORM SHUTDOWN IMMEDIATE' TO  WS-COMMAREA

and re-issue the command......

Seriously, though:  
a) The SP Translator option can be set in the CBL XOPTS statement at the beginning of the
source code, therefore there is always a chanve to change/set it

b) SPI commands might be restricted via RACF or others, and programs using them will not
work for most end-users (as I cannot imagine that SET TDQUEUE would be allowed for the
"plain vanilla" enduser


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```
