#### ↳ Re: Q: exec cics set tdqueue -- SP option?

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EA957E884A42678F.9669988F47C92E1C.75FF962D9A8C7E11@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<hodori-2103991434130001@netwlls-s01-182.port.shore.net>`

```
On Sun, 21 Mar 1999 14:34:11 -0500, hodori@shore.net (Yaung-Moon Cho
(Doug)) enlightened us:

>Seeking help:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks again!

This is stretching my memory a bit, but I remember running into this
about 5 or 6 years ago.  You are issuing a CICS command that is
normally done using CEMT.  The SP parameter refers to a supervisor
option.  I remember I had a hard time finding the parameters for it,
but it is documented in one of the many (don't remember exactly which
one) IBM CICS manuals.  You might try looking in the CICS Programmers
Reference Manual.

Maybe another CICS programmer in the group can help you out a little
more if you can't find the answer in the manuals.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Psychiatrists say that 1 of 4 people are mentally ill.  Check three
friends.  If they're OK, you're it.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: exec cics set tdqueue -- SP option?

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F72E79.2E0D131F@att.net>`
- **References:** `<hodori-2103991434130001@netwlls-s01-182.port.shore.net>`

```
"Yaung-Moon Cho (Doug)" wrote:
> 
> Seeking help:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 4 END-EXEC.

You need to add "SP" to your parameters for the CICS translator. Certain
commands, e.g., SET, are considered part of the Systems Programming
interface and require the additional parameter.

Regarding the queue you're closing, have you insured that it's an
extrapartition destination? You can't close an intrapartition
destination.

Bill Lynch
```

## Thread: rm/cobol under sco openserver 5.0.4 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### rm/cobol under sco openserver 5.0.4

- **From:** Dave Trausch <aceco@ginetworks.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F57842.51896536@ginetworks.com>`

```
os = SCO Openserver Rel. 5.0.4
compiler version = Liant rm/cobol

What I need is a little script that will run on top of existing compiled

scripts.  Basically I want the script to be able to enter data out of a
comma
delimited ascii (or .dbf) file.  The script would enter the data as so:

 existing function                            wanna-be script function

1. Enter Part Number                    123456789
2. Enter Cross Reference               987654321
3. Weight                                       12 kilos
4. Price                                           20,000

and so on.

What I'm trying to do is take price listings out of manufacturer price
lists
in .dbf form and plug them into fields in a cobol compiled database.

I don't want free custom programming, but what I do want is some
guidance
into how to accomplish this.  I know it's probably not difficult (wait,
enter,
wait, enter).  Really, if this sounds absolutely retarded, I apologize
in
advance.
Thank you!

Dave Trausch
dave@appliedcontrols.net
```

#### ↳ Re: rm/cobol under sco openserver 5.0.4

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F6185A.396BA5D6@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<36F57842.51896536@ginetworks.com>`

```
Dave Trausch wrote:

> os = SCO Openserver Rel. 5.0.4
> compiler version = Liant rm/cobol
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> dave@appliedcontrols.net

Easy (if I understand your problem right).

"runcobol program < datafile"

If you need extra "confirm operation" answers, beside the actual data items,
it also can be done - just a little more complicated.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

#### ↳ Re: REGAIN CONTROL AFTER A CONTROL BREAK

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d3qk7$m2n$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36F528CF.571D@erols.com>`

```
Make them type "N" when the computer asks Abort? (Yes/No)

(you could replace the message with (Explode Violently)(Y/N), that might
work.

Baring that, prevent them from typing it by trapping it at the OS level)

Else, you are done.
.

Mark J. Lavine wrote in message <36F528CF.571D@erols.com>...
>DOES ANYONE KNOW HOW TO REGAIN CONTROL WITHIN YOUR PROGRAM AFTER THE
>USER DOES A CONTROL BREAK
>
>THANKS

#### ↳ Re: REGAIN CONTROL AFTER A CONTROL BREAK

- **From:** "Stephen Thomas" <thomass@gobble.logica.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d5at2$gkl@romeo.logica.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36F528CF.571D@erols.com>`

```
You don't mention OS or compiler/runtime vendor.....

By 'regain control' do you mean 'detect and take some action' or you just
want the program to ignore it.  If the latter ....

On Micro Focus COBOL, there's a switch in special-names which you can set to
get the runtime to ignore control+break.  It's a few years since I used it,
and I no longer work in a COBOL environment, so can't say exactly what it
is.
```

We had the same proplem printing DOS apps in windows...I adjusted my
CONFIG.SYS file Like this
FILES=255
BUFFERS=30
FCBS=32,8

Since then our printing problems went away..

## Thread: NetExpress and WinAPI concerning windows-messages — 3 msgs

### Re: NetExpress and WinAPI concerning windows-messages

- **From:** Florian Teichert <floteichNO@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F67364.D4D54193@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de>`
- **References:** `<36EF816E.DE3257FA@hrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de> <7coajg$eap@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Hi again,

> The most straightforward way to achieve this (assuming that you are not
> using Dialog System or the object COBOL class libraries)

I could although use DS is there an easy method, too??

> To be able to do this you should also process the Windows message queue
> regularly - ie your program should ideally be callback-based.

What do you mean by "callback-based"? Do I have to look at the message queue
at specific places in my program to check if something is in for me?
I'd rather want an event to be triggered when a message is posted to me.

Thanks YOU, and bye,
Florian
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and WinAPI concerning windows-messages

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d7pno$vh7@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36EF816E.DE3257FA@hrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de> <7coajg$eap@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <36F67364.D4D54193@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de>`

```
Florian Teichert <floteichNO@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de> wrote:
>I could although use DS is there an easy method, too??

It would essentially be the same method, except that you would have to 
subclass (ie create another callback procedure for, see below) one of your 
Dialog System windows. Dialog System would also take care of the Windows 
message loop.

>What do you mean by "callback-based"? Do I have to look at the message queue
>at specific places in my program to check if something is in for me?
>I'd rather want an event to be triggered when a message is posted to me.

Callback-based means that you have to export an entry point in your 
program that the system calls you back on to inform you that something has 
happened. The whole of the Windows GUI is a callback-based system, where 
you create a window with a registered callback procedure, and Windows 
sends you messages on that procedure.

If you want to pick up a specific message sent to the window, you evaluate 
the messages sent to the window in the procedure. You can then call 
whatever message/"event"-handler you want. In the example I pointed you 
to, the procedure "MyWndProc" is the callback, and it deals with some 
standard Windows messages such as WM_COMMAND.

So, if you can get Delphi to post a message to your window, you can check 
for that value in the callback procedure that handles messages for that 
window! I don't know Delphi, but it is almost certainly doing something 
similar.

Steve.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress and WinAPI concerning windows-messages

- **From:** Florian Teichert <floteichNO@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de>
- **Date:** 1999-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36FE77B2.D321836C@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de>`
- **References:** `<36EF816E.DE3257FA@hrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de> <7coajg$eap@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <36F67364.D4D54193@SPAMhrzpub.tu-darmstadt.de> <7d7pno$vh7@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Hi Steve, hi all,

your answers have been very tutorial to me, I nearly managed/understood the
topic, thank you very much so far,
but there is still one minor problem left:

Steve Biggs wrote:

> If you want to pick up a specific message sent to the window, you evaluate
> the messages sent to the window in the procedure. You can then call
> whatever message/"event"-handler you want. In the example I pointed you
> to, the procedure "MyWndProc" is the callback, and it deals with some
> standard Windows messages such as WM_COMMAND.

What I don't know yet is where to assign my event-handling-procedure to, the
MyWndProc is called by a window created with Cobol/WinAPI, the window I have to
service is created with Dialog System.

Could you help once more??

Thanx in advance, Florian.
```

I don't think you will get a useful answer to this question.  Some of us can
tell you the difference between AS/400 COBOL and
   - ANSI/ISO Standard COBOL
  - S/35 or S/38 COBOL
  - OS/390 COBOL
  -  many other flavors of COBOL

but it is impossible to compare AS/400 COBOL with "non-specific" COBOL.  If
you give us a little background on your question, we may be able to help
more.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 X"86" command — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 X"86" command

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990322081900.09596.00000005@ng139.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I am trying to duplicate a "Debug" command with the MicroFocus X"86" command. 
The following is the debug command:

Debug
o 240 28
o 241 06
o 240 29
o 241 01
q

The following is  a sample of my COBOL program:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  MEMORY-SEGMENT-INDEX          PIC 9(05)  VALUE 576.
01 MEMORY-SEGMENT-DATA            PIC 9(05)  VALUE 577.

01  MEMORY-OFFSET                        PIC 9(05)  VALUE ZEROS.
01  MEMORY-DATA-VALUE                PIC X(01).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

MOVE X"28" TO MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

CALL X"86" USING MEMORY-SEGMENT-INDEX,
                            MEMORY-OFFSET,
                            MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

MOVE X"06" TO MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

CALL X"86" USING MEMORY-SEGMENT-DATA,
                             MEMORY-OFFSET,
                             MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

MOVE X"29" TO MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

CALL X"86" USING MEMORY-SEGMENT-INDEX,
                            MEMORY-OFFSET,
                            MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

MOVE X"01" TO MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

CALL X"86" USING MEMORY-SEGMENT-DATA,
                            MEMORY-OFFSET,
                           MEMORY-DATA-VALUE.

EXIT PROGRAM.

STOP RUN.

When I use the "Debug" command i get the desired results (i.e. the cash drawer
opens).  When i run my progam nothing happens.  Can any one spot where my error
might be ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 X"86" command

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d5mgc$a07$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<19990322081900.09596.00000005@ng139.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 wrote in message <19990322081900.09596.00000005@ng139.aol.com>...
>Good Morning,
>
>I am trying to duplicate a "Debug" command with the MicroFocus X"86"
command.
>The following is the debug command:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Use X"88" to output to a port.

01 port pic 9(5) value 576.
01 data-value pic x value x"28".

CALL X"88" USING port, data-value.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 X"86" command

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00

```
Hi Rick,

That worked great.  How did you know to use the "88" instead of the "86" ?  

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL 3.2 X"86" command

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dbeja$g4e$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7d5mgc$a07$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990324084253.13537.00003456@ng127.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 wrote in message <19990324084253.13537.00003456@ng127.aol.com>...
>Hi Rick,
>
>That worked great.  How did you know to use the "88" instead of the "86" ?
>
The DEBUG O-command worked.
The DEBUG O-command outputs to a port.
The Micro Focus CALL X"88" writes a byte to a port.
Therefore, the X"88" should be the CALL to use.

Simple deductive reasoning.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-e3f0b079d6"></a>
## Thread: Report Writer Question — 3 msgs

### Report Writer Question

- **From:** Pat Newton <patricia@america.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F6EA3E.82F4712C@america.net>`

```
I have a Report Writer problem I've been struggling with and would like
to ask for help. I have input data that includes employee numbers and
salaries. For IN-EMPLOYEE-NO = 999 I need to add asterisks in front of
the name and have an output that is a different PIC than the
others(employee numbers are not unique; there can be more than one
employee coded 999).  The PIC clause for all others employees is
$$$,$$9, whereas the PIC for the employees with number 999 is ***,**9. I
have the asterisks part done and thought I'd use that field as a control
break, but it causes the employee's salary to print three times at the
control break. Any ideas for something simpler and/or better? Thanks in
advance for any advice.
```

#### ↳ Re: Report Writer Question

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F8842A.1DF9@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<36F6EA3E.82F4712C@america.net>`

```
Pat:

There have been some improvements in this are of Report Writer (i.e. 
choices of contents) in the latest Standard and some implementations that 
anticipate it.  But I'll assume you're using a current standard, such as 
IBM OS/VS COBOL or Micro Focus or Fujitsu etc.  (This way would work 
on any platform.)  And I'll sketch the improved method later.

First of all, forget control breaks.  These cause extra lines (well, 
extra report groups) to be printed and you DON'T want extra lines here, 
right?

You have to define a Working-Storage area and use that as a Source. Then 
before you GENERATE the line(s) set it up in the format you want.  E.g.

 05  WS-REP-EMPLOYEE.
   07  WS-REP-EMP-999.
      09  WS-REP-EMP-SAL-AST      PIC ***,**9.
   07  WS-REP-EMP-OTH  REDEFINES WS-REP-RMP-999.
      09  WS-REP-EMP-SAL-OTH      PIC $$$,$$9.
...
(in Report Section)
 01  EMPLOYEE-LINES   TYPE DE.
  05  LINE ...
    07  COLUMN ...   PIC X(7)  SOURCE IS WS-REP-EMPLOYEE.
...
 (in Procedure Division)
   IF  IN-EMPLOYEE-NO = 999 
       MOVE IN-EMPLOYEE-SAL TO WS-REP-EMP-SAL-AST
   ELSE
       MOVE IN-EMPLOYEE-SAL TO WS-REP-EMP-SAL-OTH.
   GENERATE EMPLOYEE-LINES

You'll get the general idea even if the details vary a little.

With the new standard, you can put the condition in the Report Section, 
thus:
 01  EMPLOYEE-LINES   TYPE DE.
  05  LINE ...
    07  COLUMN ...   PIC ***,**9  SOURCE IS IN-EMPLOYEE-SAL
        PRESENT WHEN IN-EMPLOYEE-NO = 999.
    07  COLUMN ...   PIC $$$,$$9  SOURCE IS IN-EMPLOYEE-SAL
        PRESENT WHEN IN-EMPLOYEE-NO NOT = 999.

For further info and a whole gallery of examples, see
  www.spc-systems.com/refce/cobrwr.htm.

Pat Newton wrote:
> 
> I have a Report Writer problem I've been struggling with and would like
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> advance for any advice.
```

#### ↳ Re: Report Writer Question

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PC.IC.212KMKEe9mq4CDqs4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<36F6EA3E.82F4712C@america.net>`

```
    Pat,


    Have you tried:

1)  making a separate *(e.g., 01  EMP-NO-999 TYPE IS) DETAIL* line
for the IN-EMPLOYEE-NO = 999 2)  test in the PROCEDURE DIVISION the
EMPLOYEE-NO whether or not it = 999
    IF IN-EMPLOYEE-NO NOT = 999
        GENERATE the origianl DETAIL line ELSE GENERATE the new (for
        999 only) DETAIL line.

    This should keep things neat & tidy.

And get rid of your CONTROL BREAKS, since all you want to do is to
GENEREATE a different DETAIL only for EMPLOYEE- NO = 999.

Jay
```

<a id="t-fb177e330c"></a>
## Thread: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!! — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EACJ2.1455$%u2.4199@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au>`

```
There are a ton of posts on this already, but here is my take (MVS
COBOL II only).

When a dataset is created on either DASD or Tape, it gets a dataset
label.  The operating system gives a DASD dataset a label
automatically.  I can't imagine why you would create a disk dataset
without a label, but there might be a reason.

However, for tapes in MVS, each tape can either have a "tape label"
written to it, or it can go without.  A tape label is a set of records
on a tape.  A "standard" IBM MVS tape label starts with a 6 byte
volume serial, followed by an 80 byte Header-1 record, and an 80 byte
Header-2 record (there are also "tape marks" and End of Volume and End
of Tape records, and a lot of rules about datasets spanning tapes, but
I'll skip that discussion) .  These records contained bottom-line
information about what was on the tape (dataset name, lrecl, recfm,
blksize, etc.).  Most of the time, having this data directly on the
tape facilitates tape management software, and is a good last resort
to look at via a tape dump utility called DITTO if you've got no clue
what is going on with a particular tape (label ripped off, from
offsite, etc.).  In some ways, it was middlin good for security (if
the sysadmin cared to control the ways around using labels).   Going
without was good for portability.  By specifying NL in the JCL
(non-labeled processing), an OS wouldn't even check for a label and
would just start reading bytes, which prevented the OS from choking on
some foreign label system that it couldn't read.

By specifying LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD, you are telling your program
to expect the dataset you are using to have a "standard" dataset
label.  For DASD this is pretty much pointless (or at least it is in
my shop).  All DASD datasets have standard labels (I've never seen
someone try to force a disk dataset to go NL).  For tape, however,
it's a real point.  Specifying LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED forces the
JCL to require NL on the LABEL parm.  Specifying LABEL RECORDS ARE
STANDARD forces the TAPE to have a "standard" label, if it doesn't
have such a label, your program probably will take some sort of open
macro error; and the JCL hadn't better try to say LABEL=(1,NL) when
the program is looking for something else.

The bottom line is, the information in this line can be specified in
JCL, which is easier to change than the program (usually).  Avoiding
the LABEL RECORDS clause in your programs can help avoid problems, and
can make change management easier.


In no way would either OMITTED or STANDARD affect file format.  These
labels precede the data and identify information about the data to the
OS.




Moose <moose@rocknet.net.au> wrote in message
news:36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au...
>
> I have been trying to find out about the LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED /
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and a few FAQs and loads of old programs - nothing sheds light on
the
> difference.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7797C.B16BB317@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au> <EACJ2.1455$%u2.4199@news.uswest.net>`

```
ChrisOsborne wrote:
> 
> When a dataset is created on either DASD or Tape, it gets a dataset
> label.  The operating system gives a DASD dataset a label
> automatically.  I can't imagine why you would create a disk dataset
> without a label, but there might be a reason.

DASD datasets do not have a label.  They have a catalog entry that tells
you all about them but it is not a label.

The cobol program LABEL statement acts as a comment only as stated
before.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zqOJ2.1620$%u2.4310@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36EDDFD7.9845BDBA@rocknet.net.au> <EACJ2.1455$%u2.4199@news.uswest.net> <36F7797C.B16BB317@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:36F7797C.B16BB317@zip.com.au...
> ChrisOsborne wrote:
> >
> > When a dataset is created on either DASD or Tape, it gets a
dataset
> > label.  The operating system gives a DASD dataset a label
> > automatically.  I can't imagine why you would create a disk
dataset
> > without a label, but there might be a reason.
>
> DASD datasets do not have a label.  They have a catalog entry that
tells
> you all about them but it is not a label.
>
> The cobol program LABEL statement acts as a comment only as stated
> before.
>

Ken Foskey,

Technically, you are correct, however:

From Expert MVS/ESA JCL: A Guide to Advanced Techniques, page 11.

"The VTOC contains entries which are known as DSCBs (Data Set Control
Blocks).  There are several different types of DSCBs, the most common
of which is a Format 1 DSCB.  For each non-VSAM data set on a volume
there exists such a DSCB in the VTOC of the volume describing it.  It
is in essence the label of a disk data set."

I was thinking of these DSCB entries, but not actually describing them
to avoid getting into something that is pretty much in the hands of
the access methods, when I was talking about a DASD dataset label.
DSCBs come in various formats and do more things than tape labels,
like noting where on the volume a dataset's extents are.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: LABEL RECORDS ARE .... Confusing !!!

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990323184655.23933.00000603@ng120.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<zqOJ2.1620$%u2.4310@news.uswest.net>`

```
Somebody wrote...

>>
>> DASD datasets do not have a label.  They have a catalog entry that
>tells
>> you all about them but it is not a label.
>>

Wrong. All DASD data sets have a DSCB, which _is_ the label for a disk data
set. A DASD data set does not need to have a catalog entry (you can have
uncataloged DASD data sets, though I don't know why one would do that today).
If a DASD data set is cataloged, then it also has a catalog record (entry). The
catalog entry does _not_ "tell you all about them". The catalog entry contains
the data set name, device type (UNIT), and volume serial(s) (VOL). It is the
DSCB that has information like extents (where the data are found on the disk),
DCB information, creation date / expiration date, etc.

Regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d5gng$gmq$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Recently, I found a module with the name IGZTRCL in a library in the linklist
of our OS/390 system. It has a compile date of 86/10/14, was last linked at
1988/05/16 by LINKXA and has the following IDENT: 86/10/15 RSI62871168. AMODE
is 31, RMODE is ANY and it contains a copyright by IBM, but no product
information.

Is it part of the old OS/VS COBOL runtime (we de-installed OS/VS COBOL 1990 or

runtime? I have some IMS transactions which behave differently with LE in
STEPLIB than with LE in the LINKLIST.

Daniel

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>

```
Daniel Jacot wrote:

> Recently, I found a module with the name IGZTRCL in a library in the linklist
> of our OS/390 system. It has a compile date of 86/10/14, was last linked at
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> STEPLIB than with LE in the LINKLIST.

It has been a LONG time since I've worked with this, but I recall there is an
option in AMASPZAP or "IMASPZAP ?!?" to dump a program or module in a program into
hex, displaying the value of the data and giving the machine instruction for that
code if there is one.  Try to determine the entry point addresses for the modules
actually called, your cobol program would generate a call to IGZTRCL0 or IGZTRCL1,
and these wouldn't necessarily be at the start of your module.  HTH
```

```
Daniel Jacot wrote:
> 
> Recently, I found a module with the name IGZTRCL in a library in the linklist
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1991)?

There was a VS Cobol II run-time module of that name way back. It does
not exist in the current release - it was removed in release 3. The
release 2 one did have the product number in it though:
 +0  90ECD00C 47F0F070 40404040 4040F5F6  *.....00.      56*
     F6F860F9 F5F840C3 D6D7E8D9 C9C7C8E3  *68-958 COPYRIGHT*
+20  40C9C2D4 40C3D6D9 D740F1F9 F8F34040  * IBM CORP 1983  *
     40404040 D3C9C3C5 D5E2C5C4 40D4C1E3  *    LICENSED MAT*
+40  C5D9C9C1 D3406040 D7D9D6C7 D9C1D440  *ERIAL - PROGRAM *
     D7D9D6D7 C5D9E3E8 40D6C640 C9C2D440  *PROPERTY OF IBM *
+60  6F6F6F6F 6F6F6F6F C9C7E9E3 D9C3D340  *????????IGZTRCL *
     05B01BFF 50FD0010 58F00010 41FF0074  *....&....0......*
+80  9180F000 4710B01C 98ECD00C 07FE98EC  *j.0.....q.....q.*
     D00C0B0E 00000000 90ECD00C 47F0F070  *.............00.*

> 
> Could the presence or absence of this module cause interferences with the LE
> runtime? I have some IMS transactions which behave differently with LE in
> STEPLIB than with LE in the LINKLIST.

There should be no conflict of that module with any LE module, but you
can probably just get rid of it to be sure. Perhaps you have some other
"old" run-time modules in the linklist? 

>

## Thread: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** alexchapli@aol.com
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I know this isn't strictly COBOL but I'm sure plenty of JCL gurus read this
newsgroup.  Does anyone know of a way that IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF statement can
be overridden in JCL? For example

//CCTEST 	IF 	(PREVSTEP.ABENDCC = U0100) THEN
//FIRST  	EXEC  	PGM=PGM01
//NOCC 	    	ELSE
//SECOND 	EXEC  	PGM=PGM02
// 	        ENDIF


How would I code overrides to the IF and the ELSE part of this JCL, say if I
wanted to the following to happen

//CCTEST 	IF 	(PREVSTEP.RC = 4) THEN
//FIRST  	EXEC  	PGM=PGM01
//** ENDIF IS NOW BELOW IF RATHER THAN ELSE
//              ENDIF
//** ELSE STATEMENT REMOVED FROM HERE
//NOCC
//SECOND 	EXEC  	PGM=PGM02
//** ENDIF STATEMENT REMOVED FROM HERE

So step FIRST executes if the return code from PREVSTEP equals 4 and step
SECOND always executes.  Thanks in advance for any help or advice with this.


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990322141354.00969.00000226@ng-ch1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
my first thought would be if you are no longer testing for that condition why
not just comment it out?
```

#### ↳ Re: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-03-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f6a099.909547@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Mon, 22 Mar 1999 18:50:59 GMT, alexchapli@aol.com wrote:

>I know this isn't strictly COBOL but I'm sure plenty of JCL gurus read this
>newsgroup.  Does anyone know of a way that IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF statement can
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own    

I do not quite understand what you mean with "override".  Are you dealing with a "canned"
procedure?  Then overiding the IF THEN ELSE JCL statements is not an option.  But you can
use the blasted COND=(rc,relational operand[,stepname]) syntax to overide condition code
checking.  Unfortunately, though, using this syntax there is no checking for explicit user
or sysytem abend codes

If you are coding your own JCL - there are few limits.  Just go ahead and code for them
g
with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)

```

#### ↳ Re: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7779B.5FDD2E6@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
alexchapli@aol.com wrote:
> 
> I know this isn't strictly COBOL but I'm sure plenty of JCL gurus read this
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

First when you use the IF statement it is for a STEP not for a command
so you cannot swap the exec statement only.

>From memory you cannot use an ELSE statement however if you use:

IF X = 4
exec pgm=y
dd
dd
dd
endif
if x ^= 4
exec pgm=z
dd
dd
dd
endif

that fairly simple.

For the cond code diehards it is actually the recommended way of doing
thing in the JCL manual now.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7A4EC.8E3D21E5@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36F7779B.5FDD2E6@zip.com.au>`

```
alexchapli@aol.com wrote:

> I know this isn't strictly COBOL but I'm sure plenty of JCL gurus read this
> newsgroup.  Does anyone know of a way that IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF statement can
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> SECOND always executes.  Thanks in advance for any help or advice with this.

If you don't find a straightforward way, you can always be devious.  For
instance, you can create a step which has its sole purpose of checking
to see which temporary file exists and returning an appropriate return
code.  Then override the file creation in a previous step.
```

#### ↳ Re: Overriding JCL (JES3) IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d8en8$2kr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <7d63ee$253$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  alexchapli@aol.com wrote:
> I know this isn't strictly COBOL but I'm sure plenty of JCL gurus read this
> newsgroup.  Does anyone know of a way that IF/THEN/ELSE/ENDIF statement can
> be overridden in JCL?

<snip the rest>

Yes, you can 'override' every line of your JCL if you don't code it directly,
but rather include this statement. You can perfectly INCLUDE one or another IF
statement via symbolic parameters. A sample:

//XXXX PROC IF1=$$IF1,IF2=$$IF2
//  INCLUDE MEMBER=&IF1
//S1 EXEC PGM=IEFBR14
//  INCLUDE MEMBER=&IF1.END
//  INCLUDE MEMBER=&IF2
//S2 EXEC PGM=IEFBR14
//  INCLUDE MEMBER=&IF2.END
//   PEND
//GO1 EXEC PROC=XXXX
//GO2 EXEC PROC=XXXX,IF1=X1,IF2=X2

This sample-members contain:
$$IF1....:  //COND1 IF RC = 0 THEN
$$IF1END.:  //COND1   ENDIF
$$IF2....:  //COND2 IF RC = 0 THEN
$$IF2END.:  //COND2   ENDIF
X1.......:  //COND1 IF RC < 4 THEN
X1END....:  //*
X2.......:  //COND1 ELSE
X2END....:  //COND1 ENDIF

And the results:
GO1      S1          00
GO1      S2          00
GO2      S1          00
GO2      S2       FLUSH

Was it that what you were looking for?

Regards
Daniel
------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

> Does anybody use the API-function (Win32) GetOpenFileName from a
> Cobol-Program?
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I've done a little Win32 API programming in my Cobol pgms, though not that
particular call.  The problem is almost always in properly defining the WS
fields used as call parameters.  Send me the text of your CALL statements and
the results you're getting and I'll see if I can help get you on track.  Once
you've done a couple and see how to translate the "C" oriented documentation
to Cobol, it gets easier.

Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)

<a id="t-c6412402c6"></a>
## Thread: Y2K - 3 digit years in 2 bytes — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K - 3 digit years in 2 bytes

- **From:** "Lanny Levine" <llevine@sprint.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JsFJ2.38414$Mb.18714431@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>`

```
The system I work on is about 25 or 30 years old and, from what I've been
told, was based on an Insurance Admininstration system developed by IBM.

All of our internal dates are:
   YYY      PIC S9(03) COMP-3
   DDD     PIC S9(03) COMP-3

YYY is an offset from 1800. eg X'199C'
DDD are the Julian days

If it weren't for the auditors and certification we would have had
practically no Y2K work to do.

I would be interested in any comments as to why this technique was
(apparently) not used to any great extent.

Lanny
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K - 3 digit years in 2 bytes

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0jKJ2.15138$Ek.14136864@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<JsFJ2.38414$Mb.18714431@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>`

```
Lanny Levine wrote:
>The system I work on is about 25 or 30 years old and, from what I've been
>told, was based on an Insurance Admininstration system developed by IBM.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>(apparently) not used to any great extent.

Processing (entering, displaying, printing) a date in coded form is
more complex than processing a simple year, because it must be coded
or decoded between all human interface.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K - 3 digit years in 2 bytes

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jVyK2.114$s5.825254@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<JsFJ2.38414$Mb.18714431@newscontent-02.sprint.ca> <0jKJ2.15138$Ek.14136864@news1.mia>`

```
>>The system I work on is about 25 or 30 years old and, from what I've been
>>told, was based on an Insurance Admininstration system developed by IBM.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>(apparently) not used to any great extent.


I have seen it used in a vsam database system called Tesseract. Although I
believe they used 1900 as the base year. I also believe in older versions of
CICs the same technique is used in one of the system dates passed back and
forth in the comm area, its been a while so I may be a little off with that
one.

If your going to store dates in expanded EBCDIC/ASCII format you shouldn't
switch to this format just because its easier. Its almost as easy as using a
sliding window for the century or even a constantly updated fixed window.

In my opinion it is just lazy programming. Add 2 bytes and fix it for good.
Certainly no source code which exists today will be functioning in
01/01/8299 but that is what they said about 1999. Faster hardware doesn't
always mean new code.

That's just my opinion but I could be wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K - 3 digit years in 2 bytes

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36FF9F41.C1C2A9FE@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<JsFJ2.38414$Mb.18714431@newscontent-02.sprint.ca> <0jKJ2.15138$Ek.14136864@news1.mia> <jVyK2.114$s5.825254@storm.twcol.com>`

```
> If your going to store dates in expanded EBCDIC/ASCII format you shouldn't
> switch to this format just because its easier. Its almost as easy as using a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's just my opinion but I could be wrong.

But the main advantage in adding century characters is that it always
works.  It will work when the requirements change.   Since requirements
do change, why not code the year logic just once.  I can see people
saying that school kids have to be under 100 years old and they will be
usually right.   Make 100 assumptions like that though, and I guarantee
one of them will be wrong.  Or the law or business rules will change.

It's sort of like deciding how large of a table we will need in our
COBOL program to handle future needs.  First, decide how expensive it
would be to eliminate the table altogether, replacing it with logic
which won't break down if things change too much.
```

#### ↳ Re: Y2K - 3 digit years in 2 bytes

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7A358.D666EEEB@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<JsFJ2.38414$Mb.18714431@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>`

```
Sounds somewhat similar to a seven digit date a days-between-dates set of
copybooks used in our legacy code in which 1900 is subtracted from the year
in YYYMMDD format.  A lot of our code moved a six digit date to that area,
relying on an automatic left zero fill.  While doing Y2K here, I developed a
script to handle the replacement of that code.

It looks like YOUR legacy code won't give you any problems.  Enjoy the new
year's eve celebration.

Lanny Levine wrote:

> The system I work on is about 25 or 30 years old and, from what I've been
> told, was based on an Insurance Admininstration system developed by IBM.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> (apparently) not used to any great extent.
```

#### ↳ Re: now that y2k is . . . .

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36f717c8.2161552@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7d6nf0$kfe$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 Mar 1999 00:32:32 GMT, kenward7@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>Now that Y2K is winding down the next phase must be Multiple guess as follows
> A. cleaning all those 'time bomb' windows.
> B. getting ready for the Y10K bug.
> C. catching up on everything else.
> D. all of the above.


None of the above.  How about:

E.  Figuring out how to fix the 2037 problem.

But then again, when I think of it 37 years ago, I was just being
born, and a computer related career was the rare exception.  We have
come quite a long way in 37 years - but most of that advancement was
in the first 20.  The last 17 have shown relately little in the way of
applications etc.  While it might seem a lot -- it really isn't.

So the question really is, will the legacy code that is in use today,
with a built in 2037 problem, still be around when the problem is set
to occur?
```

Compiler Version 3 on a Sun Solaris. If I try to load this library with the
dlopen function from a program written in C, I get the following error

Martin Herbst

#### ↳ Re: Using Fujitsu COBOL V3 on Sun Solaris together with a C main program

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d7uis$bbs$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7d7hnp$hla$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>`

```
You may have a problem with case sensitivity. I have seen this error in
calling C from Cobol fairly often, and it is usually fixed by using the
Cobol option that turns off link sensitively to case.  Going in the opposite
direction, I do not know.

Martin Herbst wrote in message <7d7hnp$hla$1@news08.btx.dtag.de>...
>I created a shared library from a COBOL subprogram using the Fujitsu COBOL
>Compiler Version 3 on a Sun Solaris. If I try to load this library with the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-5dbd5c2c51"></a>
## Thread: New Jersey's Woes - An Example — 42 msgs

### New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<7d91j7$j1s$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```


So I get an email from a buddy o' mine... 'Hey, what's up with this New
Jersey screwup, how did a simple Y2K patch make the checks go out early?'
I sat at the keyboard and I coded up the following which I offer, gratis,
to those who might need such a thing.  Systems-programmers, bare-metal
coders, folks who design compilers and the like may sneer at the quaint
COBOL which follows... but like it or not, this, or something quite like
it, is running on the Jersey Iron.

-- begin quote of myself:

Could be... consider the following two bits of code:

MOVE MFCS-CUST-NO-01 TO WS-CUSTNO.
IF INDEPENDENT-CUSTOMER
    PERFORM A53102-ALLOCATE-LOT THRU A53102-EXIT
    PERFORM C88092-CALC-CUBES   THRU C88092-EXIT
    PERFORM E35702-FIND-SHPWGHT THRU E35702-EXIT
    INSPECT WS-CUSTNO TALLYING WS-PERFORM-SUB FOR ALL 'QZ'
    IF WS-PERFORM-SUB > 0 AND WS-DT-REC-ADDED < WS-CUTOFF
        MOVE 'Y'  TO SPECL-CUST-FLAG
        PERFORM B28095-PROCESS-SPECUST THRU B28095-EXIT
         VARYING SUB1 FROM WS-PERFORM-SUB BY 1
          UNTIL SUB1 > WS-PERFORM-SUB
    END-IF
END-IF.

MOVE MFCS-CUST-NO-01 TO WS-CUSTNO.
IF INDEPENDENT-CUSTOMER
    PERFORM A53102-ALLOCATE-LOT THRU A53102-EXIT
    PERFORM C88092-CALC-CUBES   THRU C88092-EXIT
    PERFORM E35702-FIND-SHPWGHT THRU E35702-EXIT
    INSPECT WS-CUSTNO TALLYING WS-PERFORM-SUB FOR ALL 'QZ'
    IF WS-DT-REC-ADDED-YY < 40
        MOVE 20 TO WS-DT-REC-ADDED-CC
    ELSE
        MOVE 19 TO WS-DT-REC-ADDED-CC
    END-IF
    IF WS-CUTOFF-YY < 40
        MOVE 20 TO WS-CUTOFF-CC
    ELSE
        MOVE 19 TO WS-CUTOFF-CC
    END-IF.
    IF WS-PERFORM-SUB > 0 AND WS-DT-REC-ADDED-CCYYMMDD <
WS-CUTOFF-CCYYMMDD
        MOVE 'Y'  TO SPECL-CUST-FLAG
        PERFORM B28095-PROCESS-SPECUST THRU B28095-EXIT
         VARYING SUB1 FROM WS-PERFORM-SUB BY 1
          UNTIL SUB1 > WS-PERFORM-SUB
    END-IF
END-IF.


(note: I made this up more-or-less on the spot; the syntax is a more
modern dialect of COBOL called 'COBOL '85' or 'COBOL II'... it would have
been *much* more difficult to implement under COBOL '68 or COBOL '74)

This is a very commonly-used technique called 'windowing'.  A 'pivot
date', in this case the year (20)40, is decided-upon... of the year is
less than 40 (00 - 39) then it is considered to be in the 21st century, if
the year is 40 or higher then it is assumed to be in the 20th century.

Stop pissing and whining about how this is an incomplete solution; I
*know* that already... what is important is:

1) Windowing is a solution already decided-upon by many organisations
(and, interestingly enough, will be satisfactory for most of their
applications)

2) the code above, shown 'pre-window' and with window inserted, contains
an introduced logical flaw which will cause it to malfunction.

That's right... the code I inserted to make the program Y2K-compliant has
introduced a bug.  Where is it?

(oh... and while you're looking for the bug remember that your boss, his
boss and *her* boss are all looking over your shoulder and/or pacing
outside of your cubicle, reminding you how Importantit is that this get
fixed Right Now because it is Costing Money because the system is down
*and* they *can't* understand why you didn't get it right the First
Time... just to give you a bit of atmosphere, don'cha know)

(clock's ticking... welfare-moms are spending the money while you're
looking... where's the error, Captain COBOL?)

--end quoted text

Gosh... in the past week I've started threads about sending files via ftp
from NT workstations to mainframes and back again *and* this'un about
remediation difficulties in real live code... I must be slipping,
*everyone* knows that I'm only good for one-liners and demonstrating that
gold isn't money 'cause I don't get no Dime-A-Rands in my pocket-change.

DD
```

Nice sample code, and no, I didn't find the bug that you introduced,
but I thought the error was reported as "1999" being entered as "199 "
and the blank got replaced with a zero, making it "1990".

I didn't see that particular piece of logic in your example.

I think the reports are of a *typing* error, not a program logic
error, per se.

Then again, maybe I'm confusing it with some other report.

DS

#### ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** Robert Egan <egan263@nospam_allowed.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36F83DE0.83335E1F@nospam_allowed.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7d91j7$j1s$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
The only explanation I can think of is that your source data exceeds
your window range. In other words, there are some valid records from
1939, etc, still in your database which are now processed incorrectly.

This is, of course, a danger inherent in all windowing solutions.

It would help if you could provide some "dummy" data <g>


Regards
Robert Egan
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00

> The only explanation I can think of is that your source data exceeds
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

The error, Mr Egan, is data-*in*dependent; it is a 'logic flaw' which
alter the flow of the code after a certain point no matter *which* data
bring it there.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** Robert Egan <egan263@nospam_allowed.ix.netcom.com>

Thanks for the tip. I guess the real culprit is the extra period,
prematurely terminating the outermost IF. Like most errors, blindingly
obvious once someone points it out <g>.

I don't understand why the compiler would allow the unmatched END-IF,
but I've been away from COBOL for many years.

Regards
Robert Egan

Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On 23 Mar 1999 21:37:43 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> with kind regards

That is correct, Mr Bandke... when I formulated the example I cut off
the final END-IFs and placed the period (full stop) after the last
WS-PERFORM-SUB; when I cut-n-pasted the example to the group I did so in
a sloppy manner and I apologise for the syntax error which it
introduced.

I would say, then, that based on my error this example should be
declared null and void and I once again apologise for sullying your eyes
with such laxity.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** The Goobers <docdwarf@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000

```
cory hamasaki wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 23 Mar 1999 21:37:43, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> something ccyymmdd and store it as ccyyddmm.

That is correct, Mr Hamasaki; I am not playing any tricks with Bad Data
or Funky Variable Mis-Naming.

> 
> I'm guessing that this routine authorizes the money if the above test is
> true.  It used to evaluate false.

You're taking the example too literally... but that is to be expected
from a bit-burner; paragraph-names like A53102-ALLOCATE-LOT,
C88092-CALC-CUBES and E35702-FIND-SHPWGHT are meant to be generic COBOL
routines, such as those found in a shipping-and-handling system.

> 
> The data used to be  990321 < 990315  which is false, no funds issued
>             if transaction date < cutoff the money date, issue the funds
> 
> In the expansion,  19990321 < 19990315 which is still false

Exactly... you're looking too hard for me to match New Jersey *exactly*,
rather than merely give a general example of how remediation can botch
code.

> 
> */
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> //sysudump  dd sysout=a   Give it to me.

It won't show up there.

> 
> > (clock's ticking... welfare-moms are spending the money while you're
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> OK, I give up, you are Caaaaaptain COOOOOBOL.

Not yet, Mr Hamasaki... go back and re-examine the specimen without
looking for it to reflect the New Jersey situation so precisely.

I'll even give you a hint... your first assertion is wrong.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36f86959.88591620@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7d91j7$j1s$1@callisto.clark.net>`

```
On 23 Mar 1999 21:37:43 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

>
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-IF.

There's been no windowing applied at this point - it occurs later.
What's going to happen is as soon as the cuttoff year is in 2000, this
code will execute (The IF will be true) - where last month it would
not have.    

This could have been remediated years ago, and just now fail.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<7dddum$bq4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7d91j7$j1s$1@callisto.clark.net> <36F893A1.FF4DF722@netscape.net>`

```
In article <36F893A1.FF4DF722@netscape.net>,
  burnt out case <burntoutcase@netscape.net> wrote:
>
>
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It does nothing for legacy code, but this is the very reason I advocate
adopting statement delimiters (END-IF, END-PERFORM, etc.) which then allows
the elimination of all procedure division periods except to terminate
paragraph names and paragraphs themselves.

A1000-DO-SOME-WORK.
    MOVE A TO B
    IF C = D
        ....
    ELSE
        ....
    END-IF

    EVALUATE PROCESS-FLAG
         ....
    END-EVALUATE
    .       <*---  only period ever needed.

Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New Jersey's Woes - An Example

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<36FA49E4.A8566129@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7d91j7$j1s$1@callisto.clark.net> <36F893A1.FF4DF722@netscape.net> <7dddum$bq4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
> It does nothing for legacy code, but this is the very reason I advocate
> adopting statement delimiters (END-IF, END-PERFORM, etc.) which then allows
> the elimination of all procedure division periods except to terminate
> paragraph names and paragraphs themselves.

I usually code periods out of habit, but sometimes I do so deliberately
around pre-compiler statements.  If I am not entirely comfortable with
the way a pre-compiler (in my case IDMS) expands code, I like to have
periods.

IDMS expanded the two commented lines below:
009100*    READY IASAR902-REGION USAGE-MODE RETRIEVAL
009200*        ON ANY-ERROR-STATUS
                 MOVE 2 TO DML-SEQUENCE
                 CALL 'IDMS' USING SUBSCHEMA-CTRL
                         IDBMSCOM (37)
                         IASAR902-REGION
                 IF NOT ANY-ERROR-STATUS PERFORM IDMS-STATUS;
                 ELSE
009300             PERFORM 9000-GENERAL-IDMS-ERROR.

I have seen in the past where it messed up when I have complex
conditions, so I always keep things simple (performing paragraphs,
avoiding NOT, etc).  I think twice and check the expansion when a DML
statement is within an EVALUATE or IF.  And terminate when I can.
```

the press pulpit and defend themselves.  (Is their assertion true?  I
dunno).

old data entry program would not let you enter 9_, but the new
expanded entry probably does not perform a proper data validation.

<a id="t-129c6eba85"></a>
## Thread: Compiler .... — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler ....

- **From:** Thomas Kessler <tkessler@ulb.ac.be>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F7BC77.FF9352B2@ulb.ac.be>`

```
Hi,
I'm beginner in cobol and I'm looking for a free COBOL compiler for
LINUX ....
Can someone help me ?

<a id="t-14491c7d83"></a>
## Thread: Status of Government Y2K? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Status of Government Y2K?

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F71FB5.6BF@bellsouth.net>`

```
I don't think Judson McClendon got a responses from anyone in the
government on the thread he started about the status of Y2K.  (Maybe
because he asked for "direct knowledge?")  I don't have direct knowledge
either, but . . .

The contractors we hired almost three years ago to make our VSE programs
work in MVS are still hard at work.  The absolute deadline of March 1
has been changed to March 23, but they won't be ready then either. 
(There is a tie-in to Y2K here, bear with me.)

The programs they are working with are maybe 75 programs which are, say
steps 1 through 100 of our Claims run, which is run nightly.  All the
tapes we generated are now made into disk files.  All this is done in
Washington.  Just this week, someone here was looking at one of their
output files and noticed that instead of a control record followed by
data records, it is a control record, a data record, control record,
data record, and so on.  The contractors were aware of another problem,
the program was supposed to create (in our case) three files but was
only creating one.  They weren't aware of how the file looked - control,
data, control, data.

Since this program is one I inherited ten years ago, I looked at our
code and found that a FIRST-RECORD-SW is set to determine when a control
record is written for each file, and is also used to close and open the
files.  Then I looked at their code.  Unbelievable.  They didn't replace
the switch, it was just commented out in two places so the switch is
always set to '1'.  There are four places in the program that tests the
switch for '0'; if it's '0' the file is closed.  In their version the
instruction 'move '0' to first-record-sw is commented out, replaced by
their own test; if-the-file-is-closed, open it, then set it to
file-is-open.  They never use their test to close the file, so we have
one file instead of three.  The first-record-sw, when set to '0'
bypasses the creation of the control record, but it's never set to zero,
hence the control record for every data record.

My point is this:  They've been testing these programs for over a year. 
All the output tapes, maybe a hundred of them are now put on disk.  It
never once occurred to them TO LOOK AT THE OUTPUT???  In the case of
'my' program, they changed the logic by messing with the switch, and
even then NEVER CHECKED THE OUTPUT??  And apparrently never had any
intention of looking at it, because we've been told several times "the
programs are ready, we're just having a little problem with the
panels."  "The panels are working, but one of the programs blah blah
blah."

Now for the Y2K tie-in:  this same company of buffoons and idiots are
also our Y2K contractors.  And the Y2K group and the MVS group are
working on the same programs at the same time.
```

#### ↳ Re: Status of Government Y2K?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KMKJ2.15165$Ek.14153396@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36F71FB5.6BF@bellsouth.net>`

```

Kitty Carr wrote:
>I don't think Judson McClendon got a responses from anyone in the
>government on the thread he started about the status of Y2K.  (Maybe
>because he asked for "direct knowledge?")  I don't have direct knowledge
>either, but . . .
(snip horror story)
>Now for the Y2K tie-in:  this same company of buffoons and idiots are
>also our Y2K contractors.  And the Y2K group and the MVS group are
>working on the same programs at the same time.

Thanks for the response Kitty.  That company reminds me of one who is
doing some of the Y2K work for one of my clients.
```

  roberto.minotti@NoSpam.accesinternet.com (Roberto Minotti) wrote:

<snip>
> Bref, il a un fichier data en Cobol, (ce qui pose pas de probl�mes) mais
> en compress�!
> Avec qqls chose comme une d�finition de taille de la data concern�e,
> suivi d'un laconique code "-3" comme s'il y avait plusieurs niveaux de
> compressions possibles dans ce langage.
<snip the rest>

COMP-3 ne veut pas dire 'comprim�', mais plut�t 'computational'. Donc, il
s'agit des donn�es num�riques. Le contenu correct d�pend du compilateur que
vous avez utilis�. S'il s'agit d'un COBOL sous IBM MVS ou OS/390, COMP-3 est
�gale a PACKED-DECIMAL, des autres compilateurs le font autrement. Vous
devriez indiquer le compilateur et le syst�me d'exploitation que vous avez
utilis�.

PACKED-DECIMAL S9(3)V99 (�gale 'S999V99' ou 'S9(3)V9(2)')par �xemple a besoin
de 3 octets. Chacque demi-octet contient une chifre (binaire 0 a 9, donc
'0000'B a '1001'B), le dernier demi-octet contient 'C' ('1100'B) si c'est
positive ou 'D' ('1101'B) si c'est n�gative. Le 'V' indique le virgule
d�cimale que vous ne voyez pas dans le fichier mais que vous devez conna�tre.

1234.56 dans cet �xemple est '12345C'X, -543.21 est '54321D'X

J'�sp�re que �a vous aide un peu. Et veuillez excuser mon fran�ais, je l'ai
appris a l'�cole et je n'ai plus �cris des lettres depuis des ann�es.

Bonnes salutations

Daniel

what a nasty combination eh?

I use (the ancient) IBM cobol I  and  multi user btrieve for the files.

All this  under Novel Netware 3.12 and (unfortunately) the 2 workstations
 have windows 95B as o/s.

the problem: when the 2 users  try to run the same programme at the same time
 they get a lovely "access denied" message (and this has nothing to do with
btrieve, I have checked).

The same programmes can be accessed with no problem from terminals
 with dos as the main o/s.

Any idea what's up? It's the bloody windows isinit?

I would appreciate any help

Zaffy :)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-BnLrlyC6gIGY@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7d8i1m$5mo$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 Mar 1999 17:12:30, zaffy@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> what a nasty combination eh?
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would appreciate any help

Get the Novell Client 32 drivers from Novells website.  The Microsoft 
Novell drivers "SEEM" designed to frustrate.

Go to your control panel, system settings.  Choose the Performance 
tab, then the File System button. Next, click the troubleshooting tab.
 Make sure the checkbox next to "Disable new file sharing and locking 
semantics".  Apply, and restart the workstations.  

Once you do all this, things will behave much better.
```

- **From:** glemol@aol.com (GLEMOL)
- **Date:** 1999-03-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990323125541.00663.00000529@ng101.aol.com>`

```
Hi, folks

We have a bunch of the EZtrieve programs that need to be made Y2K compliant.
Does new version of the EZtrieve have imbedded windowing? 
If someone has an experience in Y2K EZtrieve project we would appreciate any
assistance you might be able to provide.  Thanks in advance.

Alex

<a id="t-500921d6d9"></a>
## Thread: Another contest — 31 this month / 34 total

### Another contest

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl>`

```
Hello c.l.c,

It seems a new sport to post COBOL source with intentional flaws in it
or being a puzzle.
Here is mine:

identification division.
program-id. frog.
data division.
working-storage section.
01  sysdate.
    03  yy  pic xx.
    03  mm  pic xx.
    03  dd  pic xx.
01  editdate  pic xx/xx/xx.
procedure division.
p.
    accept sysdate from date
    move sysdate to editdate
    display editdate
    stop run
    .
end program frog.

Now my question:
What characters are displayed?
(answers in hex are allowed, but state ASCII or EBCDIC)

My students are often giving the wrong answers. How do you do?

The Frog
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zTrK2.64205$rs2.18405997@client.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Wed, 24 Mar 1999 22:50:37 +0100, "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)"
><H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>or being a puzzle.  Here is mine:
[snip]
>What happens if you do this instead:
>
>Move Sysdate (1:6) to Editdate?
>
>Neat eh?  One compiler I have is broken and doesn't do this correctly.


Since the purpose of these should be instructional, here's a hint for those
still puzzling this out:

Look up in your language reference manual the difference between a move
involving a group and a strictly alphanumeric move.  This helps you
understand the original puzzle.

To understand "Thane's change" (and his concern about the broken compiler),
check out the definition of reference modification.  What is the difference
between SYSDATE and SYSDATE(1:6)?

Tom Morrison
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36fb22dc.47012504@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net>`

```
'Twas Thu, 25 Mar 1999 02:33:38 GMT, when redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>>identification division.
>>program-id. frog.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>What characters are displayed?

"990325  " of course.

>What happens if you do this instead:
>
>Move Sysdate (1:6) to Editdate?

I would have expected it to still return "990325  ", but since you're
asking I looked it up in an online IBM manual and it says Sysdate (1:6) is
an elementary field and thus the result is "99/03/25".

Of course it doesn't actually say that reference modification is legal
with a group field, but we all know that it is -- isn't it?  This is what
IBM says at
http://ppdbooks.pok.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYLR201/1.5.1.9

>data-name-1 
>Must reference a data item whose usage is DISPLAY. 
<snip>
>If the data item referenced by data-name-1 is described as numeric, numeric-edited, alphabetic, or alphanumeric-edited,
>it is operated upon for purposes of reference modification as if it were redefined as an alphanumeric data item of the
>same size as the data item referenced by data-name-1. 

Is a group item DISPLAY?  I don't think so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews>`

```
On Fri, 26 Mar 1999 07:06:36 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
wrote:

>Is a group item DISPLAY?  I don't think so.

Eewwww.... what a nasty question!  Standards GURUs?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZUNK2.7$0V1.58126@client.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Fri, 26 Mar 1999 07:06:36 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
>wrote:
>>Is a group item DISPLAY?  I don't think so.
>Eewwww.... what a nasty question!  Standards GURUs?


No it is not.  But the discussion has been the fine distinction in the
context of a MOVE statement.  In this context, editing only occurs on
elementary moves, and a group source or destination operand changes the
rules.

We are now swerving into different territory.  I would conjecture that most
compilers do not enforce the rule quoted by Mr. Bart, since the programmer
can use REDEFINES to achieve the result, only with more aggravation and
obfuscation.  [RM/COBOL does not, even though it appears in our language
reference manual.]

Related to this, but not directly addressing the issue, is the fact that the
class of a group item is treated at object time as alphanumeric regardless
of the class of the elementary items subordinate to that group item.

Also, the USAGE DISPLAY clause on a group item applies to the elementary
items subordinate to that group item, not to the group item itself.

Regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<922465052.5186.0.nnrp-02.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net> <ZUNK2.7$0V1.58126@client.news.psi.net>`

```

Tom Morrison wrote in message ...
>
>Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Related to this, but not directly addressing the issue, is the fact that
the
>class of a group item is treated at object time as alphanumeric regardless
>of the class of the elementary items subordinate to that group item.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sometimes it depends on the compiler.  Due to a 'fix' error we had the
following type of move:

03  FLDA-C.
       05 FLDA  PIC Z(12)9.99.
03  FLDB-C.
       05 FLDB PIC 9(13).99.

MOVE FLDA-C TO FLDB-C.

When referring to FLDB, later, the AS400 processed it happily but MFCOBOL on
unix didn't like the fact that it contained spaces.  Unfortunately for us it
was only properly tested on the AS400.

Normally we have found the AS400 less forgiving than MFCOBOL.  In this case
we were wrong.

BTW the bug arose in a working program because someone thought they had to
change the PICs for a Euro change.  Unfortunately they missed one out.  As
far as I can remember, if the moves were at the elementary item level or all
the PICs were the same it would have been OK.

I guess this may not be relevant but the point is that the way some rules
are processed still depend on how forgiving/smart the compiler's generated
code is.

Regards
Rick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36fd80e3.111684769@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net> <ZUNK2.7$0V1.58126@client.news.psi.net> <922465052.5186.0.nnrp-02.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
'Twas Fri, 26 Mar 1999 16:18:15 -0000, when "Rick Price"
<rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>03  FLDA-C.
>       05 FLDA  PIC Z(12)9.99.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>was only properly tested on the AS400.

On a compiler compliant with X3.23-1985, this move is valid, and the
leading spaces should be converted to zeros.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7do2k4$hhc$1@client2.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net> <ZUNK2.7$0V1.58126@client.news.psi.net> <922465052.5186.0.nnrp-02.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <36fd80e3.111684769@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote in message <36fd80e3.111684769@netnews>...
>'Twas Fri, 26 Mar 1999 16:18:15 -0000, when "Rick Price"
><rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>When referring to FLDB, later, the AS400 processed it happily but MFCOBOL
on
>>unix didn't like the fact that it contained spaces.  Unfortunately for us
it
>>was only properly tested on the AS400.
>
>On a compiler compliant with X3.23-1985, this move is valid,

True so far...

> and the leading spaces should be converted to zeros.

Absolutely not!  Certainly not on the MOVE, as this would violate explicit
rules for the MOVE set down in the ANSI/ISO standard.  These rules are
tested in the 1985 CCVS compliance tests.

Mr. Price is referring to "later" use.   I presume the AS400 was happy in
its firmware to interpret SPACES as ZEROS, but Micro Focus was well within
its implementorial rights to
refuse to do so.  As M.F.'s milieu is 360/370/390 COBOL, I would guess that
their behaviour is mimicking the response of the mainframe.

Tom Morrison
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7di7vr$1v1@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net> <ZUNK2.7$0V1.58126@client.news.psi.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GZEpimwxqnzu@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Fri, 26 Mar 1999 15:41:45, "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
>wrote:
>
>>
>> Related to this, but not directly addressing the issue, is the fact that
the
>> class of a group item is treated at object time as alphanumeric
regardless
>> of the class of the elementary items subordinate to that group item.
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I know of no compiler that disallows it.

Thane,
  I agree with you that most (all?) compilers allow it, but I don't
understand why you think that this is "legal" according to the Standard.
The Standard requires that the data-item being referenced modified be USAGE
DISPLAY - not that it be ALPHANUMERIC.  The latter is true for group items,
but I don't think the former is (according to the Standard).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dojhg$dk8@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net> <ZUNK2.7$0V1.58126@client.news.psi.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-GZEpimwxqnzu@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7di7vr$1v1@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-fJcDGGTdh2Vc@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

>> >
>> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The Standard requires that the data-item being referenced modified be
USAGE
>> DISPLAY - not that it be ALPHANUMERIC.  The latter is true for group
items,
>> but I don't think the former is (according to the Standard).
>
>Agreed.  But are not Alphanumeric items considered Usage Display?
>

I have been looking thru the Standard and I can't find anything that says
that a group item has ANY "implicit" or "explicit" USAGE.  It is legal to
specify a USAGE on a group item - but it doesn't give the group that USAGE.
Therefore, I still think that reference modification of group items is
non-Standard. HOWEVER, as this is so commonly accepted, I am more than
willing to listen to someone who can show me where I am wrong or what I am
missing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-03-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36FF8DBB.BEA32225@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36f9a043.45453479@news1.ibm.net> <36fb22dc.47012504@netnews> <36fb8933.44493730@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I thought I'd test this on COBOL for MVS:
       01  SYSDATE.
           05   TEST-YY            PIC XX.
           05   TEST-MM            PIC XX.
           05   TEST-DD            PIC XX.
       01  EDITDATE  PIC XX/XX/XX.
-  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  7 Line(s) not Displayed
005700 PROCEDURE DIVISION .
005800 0000-ALL-SECTIONS-EXCEPT-ABORT SECTION.
005900 0100-MAINLINE.
006000
           ACCEPT SYSDATE FROM DATE
>>         MOVE SYSDATE TO EDITDATE
>>         DISPLAY 'TEST1 =' EDITDATE '.'
           MOVE SYSDATE (1:6) TO EDITDATE
>>         DISPLAY 'TEST2 =' EDITDATE '.'
006100     DISPLAY 'HJBTEST STARTED ' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (9:2)
006200                          ':' FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (11:2).
006300     DISPLAY ' '


produces:
TEST1 =990329  .
TEST2 =99/03/29.
HJBTEST STARTED 07:20

(The last lines are standard which I already had in HJBTEST (which I
wrote to test a called program)).  It's a bit inefficient, calling the
function twice (depending on compiler optimization, I suppose) - but is
only one cut and paste with everything visible.  After all that line
only gets executed once (a similar line gets executed upon exiting the
program).

Note to new programmers.  It is a good practice to display delimiters to
your displays.  See the two spaces after the first display before the
period?
```

#### ↳ Re: Another contest

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-03-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dfhu8$93v$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl>`

```
Since the sending field (sysdate) is a group item rather than an elementary
item, no editing is done. So, the receiving field contains '990326  ' today.
If sysdate were defined as pic x(6), the result would be '99/03/26'.

In article <36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl>,
  "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:

#### ↳ Re: Another contest

- **From:** "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)" <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-03-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36FE28C1.7E977541@IMN.nl>`

```
Hi all,

I waited a couple of days before looking at the answers.
[My time to read c.l.c. is limited. :( ]

Here is the outcome:

Date       GMT   R / W Who / Reaction
---------- ----- ----- ---------------------------------
1999-03-24 22:52 Right Frederico Fonseca
                       No explanation given.
     03-25 02:33 ?     Thane Hubbell
                       Very subtle workaround. I am most
                       sure Thane understands the case.
                       But he gave no answer nor explanation.
     03-25 14:38 ?     Tom Morrison.
                       Like Thane.
     03-25 19:25 Wrong Guy C. Fountain
                       Guy wrongly (IMHO) assumes 8 digits for
                       DATE i.s.o. 6. Secondly he overlooked
                       the type of move.
     03-25 19:50 Right Frederico Fonseca (again)
                       Answered correctly Thane's question.
     03-26 07:06 Right Randall Bart
                       Barticus shows lots of understanding too.
                       And he started a nice sub-thread about
                       (the interpretation of) the Standard.
                       But still no real explanation.
     03-26 08:53 Right Daniel Jacot
                       With explanation, short and right to
                       the point. Thanks Daniel.

The first right answer came from Frederico Fonseca, not more than 1 hour 2
minutes after the initial question. Lots of others followed, showing deep
understanding of COBOL. And allthough Daniel Jacot reacted later, some 34
hours, he was the first with an immediate explanation. But I appreciate
the earlier answers for not revealing all info: it stayed a puzzle that
way.
So, you are all smart guys. I'm proud of you!

In the Netherlands, and perhaps some other countries, it is probably more
overlooked than in the US, that a group move does no editing. Reasons:
1) The edit features for alphanumeric data, are seldom used (IME). Picture
character B(n) not often, because fillers X(n) will do the same. And the /
is seldom used, because dates are da-sh-ed- i.s.o sl/as/hed over here. So
lots of people never doing editing with @# data.
2) Editing numeric (f.e. ZZ9) is of course nearly never done by moving
ALHANUMERIC TO NUM-EDIT.
Thus people never move groups to edit, so never experiencing (i.s.o.
knowing) it doesn't work.

Happy days!
The Frog
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-03-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37006d72.179103284@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36FE28C1.7E977541@IMN.nl>`

```
'Twas Sun, 28 Mar 1999 15:04:01 +0200, when "COBOL Frog (Huib Klink)"
<H.Klink@IMN.nl> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>     03-26 07:06 Right Randall Bart
>                       Barticus shows lots of understanding too.
>                       And he started a nice sub-thread about
>                       (the interpretation of) the Standard.
>                       But still no real explanation.

You asked only for the results not an explanation.  If no one else
explained it, then I might have.  I explained it here last year.  In 1980,
I reported this condition as a bug against the Burroughs Medium Systems
CobolV compiler, and was directed to the right part of the standard.
Years later I had the honor of writing an official reply when someone else
filed a bug report for this same condition against Burroughs Large System
Cobol74.  This is an oddity I have been familiar with for a long time.

But Thane brought a new wrinkle:  Reference modification.  I thought a
reference modified field always kept the same attributes, thus it would be
a group, and it turns out that I'm wrong, but in part I'm wrong because
the standard is very vague.  

The draft standard endorses reference modification on groups, and makes
the resulting field elementary.
```
